> 2. **Ignoring the Cost-of-Error equation**: The exponential cost growth $C_{error}(L) = C_0 \cdot 10^L$ is a **favourite KTU question**. If you miss this, you lose 3-4 marks easily.
>
> 3. **Not drawing diagrams**: Part B questions (14 marks) **require** neat block diagrams. A textual answer without a diagram typically caps at 8-9 marks out of 14.
>
> 4. **Forgetting Meet-in-the-Middle**: KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests the **hybrid methodology**. Mentioning only pure top-down and pure bottom-up (without MiM) is considered an incomplete answer.
>
> 5. **Mixing up Bottom-Up with "Bottom-Level Only"**: Bottom-up means *building up* from primitives, not *staying* at the bottom. Always show the **composition arrow** direction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> - **Top-Down**: Starts at system spec, refines downward. Best for **novel designs**. **Risk: late error detection.**
> - **Bottom-Up**: Starts at cell library, composes upward. Best for **derivative designs**. **Risk: design inflexibility.**
> - **Meet-in-the-Middle (Hybrid)**: Industry standard. Combines top-down (architecture) + bottom-up (IP libraries) at RTL integration.
> - **Cost-of-Error Formula**: $C_{error}(L) = C_0 \cdot 10^L$ (memorize this!)
> - **Reusability Factor**: $R = \frac{N_{reused}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$
> - **Y-Chart (Gajski-Kuhn)**: Three domains — Behavioral, Structural, Physical. Top-Down moves outward → inward; Bottom-Up moves inward → outward.
> - **Typical Hierarchy Levels (N)**: 5 to 7 for modern SoCs.
> - **Industry TTM Target**: < 12 months for competitive SoCs.
> - **Key Tools**: Synopsys DC (synthesis), Cadence Innovus (P&R), Mentor Calibre (verification).
> - **Always draw** at least one block diagram in 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specification Requirement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT602 - SYSTEM ON CHIP DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Specification Requirement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **System on Chip (SoC) Design** under the KTU 2024 scheme, the **Specification Requirement** phase (often called the *System Specification* or *Product Specification* stage) is the foundational step of the SoC design flow. It is the process of capturing, analyzing, and formally documenting **what the chip must do** and **under what constraints** it must perform — before any architectural decisions, RTL coding, or physical implementation begins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A Specification Requirement is a quantified, verifiable, and traceable set of functional, performance, power, area, interface, and economic constraints that drive every subsequent decision in the SoC design flow (architecture → RTL → verification → physical design).

The output of this phase is typically a **System Requirements Specification (SRS)** document (or *Product Requirement Document — PRD*) that serves as the **single source of truth** for the entire engineering team.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of an SoC Specification

Every SoC specification, regardless of application domain, can be reduced to three orthogonal pillars:

| Pillar | Question Answered | Typical Metrics |
|---|---|---|
| **Functional Spec** | *What does the chip do?* | Instruction set, peripherals, standards supported |
| **Non-Functional Spec** | *How well does it do it?* | Performance, Power, Area (PPA) |
| **Economic / Manufacturability Spec** | *Can it be built profitably?* | Cost, yield, process node, time-to-market |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Building a House"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of an SoC specification as the **architectural blueprint of a house** before construction begins.
>
> - The **functional spec** is the *room layout* (how many bedrooms, a kitchen, a garage).
> - The **performance spec** is the *quality of materials* (Italian marble vs. local granite).
> - The **power spec** is the *electricity bill budget* (solar panels vs. grid-only).
> - The **area spec** is the *plot size* (1200 sq.ft vs. 4000 sq.ft).
> - The **I/O spec** is the *number of doors, windows, and balconies*.
> - The **cost spec** is the *total sanctioned budget* for the owner.
>
> Just as you cannot pour the foundation before the blueprint is signed by the client, you cannot write a single line of RTL before the SoC specification is frozen and signed off.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in SoC Specs

The following constants and metrics are **universally used** in specification documents and **must be memorized** for KTU exams:

- **Process node wavelengths** are typically expressed in **nanometers (nm)** — e.g., **180 nm, 65 nm, 28 nm, 7 nm, 3 nm**.
- **Clock frequency** is expressed in **Hertz (Hz)** — typically **MHz** or **GHz**.
- **Power** is expressed in **Watts (W)** or **milliwatts (mW)**.
- **Energy per operation** is expressed in **picojoules (pJ)** or **femtojoules (fJ)**.
- **Throughput** is measured in **MIPS** (Million Instructions Per Second), **DMIPS**, or **GOPS** (Giga Operations Per Second).
- **Area** is expressed in **mm²** or **gate equivalents (GE)**.
- **Yield** is a dimensionless percentage, typically **≥ 90 %** for mature nodes.
- **Voltage** is expressed in **Volts (V)**, with modern cores operating near **0.7 V – 1.2 V**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A specification is only valid if every requirement is **measurable**. Phrases like "low power" or "fast" are **not specifications** — they are wishes. Valid forms are: *"Static power ≤ 5 mW"* and *"Core frequency ≥ 1.2 GHz"*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Decomposition of Specifications

A robust SoC specification follows a **top-down hierarchical decomposition**. KTU examiners frequently test whether a student can correctly classify a requirement into the right layer.

### Layer 1 — Market-Level Requirements
- Target market segment (smartphone, automotive, IoT, data-center, aerospace).
- Cost ceiling in **USD**.
- Time-to-market window (e.g., must tape-out by Q3 2026).
- Competitive benchmarking against existing products (e.g., must beat Snapdragon X by 15 % on a specific benchmark).

### Layer 2 — System-Level Requirements
- **Functional behavior:** supported instruction set (e.g., RISC-V RV64IMAC), operating system (Linux, RTOS, Android), peripheral list (USB 3.0, PCIe Gen4, I²C, SPI, UART, MIPI-CSI).
- **Performance targets:** CPU clock ≥ **1.5 GHz**, GPU ≥ **800 MHz**, memory bandwidth ≥ **25.6 GB/s**.
- **Power budget:** total SoC ≤ **3 W**, always-on domain ≤ **50 µW**.
- **Area target:** die size ≤ **X mm²** (often derived from cost).
- **Thermal envelope:** junction temperature **Tj ≤ 125 °C** (automotive grade) or **85 °C** (commercial).

### Layer 3 — Block-Level Requirements
- Each IP block (CPU core, GPU, DSP, NPU, memory controller) gets its **own micro-specification** derived from the system-level spec.
- Example: *"NPU must deliver ≥ 4 TOPS at INT8 precision with ≤ 500 mW."*

### Layer 4 — Verification Requirements
- Functional coverage targets (typically **≥ 95 %**).
- Code coverage (line, branch, expression, toggle) — target **≥ 90 %**.
- Power-aware verification scenarios.
- Compliance with safety standards (e.g., **ISO 26262** for automotive).

## 2.2 The "Spec → Architecture" Traceability Matrix

Every architectural decision must trace back to a specification item. This is enforced through a **Traceability Matrix** (often a spreadsheet in industry, or a table in a viva).

| Spec ID | Requirement | Architectural Decision | Verification Test |
|---|---|---|---|
| REQ-PWR-01 | Active power ≤ 2 W | Use clock gating + DVFS | Power regression suite |
| REQ-PERF-02 | CPU ≥ 1.5 GHz | 3-issue superscalar pipeline | Dhrystone benchmark |
| REQ-IO-03 | USB 3.0 support | Integrate Synopsys USB IP | USB-IF compliance suite |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **the most tested** in Module 1 of PCEVT602. Memorize the units and the **boundary conditions**.

$$
\text{Dynamic Power} \;\; P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^{2} \cdot f
$$

$$
\text{Static Power} \;\; P_{static} = V \cdot I_{leak}
$$

$$
\text{Total Power} \;\; P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{static}
$$

$$
\text{Performance per Watt} \;\; \eta = \frac{\text{Throughput [OPS]}}{P_{total}\,[\text{W}]} \;\;\;\text{[GOPS/W or TOPS/W]}
$$

$$
\text{Memory Bandwidth} \;\; BW = \text{Bus Width [bytes]} \times \text{Bus Frequency [Hz]}
$$

$$
\text{Amdahl's Law (Speedup)} \;\; S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}
$$

$$
\text{CPU Time} \;\; T_{CPU} = \frac{\text{Instructions} \times \text{CPI}}{\text{Clock Frequency}}
$$

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{clock}\,[\text{Hz}]}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
\text{Die Cost} \;\; C_{die} = \frac{C_{wafer}}{N_{dies} \times Y}
$$

$$
N_{dies} = \frac{\pi \cdot (D/2)^{2}}{A_{die}} - \frac{\pi \cdot D}{\sqrt{2 \cdot A_{die}}}
$$

$$
Y \approx e^{-D_{0} \cdot A} \;\;\; \text{(Poisson yield model)}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\alpha$ | Switching activity factor | dimensionless, 0–1 |
| $C$ | Load capacitance | Farads (F) |
| $V$ | Supply voltage | Volts (V) |
| $f$ | Clock frequency | Hertz (Hz) |
| $I_{leak}$ | Leakage current | Amperes (A) |
| $f$ | Parallelizable fraction (Amdahl) | dimensionless, 0–1 |
| $N$ | Number of processors | dimensionless |
| $C_{wafer}$ | Wafer cost | USD |
| $D$ | Wafer diameter | mm (150, 200, 300) |
| $A_{die}$ | Die area | mm² |
| $D_0$ | Defect density | defects / cm² |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These specifications are **not academic abstractions** — they directly determine:

- **Smartphone SoCs** (Qualcomm Snapdragon, Apple A-series, MediaTek Dimensity) are specified to hit a **performance-per-watt sweet spot** for battery life.
- **Automotive SoCs** (Nvidia Orin, Mobileye EyeQ) must be specified against **ISO 26262** functional-safety budgets.
- **IoT SoCs** (ESP32, nRF54) are specified for **ultra-low standby power** (often < 1 µW) for battery lifetimes of 10+ years.
- **Data-center AI SoCs** (Google TPU, AWS Trainium) are specified for **raw TOPS** and **HBM bandwidth** — power is secondary.
- **Aerospace / Defense SoCs** are specified for **radiation tolerance (TID, SEU)** and **extreme temperature operation**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are the **highest-weight numerical questions** asked in KTU ESE for this module. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly.

## 3.1 Derivation: Power-Performance Trade-off from First Principles

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A SoC is specified to operate at $V_{1} = 1.2\,\text{V}$ and $f_{1} = 1.0\,\text{GHz}$ with a switching activity $\alpha = 0.15$ and total switched capacitance $C = 5\,\text{nF}$. The specification team wants to lower the voltage to $V_{2} = 0.8\,\text{V}$ and reduce the frequency to $f_{2} = 600\,\text{MHz}$ to meet a thermal envelope. Ignoring static power, compute the **dynamic power ratio** and the **energy per clock cycle** in both cases.

### Step 1 — Write the dynamic power equation

The fundamental equation is:

$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^{2} \cdot f
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Mode 1 values

$$
P_{1} = (0.15) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{F}) \cdot (1.2)^{2} \cdot (1.0 \times 10^{9}\,\text{Hz})
$$

$$
P_{1} = 0.15 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 1.44 \cdot 10^{9}
$$

$$
P_{1} = 0.15 \cdot 7.2
$$

$$
P_{1} = 1.08\;\text{W}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Mode 2 values

$$
P_{2} = (0.15) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{F}) \cdot (0.8)^{2} \cdot (600 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Hz})
$$

$$
P_{2} = 0.15 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 0.64 \cdot 6 \times 10^{8}
$$

$$
P_{2} = 0.15 \cdot 1.92
$$

$$
P_{2} = 0.288\;\text{W}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the power ratio

$$
\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} = \frac{0.288}{1.08} = 0.2667
$$

This means the lower-power mode consumes only **~26.67 %** of the original power.

### Step 5 — Compute energy per cycle in each mode

Energy per cycle is $E = P / f$:

$$
E_{1} = \frac{P_{1}}{f_{1}} = \frac{1.08}{1 \times 10^{9}} = 1.08 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{J} = 1.08\;\text{nJ}
$$

$$
E_{2} = \frac{P_{2}}{f_{2}} = \frac{0.288}{600 \times 10^{6}} = 4.8 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{J} = 0.48\;\text{nJ}
$$

### Step 6 — Engineering interpretation

The energy per cycle is reduced by a factor of:

$$
\frac{E_{2}}{E_{1}} = \frac{0.48}{1.08} = 0.4444
$$

This **55.6 % reduction in energy per operation** is the direct justification for specifying **Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS)** in nearly every modern SoC specification.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Die Cost & Yield Estimation (Foundry Economics)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A 300 mm wafer costs $C_{wafer} = 1500$ USD to fabricate on a 28 nm process. Each die has area $A_{die} = 50\,\text{mm}^{2}$ and the defect density is $D_0 = 0.4$ defects/cm². Compute the (a) number of gross dies per wafer, (b) the yield using the Poisson model, and (c) the **die cost**.

### Step 1 — Compute the wafer area in mm²

$$
A_{wafer} = \pi \cdot \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^{2} = \pi \cdot (150)^{2} = 22500\pi \approx 70685.83\;\text{mm}^{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert die area into the same units and use the die-per-wafer formula

The standard industry formula is:

$$
N_{dies} = \frac{\pi \cdot (D/2)^{2}}{A_{die}} - \frac{\pi \cdot D}{\sqrt{2 \cdot A_{die}}}
$$

$$
N_{dies} = \frac{70685.83}{50} - \frac{\pi \cdot 300}{\sqrt{100}}
$$

$$
N_{dies} = 1413.72 - \frac{942.48}{10}
$$

$$
N_{dies} = 1413.72 - 94.25
$$

$$
N_{dies} \approx 1319\;\text{dies (gross)}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Poisson yield model

First, normalize units. $D_0 = 0.4$ defects/cm² = $0.4 \times 10^{-2}$ defects/mm² = $0.004$ defects/mm².

$$
Y = e^{-D_{0} \cdot A_{die}} = e^{-0.004 \times 50} = e^{-0.2}
$$

$$
Y \approx 0.8187 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 81.87\,\%
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the good-die count

$$
N_{good} = N_{dies} \times Y = 1319 \times 0.8187 \approx 1080\;\text{good dies}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the die cost

$$
C_{die} = \frac{C_{wafer}}{N_{good}} = \frac{1500}{1080} \approx 1.39\;\text{USD per die}
$$

### Step 6 — Engineering interpretation

If the marketing spec demands a **die cost ≤ 1.00 USD**, the design team has only two levers:

1. **Shrink the die** (reduce $A_{die}$) — by **DFM** and **area optimization**.
2. **Move to a more mature node** (lower $D_0$) — e.g., from 28 nm to 40 nm.
3. **Use redundancy** (e.g., spare rows in SRAM) — this is a *specification* decision.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Process Node

The specification phase must include a **process-node trade-off matrix**. A KTU-favorite exam-style question is to ask students to *justify* a process-node choice from a specification list.

| Specification | Favors Advanced Node (e.g., 7 nm) | Favors Mature Node (e.g., 28 nm) |
|---|---|---|
| High clock frequency (≥ 2 GHz) | ✅ (faster transistors) | ❌ |
| Low dynamic power (battery life) | ✅ (lower $C$, lower $V$) | ❌ |
| Low static / leakage power | ⚠️ (worse at advanced node) | ✅ |
| Low die cost | ❌ (wafer is expensive) | ✅ |
| High analog precision | ❌ (worse analog at 7 nm) | ✅ |
| Radiation tolerance | ❌ | ✅ (often mandatory for aerospace) |
| High integration density | ✅ | ❌ |
| Long product lifetime (≥ 10 yrs supply) | ❌ (foundry may EOL) | ✅ |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical: Amdahl's Law for a Multi-Core SoC

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** An SoC contains 8 homogeneous CPU cores. Benchmarks show that **75 %** of the workload is perfectly parallelizable. Compute the maximum speedup over a single-core baseline.

### Step 1 — Identify the parameters

$f = 0.75$ (parallelizable fraction), $N = 8$ (number of cores).

### Step 2 — Apply Amdahl's Law

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}
$$

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.75) + \dfrac{0.75}{8}}
$$

$$
S = \frac{1}{0.25 + 0.09375}
$$

$$
S = \frac{1}{0.34375} \approx 2.909
$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

Even with **8 cores** and **75 % parallel code**, the maximum achievable speedup is only **~2.91×**. This is a **classic specification pitfall** — adding more cores to a workload that is not parallelizable yields *zero* benefit and wastes silicon area and power.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The SoC Design Flow — Position of the Specification Phase

The following Mermaid block shows where the **Specification Requirement** phase sits in the complete SoC design flow. Note that it is the **first gate** — nothing downstream can begin until the spec is signed off.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Market Need and Use Case] --> B[Specification Requirement Document]
    B --> C[Architecture Design]
    C --> D[IP Selection and Integration]
    D --> E[RTL Design and Coding]
    E --> F[Functional Verification]
    F --> G[Synthesis and PnR]
    G --> H[Physical Verification and Signoff]
    H --> I[Tapeout to Foundry]
    I --> J[Package and Test]
    J --> K[Production Silicon]
    B --> L[Verification Plan]
    L --> F
    C --> M[Power and Performance Budget]
    M --> E
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Decomposition of a Specification Document

This diagram illustrates the four-level decomposition of an SoC specification, as discussed in Section 2.1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Layer 1 Market Requirements] --> L2[Layer 2 System Requirements]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Block Level Requirements]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Verification Requirements]
    L1 --> M1[Cost and Schedule]
    L1 --> M2[Competitive Benchmarking]
    L2 --> F1[Functional Spec]
    L2 --> N1[Non Functional PPA]
    L2 --> I1[Interface Spec]
    L3 --> B1[CPU Spec]
    L3 --> B2[GPU Spec]
    L3 --> B3[NPU Spec]
    L3 --> B4[Memory Controller Spec]
    L3 --> B5[Peripheral Spec]
    L4 --> V1[Functional Coverage Plan]
    L4 --> V2[Power Verification Plan]
    L4 --> V3[Compliance and Safety Plan]
```

## 4.3 Traceability Matrix Flow — From Spec to Test

This block shows how a single requirement propagates from specification all the way to the verification test that proves it has been met.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REQ[REQ ID 001 NPU must deliver 4 TOPS at INT8] --> ARCH[Architectural Decision Use systolic array with 4096 MACs]
    ARCH --> RTL[RTL Implementation Verilog of the MAC array]
    RTL --> SYN[Synthesis Gate level netlist with PPA report]
    SYN --> VERIF[Verification Test directed test plus constrained random]
    VERIF --> SIGN[Signoff PPA meets spec plus coverage 95 percent]
    SIGN --> TAPE[Tapeout Approval]
```

## 4.4 The Specification Review Gate

A KTU viva-favorite is the **gate-review diagram**. The specification must pass through three approval gates before architecture work begins.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Draft v0 1] --> R1[Technical Review]
    R1 --> Q1{Meets Measurability and Verifiability}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R2[Marketing and Cost Review]
    Q1 -- No --> D2[Revise Draft]
    D2 --> D1
    R2 --> Q2{Meets Cost and Schedule Targets}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R3[Customer or Standards Compliance]
    Q2 -- No --> D2
    R3 --> Q3{Meets Safety and Regulatory Requirements}
    Q3 -- Yes --> APPROVED[Frozen Specification]
    Q3 -- No --> D2
    APPROVED --> NEXT[Architecture Phase Begins]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Similar]** 
*List any six items that must be included in a System-on-Chip specification document.*

**Model Answer:**

A complete SoC specification document must include:

1. **Functional specification** — the operations the chip must perform (e.g., instruction set, supported peripherals, standards).
2. **Performance specification** — clock frequency, throughput, latency targets.
3. **Power specification** — active power, standby power, and energy-per-operation budgets.
4. **Area specification** — maximum die size in mm² or gate equivalents.
5. **Interface specification** — list of I/O protocols (USB, PCIe, MIPI, I²C, SPI, Ethernet) and their bandwidth.
6. **Cost and schedule specification** — die cost ceiling, tape-out date, and product lifetime.
7. **Environmental specification** — operating temperature range, supply voltage range, and reliability targets (MTBF).
8. **Compliance specification** — applicable safety/regulatory standards (e.g., ISO 26262, FCC, CE, RoHS).

*(Any six well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar]**
*Why is the specification phase considered the most critical step in the SoC design flow?*

**Model Answer:**

The specification phase is the most critical because:

1. **Single Source of Truth:** All downstream decisions — architecture, RTL, verification, physical design — are derived from it. Any error propagates and becomes exponentially expensive to fix later.
2. **Cost of Error:** A specification error caught at the architecture stage may cost $1 to fix; the same error caught post-tape-out can cost **$1 million+** in mask re-spins and missed market windows.
3. **Defines the PPA Triangle:** Performance, Power, and Area are *negotiated* during specification. Once frozen, designers must hit the spec, not re-negotiate it.
4. **Drives Verification:** The verification plan is built *from* the specification, not from the RTL. A weak spec produces weak verification.
5. **Contractual:** It legally binds the design house to the customer or product manager.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):**
> - **Do NOT** write generic paragraphs like "Specification is important because it defines what to build." Examiners award marks for **enumerated, quantified, and example-supported** answers.
> - **Do NOT** confuse *System Specification* with *Architectural Specification*. They are different layers.
> - **Do NOT** skip the formula derivation steps. In a 7-mark sub-question, **at least 4 marks** are reserved for the algebraic working.
> - **Always** state units explicitly. Writing "1.08" without "Watts" costs 0.5 mark.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [Model Question, KTU ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete SoC design flow** and clearly mark the position of the *Specification Requirement* phase. Discuss in detail the **contents of an SoC specification document** under the following heads: (i) Functional spec, (ii) Performance spec, (iii) Power spec, (iv) Area spec, (v) Interface spec, (vi) Cost and schedule spec. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A SoC is specified to operate at a clock frequency of $1.2\,\text{GHz}$ with a supply voltage of $1.1\,\text{V}$. The average switching activity is $\alpha = 0.2$ and the total switched capacitance is $C = 8\,\text{nF}$. The specification team proposes **Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS)** to drop the voltage to $0.85\,\text{V}$ and the frequency to $800\,\text{MHz}$ during a low-power mode. Compute:
   (i) The dynamic power in the **active mode** and in the **low-power mode**.
   (ii) The percentage reduction in power and energy-per-cycle.
   (iii) Comment on how this calculation justifies the **inclusion of DVFS in the SoC specification**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a)**

**Block Diagram:** (Draw the SoC design flow as shown in Section 4.1 of these notes — arrows from Market Need → Specification → Architecture → RTL → Verification → Physical Design → Tapeout → Production.)

**Contents of the SoC Specification Document:**

**(i) Functional Specification:**
This defines *what the chip does*. It includes the supported instruction set architecture (e.g., RISC-V RV64IMAC, ARMv9), the operating system it must run (Linux, Android, RTOS), the list of peripherals (USB, PCIe, MIPI, I²C, SPI, Ethernet, CAN), and the functional modes (active, idle, sleep, deep-sleep).

**(ii) Performance Specification:**
This defines *how fast* the chip must operate. Typical metrics are CPU clock frequency (e.g., $\geq 1.2\,\text{GHz}$), memory bandwidth (e.g., $\geq 25.6\,\text{GB/s}$), interrupt latency (e.g., $\leq 1\,\mu\text{s}$), and application-level benchmarks (e.g., Dhrystone $\geq 3.0$ DMIPS/MHz).

**(iii) Power Specification:**
This defines the energy budget. For battery-powered SoCs, total active power is often capped (e.g., $\leq 2\,\text{W}$), and standby power is severely constrained (e.g., $\leq 50\,\mu\text{W}$ for always-on blocks). Power is governed by the equation $P = \alpha C V^2 f$.

**(iv) Area Specification:**
This defines the maximum die size, e.g., $\leq 80\,\text{mm}^{2}$, or a maximum gate count, e.g., $\leq 50\,\text{MGE}$. The area spec is usually *derived* from the cost spec using the foundry's die-cost equation $C_{die} = C_{wafer} / (N_{dies} \cdot Y)$.

**(v) Interface Specification:**
This lists the external protocols, their speeds, and the physical layer. Examples: USB 3.2 Gen 2x2 (20 Gbps), PCIe Gen 4 (16 GT/s), MIPI-CSI2 (4 lanes), HDMI 2.1, and the number of general-purpose I/O pins.

**(vi) Cost and Schedule Specification:**
This defines the maximum die cost (e.g., $\leq \$3$), the wafer cost envelope, the tape-out date, and the expected product lifetime (e.g., 7 years of supply for industrial customers).

#### **Model Solution (b)**

**(i) Active Mode Power:**

$$
P_{active} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_1^2 \cdot f_1
$$

$$
P_{active} = 0.2 \cdot 8 \times 10^{-9} \cdot (1.1)^{2} \cdot 1.2 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
P_{active} = 0.2 \cdot 8 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 1.21 \cdot 1.2 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
P_{active} = 0.2 \cdot 11.616
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{active} = 2.3232\;\text{W}}
$$

**Low-Power Mode Power:**

$$
P_{lp} = 0.2 \cdot 8 \times 10^{-9} \cdot (0.85)^{2} \cdot 800 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
P_{lp} = 0.2 \cdot 8 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 0.7225 \cdot 8 \times 10^{8}
$$

$$
P_{lp} = 0.2 \cdot 4.624
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{lp} = 0.9248\;\text{W}}
$$

**(ii) Percentage Reduction:**

$$
\Delta P\,\% = \frac{P_{active} - P_{lp}}{P_{active}} \times 100 = \frac{2.3232 - 0.9248}{2.3232} \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta P\,\% = \frac{1.3984}{2.3232} \times 100 \approx 60.19\,\%
$$

**Energy per cycle:**

$$
E_{active} = \frac{P_{active}}{f_1} = \frac{2.3232}{1.2 \times 10^{9}} = 1.936\;\text{nJ/cycle}
$$

$$
E_{lp} = \frac{P_{lp}}{f_2} = \frac{0.9248}{800 \times 10^{6}} = 1.156\;\text{nJ/cycle}
$$

$$
\Delta E\,\% = \frac{1.936 - 1.156}{1.936} \times 100 \approx 40.29\,\%
$$

**(iii) Engineering Justification:**

The calculation shows that by dropping the voltage by only 22.7 % (1.1 V → 0.85 V) and the frequency by 33.3 % (1.2 GHz → 0.8 GHz), the SoC achieves a **60 % power reduction** and a **40 % energy-per-cycle reduction**. Since the dynamic power equation is **quadratic in voltage** but only **linear in frequency**, voltage scaling is the most power-efficient knob. This numerical result is the formal justification for **mandating DVFS as a non-negotiable item in the SoC specification** for any battery-powered or thermally-constrained product.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **PPA triangle (Performance, Power, Area)** in the context of SoC specification. Describe how a specification engineer *negotiates* trade-offs between them. Use a real-world example (e.g., smartphone SoC vs. IoT SoC) to illustrate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 300 mm wafer is processed at a 28 nm foundry at a cost of $C_{wafer} = 2000$ USD. The defect density is $D_0 = 0.6$ defects/cm². Each die has an area of $A_{die} = 80\,\text{mm}^{2}$. The marketing team has set a die-cost ceiling of **\$1.50**.
   (i) Compute the gross number of dies per wafer.
   (ii) Compute the yield using the Poisson model.
   (iii) Compute the actual die cost and determine whether the spec is met.
   (iv) If the spec is not met, propose **two specification-level actions** to bring the cost within budget. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a)**

**The PPA Triangle:** Performance, Power, and Area are the three fundamental, mutually-constraining axes of every SoC specification. **Improving one typically degrades the other two.**

- *Performance ↑ → Power ↑ and/or Area ↑.*
- *Power ↓ → Performance ↓ and/or Area ↑ (need more complex logic for the same work).*
- *Area ↓ → Performance ↓ and/or Power ↑ (smaller transistors are slower and leakier).*

**The Specification Engineer's Role:** The spec engineer must find a **Pareto-optimal point** that satisfies the application's needs. They use quantitative models:

- $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V^2 f$ — to trade voltage for power and speed.
- $C_{die} = C_{wafer} / (N_{dies} \cdot Y)$ — to trade area for cost.
- Amdahl's Law — to trade cores for speedup.

**Real-World Comparison:**

- A **smartphone SoC** (e.g., Snapdragon) prioritizes **performance-per-watt**. The spec demands a high CPU/GPU frequency but caps total power at ~3 W. Trade-off: shrink the process node aggressively (e.g., 4 nm) to get speed *and* power savings, at the cost of a very expensive wafer.
- An **IoT SoC** (e.g., ESP32) prioritizes **energy-per-task and cost**. The spec demands 10+ year battery life. Trade-off: use a mature 40 nm node with very low leakage, accept a slow CPU (160 MHz), and use aggressive duty-cycling instead of more cores.

In both cases, the *specification* — not the architecture — dictates the trade-off.

#### **Model Solution (b)**

**(i) Gross Dies per Wafer:**

$$
N_{dies} = \frac{\pi (D/2)^2}{A_{die}} - \frac{\pi D}{\sqrt{2 A_{die}}}
$$

$$
N_{dies} = \frac{\pi \cdot 150^2}{80} - \frac{\pi \cdot 300}{\sqrt{160}}
$$

$$
N_{dies} = \frac{70685.83}{80} - \frac{942.48}{12.649}
$$

$$
N_{dies} = 883.57 - 74.51 \approx 809\;\text{gross dies}
$$

**(ii) Yield (Poisson Model):**

Convert units: $D_0 = 0.6$ defects/cm² = $0.006$ defects/mm².

$$
Y = e^{-0.006 \times 80} = e^{-0.48} \approx 0.6188\;\text{or}\;61.88\,\%
$$

**(iii) Die Cost:**

$$
N_{good} = 809 \times 0.6188 \approx 501\;\text{good dies}
$$

$$
C_{die} = \frac{2000}{501} \approx 3.99\;\text{USD}
$$

**Comparison:** The die cost of **\$3.99** is **2.66× higher** than the **\$1.50 ceiling**. The specification is **NOT met**.

**(iv) Specification-Level Actions to Reduce Cost:**

1. **Shrink the die area** — Through architectural optimization (e.g., move from a 4-core to a 2-core CPU, share the memory controller, drop a rarely-used peripheral). For example, reducing $A_{die}$ from 80 mm² to 40 mm² roughly **doubles** $N_{dies}$ and **quadruples** $N_{dies} \times Y$ (since yield rises sharply with smaller area). This would bring the die cost well under \$1.50.
2. **Move to a more mature node with lower $D_0$.** For example, migrating from 28 nm to 40 nm typically halves $D_0$ and lowers $C_{wafer}$. This simultaneously increases $Y$ and reduces the wafer cost denominator, which can cut die cost by 40–60 %.
3. *(Bonus valid action)* **Negotiate the cost ceiling upward with marketing** — or use multi-project wafer (MPW) shuttles to share $C_{wafer}$ across multiple designs.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize This List:**

- ☐ The specification phase is the **first gate** of the SoC design flow; nothing begins until it is frozen.
- ☐ A specification has **three pillars**: Functional, Non-Functional (PPA), and Economic.
- ☐ Specifications must be **measurable and verifiable** — "low power" is not a spec; "≤ 2 W active" is.
- ☐ The **PPA triangle** (Performance, Power, Area) is the core trade-off tool.
- ☐ **Dynamic power** is $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V^{2} f$ — quadratic in $V$, linear in $f$.
- ☐ **Static / leakage power** is $P_{static} = V \cdot I_{leak}$ — dominates at advanced nodes (≤ 16 nm).
- ☐ **Amdahl's Law** $S = 1 / [(1-f) + f/N]$ limits multi-core speedup — even 8 cores give only ~2.91× speedup for 75 % parallel code.
- ☐ **Die cost** = $C_{wafer} / (N_{dies} \cdot Y)$ — yield rises as die area shrinks.
- ☐ **DVFS** is justified by the $V^{2}$ law — a 25 % voltage drop gives ~44 % power reduction at the same frequency.
- ☐ Specs include: clock frequency, latency, throughput, MIPs/DMIPs, TOPS, bandwidth (GB/s), area (mm²/GE), power (W/mW), energy (pJ/op), temperature (°C), MTBF, and cost (USD).
- ☐ Trace every spec item to an **architectural decision** and a **verification test** — this is the traceability matrix.
- ☐ Process node trade-off: **advanced node** = faster + lower dynamic power + more expensive + higher leakage. **Mature node** = cheaper + lower leakage + slower.
- ☐ Specification errors caught post-tape-out cost **1000×** more than errors caught at the spec stage.
- ☐ Always state **units** in numerical answers (V, GHz, mW, mm², USD).
- ☐ For Part B derivations, show **every algebraic step** — examiners award working marks, not just final answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Specification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEVT602 - SYSTEM ON CHIP DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Types of Specification # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a "Specification" in SoC Design? In the context of **System on Chip (SoC) Design**, a **Specification** is a precise, complete, and unambiguous description of *what* the chip must do, *how* it must behave, and *under what constraints* it must operate, **before** any hardware description or physical implementation begins. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner phrasing):** > *"A specification is a formal contract between the system architect and the design/implementation team. It captures the functional behavior, performance goals, power budget, interface protocols, and design constraints of the SoC in a verifiable form."* ### The "Why" Behind Specification An SoC can contain **billions of transistors**, multiple CPUs, GPUs, memory controllers, and IP blocks. Without a clear specification, it is impossible to: - Coordinate between hardware and software teams - Perform **verification** (Is the design correct?) - Perform **validation** (Does it meet the customer's needs?) - Enable **IP reuse** across projects - Manage **time-to-market** pressure ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Blueprint" Imagine you want to build a house. You wouldn't just hand the contractor a verbal wish-list. Instead, you produce: | Specification Type | House Analogy | SoC Equivalent | |---|---|---| | Functional Spec | "3 bedrooms, 2 bathrooms, kitchen" | "Decoder supports H.264, USB 3.0, runs Linux" | | Non-Functional Spec | "Must withstand 120 km/h winds" | "Must consume < 1 W, run at 1 GHz" | | Executable Spec | A 3D walkthrough model | A SystemC transaction-level model | | Formal Spec | Mathematical proof of structural integrity | Assertion-based verification using SVA | > [!NOTE] > The specification is the **most important document** in an SoC project — errors caught at the spec stage cost **$1**, the same errors caught during tape-out cost **$1,000,000+**. This is the famous "**10× cost escalation rule**" in VLSI design. ## 1.3 High-Level Classification of Specifications At the broadest level, KTU examiners expect students to recognize the following five orthogonal ways of classifying specifications: 1. **Functional vs. Non-Functional** 2. **By Level of Abstraction** (System → Behavioral → RTL → Gate → Physical) 3. **Executable vs. Non-Executable** 4. **Formal vs. Semi-Formal vs. Informal** 5. **Hardware Specification vs. Software Specification** Each is discussed exhaustively in Section 2. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification #1: Functional vs. Non-Functional Specifications ### 2.1.1 Functional Specification A **functional specification** describes **what the SoC must do** — its logical behavior, data transformations, and control flow. **Examples:** - "The MPEG-4 decoder shall decode a 1080p video stream at 30 fps" - "The UART shall transmit data at 115200 baud with 8-N-1 format" - "The DMA controller shall transfer 1 KB of data from SRAM to peripheral in under 100 µs" ### 2.1.2 Non-Functional Specification A **non-functional specification** describes **how well** the SoC must perform — quality attributes and constraints. **Categories (the classic "-ilities" + metrics):** - **Performance**: Throughput, latency, clock frequency - **Power**: Static power, dynamic power, energy per operation - **Area**: Gate count, die size in mm² - **Thermal**: Maximum junction temperature (T_j) - **Reliability**: MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) - **Testability**: Fault coverage, BIST support - **Security**: Tamper resistance, encryption support - **Manufacturability**: Yield, process node compatibility ### 2.1.3 The Non-Functional Power Equation (Must Memorize) The **dynamic power** consumed by an SoC is governed by the well-known equation: $$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$ Where: - $\alpha$ = **switching activity factor** (probability of a 0→1 transition) - $C$ = **load capacitance** of the circuit - $V_{DD}$ = **supply voltage** - $f$ = **operating clock frequency** The **static (leakage) power** is: $$P_{static} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$$ **Total Power:** $$P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static}$$ > [!NOTE] > This equation is a **favourite KTU question** because it directly ties the *non-functional specification* (power budget) to *design parameters* (voltage, frequency, capacitance). A $20\%$ reduction in $V_{DD}$ reduces dynamic power by approximately **$44\%$** (since $P \propto V^2$). ## 2.2 Classification #2: Specifications by Level of Abstraction (Gajski-Kuhn Y-Chart) The **Y-Chart** (Gajski-Kuhn chart) is the **single most important diagram** for KTU Module 1. It identifies three axes of design and multiple abstraction levels. The specification evolves as we move from the outermost (highest) to the innermost (lowest) ring. ### The Three Axes of the Y-Chart: - **Behavioral Axis** — *What the system does* (algorithm, data flow) - **Structural Axis** — *How it is built* (blocks, modules, gates, transistors) - **Geometric (Physical) Axis** — *Where it is placed* (floorplan, layout) ### The Abstraction Rings (Specification Types) | Ring Level | Specification Type | Description | Typical Language | |---|---|---|---| | **R5 (System)** | System-Level Specification | Entire SoC functionality, SW/HW partitioning | SystemC, SpecC, UML/MARTE | | **R4 (Algorithmic)** | Behavioral/Algorithmic Specification | Pure algorithm, no timing | C/C++, MATLAB | | **R3 (Instruction-Set)** | ISA Specification | Programmer's view of the processor | Instruction set manual | | **R2 (RTL)** | Register-Transfer Level Specification | Cycle-accurate bit-level behavior | VHDL, Verilog, SystemVerilog | | **R1 (Logic/Gate)** | Gate-Level Specification | Boolean equations, gate netlist | Netlist (EDIF) | | **R0 (Circuit/Layout)** | Transistor/Physical Specification | Transistor sizing, layout | SPICE, GDSII | ### 2.2.1 Design Productivity Gap (Important KTU Concept) The gap between **chip complexity** (growing per Moore's Law) and **design productivity** is captured by: $$\text{Productivity Gap} = \frac{\text{Transistors per Chip (Moore's Law)}}{\text{Designed Transistors per Engineer-Month}}$$ Moore's Law: $N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{t/2}$, where $t$ is in **years**. > [!IMPORTANT] > This gap is the **fundamental reason** why high-level (executable) specifications are mandated in modern SoC design. Writing RTL by hand for a billion-transistor chip is infeasible — we must specify at higher abstraction levels and let synthesis tools do the rest. ## 2.3 Classification #3: Executable vs. Non-Executable Specifications ### 2.3.1 Executable Specification A specification that can be **compiled and simulated** directly. It is the *golden reference model* against which the RTL is verified. **Languages used:** - **SystemC / TLM 2.0** — De facto standard for SoC - **SpecC** — Academic alternative - **C/C++/MATLAB** — Algorithm-level - **SystemVerilog** — At RTL level (also verification) - **Python** — Quick prototyping (not synthesizable) - **Bluespec** — High-level HDL ### 2.3.2 Non-Executable Specification A specification that **cannot be run** but describes the system in text or graphical form. **Forms:** - **Natural Language** (English requirements document) - **UML Diagrams** (use-case, sequence, activity) - **Block Diagrams** (architectural drawings) - **Timing Diagrams** (waveform sketches) - **Data Sheets / Manuals** ### 2.3.3 The TLM (Transaction-Level Modeling) Abstraction In TLM, communication between modules is modeled as **function calls** rather than signal-level wire toggling. This is the **dominant KTU exam topic** for Module 1. The simulation **speedup** of TLM over RTL is typically: $$\text{Speedup}_{TLM} = \frac{T_{RTL}}{T_{TLM}} \approx 100\times \text{ to } 1000\times$$ > [!NOTE] > In TLM, we do not model **bit-accurate** or **cycle-accurate** behavior — only **transaction accuracy** (the *what*, not the *when*). This makes it ideal for early software development on a virtual platform. ## 2.4 Classification #4: Formal vs. Semi-Formal vs. Informal | Class | Definition | Example | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Informal** | Ambiguous, natural-language description | English requirements doc | Initial customer interaction | | **Semi-Formal** | Graphical/textual with defined syntax | UML, block diagrams, message sequences | Architectural design | | **Formal** | Mathematical, machine-checkable | Property Specification Language (PSL), SystemVerilog Assertions (SVA), Z-notation, Finite State Machines with CTL | Verification, model checking | ### 2.4.1 Formal Specification — The KTU "Show-Off" Point A specification is **formal** if it has a mathematically defined syntax, semantics, and can be subjected to **model checking** or **theorem proving**. **Standard properties verified:** - **Safety**: "Bad state is never reached" — $G(\neg \text{bad})$ - **Liveness**: "Something good eventually happens" — $G(\text{request} \rightarrow F \text{ack})$ - **Fairness**: "No starvation" — $GF(\text{granted})$ ## 2.5 Classification #5: Hardware vs. Software Specification In a modern SoC, **both hardware and software** are specified together (Hardware/Software Co-Design): - **Hardware Specification**: Behavior of ASIC/FPGA logic, IP blocks - **Software Specification**: Firmware, OS drivers, application code - **Interface Specification**: How HW and SW communicate (APB, AHB, AXI) ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables / Units | Where Used | |---|---|---|---| | Dynamic Power | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$ | $\alpha$ = activity, $C$ = F, $V$ = V, $f$ = Hz | Power spec | | Static Power | $P_{stat} = I_{leak} V_{DD}$ | $I$ in A | Leakage spec | | Energy per Operation | $E = C V_{DD}^2$ | Joules | Low-power design | | Moore's Law | $N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{t/2}$ | Transistors | Productivity gap | | TLM Speedup | $S = T_{RTL}/T_{TLM}$ | Dimensionless | Verification speed | | Energy-Delay Product | $EDP = E \cdot \tau_{delay}$ | J·s | Optimization metric | | Amdahl's Law (SoC acceleration) | $S_{total} = \frac{1}{(1-f) + f/S}$ | $f$ = parallel fraction | HW/SW partitioning | | Performance (MIPS) | $\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{clk}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^6}$ | Millions of Instructions/s | CPU spec | | Real-time deadline check | $T_{exec} \leq T_{deadline}$ | seconds | Constraint spec | | Yield formula | $Y = e^{-D \cdot A}$ | $D$ = defect density, $A$ = area | Manufacturability spec | # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Impact of Voltage Scaling on Power (The $V_{DD}^2$ Story) This is a **classic KTU 14-mark question** because it requires you to derive the savings, not just state the formula. ### Problem Setup A KTU-SoC specification states that the chip must operate at $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V with a maximum power budget of $P_{max} = 500$ mW. The design team proposes scaling $V_{DD}$ down to $1.0$ V (a $0.83\times$ reduction) at the same frequency. Calculate: (a) The new dynamic power consumption. (b) The percentage power reduction. (c) Comment on the side effect (performance penalty). ### Step 1 — Identify the governing equation For a CMOS gate, the propagation delay is: $$\tau_d = \frac{C \cdot V_{DD}}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{(V_{DD} - V_{th})^{\alpha_{sat}}}$$ where $V_{th}$ is the threshold voltage and $\alpha_{sat}$ is the velocity saturation index (typically $1$ to $2$). For simplicity (the standard KTU assumption), $\tau_d \propto \frac{V_{DD}}{(V_{DD} - V_{th})^2}$ (using $\alpha_{sat} = 2$). ### Step 2 — Write the dynamic power equation $$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$ Since $f = 1/T_{clk}$ and $T_{clk} \propto \tau_d$, we can express power in terms of voltage: $$P_{dyn} \propto \frac{V_{DD}^2}{\tau_d} \propto \frac{V_{DD}^2 (V_{DD} - V_{th})^2}{V_{DD}} = V_{DD} (V_{DD} - V_{th})^2$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute numerical values Assume $V_{th} = 0.3$ V (typical for 65 nm node), $\alpha C f$ is constant (call it $K$). **At $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V:** $$P_{dyn,1.2} = K \cdot (1.2)^2 \cdot 1.0 = K \cdot 1.44 \text{ (relative units)}$$ **At $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V:** $$P_{dyn,1.0} = K \cdot (1.0)^2 \cdot 1.0 = K \cdot 1.00 \text{ (relative units)}$$ ### Step 4 — Calculate the percentage reduction $$\Delta P \% = \frac{P_{1.2} - P_{1.0}}{P_{1.2}} \times 100\%$$ $$\Delta P \% = \frac{1.44 - 1.00}{1.44} \times 100\% = \frac{0.44}{1.44} \times 100\%$$ $$\Delta P \% \approx 30.56\%$$ ### Step 5 — Calculate the new actual power Given $P_{max} = 500$ mW at $1.2$ V (assume all power is dynamic for simplicity): $$P_{new} = 500 \text{ mW} \times 0.6944 \approx 347 \text{ mW}$$ where $0.6944 = 1.0/1.44$ is the ratio. ### Step 6 — Compute the delay penalty Delay at 1.2 V: $$\tau_{d,1.2} \propto \frac{1.2}{(1.2 - 0.3)^2} = \frac{1.2}{(0.9)^2} = \frac{1.2}{0.81} \approx 1.481$$ Delay at 1.0 V: $$\tau_{d,1.0} \propto \frac{1.0}{(1.0 - 0.3)^2} = \frac{1.0}{(0.7)^2} = \frac{1.0}{0.49} \approx 2.041$$ Delay increase: $$\frac{\tau_{d,1.0}}{\tau_{d,1.2}} = \frac{2.041}{1.481} \approx 1.378$$ So delay **increases by ~37.8%**, which means the maximum operating frequency **drops by ~27.4%**. ### Step 7 — Final Engineering Trade-off Table | Metric | At $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V | At $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V | Change | |---|---|---|---| | Dynamic Power | 500 mW | 347 mW | $\downarrow 30.6\%$ | | Delay | $\tau_0$ | $1.378 \tau_0$ | $\uparrow 37.8\%$ | | Max Frequency | $f_0$ | $0.726 f_0$ | $\downarrow 27.4\%$ | > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** Voltage scaling is a powerful non-functional specification lever. Designers must balance the power savings against the performance penalty — this is the famous **Pareto trade-off** in SoC design. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Amdahl's Law for HW/SW Co-Design When a KTU-SoC specification calls for hardware acceleration, Amdahl's Law quantifies the maximum achievable speedup. ### Problem A video decoder is specified. Currently, $f = 60\%$ of its execution time can be parallelized and moved to a hardware accelerator that runs $50\times$ faster than software. The remaining $40\%$ must remain in software. ### Step 1 — State Amdahl's Law $$S_{total} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{S_{HW}}}$$ Where $f$ = parallelizable fraction, $S_{HW}$ = hardware speedup factor. ### Step 2 — Substitute values $$S_{total} = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.60) + \frac{0.60}{50}}$$ $$S_{total} = \frac{1}{0.40 + 0.012}$$ $$S_{total} = \frac{1}{0.412}$$ $$S_{total} \approx 2.43\times$$ ### Step 3 — Interpret Even with infinite hardware speedup, the maximum possible total speedup is: $$S_{max} = \frac{1}{1 - f} = \frac{1}{0.40} = 2.5\times$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This derivation is a favourite **14-mark** question because it ties the *specification* (which parts to accelerate) directly to the *design decision* (HW vs. SW partitioning). KTU examiners love to see the explicit algebraic step. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Specification Types (The Master Table for KTU) | Spec Type | Abstraction | Executable? | Verification Method | Used For | Example Tool | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | System-Level (TLM) | Highest | Yes | SW simulation, virtual platform | Architecture exploration, SW dev | SystemC + TLM 2.0 | | Algorithmic | High | Yes | Algorithm validation | Algorithm selection | MATLAB/Simulink, C | | Instruction-Set (ISA) | High | No (manual) | ISA simulation | Processor design | Spike, gem5 | | RTL | Medium | Yes | RTL simulation, formal | Synthesis input | Verilog, VHDL, SV | | Gate-Level | Low | Yes | Gate sim, formal equiv. check | Netlist verification | Netlist + SDF | | Physical (Layout) | Lowest | No (drawn) | DRC, LVS, ERC | Fabrication | Cadence Virtuoso | | Informal (NL) | Variable | No | Manual review | Requirements capture | Word doc | | Semi-Formal (UML) | Variable | Partial | Model simulation | Architecture | Rhapsody, Papyrus | | Formal (Property) | Variable | Yes (checked) | Model checking | Verification | SVA, PSL, JasperGold | | IP Specification | Medium | Yes | Reuse verification | IP block integration | IP-XACT (IEEE 1685) | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1: The Gajski-Kuhn Y-Chart (Conceptual) This is the **most-requested diagram** in KTU Module 1. It shows how the three domains (Behavioral, Structural, Physical) intersect at increasing levels of abstraction, with the specification types populating the outermost ring. ```mermaid flowchart TB start([SoC Design Gajski-Kuhn Y-Chart]) --> axisB[Behavioral Domain
What the system does] start --> axisS[Structural Domain
How it is built] start --> axisP[Physical Domain
Where it is placed] axisB --> b5[R5 System Level Spec] axisB --> b4[R4 Algorithmic Spec] axisB --> b3[R3 ISA Spec] axisB --> b2[R2 RTL Spec] axisB --> b1[R1 Gate Level Spec] axisB --> b0[R0 Transistor Spec] axisS --> s5[R5 Processor and Bus Spec] axisS --> s4[R4 ALUs Registers Spec] axisS --> s3[R3 Pipeline and Datapath Spec] axisS --> s2[R2 Flip Flops and ALUs Spec] axisS --> s1[R1 Gates Spec] axisS --> s0[R0 Transistor Netlist Spec] axisP --> p5[R5 System Floorplan] axisP --> p4[R4 Block Placement] axisP --> p3[R3 Cell Rows] axisP --> p2[R2 Standard Cells] axisP --> p1[R1 Gate Layout] axisP --> p0[R0 Transistor Layout and Routing] b5 -.- note1[Specification evolves from outer ring to inner ring] note1 -.- p0 ``` ## 4.2 Diagram 2: Hierarchical Tree of Specification Types ```mermaid flowchart TB root([SoC Specifications]) --> A[Functional Spec] root --> B[Non Functional Spec] root --> C[Hardware vs Software Spec] root --> D[By Formality] root --> E[By Executability] A --> A1[Behavioral Spec] A --> A2[Interface Spec] A --> A3[Protocol Spec] B --> B1[Performance] B --> B2[Power] B --> B3[Area] B --> B4[Thermal] B --> B5[Reliability] B --> B6[Security] C --> C1[HW Spec
VHDL Verilog SV] C --> C2[SW Spec
C C++ Firmware] C --> C3[Interface Spec
AXI AHB APB] D --> D1[Informal
Natural Language] D --> D2[Semi Formal
UML Block Diagrams] D --> D3[Formal
SVA PSL CTL] E --> E1[Executable
SystemC TLM C MATLAB] E --> E2[Non Executable
English Doc UML Diagrams] E1 --> E1a[Transaction Level Model TLM] E1 --> E1b[Behavioral Model] E1 --> E1c[RTL Model] E1 --> E1d[Gate Level Model] ``` ## 4.3 Diagram 3: The SoC Design Flow with Specification Stages ```mermaid flowchart LR s1([Customer Requirements]):::in --> s2[Informal Spec
English Document] s2 --> s3[Semi Formal Spec
UML Block Diagrams] s3 --> s4[System Level Spec
SystemC TLM Executable] s4 --> s5[Algorithmic Spec
C MATLAB] s5 --> s6[RTL Spec
Verilog VHDL SV] s6 --> s7[Gate Level Spec
Netlist] s7 --> s8[Physical Spec
GDSII Layout] s8 --> s9([Fabrication and Testing]):::out s4 -.- verif1[Virtual Platform for SW Development] s6 -.- verif2[RTL Verification and Synthesis] s7 -.- verif3[Formal Equivalence Checking] classDef in fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff,stroke:#000 classDef out fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Diagram 4: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Typical SoC (Spec Target) A typical smartphone SoC specification must capture these interacting blocks. The diagram maps the **scope** of what a specification must describe. ```mermaid flowchart TB soc([Typical SoC Architecture]):::title soc --> cpu[Application CPU Core
ARM Cortex A class] soc --> dsp[DSP Core
Signal Processing] soc --> gpu[GPU Core
Graphics] soc --> mem[Memory Controller
DDR and LPDDR] soc --> noc[Network on Chip
Bus Fabric AXI] soc --> ip1[Peripheral IP Block
USB UART SPI I2C] soc --> ip2[Video Codec IP
H264 H265 Decoder] soc --> sec[Security Module
Crypto Engine] soc --> pm[Power Management Unit
PMU] soc --> clk[Clock Management
PLL] cpu --- noc dsp --- noc gpu --- noc ip1 --- noc ip2 --- noc sec --- noc mem --- noc pm --- clk clk --- cpu clk --- dsp clk --- gpu classDef title fill:#000,color:#fff,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > The specification of this SoC must capture, for *each block*, its: **functional behavior**, **interface protocol**, **clock/frequency requirements**, **power budget**, **area budget**, and **verification strategy**. KTU examiners will often ask: *"Draft the specification outline for block X of a typical SoC."* ## 4.5 Diagram 5: Executable Specification — TLM Modeling Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB req([System Requirements]) --> a[Architect Defines TLM Spec] a --> b[Write SystemC plus TLM 2.0 Model] b --> c[Compile and Simulate] c --> d{Performance Acceptable?} d -- Yes --> e[Lock Architecture] d -- No --> f[Modify Spec Partition HW SW] f --> a e --> g[Develop Software on Virtual Platform] e --> h[Hand Off TLM Reference to RTL Team] h --> i[RTL Implementation in Verilog] i --> j[Verify RTL against TLM Golden Reference] ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Differentiate between Functional and Non-Functional Specifications in SoC design. Give two examples of each. **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** **Model Answer:** **Functional Specification** describes **what the SoC does** — its logical behavior, algorithms, and data transformations. - *Example 1:* "The UART shall transmit 8-bit data at a baud rate of 115200 bps with one stop bit and no parity." - *Example 2:* "The H.264 video decoder shall decode a 1080p stream at 30 frames per second." **Non-Functional Specification** describes **how well** the SoC performs — quality attributes and constraints. - *Example 1:* "The entire SoC must consume less than 1.2 W of total power (TDP)." - *Example 2:* "The chip die area must not exceed 25 mm² and must operate at a maximum junction temperature of 85°C." | Aspect | Functional | Non-Functional | |---|---|---| | Question Answered | "What does it do?" | "How well does it do it?" | | Examples | Algorithm, protocol, behavior | Power, area, speed, reliability | | Verifiable by | Simulation | Measurement, formal analysis | --- ### Q2. What is Transaction-Level Modeling (TLM) and why is it preferred as a specification style for SoC? **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **Model Answer:** **Transaction-Level Modeling (TLM)** is an *executable specification* style in which the communication between SoC modules is modeled as **function calls (transactions)** rather than as bit-accurate, cycle-accurate signal toggles. **Why TLM is preferred:** 1. **Faster Simulation** — TLM models simulate 100× to 1000× faster than RTL, enabling early software development. 2. **Faster Architectural Exploration** — Designers can quickly evaluate different HW/SW partitions and bus architectures. 3. **Abstraction from Implementation** — The spec focuses on *what* data is transferred, not *how* bits are wired. 4. **Decouples SW and HW development** — Firmware teams can start writing drivers before any RTL exists. 5. **Industry Standard** — TLM 2.0 (from OSCI/Accellera) is the de facto standard for virtual platforms. --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice Pattern ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the **Gajski-Kuhn Y-Chart** with a neat diagram. List the three domains and at least four abstraction levels. Discuss how specifications evolve as we move from the outer ring to the inner ring. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the different **types of specifications** in SoC design based on: (i) Executability (executable vs non-executable) (ii) Formality (formal vs semi-formal vs informal) Give suitable examples and languages for each category. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q.A(a): The **Gajski-Kuhn Y-Chart** (proposed by Daniel Gajski and Robert Kuhn in 1983) is a fundamental diagram that describes the **three domains** of electronic system design and the **multiple abstraction levels** within each domain. **The Three Domains:** 1. **Behavioral Domain** — Describes *what* the system does (algorithm, data flow, control logic). 2. **Structural Domain** — Describes *how* the system is built (the components and their interconnections). 3. **Geometric (Physical) Domain** — Describes *where* the components are placed physically (layout, floorplanning). **The Abstraction Levels (from outermost/highest to innermost/lowest):** 1. **System Level** — Entire SoC as a single entity. Spec: SystemC, UML. 2. **Algorithmic / Behavioral Level** — Pure C/MATLAB, no clock. 3. **Instruction-Set Level (ISA)** — Programmer's view of the processor. 4. **Register-Transfer Level (RTL)** — Cycle-accurate, bit-accurate. Spec: VHDL/Verilog. 5. **Logic / Gate Level** — Boolean gates and flip-flops. Spec: netlist. 6. **Circuit / Transistor Level** — SPICE, GDSII layout. **Evolution of Specifications:** As we move from the outer ring to the inner ring, the specification becomes **more detailed, more concrete, and more constrained**: - Outer ring (System) → few details, high abstraction, fast to write, slow to verify. - Inner ring (Physical) → full details, low abstraction, slow to write, directly synthesizable/fabricable. The **specification flows from outer to inner** during top-down design and from inner to outer during bottom-up verification. The "Y" shape comes from the fact that the three domains converge at the center (the fabricated chip). #### Model Solution for Q.A(b): **(i) Executable vs Non-Executable Specifications:** | Aspect | Executable Specification | Non-Executable Specification | |---|---|---| | Definition | Can be compiled and run on a simulator | Cannot be executed; only read/interpreted | | Form | Code in SystemC, C, MATLAB, Verilog | English text, UML diagrams, block diagrams | | Use | Golden reference model for verification | Requirements capture, initial design | | Speed of validation | Fast (automated) | Slow (manual review) | | Example | SystemC TLM model of an SoC | "The chip shall support USB 3.0" | **Examples of Executable Specifications:** - **SystemC + TLM 2.0** — System-level SoC simulation - **C/C++/MATLAB** — Algorithmic validation - **SystemVerilog** — RTL specification - **Bluespec** — High-level functional specification **Examples of Non-Executable Specifications:** - **English requirements document** — Customer-facing spec - **UML use-case / sequence diagrams** — Architectural spec - **Timing diagrams** — Protocol definition - **Data sheets** — IP block description **(ii) Formal vs Semi-Formal vs Informal Specifications:** **Informal Specification:** Written in natural language; ambiguous, but easy to create. - *Example:* "The processor should be fast and low-power." **Semi-Formal Specification:** Uses a defined graphical or textual notation with limited rigor. - *Example:* UML class diagram, message sequence chart, block diagram with typed ports. **Formal Specification:** Has a mathematically defined syntax and semantics; can be machine-checked. - *Example:* SystemVerilog Assertions (SVA), Property Specification Language (PSL), Computation Tree Logic (CTL) for model checking. **Why formal specifications matter:** They enable **exhaustive proof** of correctness — verifying *all* possible input sequences, not just the ones in a testbench. This is critical for safety-critical SoCs (automotive, medical, aerospace). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a)** What is a **system specification**? Discuss the role of **executable specifications** in modern SoC design. Compare the use of SystemC/TLM with that of C/C++/MATLAB as specification languages. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A SoC specification requires the chip to operate at a maximum frequency of 800 MHz at $V_{DD} = 1.1$ V, consuming 600 mW of dynamic power. The design team proposes a voltage-scaling optimization to $V_{DD} = 0.9$ V. Assuming the activity factor ($\alpha$) and load capacitance ($C$) remain constant, and the threshold voltage $V_{th} = 0.35$ V: (i) Calculate the new dynamic power consumption. (ii) Calculate the percentage power reduction. (iii) Compute the new maximum operating frequency, given that delay $\tau_d \propto V_{DD}/(V_{DD} - V_{th})^2$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q.B(a): A **system specification** is the highest-level description of an SoC. It defines the system's *purpose*, *functions*, *interfaces*, *performance goals*, *power budget*, and *constraints* before any implementation begins. It serves as the **contract** between system architects, hardware designers, software engineers, and verification teams. **Role of Executable Specifications in Modern SoC Design:** 1. **Single Source of Truth** — Acts as the golden reference against which RTL is verified. 2. **Faster Time-to-Market** — Enables parallel development of hardware and software. 3. **Architectural Exploration** — Allows "what-if" analysis of different HW/SW partitions. 4. **Virtual Prototyping** — Software engineers can run firmware on a virtual platform months before silicon is available. 5. **Reduces Re-spins** — Errors caught at the executable spec level cost 10×–1000× less than errors caught post-tape-out. **Comparison: SystemC/TLM vs C/C++/MATLAB:** | Feature | SystemC + TLM 2.0 | C/C++/MATLAB | |---|---|---| | Abstraction Level | System / Transactional | Algorithmic | | Timing | Approximate (not cycle-accurate) | No timing (pure algorithm) | | Concurrency | Yes (via `sc_thread`, `sc_method`) | No (sequential) | | HW Modeling | Yes (modules, ports, channels) | No (pure math) | | Standardization | OSCI/Accellera standard | Vendor-specific (MATLAB) | | Simulation Speed | Medium (1000s of cycles/sec) | Very fast | | Use Case | HW/SW co-simulation, virtual platform | Algorithm exploration | | Synthesis Path | Indirect (must be rewritten in RTL) | None (algorithmic only) | > **Conclusion:** SystemC/TLM is the *bridge* between algorithm (C/MATLAB) and implementation (RTL). It captures the **structure** and **concurrency** that pure C/C++ lacks, while being faster and more abstract than RTL. #### Model Solution for Q.B(b): **Given:** - $V_{DD,1} = 1.1$ V, $P_{dyn,1} = 600$ mW, $f_1 = 800$ MHz - $V_{DD,2} = 0.9$ V, $V_{th} = 0.35$ V - $\alpha, C$ = constant **Step 1 — Calculate the new dynamic power using $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$** Since $\alpha C$ is constant, the power ratio is: $$\frac{P_{dyn,2}}{P_{dyn,1}} = \left(\frac{V_{DD,2}}{V_{DD,1}}\right)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{f_2}{f_1}\right)$$ We need $f_2$ first. **Step 2 — Calculate delay ratio** $$\tau_d \propto \frac{V_{DD}}{(V_{DD} - V_{th})^2}$$ **At $V_{DD} = 1.1$ V:** $$\tau_{d,1} \propto \frac{1.1}{(1.1 - 0.35)^2} = \frac{1.1}{(0.75)^2} = \frac{1.1}{0.5625} = 1.9556$$ **At $V_{DD} = 0.9$ V:** $$\tau_{d,2} \propto \frac{0.9}{(0.9 - 0.35)^2} = \frac{0.9}{(0.55)^2} = \frac{0.9}{0.3025} = 2.9752$$ **Delay ratio:** $$\frac{\tau_{d,2}}{\tau_{d,1}} = \frac{2.9752}{1.9556} = 1.5213$$ **Step 3 — Calculate new maximum frequency** $$f_2 = \frac{f_1}{\tau_{d,2}/\tau_{d,1}} = \frac{800 \text{ MHz}}{1.5213} \approx 525.9 \text{ MHz}$$ **Step 4 — Calculate new dynamic power** $$P_{dyn,2} = P_{dyn,1} \cdot \left(\frac{V_{DD,2}}{V_{DD,1}}\right)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{f_2}{f_1}\right)$$ $$P_{dyn,2} = 600 \text{ mW} \cdot \left(\frac{0.9}{1.1}\right)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{525.9}{800}\right)$$ $$P_{dyn,2} = 600 \cdot 0.6694 \cdot 0.6574$$ $$P_{dyn,2} = 600 \cdot 0.4401 \approx 264.1 \text{ mW}$$ **Step 5 — Percentage power reduction** $$\Delta P \% = \frac{600 - 264.1}{600} \times 100\% = \frac{335.9}{600} \times 100\% \approx 55.98\%$$ **Final Answer Table:** | Parameter | Before Scaling | After Scaling | Change | |---|---|---|---| | $V_{DD}$ | 1.1 V | 0.9 V | $\downarrow 18.2\%$ | | Max Frequency | 800 MHz | 525.9 MHz | $\downarrow 34.3\%$ | | Dynamic Power | 600 mW | 264.1 mW | $\downarrow 56.0\%$ | > **Engineering Insight:** A modest $18\%$ voltage reduction yields a dramatic $56\%$ power saving, but at the cost of a $34\%$ frequency drop. This is the central trade-off in low-power SoC specification. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE marks in this topic (verified from KTU valuation patterns):** > > 1. **Confusing "specification" with "implementation".** Specification says *what*; implementation says *how*. A common blunder: writing Verilog code when asked to "write a specification". > > 2. **Forgetting the Y-Chart's three domains.** Examiners expect *Behavioral + Structural + Physical*. Writing only abstraction levels without the three axes is a $-2$ to $-3$ mark error. > > 3. **Stating $P \propto V^2$ without deriving the $f$ penalty.** Voltage scaling reduces $f$ because delay increases — students who ignore this lose 2 marks in 14-mark questions. > > 4. **Mixing up TLM and RTL.** TLM is *not* cycle-accurate and is *not* synthesizable. Calling TLM "an HDL" is a red flag. > > 5. **Not giving examples.** Every "type" must be illustrated with a concrete SoC example (USB controller, H.264 decoder, etc.) — abstract definitions alone fetch only 60% marks. > > 6. **Skipping the trade-off discussion.** A specification is never free; power vs. performance, area vs. speed — the examiner looks for *engineered* reasoning, not just facts. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [ ] **Specification = contract** that captures *what* the SoC does and *under what constraints*. - [ ] **Functional Spec** → behavior, algorithms, protocols. **Non-Functional Spec** → power, area, speed, reliability, security. - [ ] **Dynamic power**: $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$. Voltage scaling is the most powerful lever. - [ ] **Y-Chart** has **3 domains** (Behavioral, Structural, Geometric) and **multiple rings** (System, Algorithmic, ISA, RTL, Gate, Transistor). - [ ] **Design flows outer → inner** ring during implementation; verification flows **inner → outer**. - [ ] **Executable specs** = SystemC, TLM 2.0, C/C++, MATLAB, SystemVerilog. Run on a simulator. - [ ] **Non-executable specs** = English text, UML, block diagrams, timing diagrams. Read, not run. - [ ] **TLM 2.0** is the de facto **system-level executable spec** standard. Models *transactions*, not signals. - [ ] **TLM speedup over RTL** = 100× to 1000×. Enables parallel HW/SW development. - [ ] **Formal specs** use SVA, PSL, CTL. Enable model checking (safety + liveness properties). - [ ] **Informal specs** are written in English; **semi-formal** use UML; **formal** use math. - [ ] **Amdahl's Law** $S = 1/[(1-f) + f/S_{HW}]$ governs HW/SW co-design trade-offs. - [ ] **Moore's Law** $N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{t/2}$ explains the productivity gap that *forces* high-level specs. - [ ] **IP specification** uses **IP-XACT (IEEE 1685)** for portable, tool-independent IP descriptions. - [ ] A specification must address **6 items** for every SoC block: function, interface, timing, power, area, and verification strategy. - [ ] The **SoC design flow** proceeds: Customer → Informal → Semi-formal → System (TLM) → Algorithmic → RTL → Gate → Physical (GDSII). - [ ] The **executable spec (TLM)** serves as the **golden reference model** for verifying the RTL.

topics.title: System Design Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT602 - SYSTEM ON CHIP DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** System Design Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **System Design Process** is a structured, multi-disciplinary engineering methodology used to conceive, define, architect, implement, integrate, and validate a complex electronic/computing system (such as a System-on-Chip). According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, it represents the systematic translation of a *customer requirement* into a *manufacturable, testable, and verifiable product specification*, encompassing both hardware (HW) and software (SW) sub-systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The system design process is the *backbone* of SoC engineering. It transforms an abstract idea (e.g., "a smart wearable fitness tracker") into a fully functional silicon die and embedded firmware. Examiners frequently test the student's ability to map real-world requirements to specific design phases.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the System Design Process as **building a custom house**:
1. **Requirements Phase**: The family tells the architect — "We need 4 bedrooms, a kitchen, a garden, solar power, and a rainwater harvesting system." (High-level user needs)
2. **Specification Phase**: The architect drafts a formal blueprint — exact dimensions, number of windows, load-bearing wall locations, plumbing layout. (Detailed technical specification)
3. **Architecture/Functional Design Phase**: Structural engineers decide which parts will be load-bearing concrete vs. steel beams; the homeowner chooses modular kitchen vs. custom carpentry. (HW/SW Partitioning)
4. **Implementation Phase**: Masons lay bricks, electricians wire, plumbers install pipes. (RTL design, coding)
5. **Integration & Testing Phase**: The house is assembled; the engineer checks if the solar panel charges the inverter, if the rainwater tank fills the overhead tank correctly. (Verification & Validation)
6. **Deployment & Maintenance**: Family moves in; the warranty and maintenance schedule kicks in. (Tape-out, production, and firmware updates)

Just like a poorly designed house collapses, a poorly defined SoC system will fail timing closure, burn excessive power, or simply not meet user expectations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary):**
> - **System**: A collection of inter-operating elements (HW + SW) that collectively perform a function.
> - **Specification**: A precise, testable, and unambiguous description of what the system must do.
> - **Architecture**: The structural organization of the system's functional blocks and their interconnections.
> - **Partitioning**: The decision-making process that decides which functionality is implemented in hardware and which in software.

### 1.3 Standard Design Metrics (Bolded Constants & Metrics)

The four pillars that govern every system design decision are:
- **Performance (Throughput / Latency)** — measured in *MIPS*, *MOPS*, or execution time in **seconds**.
- **Power Consumption** — measured in **Watts (W)** or **milliwatts (mW)**.
- **Silicon Area (Cost)** — measured in **mm²** or **gate equivalents (GE)**.
- **Time-to-Market** — measured in **months** or **calendar quarters**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Phases of the System Design Process (Detailed Breakdown)

The KTU 2024 Scheme identifies the following sequence of phases, which together form the canonical design flow:

**Phase 1: Requirements Analysis (Customer to Engineer Translation)**
- Capture *what* the system must do — functionality, user interface, environmental constraints.
- Identify non-functional requirements: cost ceiling, target clock frequency, maximum power dissipation, expected operating temperature range (e.g., **-40°C to +125°C** for automotive Grade-1).
- Output: *Requirements Specification Document* (often a User Requirement Specification — URS).

**Phase 2: System Specification (Mathematical/Unambiguous Description)**
- Convert the natural-language requirements into a *formal, testable* specification.
- Use modeling languages like **SystemC**, **SpecC**, or plain **UML/SysML**.
- Define interfaces, data types, timing constraints (latency deadlines, throughput targets), and protocol standards (e.g., **AMBA AXI**, **USB 3.0**, **I²C**).
- Output: *System Functional Specification*.

**Phase 3: Functional Design / Architecture Exploration**
- Identify the major functional blocks (CPU core, memory controller, DSP, peripherals, interconnect bus).
- Explore architectural alternatives: e.g., single-core vs. multi-core, RISC vs. CISC, shared memory vs. distributed memory, hardwired logic vs. programmable processor.
- Evaluate each alternative against the *design metrics* (PPA: Power, Performance, Area) and select the optimal candidate.
- Output: *High-Level Architecture Diagram* + *Architecture Trade-off Report*.

**Phase 4: Hardware/Software Partitioning & Co-Design**
- The defining phase of SoC design. Decide which functions go to:
  - **Hardware (HW)**: ASIC gates, dedicated accelerators, FPGA fabric — fast but inflexible and expensive in NRE.
  - **Software (SW)**: Firmware running on an embedded processor — flexible, easy to update, slower in raw speed.
- This decision is governed by a cost function balancing performance, flexibility, and time-to-market.
- Output: *HW/SW Partitioning Table* + *Co-simulation environment*.

**Phase 5: Implementation (Synthesis, Layout, Firmware Coding)**
- Hardware: HDL coding (Verilog/VHDL/SystemVerilog) → Logic Synthesis → Place & Route → GDSII generation.
- Software: Cross-compilation of C/C++/Assembly for the target processor core.
- Output: *Netlist* + *Object Code* + *GDSII Layout Database*.

**Phase 6: Integration, Verification & Validation (V&V)**
- Integration: combine HW and SW sub-modules into a complete SoC prototype (FPGA emulation or silicon bring-up).
- Verification: Does the design meet the *specification*? (functional correctness) — answered by simulation, formal verification, and emulation.
- Validation: Does the design meet the *customer's actual needs*? — answered by prototype testing in real environments.
- Output: *Test Coverage Report* + *Tape-out Sign-off*.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Phase:** Each phase exists to *minimize the cost of fixing a defect*. A bug found in Phase 1 costs roughly **$1** to fix; the same bug found after tape-out can cost **>$1,000,000** in mask re-spins. This is the "**Rule of Ten**" in VLSI economics.

### 2.2 Design Methodologies Compared

| Methodology | Flow Direction | Best Suited For | Major Drawback |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Waterfall** | Top-down, strictly sequential | Well-understood, stable requirements | Late discovery of design errors |
| **Spiral (Boehm)** | Iterative risk-driven loops | High-risk, complex research projects | Heavy management overhead |
| **Top-Down** | System → sub-systems → gates | Structured digital design | May miss low-level optimization opportunities |
| **Bottom-Up** | Gates → sub-systems → system | Library-based IP reuse | Hard to verify system-level behavior early |
| **Platform-Based** | Reuse of pre-verified SoC platform | Time-to-market critical products | May constrain innovation |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CPU Time** | $T_{CPU} = N_{cycles} \times T_{clk} = \dfrac{N_{cycles}}{f_{clk}}$ | Total execution time for a program | seconds (s) |
| 2 | **Speedup (S)** | $S = \dfrac{T_{original}}{T_{enhanced}}$ | Ratio of performance gain | dimensionless |
| 3 | **Amdahl's Law** | $S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$ | Theoretical max speedup from parallel enhancement | dimensionless |
| 4 | **Throughput** | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{\text{Tasks completed}}{T_{total}}$ | Tasks per unit time | tasks/s |
| 5 | **Power (Dynamic)** | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | CMOS switching power | Watts (W) |
| 6 | **Power (Static)** | $P_{stat} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Leakage current dominated | Watts (W) |
| 7 | **Design Cost per Die** | $C_{die} = \dfrac{C_{wafer}}{N_{dies} \times Y_{yield}}$ | Cost of one functional die | USD/die |
| 8 | **Yield Model** | $Y = e^{-D \cdot A}$ | Poisson yield approximation | dimensionless |
| 9 | **Cost of Fixing a Defect** | $C_{fix}(n) = 10^{n-1} \times C_{0}$ | "Rule of Ten" — increases 10x per phase | USD |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Markdown Note:** In the table above, the absolute value / division bars inside formulas (e.g., $\dfrac{N_{cycles}}{f_{clk}}$) use `\dfrac` and `\frac` rather than the vertical pipe `|`, ensuring the KTU cheat-sheet renders correctly across PDF compilers.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The system design process is the foundational workflow behind every commercial SoC — from the **Apple M-series** chips in laptops, to **Qualcomm Snapdragon** mobile processors, to **NVIDIA Drive** autonomous-driving SoCs. In the IoT era, the same process (with stronger emphasis on ultra-low power) governs the design of **smart sensor hubs**, **wearables**, and **edge-AI accelerators**. Mastering this process is what differentiates a *chip user* (a programmer) from a *chip architect* (a system designer).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Amdahl's Law (for Parallel Speedup)

Amdahl's Law is the single most important theoretical tool for evaluating the *theoretical upper bound* of any performance enhancement. It is a guaranteed question in KTU exams when discussing the system design trade-off phase.

**Step 1 — Define the variables.**
Let the fraction of execution time that can be improved (parallelized or accelerated) be denoted as $f$. Then the fraction that *cannot* be improved (the sequential bottleneck) is $(1 - f)$.

**Step 2 — Express the original execution time.**
Assume the total original execution time is $T$. Without loss of generality, we can normalize $T = 1$ (i.e., work in units of "original time").

$$
T_{original} = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Express the time spent in the improvable portion.**
The time spent in the improvable portion is $f \times T_{original} = f \times 1 = f$.

**Step 4 — Express the time spent in the non-improvable portion.**
The time spent in the sequential portion is $(1 - f) \times T_{original} = (1 - f) \times 1 = 1 - f$.

**Step 5 — Apply the speedup factor $n$ to the improvable portion only.**
If the improvable portion is enhanced by a factor of $n$ (e.g., by adding $n$ parallel processors, or by using a hardware accelerator that is $n$ times faster), then its new execution time becomes $\dfrac{f}{n}$.

**Step 6 — Sum the new times of both portions.**
The new total execution time is the sum of the improved portion and the unchanged portion:

$$
T_{new} = \dfrac{f}{n} + (1 - f)
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the overall speedup as the ratio of original to new time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{overall} &= \dfrac{T_{original}}{T_{new}} \\[6pt]
S_{overall} &= \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{f}{n} + (1 - f)} \\[6pt]
S_{overall} &= \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **Amdahl's Law**.

**Step 8 — Analyze the asymptotic limit as $n \to \infty$.**
