# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]
**Define a "macro" in the context of SoC design. Differentiate between hard, soft, and firm macros with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

A **macro** in SoC design is a pre-designed, self-contained, reusable functional block with a well-defined interface that can be integrated into a larger chip design.

**Hard Macro:** A fully placed and routed block delivered as GDSII layout. It is technology-specific, has fixed geometry, and offers the highest performance and area efficiency. **Example:** Compiled SRAM, analog PLL, standard cell library macros.

**Soft Macro:** A macro delivered as synthesizable HDL (RTL) code. It is technology-independent and offers maximum flexibility for re-synthesis. **Example:** A CPU core like ARM Cortex-A53 in synthesizable form, custom DSPs, FIFO controllers.

**Firm Macro:** A macro delivered as a gate-level netlist with some physical constraints but not fully placed. It balances flexibility and performance. **Example:** A custom arithmetic unit where netlist is fixed but placement can be optimized.

| Property | Hard | Soft | Firm |
|---|---|---|---|
| Form | GDSII | RTL | Netlist |
| Flexibility | None | Full | Partial |
| Performance | Highest | Variable | Medium |
| Predictability | Highest | Lowest | Medium |

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]
**What is pin planning? Why is it a critical step in top-level SoC design?**

**Model Answer:**

**Pin planning** is the process of assigning physical locations and types to all I/O, power, and ground pads on the die periphery (for wire-bond) or area array (for flip-chip). It defines the pad order, pad pitch, ESD protection, and signal-to-pad mapping.

**Why Pin Planning is Critical:**

1. **Package compatibility:** Pad pitch and ordering must match the package substrate; mistakes cause unroutable packages.
2. **Signal integrity:** Sensitive analog signals must be placed far from noisy digital switching pads to minimize coupling.
3. **Power integrity:** Power and ground pads must be distributed uniformly to minimize IR drop and provide low-inductance current return paths.
4. **ESD protection:** Every signal pad needs an ESD clamp; the pad ring topology determines the discharge current path.
5. **First-pass silicon:** Post-silicon re-bumping is impossible in wire-bond packages; pad errors are fatal.
6. **Manufacturing yield:** Poor pad placement causes bond-wire shorts, package delamination, and test failures.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice. Students must answer either Option A OR Option B. Each option typically has two sub-parts (7 marks each).

---

### Option A (14 Marks)

#### Question A. [KTU University Exam - Model 2024]
**(a)** Explain the three classifications of macros in SoC design with their advantages, disadvantages, and use cases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A top-level SoC has **N = 256** functional blocks. If each metal layer can support 4-way partitioning of inter-block routing, calculate the **minimum number of metal layers** required. If 2 additional layers are needed for power grid and clock shielding, what is the **total metal stack**? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Macro Classification:**

The three macro classifications per the VSIA standard are:

**1. Hard Macro:**
- **Form:** GDSII (fully placed and routed layout).
- **Advantages:** Best PPA (Power, Performance, Area); fully verified; shortest time to integration.
- **Disadvantages:** Not portable across technology nodes; cannot be modified internally; high NRE cost.
- **Use Cases:** SRAM/DRAM compilers, analog blocks (PLL, ADC), standard cell hard macros, IP from foundries.

**2. Soft Macro:**
- **Form:** Synthesizable HDL (Verilog/VHDL RTL).
- **Advantages:** Portable across technologies; can be re-synthesized for PPA optimization; easy to modify.
- **Disadvantages:** Quality depends on synthesis tool; unpredictable area/timing; may need manual tuning.
- **Use Cases:** CPU cores (ARM, RISC-V), DSP blocks, custom accelerators, RTL IP libraries.

**3. Firm Macro:**
- **Form:** Gate-level netlist with partial physical constraints (placement hints, floorplan guidance).
- **Advantages:** Better timing prediction than soft; more flexible than hard; can be re-placed but not re-synthesized.
- **Disadvantages:** Partial reusability; netlist may not match new technology's standard cells.
- **Use Cases:** Custom arithmetic units, interface controllers where timing closure is critical but some flexibility is desired.

**Comparison Table (essential for full marks):**

| Parameter | Hard | Soft | Firm |
|---|---|---|---|
| Form | GDSII | HDL | Netlist |
| Tech Dependency | Yes | No | Partial |
| Time to Integrate | Lowest | Highest | Medium |
| Flexibility | None | Highest | Medium |
| Performance | Best | Variable | Good |
| Risk | Lowest | Highest | Medium |

**Part (b) — Metal Layer Calculation:**

Given $N = 256$ blocks, and each metal layer supports 4-way partitioning, the minimum signal routing layers required:

$$
k = \frac{1}{2} \log_{2}(N) = \frac{1}{2} \log_{2}(256)
$$

Since $2^{8} = 256$:

$$
\log_{2}(256) = 8
$$

$$
k = \frac{8}{2} = 4 \text{ signal routing layers}
$$

**Adding 2 layers for power grid and clock shielding:**

$$
N_{metal,total} = 4 + 2 = 6 \text{ layers}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer: Minimum 4 routing layers, total 6 metal layers required.**

---

#### Question B. [KTU University Exam - Model 2024 - Alternative Choice]
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **top-level architecture of a typical SoC** showing compute, memory, I/O, interconnect, and power/clock subsystems. Explain the role of the **interconnect fabric** with a comparison of bus, crossbar, and NoC. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A top-level macro draws a peak current of $I_{peak} = 80$ mA during a switching event lasting $\Delta t = 200$ ps. The supply voltage is $V_{DD} = 0.85$ V and the allowable voltage droop is **5% of $V_{DD}$**. Calculate the **minimum decoupling capacitance** required. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Top-Level SoC Architecture:**

(See Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram in this document for the visual answer.)

**Textual description of the diagram:**

The SoC has five top-level subsystems:
1. **Compute Subsystem** — CPUs, GPU, DSP.
2. **Memory Subsystem** — L1/L2 cache, SRAM, DRAM controller.
3. **I/O Subsystem** — USB, PCIe, UART, GPIO.
4. **Interconnect Subsystem** — AXI crossbar, AHB-APB bridges.
5. **Power/Clock Subsystem** — PMU, PLL, global clock tree, PDN.

**Role of Interconnect Fabric:**

The interconnect fabric enables data exchange between masters (CPUs, DMA, GPU) and slaves (memory, peripherals).

**Bus (e.g., AMBA APB):** Single shared set of wires with arbitration. Low area, low power, but only one transaction at a time. Used for low-bandwidth peripherals.

**Crossbar (e.g., AMBA AXI):** Matrix switch allowing simultaneous master-slave pairs. Higher bandwidth, predictable latency, but area grows as $O(N \times M)$. Used for high-performance subsystems.

**Network-on-Chip (NoC):** Routed packet network with routers and links. Highest scalability, supports many cores, but adds router area/power. Used in modern multi-core AI/ML SoCs.

**Part (b) — Decoupling Capacitance:**

Given:
- $I_{peak} = 80$ mA $= 80 \times 10^{-3}$ A
- $\Delta t = 200$ ps $= 200 \times 10^{-12}$ s
- $V_{DD} = 0.85$ V
- $\Delta V = 5\% \text{ of } V_{DD} = 0.05 \times 0.85 = 0.0425$ V

Using the formula:

$$
C_{decoup} = \frac{I_{peak} \times \Delta t}{\Delta V}
$$

Substituting:

$$
C_{decoup} = \frac{80 \times 10^{-3} \times 200 \times 10^{-12}}{0.0425}
$$

$$
C_{decoup} = \frac{16000 \times 10^{-15}}{0.0425}
$$

$$
C_{decoup} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-11}}{0.0425}
$$

$$
C_{decoup} \approx 376.5 \text{ pF}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer: Minimum decoupling capacitance ≈ 376.5 pF** must be distributed near the macro to suppress the voltage droop.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Top-Level Macro Design Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "macro" with "module" or "cell":** A macro is a complete functional IP block, not a single gate. State this distinction explicitly.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to justify macro type selection:** Don't just list hard/soft/firm — *explain why* a given macro type is chosen for a specific block (e.g., "SRAM uses hard macro because bit-cell regularity enables manual layout optimization").
>
> 3. **Ignoring the power/clock overlays:** Students often draw the functional blocks and forget to overlay the **clock distribution network** and **PDN**. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
>
> 4. **Using the wrong number of metal layers in formulas:** KTU problems often test the formula $N_{metal} = \lceil \tfrac{1}{2} \log_2(N) \rceil + 2$. State it cleanly with the "+2" overhead.
>
> 5. **Mixing units in numerical problems:** Always convert mA → A, ps → s, and μm → m *before* substituting. Show units in every step.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the dynamic power formula context:** $P = \alpha C V^2 f$ applies to *dynamic* power; leakage power is separate. Examiners penalize confusion.
>
> 7. **Not defining ESD, IR drop, or skew the first time they are used:** KTU valuation requires precise terminology.
>
> 8. **Drawing the SoC diagram without arrows showing data flow:** A block diagram without directional arrows is incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section as your last-page revision sheet before the KTU exam.**

- [ ] **Macro** = reusable, self-contained functional block in SoC.
- [ ] **Hard macro** = GDSII, no flexibility, best PPA (use for memory/analog).
- [ ] **Soft macro** = RTL, fully flexible, technology-independent (use for processors).
- [ ] **Firm macro** = netlist + constraints, balanced (use for critical arithmetic).
- [ ] **Hierarchical partitioning** is mandatory for SoCs > 10M gates.
- [ ] Partitioning can be **functional, dataflow, clock-domain, power-domain, or voltage-island**.
- [ ] **Interconnect options:** Bus (APB) → Crossbar (AXI) → NoC (multi-core).
- [ ] **NoC** scales to many cores; **Crossbar** is best for moderate SoCs; **Bus** is for low-bandwidth peripherals.
- [ ] **Pin planning** must match package; ESD clamps on every signal pad.
- [ ] **Power:Ground pad ratio** ≈ 1:2 to 1:3.
- [ ] **Aspect ratio target** ≈ 1.0 ± 0.1.
- [ ] **Area utilization target** = 70%–85%.
- [ ] **IR drop budget** < 5% of $V_{DD}$.
- [ ] **Decoupling capacitance formula:** $C = \tfrac{I_{peak} \times \Delta t}{\Delta V}$.
- [ ] **Clock skew budget** < 5% of clock period.
- [ ] **Setup time constraint:** $T_{clk} \geq T_{cq} + T_{logic} + T_{setup} + T_{skew}$.
- [ ] **Metal layers formula:** $N_{metal} = \lceil \tfrac{1}{2} \log_2(N) \rceil + 2$.
- [ ] **Dynamic power:** $P = \alpha C V^2 f$.
- [ ] **Rent's Rule** (pin estimation): $T = K N^p$ with $p \approx 0.5$–$0.75$.
- [ ] **Pad ring** = top-level physical I/O structure; **pin** = logical abstraction.
- [ ] **Voltage islands** enable DVFS for power savings (30–60% in mobile SoCs).
- [ ] **Power gating** uses sleep transistors for leakage reduction.
- [ ] **PMU** (Power Management Unit) orchestrates all power/clock state transitions.
- [ ] **PLL** generates high-frequency clocks from a low-frequency reference.
- [ ] **Spine-and-rib, H-tree, and clock mesh** are the three clock distribution topologies.
- [ ] **First-pass silicon success** strongly correlates with rigorous top-level planning.
- [ ] Top-level decisions are **global** — they affect all macros — and must precede physical design.
- [ ] **AMBA** (Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture) family = APB < AHB < AXI < CHI in performance.

---

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Top Level Macro Design Process** in System on Chip (SoC) design refers to the structured, hierarchical methodology used to architect, partition, integrate, and verify the major functional blocks (macros) of a complex integrated circuit at the chip's highest level of abstraction. It encompasses the planning of design hierarchy, the specification of macro interfaces, the construction of the chip-level integration environment, and the establishment of design rules that govern every sub-block.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this process is treated as the bridge between the **algorithmic/RTL specification phase** and the **physical implementation (Place & Route) phase**, ensuring that the SoC meets its power, performance, and area (PPA) targets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCEVT602 — Module 2):**
> The macro design process is the *foundation* upon which the entire SoC is constructed. Any architectural flaw introduced at this stage propagates exponentially through downstream RTL synthesis, floorplanning, and physical design, making it the single most important design decision phase.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "City Planning" Analogy

Imagine you are an **urban planner** tasked with designing a new smart city. You cannot lay individual bricks or build a single house first; instead, you must first plan:

- **Zones** (residential, commercial, industrial) → analogous to **functional macros** (CPU core, memory controller, peripheral blocks)
- **Roads connecting zones** → analogous to **on-chip interconnects and buses** (AXI, AHB, NoC)
- **Power and water supply grid** → analogous to **clock and power distribution networks**
- **City rules and zoning laws** → analogous to **design constraints** (timing, power budget, area)

The **Top Level Macro Design Process** is exactly this: before any transistor is placed, the architect decides *what blocks exist*, *how they talk to each other*, *what protocols they use*, and *what constraints they must obey*. Once this "city plan" is approved, individual teams can independently build each block (the houses), and the chip integrator simply "hooks them up" along the predefined roads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:**
> A macro in SoC terminology is **not** the same as a single gate. A macro is a *substantial, reusable, and often pre-designed IP block* (like a UART controller, DDR PHY, or ARM Cortex core). The "Top Level" indicates we are working at the chip-wide scope, looking at the SoC as a collection of these macros.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants in Macro Design

The following **standard engineering metrics** govern every macro design decision:

- **Technology Node (nm):** Typically **7 nm**, **5 nm**, **16 nm**, **28 nm**, or **40 nm** in modern SoCs.
- **Operating Voltage (V):** Nominal core voltage ranges from **0.6 V to 1.2 V** depending on the process.
- **Clock Frequency (MHz/GHz):** Modern application processors target **1 GHz to 5 GHz**.
- **Area Utilization (%):** Target die utilization typically **70–85%**; exceeding this causes routing congestion.
- **Power Budget (mW/W):** Mobile SoCs target **< 5 W**; high-performance SoCs may allow **15–125 W**.
- **IR Drop (mV):** Must remain below **5% of Vdd** (e.g., **< 60 mV** for 1.2 V supply).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Phases of the Top Level Macro Design Process

The Top Level Macro Design Process is broken into **six sequential phases**, each of which is a frequently tested concept in KTU examinations.

### Phase 1 — Specification & Architecture Planning
- **Goal:** Define the SoC's functional, electrical, and physical requirements.
- **Activities:**
  - Create the **System-Level Specification Document** (the "spec").
  - Define **Performance targets** (MIPS, throughput, latency).
  - Identify **I/O requirements** (number of pins, standards such as LVDS, PCIe, USB).
  - Lock the **power and thermal envelope**.
- **Why this matters:** Errors here are the most expensive to fix later in the flow (cost grows ~10× at each stage).

### Phase 2 — Design Partitioning
- **Goal:** Decompose the system into manageable, synthesizable macros.
- **Partitioning Strategies:**
  - **Functional Partitioning** — Group logic by function (e.g., all graphics logic in one macro).
  - **Geometric/Physical Partitioning** — Group logic by physical proximity to reduce wirelength.
  - **Hierarchical Partitioning** — Recursive breakdown until each macro meets an area threshold (e.g., **< 50 k gates**).
- **KTU-Exam Favorite:** The **rule of thumb** is that each macro should have a **bounded pin count** (typically **< 200 pins** for soft macros) to keep synthesis tractable.

### Phase 3 — Macro Interface Definition
- **Goal:** Standardize how macros exchange data, control, and clocks.
- **Interface Standards:**
  - **AMBA AXI4 / AXI4-Lite** — High-performance, used for CPU-to-memory.
  - **AMBA AHB / APB** — Lower bandwidth, used for peripheral access.
  - **TileLink / CHI** — Coherent interconnects in multi-core designs.
  - **Custom handshaking** — For domain-specific accelerators (e.g., NPU, DSP).

### Phase 4 — Top-Level RTL Integration
- **Goal:** Create the chip-level HDL (Verilog/VHDL/SystemVerilog) that instantiates and wires together all macros.
- **Key Constructs Used:**
  - **Module instantiation**
  - **Interface ports (SystemVerilog `interface` blocks)**
  - **Clock-domain crossing (CDC) wrappers**
  - **Bus functional models (BFMs)** for verification

### Phase 5 — Constraint Definition for Synthesis
- **Goal:** Translate the architecture into SDC (Synopsys Design Constraints).
- **Examples of SDC commands:**
  - `create_clock` — Defines clock frequency.
  - `set_input_delay` / `set_output_delay` — Sets I/O timing.
  - `set_max_area` — Enforces area budget.
  - `set_max_fanout` — Caps capacitive loading.

### Phase 6 — Top-Level Verification & Sign-off Planning
- **Goal:** Ensure functional correctness before physical design.
- **Activities:**
  - **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** setup.
  - **Power Intent (UPF/CPF)** definition.
  - **DFT (Design-for-Test) integration** — scan chains, BIST, JTAG.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Hierarchy

The hierarchy exists because of three fundamental physical and economic realities:

1. **Tool Scalability:** Synthesis and P&R tools choke on flat designs above **~5 million gates**. Hierarchical macros keep each block within tool capacity.
2. **Team Parallelism:** Multiple design teams can work on independent macros simultaneously, drastically reducing time-to-market.
3. **IP Reusability:** Well-designed macros (e.g., a USB 3.0 controller) can be reused across products, saving enormous engineering cost.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Parameter | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Die Area | $A_{die} = \frac{N_{gates} \cdot A_{gate}}{U}$ | $\text{mm}^2$ | Total silicon area; $U$ is utilization (0.7–0.85) |
| Wirelength Estimate | $L_{wire} \approx k \cdot \sqrt{N_{macros}} \cdot R$ | $\mu m$ | $k$ = Rent's constant; $R$ = Rent's exponent |
| Rent's Rule | $T = t \cdot N^{p}$ | dimensionless | $T$ = external pins, $N$ = gates, $p$ = Rent's exponent (typically **0.5–0.7**) |
| Max Fanout Constraint | $FO_{max} \leq \frac{I_{drive}}{C_{load}}$ | ratio | Limits capacitance-driven delay |
| IR Drop | $V_{drop} = I \cdot R_{grid}$ | $\text{mV}$ | Must be $\le 5\%$ of $V_{dd}$ |
| Critical Path Delay | $t_{pd} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_{pi}$ | $\text{ns}$ | Sum of gate + interconnect delays |
| Power (Dynamic) | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f$ | $\text{W}$ | Switching activity $\alpha$, frequency $f$ |
| Power (Static) | $P_{stat} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{dd}$ | $\text{W}$ | Subthreshold + gate leakage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rent's Rule** is a *frequently asked* topic in KTU VLSI papers. It describes the empirical relationship between the number of external connections $T$ of a block and the number of internal blocks $N$ it contains.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Top Level Macro Design Process is the **blueprint of every commercial SoC** in production today:

- **Smartphone SoCs** (Qualcomm Snapdragon, Apple A-series, MediaTek Dimensity) use a 3-tier macro hierarchy: **Application Processor cluster → IP subsystem (GPU, ISP, Modem) → Standard cell blocks**.
- **Automotive SoCs** (NXP S32, Renesas R-Car) emphasize **safety macro partitioning** with lockstep cores in isolated power domains.
- **AI Accelerator SoCs** (Google TPU, NVIDIA Drive) use **chiplet-style macro design** with dedicated NPU macros connected via NoC.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Estimating Die Area from Gate Count

This derivation is essential for **KTU Part B design problems** where you are given a gate budget and asked to estimate the die size.

**Problem Statement:** A SoC contains **20 million (N = 20 × 10⁶) NAND-2 equivalent gates**. Each gate occupies an area of **A_gate = 1.2 μm²** in a **28 nm** process. If target die utilization is **U = 0.80**, calculate the die area.

**Step 1 — State the Fundamental Relationship**

The total silicon area consumed by gates is simply the gate count multiplied by the area per gate:

$$A_{gates} = N_{gates} \cdot A_{gate}$$

This gives the *logical* area occupied by the transistors themselves, ignoring spacing, routing, and macro overhead.

**Step 2 — Substitute the Given Values**

$$A_{gates} = (20 \times 10^6) \cdot (1.2 \, \mu m^2)$$

$$A_{gates} = 24 \times 10^6 \, \mu m^2$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Standard Units**

Convert square micrometers to square millimeters (since $1 \, mm^2 = 10^6 \, \mu m^2$):

$$A_{gates} = 24 \times 10^6 \, \mu m^2 \cdot \frac{1 \, mm^2}{10^6 \, \mu m^2} = 24 \, mm^2$$

**Step 4 — Account for Utilization**

The *utilization factor* $U$ represents the fraction of the die actually covered by active logic. The rest is consumed by power rails, clock tree, routing channels, and macro padding. Therefore, the **total die area** is:

$$A_{die} = \frac{A_{gates}}{U}$$

**Step 5 — Final Calculation**

$$A_{die} = \frac{24 \, mm^2}{0.80} = 30 \, mm^2$$

**Result:** The die is approximately **30 mm²** in area, which for a square die implies a side length of $\sqrt{30} \approx 5.48 \, mm$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Estimating Number of Inter-Macro Connections via Rent's Rule

**Problem Statement:** A macro contains **N = 50,000 gates**. The macro has two interface types:
- Internal complexity parameter **t = 4**
- Rent's exponent **p = 0.6**

Calculate the number of external pins (T) required at the macro boundary.

**Step 1 — Recall Rent's Rule**

$$T = t \cdot N^{p}$$

This empirical rule was originally derived by E.F. Rent in 1971 from observations of IBM mainframe logic partitioning.

**Step 2 — Compute N^p**

$$N^{p} = (50{,}000)^{0.6}$$

We evaluate this using logarithms. Take natural log of both sides:

$$\ln(N^{p}) = 0.6 \cdot \ln(50{,}000)$$

$$\ln(50{,}000) = \ln(5 \times 10^4) = \ln(5) + 4 \ln(10)$$

$$\ln(5) \approx 1.6094, \quad \ln(10) \approx 2.3026$$

$$\ln(50{,}000) \approx 1.6094 + 4(2.3026) = 1.6094 + 9.2104 = 10.8198$$

$$\ln(N^{p}) = 0.6 \cdot 10.8198 = 6.4919$$

$$N^{p} = e^{6.4919} \approx 656.1$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by t**

$$T = 4 \cdot 656.1 = 2624.4$$

**Step 4 — Round and Interpret**

$$T \approx 2625 \, \text{pins}$$

**Interpretation:** A 50 k-gate macro following these Rent's parameters would need approximately **2,625 boundary pins** if implemented as a stand-alone block. In practice, the macro is decomposed hierarchically, and the pin count at the leaf level is much lower (typically **< 200**), which is why the **hierarchical partitioning** we discussed in Section 2 is so critical.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Path Delay Budgeting

**Problem Statement:** An SoC must operate at **f = 500 MHz** with a clock period of **T_clk = 2 ns**. The top-level timing budget allocates:
- **40%** to combinational logic
- **25%** to inter-macro interconnect
- **15%** to clock skew
- **10%** to setup margin
- **10%** to clock-to-output

Calculate the allowable time for each component.

**Step 1 — Establish the Budget Equation**

The clock period must satisfy the timing equation:

$$T_{clk} = t_{comb} + t_{wire} + t_{skew} + t_{setup} + t_{ck2q}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Each Component**

$$t_{comb} = 0.40 \cdot 2 \, ns = 0.80 \, ns$$

$$t_{wire} = 0.25 \cdot 2 \, ns = 0.50 \, ns$$

$$t_{skew} = 0.15 \cdot 2 \, ns = 0.30 \, ns$$

$$t_{setup} = 0.10 \cdot 2 \, ns = 0.20 \, ns$$

$$t_{ck2q} = 0.10 \cdot 2 \, ns = 0.20 \, ns$$

**Step 3 — Verify the Sum**

$$0.80 + 0.50 + 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.20 = 2.00 \, ns = T_{clk} \quad \checkmark$$

**Interpretation:** The combinational logic has only **800 ps** to complete its evaluation across potentially millions of gates distributed across macros. This is why **timing-driven partitioning** (placing frequently-communicating macros close together) is essential.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Partitioning Trade-offs

| Partitioning Strategy | Area Efficiency | Timing Closure | Verification Effort | IP Reuse |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Functional | Medium | Hard (long wires) | Low | High |
| Geometric/Physical | High | Easy (short wires) | Medium | Medium |
| Hierarchical | High | Easy | Medium | High |
| Flat (no partition) | Low | Very hard | Very high | None |
| Domain-Specific (AI block isolated) | Medium | Medium | Low | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Top-Level Macro Design Flow — Block-Level Architecture

The following diagram maps the **functional flow** of the Top Level Macro Design Process, showing the inputs, transformations, and outputs at each phase.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Step 0 Design Spec] --> A1[Step 1 Architecture Planning]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2 Macro Partitioning]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3 Interface Definition]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4 RTL Integration]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5 Constraint Definition]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6 Verification and Signoff]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7 Handoff to Physical Design]
    A1 -.feedback.-> A0
    A3 -.feedback.-> A2
    A5 -.feedback.-> A4
    A6 -.feedback.-> A5
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **solid arrows** represent the forward flow of the design.
- The **dashed feedback arrows** represent **iteration loops** — a critical reality in real SoC design. Almost every step forces re-evaluation of the previous one.
- For instance, after **Constraint Definition (Step 5)**, you may discover the macros you partitioned in **Step 2** cannot meet timing, forcing a re-partition.

## 4.2 Macro Hierarchy and Connectivity — Detailed Block View

This diagram shows how a typical SoC is organized at the macro level, with the CPU, memory, peripherals, and interconnect macros connected via a central on-chip bus.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SOC[SoC Top Level]
        direction TB
        CPU[CPU Core Macro]
        GPU[GPU Macro]
        DSP[DSP Macro]
        MEM[Memory Controller Macro]
        USB[USB Controller Macro]
        ETH[Ethernet Macro]
        BUS[NoC Interconnect Macro]
        CLK[Clock Manager Macro]
        PWR[Power Management Macro]

        CPU <--> BUS
        GPU <--> BUS
        DSP <--> BUS
        MEM <--> BUS
        USB <--> BUS
        ETH <--> BUS
        CLK -.clock.-> CPU
        CLK -.clock.-> GPU
        CLK -.clock.-> DSP
        CLK -.clock.-> MEM
        CLK -.clock.-> USB
        CLK -.clock.-> ETH
        PWR -.power.-> CPU
        PWR -.power.-> GPU
        PWR -.power.-> DSP
        PWR -.power.-> MEM
        PWR -.power.-> USB
        PWR -.power.-> ETH
        PWR -.power.-> BUS
    end
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **solid bidirectional arrows** represent data flow through the **NoC (Network on Chip) interconnect macro**.
- The **dashed arrows** represent **non-data signals**: clock distribution from the **Clock Manager** and power rail control from the **Power Management** macro.
- The CPU, GPU, and DSP are *computational macros*; the USB and Ethernet are *I/O macros*; the Memory Controller is a *bridge macro* between the on-chip bus and external DRAM.

## 4.3 The Iterative Verification Loop

The verification step is not a single event but a continuous loop. The following diagram illustrates this concept.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[RTL Top] --> B2[Lint Check]
    B2 --> B3[Functional Simulation]
    B3 --> B4[Synthesis]
    B4 --> B5[Static Timing Analysis]
    B5 --> B6[Power Analysis]
    B6 --> B7[Design Signoff]
    B7 --> B8[Physical Design Handoff]
    B5 -.violation.-> B1
    B6 -.power budget exceeded.-> B1
    B3 -.functional bug.-> B1
```

**Key Insight:** The three dashed feedback arrows from STA, Power Analysis, and Simulation back to the **RTL Top** show the **three primary reasons** a top-level design must be revised. This iterative refinement is a hallmark of real SoC design and is a frequent KTU exam question topic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a "macro" in the context of System on Chip design. List any two advantages of hierarchical macro-based design.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **macro** in SoC design is a *substantial, self-contained, and reusable functional block* that performs a specific set of operations — for example, a CPU core, USB controller, or memory subsystem. Macros are the building blocks of the top-level SoC and can be **soft** (synthesizable RTL), **firm** (gate-level netlist), or **hard** (pre-laid-out GDSII).

**Two advantages of hierarchical macro-based design:**

1. **Improved tool scalability:** Synthesis and physical design tools struggle with flat designs of more than ~5 million gates. Partitioning into macros keeps each sub-block within tool capacity.

2. **Parallel team productivity:** Independent design teams can develop, verify, and harden different macros concurrently, drastically reducing time-to-market.

*(Allocation: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark per advantage.)*

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is Rent's Rule? State its significance in top-level macro design.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Rent's Rule** is an empirical relationship that relates the number of external pins $T$ of a logic block to the number of gates $N$ it contains:

$$T = t \cdot N^{p}$$

where $t$ is the average number of pins per gate and $p$ is Rent's exponent (typically **0.5 to 0.7**).

**Significance in top-level macro design:**

- It **predicts the expected pin count** at macro boundaries, helping designers plan the interconnect architecture (number of bus lines, mux sizing).
- It guides the **depth of hierarchical partitioning** — overly deep hierarchies violate the rule and lead to inefficient pin distribution.
- It is used to **estimate wirelength and routing congestion** before physical design, enabling floorplan-level decisions.

*(Allocation: 1 mark for formula + 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for significance.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **six phases** of the Top Level Macro Design Process in detail with a neat block diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A SoC contains **15 million gates** with each gate occupying **1.0 μm²** in a **22 nm** process. The target die utilization is **0.75**. Calculate the **die area in mm²** and the **approximate side length** assuming a square die. Also estimate the **external pin count** for one of the macros containing **40,000 gates** with $t = 3$ and $p = 0.55$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

The Top Level Macro Design Process consists of the following six phases:

1. **Specification & Architecture Planning (1 mark):** The functional, electrical, and physical requirements of the SoC are documented. Targets for performance, power, area, and I/O are locked.

2. **Design Partitioning (1 mark):** The system is decomposed into manageable macros using functional, geometric, or hierarchical partitioning strategies.

3. **Macro Interface Definition (1 mark):** Standardized protocols (AXI, AHB, APB) are chosen for inter-macro communication. CDC strategy is also defined.

4. **Top-Level RTL Integration (1.5 marks):** A chip-level HDL module instantiates and connects all macros. SystemVerilog `interface` blocks and clock-domain-crossing wrappers are used.

5. **Constraint Definition (1.5 marks):** SDC (Synopsys Design Constraints) is written to drive synthesis, including `create_clock`, `set_input_delay`, `set_max_area`, and `set_max_fanout`.

6. **Verification & Sign-off Planning (1 mark):** Static Timing Analysis, Power Analysis (UPF), and DFT integration are set up before handoff to physical design.

The block diagram (as shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes) depicts this flow with iteration loops.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Die Area Calculation (3.5 marks):**

$$A_{gates} = N_{gates} \cdot A_{gate} = (15 \times 10^6) \cdot (1.0 \, \mu m^2) = 15 \times 10^6 \, \mu m^2$$

Convert to mm²:

$$A_{gates} = 15 \times 10^6 \, \mu m^2 \cdot \frac{1 \, mm^2}{10^6 \, \mu m^2} = 15 \, mm^2$$

Apply utilization factor:

$$A_{die} = \frac{A_{gates}}{U} = \frac{15}{0.75} = 20 \, mm^2$$

**Step 2 — Side Length (1.5 marks):**

For a square die:

$$L_{side} = \sqrt{A_{die}} = \sqrt{20 \, mm^2} \approx 4.47 \, mm$$

**Step 3 — External Pin Count via Rent's Rule (2 marks):**

$$T = t \cdot N^{p} = 3 \cdot (40{,}000)^{0.55}$$

Compute $N^{p}$ using logarithms:

$$\ln(40{,}000) = \ln(4 \times 10^4) = \ln(4) + 4 \ln(10) = 1.3863 + 9.2104 = 10.5967$$

$$\ln(N^{p}) = 0.55 \cdot 10.5967 = 5.8282$$

$$N^{p} = e^{5.8282} \approx 339.4$$

$$T = 3 \cdot 339.4 \approx 1018 \, \text{pins}$$

**Final Answer:** Die area = **20 mm²**, Side length ≈ **4.47 mm**, Macro pin count ≈ **1018 pins**.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the role of the **on-chip interconnect macro** in SoC design. Compare **bus-based** and **Network-on-Chip (NoC)** interconnect architectures. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An SoC is clocked at **1 GHz**. The timing budget allocates **45%** to combinational logic, **20%** to interconnect, **15%** to clock skew, **10%** to setup time, and **10%** to clock-to-output. Calculate the **allowable time for each component** and verify that the sum equals the clock period. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

The **on-chip interconnect macro** is the central communication backbone of the SoC, routing data, address, and control signals between all functional macros. It implements a chosen protocol (AXI, AHB, CHI, etc.) and typically includes arbiters, decoders, and bridge logic.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

Refer to **Section 4.2** of these notes. The diagram shows the NoC interconnect macro centrally connected to CPU, GPU, DSP, Memory Controller, USB, and Ethernet macros, with the Clock Manager and Power Management macros providing non-data services.

**Comparison: Bus-based vs NoC Interconnect (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Bus-Based (AXI/AHB) | Network-on-Chip (NoC) |
|---|---|---|
| Scalability | Poor (bus contention) | Excellent (parallel paths) |
| Bandwidth | Limited by shared bus | High (multiple links) |
| Latency | Variable, high under load | Predictable, low |
| Power | Lower for small SoCs | Higher due to routers |
| Area | Smaller | Larger (router overhead) |
| Best for | Small/medium SoCs (<10 macros) | Large multi-core SoCs |

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Clock Period (1 mark):**

$$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1 \times 10^9 \, Hz} = 1 \, ns$$

**Step 2 — Component-wise Calculation (5 marks):**

$$t_{comb} = 0.45 \cdot 1 \, ns = 0.45 \, ns$$

$$t_{wire} = 0.20 \cdot 1 \, ns = 0.20 \, ns$$

$$t_{skew} = 0.15 \cdot 1 \, ns = 0.15 \, ns$$

$$t_{setup} = 0.10 \cdot 1 \, ns = 0.10 \, ns$$

$$t_{ck2q} = 0.10 \cdot 1 \, ns = 0.10 \, ns$$

**Step 3 — Verification (1 mark):**

$$0.45 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.10 = 1.00 \, ns = T_{clk} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–2 marks):** Students frequently forget to convert μm² to mm² (factor of 10⁶). Always show the unit cancellation explicitly.
> 2. **Logarithm Mistakes in Rent's Rule (–1.5 marks):** Use `ln(N)` not `log10(N)`. A common error is mixing the two bases and getting a wildly incorrect pin count.
> 3. **Forgetting Utilization Factor (–1 mark):** The die area is *not* the raw gate area. Dividing by $U$ is mandatory.
> 4. **No Block Diagram (–2 marks):** In Part A of Question B, a neat block diagram is worth at least 2–3 marks. Skipping it is the most common reason for losing marks.
> 5. **Timing Budget Verification (–1 mark):** Always re-verify that your component times sum to the clock period. The examiner awards a bonus mark for this discipline.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Definition Recall:** A *macro* is a substantial, reusable IP block; *Top-Level* refers to the chip-wide scope.
- **Six Phases (in order):** Specification → Partitioning → Interface Definition → RTL Integration → Constraint Definition → Verification & Sign-off.
- **Rent's Rule:** $T = t \cdot N^{p}$ with $p \in [0.5, 0.7]$ — used to predict pin count.
- **Die Area Formula:** $A_{die} = \dfrac{N_{gates} \cdot A_{gate}}{U}$.
- **Utilization Range:** Real designs use **70–85%** utilization; going higher causes routing congestion.
- **Clock Period Equation:** $T_{clk} = t_{comb} + t_{wire} + t_{skew} + t_{setup} + t_{ck2q}$.
- **Interconnect Standards:** AXI4 (high-perf), AHB/APB (peripherals), NoC (large multi-core).
- **Hierarchy Purpose:** Tool scalability, team parallelism, IP reuse.
- **Iteration Loops:** STA, power, and simulation bugs force RTL re-work — this is *expected*, not failure.
- **Power Equation (Dynamic):** $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f$.
- **IR Drop Rule:** Must stay below **5% of $V_{dd}$**.
- **Common Macro Types:** Computational (CPU/GPU/DSP), I/O (USB/Ethernet), Bridge (Memory Controller), Service (Clock/Power Manager).
- **Pin Count Heuristic:** Keep individual leaf macros below **~200 pins** for synthesis tractability.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Activities and Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT602 - SYSTEM ON CHIP DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Activities and Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **System on Chip (SoC) Design**, **Activities and Tools** refer to the systematic sequence of engineering tasks (activities) performed across the **SoC design flow** and the corresponding **Electronic Design Automation (EDA)** software platforms (tools) used to execute, verify, and validate each task. The activities transform an abstract system specification into a **GDSII (Graphic Design System II)** layout ready for fabrication, while the tools automate, simulate, and optimize each transformation stage.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme, the SoC design activities are broadly classified into:
- **Front-End Activities** (Specification, Architecture, RTL Design, Functional Verification)
- **Back-End Activities** (Synthesis, Floorplanning, Placement, CTS, Routing, Physical Verification, Sign-off)

The **Tools** are software frameworks developed by EDA vendors (Synopsys, Cadence, Siemens EDA, Xilinx, Intel) that provide automated assistance to perform these activities.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 emphasizes understanding *what is done* (activity) and *how it is done* (tool) at each step of the SoC design flow. Students must map each activity to its primary industry-grade tool.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine building a modern **smartphone motherboard** from scratch. You cannot just place chips randomly — you need a **factory assembly line**. 

- The **Activities** are the stations on the assembly line: *writing the blueprint* (specification), *choosing components* (architecture), *drafting the circuit schematic* (RTL design), *testing the prototype on a simulator* (verification), *arranging chips on the board* (placement), *drawing the copper wires connecting them* (routing), and *final quality inspection* (sign-off).
- The **Tools** are the **machines and software** at each station — like a Computer-Aided Design (CAD) software for drafting, a 3D printer for prototype testing, and an X-ray machine for quality inspection.

Just as a factory needs the *right machine at the right station*, an SoC designer needs the *right EDA tool at the right design activity* to ensure the chip is **functional, fast, low-power, and manufacturable**.

## 1.3 Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Examiner Insight:** Questions on "Activities and Tools" frequently appear as **2-mark direct questions** ("List any four EDA tools used in SoC design") or as **7-mark descriptive questions** ("Explain the SoC design flow with the tools used at each stage"). Mastery of this topic guarantees easy marks in Part A and forms the backbone of any Part B design-flow question.

### Standard Industry Metrics (used throughout the design activities)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | Significance |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Operating Frequency** | $f$ | **MHz / GHz** | Speed of the SoC |
| **Dynamic Power** | $P_{dyn}$ | **mW / W** | Switching power consumed |
| **Static Power** | $P_{stat}$ | **µW / mW** | Leakage power when idle |
| **Core Area** | $A$ | **mm² / µm²** | Silicon real estate used |
| **Propagation Delay** | $t_{pd}$ | **ns / ps** | Time for a signal to traverse a gate |
| **Process Node** | $L$ | **nm** | Manufacturing technology size |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SoC Design Activity Flow (Step-by-Step Logic)

The SoC design flow is divided into well-defined activities. Each activity has a **specific input**, a **specific transformation**, and a **specific output** (deliverable). Below is the structured logic:

### **Activity 1: System Specification**
- **What:** Define the functionality, performance, power, area (often abbreviated as **PPAC** or simply **PPA**), and cost targets.
- **Input:** Market requirement document.
- **Output:** System specification document (e.g., processing 4K video at 60 fps, 1080 mAh power budget, under $5 cost).
- **Tool Used:** Document editors, requirement management tools (e.g., Jama Connect, DOORS).

### **Activity 2: Architecture Design & HW/SW Partitioning**
- **What:** Decide which functionality is implemented in **hardware** (RTL → silicon) and which runs in **software** (firmware on an embedded processor core like ARM Cortex-A series).
- **Input:** System specification.
- **Output:** Architecture model, partition table, choice of processor IP, bus interconnect (AMBA AXI/AHB/APB).
- **Tool Used:** **SystemC**, **MATLAB/Simulink**, **CoWare** (now Synopsys Platform Architect).

### **Activity 3: Algorithm Development & Modeling**
- **What:** Write reference models in high-level languages (C/C++/Matlab) to verify functional correctness before hardware implementation.
- **Output:** Golden reference model.
- **Tool Used:** MATLAB, C/C++ compilers, Python (for high-level modeling).

### **Activity 4: RTL Design (Hardware Description)**
- **What:** Describe the hardware using a Hardware Description Language (**HDL**) such as **Verilog**, **VHDL**, or **SystemVerilog**. The Register Transfer Level (RTL) abstracts the design as data flow between registers on clock edges.
- **Output:** Synthesizable RTL code (.v / .sv / .vhd files).
- **Tool Used:** Text editors (VS Code, Vim), HDL-aware IDEs (Sigasi, HDL Designer), Xilinx Vivado, Intel Quartus.

### **Activity 5: Functional / Behavioral Verification**
- **What:** Verify that the RTL matches the specification by applying testbenches and checking outputs.
- **Output:** Coverage report, verified RTL.
- **Tool Used:** **Cadence Xcelium**, **Synopsys VCS**, **Mentor QuestaSim**, **Verilator** (open-source).

### **Activity 6: Logic Synthesis**
- **What:** Convert the RTL code (behavioral) into a **gate-level netlist** (structural) using a standard cell library.
- **Output:** Gate-level netlist (.v), synthesis reports (area, timing, power).
- **Tool Used:** **Synopsys Design Compiler (DC)**, **Cadence Genus**, **Yosys** (open-source).

### **Activity 7: Floorplanning**
- **What:** Plan the physical macro placement — define core area, I/O pad placement, power grid, and macro positions.
- **Output:** Floorplan file.
- **Tool Used:** **Cadence Innovus**, **Synopsys IC Compiler II (ICC2)**.

### **Activity 8: Placement**
- **What:** Place all standard cells inside the core area to minimize wire length and meet timing.
- **Output:** Placed design database.
- **Tool Used:** Innovus, ICC2, OpenROAD (open-source).

### **Activity 9: Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)**
- **What:** Build a balanced clock distribution network (using inverters/buffers) so that the clock signal reaches all sequential elements with minimal **clock skew**.
- **Output:** CTS-optimized netlist.
- **Tool Used:** Innovus, ICC2.

### **Activity 10: Routing**
- **What:** Connect all placed components using metal layers per design rules.
- **Output:** Routed design (GDSII-ready).
- **Tool Used:** Innovus, ICC2, KiCad (for PCBs/FPGAs).

### **Activity 11: Physical Verification**
- **What:** Run **DRC (Design Rule Check)**, **LVS (Layout vs Schematic)**, and **ERC (Electrical Rule Check)**.
- **Output:** Clean layout database.
- **Tool Used:** **Synopsys IC Validator**, **Cadence PVS / Pegasus**, **Siemens Calibre**.

### **Activity 12: Sign-off Verification**
- **What:** Final timing closure (**STA — Static Timing Analysis**), power analysis, IR drop analysis, and formal equivalence checking.
- **Output:** Sign-off clean database.
- **Tool Used:** **Synopsys PrimeTime** (STA), **Cadence Tempus**, **Synopsys Formality** (Equivalence).

### **Activity 13: Tape-out & Fabrication**
- **What:** Generate the final **GDSII** file and send to the foundry (TSMC, Samsung, Intel Foundry).
- **Tool Used:** Stream-out tools in Innovus/ICC2.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

These are the **engineering equations** evaluated at every stage of the SoC design flow. Students must memorize these for numerical questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Power and delay equations are the most commonly asked numerical derivations. Always state the **boundary condition** (e.g., $\alpha$ = switching activity, valid range $0 \le \alpha \le 1$).

$$
P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static}
$$

$$
P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f
$$

$$
P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leakage}
$$

$$
t_{pd} = \frac{C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}}{k \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{T})^{2}}
$$

$$
T_{clk} \ge t_{clk\text{-}Q} + t_{comb} + t_{setup} \quad (\text{Setup constraint})
$$

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{clk, min}}
$$

### Master Cheat-Sheet Table

| Equation Name | Formula (using $\vert$ for absolute value) | Key Variables & Units | Where Used in Flow |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Dynamic Power** | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | $\alpha$ = activity factor, $C_{L}$ in F, $V_{DD}$ in V, $f$ in Hz | Activity 6, 12 |
| **Static Power** | $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot \vert I_{leak} \vert$ | $I_{leak}$ in A | Activity 12 |
| **Total Power** | $P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{stat}$ | W | Activity 12 |
| **Propagation Delay** | $t_{pd} = \dfrac{C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}}{k(V_{DD} - V_{T})^{2}}$ | $V_{T}$ = threshold voltage in V | Activity 6 |
| **Max Frequency** | $f_{max} = 1 / T_{clk, min}$ | $T_{clk}$ in s | Activity 12 |
| **Setup Slack** | $Slack = T_{clk} - (t_{clk\text{-}Q} + t_{comb} + t_{setup})$ | ns | Activity 12 |
| **Clock Skew** | $\delta_{skew} = \vert t_{clk,i} - t_{clk,j} \vert$ | ps / ns | Activity 9 |
| **Energy per Switch** | $E = \frac{1}{2} C_{L} V_{DD}^{2}$ | Joules | Activity 6 |
| **Area Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{A_{cells}}{A_{core}} \times 100$ | % | Activity 8 |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power-aware design:** The $P_{dyn}$ equation directly guides **clock gating**, **voltage scaling (DVFS)**, and **power gating** techniques in modern smartphone SoCs (e.g., Qualcomm Snapdragon, Apple A-series).
- **Timing closure:** The setup constraint is solved millions of times by the STA tool (PrimeTime) to ensure zero **negative slack** before fabrication.
- **EDA Industry Impact:** The global EDA market is valued at over **$15 billion USD** as of 2024, dominated by the "Big 3" — Synopsys, Cadence, and Siemens EDA.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Power-Delay Product (PDP) and Energy-Delay Product (EDP)

These two metrics are critical in SoC design — they combine the **trade-off** between speed and power, which is the central problem in modern chip design.

### **Step 1: Start with Dynamic Power**

The dynamic power dissipated by a CMOS gate switching at frequency $f$ with load capacitance $C_{L}$ is:

$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f
$$

**Logic:** Every $0 \to 1$ transition charges the load capacitor, drawing energy $\frac{1}{2} C_{L} V_{DD}^{2}$ from the supply. The factor $\alpha$ accounts for the fact that not every clock cycle causes a transition.

### **Step 2: Multiply Power by Delay to get Power-Delay Product (PDP)**

The Power-Delay Product measures the **energy consumed per switching event** (in Joules). It is independent of frequency.

$$
PDP = P_{dyn} \cdot t_{pd}
$$

Substitute the expressions:

$$
PDP = (\alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f) \cdot t_{pd}
$$

**Logic:** The $f \cdot t_{pd}$ factor represents how many switching events occur per unit time relative to the delay. We can simplify by recognizing that for a single gate, the relevant time is the delay of that gate, so $f \cdot t_{pd}$ for a representative switching cycle becomes a normalized constant. Dropping $\alpha$ for the single-event case:

$$
PDP = C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f \cdot t_{pd}
$$

For a single switching event, the energy is the integral of power over time, giving the well-known CMOS switching energy:

$$
E_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2}
$$

This is the **energy per transition**, which is the fundamental lower bound on energy in CMOS.

### **Step 3: Derive Energy-Delay Product (EDP)**

EDP combines energy with delay to assess the **quality** of a design. Lower EDP means a faster, more energy-efficient gate.

$$
EDP = E_{sw} \cdot t_{pd} = \left( \frac{1}{2} C_{L} V_{DD}^{2} \right) \cdot t_{pd}
$$

**Logic:** EDP penalizes designs that are both slow AND power-hungry. A fast design with high power may have similar PDP to a slow, low-power design, but the EDP will be lower for the fast design, favoring speed.

### **Step 4: Substituting the Delay Equation**

Recall the propagation delay:

$$
t_{pd} = \frac{C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}}{k \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{T})^{2}}
$$

Substitute into the EDP:

$$
EDP = \frac{1}{2} C_{L} V_{DD}^{2} \cdot \frac{C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}}{k (V_{DD} - V_{T})^{2}}
$$

Multiply the numerators:

$$
EDP = \frac{C_{L}^{2} \cdot V_{DD}^{3}}{2 k (V_{DD} - V_{T})^{2}}
$$

**Logic Interpretation:**
- $C_{L}^{2}$ in the numerator: **Reducing load capacitance** has a quadratic benefit on EDP.
- $V_{DD}^{3}$ in numerator: **Voltage scaling** is the most powerful lever (cubic improvement) for energy efficiency — this is why **DVFS (Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling)** is used in every modern SoC.

### **Step 5: Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)**

**Problem:** A CMOS inverter has $C_{L} = 50 \text{ fF}$, $V_{DD} = 1.2 \text{ V}$, $V_{T} = 0.4 \text{ V}$, $k = 100 \, \mu\text{A/V}^{2}$, and switches at $f = 500 \text{ MHz}$ with activity factor $\alpha = 0.2$.

**Find:** $P_{dyn}$, $E_{sw}$, PDP, and EDP.

**Solution — Step A: Compute $t_{pd}$**

$$
t_{pd} = \frac{C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}}{k (V_{DD} - V_{T})^{2}}
$$

$$
t_{pd} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 1.2}{100 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (1.2 - 0.4)^{2}}
$$

$$
t_{pd} = \frac{60 \times 10^{-15}}{100 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.64}
$$

$$
t_{pd} = \frac{60 \times 10^{-15}}{64 \times 10^{-6}} = 9.375 \times 10^{-10} \text{ s} = 0.9375 \text{ ns}
$$

**Solution — Step B: Compute $P_{dyn}$**

$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f
$$

$$
P_{dyn} = 0.2 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-15} \cdot (1.2)^{2} \cdot 500 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
P_{dyn} = 0.2 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 1.44 \cdot 5 \times 10^{8}
$$

$$
P_{dyn} = 0.2 \cdot 50 \cdot 1.44 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-15+8}
$$

$$
P_{dyn} = 72 \times 10^{-7} = 7.2 \, \mu\text{W}
$$

**Solution — Step C: Compute $E_{sw}$**

$$
E_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} C_{L} V_{DD}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 50 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 1.44 = 36 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J} = 36 \text{ fJ}
$$

**Solution — Step D: Compute PDP**

$$
PDP = P_{dyn} \cdot t_{pd} = 7.2 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 9.375 \times 10^{-10} = 6.75 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J} = 6.75 \text{ fJ}
$$

**Solution — Step E: Compute EDP**

$$
EDP = PDP \cdot t_{pd} = 6.75 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 9.375 \times 10^{-10} = 6.33 \times 10^{-24} \text{ J·s}
$$
