# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define an EDA tool. List any four EDA tools used in the SoC design flow.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
An **Electronic Design Automation (EDA) tool** is a category of software used to design, simulate, verify, and manufacture electronic systems such as integrated circuits and printed circuit boards. EDA tools automate the complex and error-prone tasks in chip design.

**Four EDA Tools:**

1. **Cadence Innovus** — Used for physical implementation (Placement, CTS, Routing).
2. **Synopsys Design Compiler** — Used for logic synthesis (RTL → Gate-level netlist).
3. **Mentor Graphics Calibre** — Used for physical verification (DRC and LVS).
4. **Synopsys VCS** — Used for functional simulation and verification of RTL.

### **Question 2: What is the difference between DRC and LVS in SoC design?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
- **DRC (Design Rule Check):** Verifies that the physical layout conforms to the **manufacturing rules** of the foundry (e.g., minimum wire width, minimum spacing between metal layers). It is checked against the foundry's **technology file**.
- **LVS (Layout vs Schematic):** Verifies that the **physical layout** is electrically equivalent to the **schematic/netlist**. It checks for connectivity, device count, and pin matching.

In short, DRC checks **"Can it be manufactured?"** while LVS checks **"Does it match the design intent?"**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A: Explain the complete SoC design flow with the EDA tools used at each stage. Highlight the role of verification in the flow.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a): Describe the front-end design activities and their tools. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **front-end** of the SoC design flow encompasses all activities from system specification to a verified, synthesized gate-level netlist. The major activities and tools are:

1. **System Specification (Tool: DOORS / Jama Connect):** The design begins with a detailed requirement specification that defines the target application, performance (clock speed), power budget, die area, and cost. For example, a smartphone SoC may target 3 GHz operation under 5 W power.

2. **Architecture Design & HW/SW Partitioning (Tool: Synopsys Platform Architect, SystemC):** The system architect decides which functions are implemented in hardware (accelerators, e.g., GPU, video decoder) and which run as software on embedded processors (e.g., ARM Cortex-A cores). The **AMBA bus** (AXI, AHB, APB) is selected for on-chip communication.

3. **Algorithm Development (Tool: MATLAB/Simulink, C/C++):** Reference algorithms (e.g., FFT, video codec) are modeled in C/MATLAB to establish a **golden reference** for later verification.

4. **RTL Design (Tool: Xilinx Vivado, Intel Quartus, or text editor):** The hardware is described in **Verilog / VHDL / SystemVerilog** at the Register Transfer Level. The RTL is **synthesizable** and represents the data flow between registers.

5. **Functional Verification (Tool: Synopsys VCS, Cadence Xcelium, Siemens QuestaSim):** Testbenches are written (often in SystemVerilog with **UVM** — Universal Verification Methodology) to apply stimulus and check responses. **Code coverage** and **functional coverage** metrics are collected. Emulation tools (Cadence Palladium, Synopsys ZeBu) are used for large SoCs.

6. **Logic Synthesis (Tool: Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus):** The verified RTL is converted into a **gate-level netlist** mapped to a specific **standard cell library** (e.g., TSMC 28 nm cells). The tool optimizes for area, timing, and power.

#### **Part (b): Describe the back-end design activities, sign-off, and the role of verification. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **back-end** flow takes the synthesized netlist and produces a **fabrication-ready GDSII file**. The activities are:

1. **Floorplanning (Tool: Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC2):** The overall die size, I/O pad placement, and macro positions (e.g., SRAM, PLL) are defined. The **power grid** is designed to ensure uniform $V_{DD}$ distribution.

2. **Placement (Tool: Innovus, ICC2):** All standard cells are placed inside the core. The placer uses algorithms to minimize wire length and meet timing.

3. **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS) (Tool: Innovus, ICC2):** A balanced clock distribution network (buffers + H-tree / fishbone topology) is inserted to minimize **clock skew** and ensure all flip-flops see the clock edge simultaneously.

4. **Routing (Tool: Innovus, ICC2):** Metal layers (M1, M2, ... M_top) are used to physically connect all cells per the netlist. **Global routing** and **detailed routing** stages are performed.

5. **Physical Verification (Tool: Siemens Calibre, Synopsys IC Validator):**
   - **DRC (Design Rule Check):** Ensures layout obeys foundry rules.
   - **LVS (Layout vs Schematic):** Confirms layout matches netlist.
   - **ERC (Electrical Rule Check):** Checks for antenna effects, short circuits.

6. **Sign-off (Tool: Synopsys PrimeTime for STA, Cadence Tempus):**
   - **Static Timing Analysis (STA):** Verifies setup and hold timing across all process-voltage-temperature (PVT) corners.
   - **Power Analysis:** Estimates dynamic and leakage power.
   - **Formal Equivalence:** Synopsys Formality confirms the post-layout netlist is functionally identical to the pre-synthesis RTL.

**Role of Verification (贯穿整个流程):**
Verification is **not a single step** but runs **continuously** throughout the flow. Bugs found after fabrication cost **$1M+** and months of delay. Hence, **verification consumes 60-70%** of total SoC design effort — making it the most critical activity.

---

### **Question B: With neat diagrams, explain the role of the major EDA tools in the SoC design flow. Compare logic synthesis, placement, and routing in terms of objectives and the tools used.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a): Explain the role of major EDA tools in the SoC design flow with a diagram. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*(See the diagrams in SECTION 4.1 and 4.2 for the flow.)*

**Role of Major EDA Tools:**

1. **HDL Simulators (VCS, Xcelium, QuestaSim):** These tools compile the RTL and testbench, then simulate the design by applying stimulus over simulated time. They produce **waveforms (.vcd/.fsdb)** for debugging and **coverage reports** to measure verification completeness.

2. **Logic Synthesizers (Design Compiler, Genus):** These tools read the RTL, perform architectural optimizations (sharing, retiming), map to a target technology library, and output a **gate-level netlist**. They also generate area, timing, and power reports.

3. **Place-and-Route (P&R) Tools (Innovus, ICC2):** These tools perform the physical implementation — floorplanning, placement, CTS, and routing. They handle **timing-driven optimization** by adjusting cell sizes, buffers, and wire routes to close timing.

4. **Physical Verification Tools (Calibre, PVS):** These tools validate that the layout is **manufacturable** (DRC) and **electrically correct** (LVS). They are mandatory before tape-out.

5. **Static Timing Analysis Tools (PrimeTime, Tempus):** These tools perform **path-based** timing analysis across all corners without requiring simulation vectors. They are the **final gatekeeper** before fabrication.

6. **Formal Verification Tools (Formality, JasperGold):** These tools mathematically prove that two representations of a design (e.g., RTL vs netlist, or pre- vs post-layout) are functionally equivalent — without test vectors.

#### **Part (b): Compare Logic Synthesis, Placement, and Routing. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | **Logic Synthesis** | **Placement** | **Routing** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Input** | RTL code (.v/.vhd) + SDC constraints | Gate-level netlist + floorplan | Placed design + routing rules |
| **Output** | Gate-level netlist + .sdf | Placed DEF with cell coordinates | Routed GDSII-ready design |
| **Primary Objective** | Convert behavior to structure; optimize for area, timing, power | Minimize wire length; place cells to meet timing closure | Connect all pins using metal layers per DRC rules |
| **Optimization Level** | Architectural + logical (gates, registers) | Spatial (x,y coordinates of cells) | Geometric (metal layer assignment) |
| **Primary Tool** | Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus, Yosys | Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC2, OpenROAD | Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC2, KiCad |
| **Key Metric** | Cell area (µm²), slack (ns) | Half-perimeter wire length (HPWL) | Congestion, DRC violations |
| **Stage in Flow** | Front-end end | Back-end beginning | Back-end middle |
| **Power Aware?** | Yes — clock gating, DVFS planning | Yes — driven by power grid IR drop | Limited — only via wire sizing |

**Conclusion:** Logic synthesis, placement, and routing are sequential, each with distinct goals. Synthesis translates **intent to gates**, placement positions those gates **optimally**, and routing **physically wires** them — together transforming a behavioral description into a manufacturable layout.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating simulation with verification:** Simulation is one *technique* under verification. Static checks (Linting, CDC, Formal Equivalence) are also verification.
> 2. **Confusing DRC and LVS:** Many students write "DRC checks the circuit" — **wrong**. DRC checks the **physical layout** against foundry rules; LVS checks **layout matches schematic**.
> 3. **Skipping the SDC file:** When listing synthesis tools, students often forget to mention the **Synopsys Design Constraints (.sdc)** file that drives synthesis.
> 4. **Ignoring DFT:** Modern SoCs include **Design-for-Test** activity (boundary scan, BIST) using tools like **Mentor Tessent** — high-value 2-mark addition.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Box — Memorize Before Exam:**

- [ ] **SoC Design Flow = 15 activities**, grouped into **Specification → Front-End → Back-End → Sign-off → Fabrication → Test**.
- [ ] **Big 3 EDA Vendors:** Synopsys, Cadence, Siemens EDA (formerly Mentor Graphics).
- [ ] **Synthesis Tool = Design Compiler (Synopsys) or Genus (Cadence)** — input is RTL, output is gate-level netlist.
- [ ] **P&R Tool = Innovus (Cadence) or ICC2 (Synopsys)** — input is netlist, output is placed-and-routed design.
- [ ] **STA Tool = PrimeTime (Synopsys) or Tempus (Cadence)** — golden sign-off tool.
- [ ] **DRC/LVS Tool = Calibre (Siemens), PVS (Cadence), IC Validator (Synopsys)**.
- [ ] **Dynamic Power Equation:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ — voltage has a **quadratic** effect.
- [ ] **Static Power Equation:** $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot \vert I_{leak} \vert$ — dominates at advanced nodes (< 28 nm).
- [ ] **Verification consumes 60-70%** of the SoC design effort.
- [ ] **GDSII** is the final output (tape-out) — sent to the foundry.
- [ ] **Open-source alternatives:** Yosys (synthesis), OpenROAD (P&R), Verilator (sim), OpenSTA (timing) — popular in academic and startup environments.
- [ ] **HW/SW Co-design tools:** MATLAB/Simulink + HDL simulator (co-simulation).
- [ ] **FPGA prototyping tools:** Xilinx Vivado, Intel Quartus — used for early software development and pre-silicon validation.
- [ ] **EDP** = Energy-Delay Product = best metric to evaluate speed-power trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Subblock Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT602 - SYSTEM ON CHIP DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Subblock Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Subblock Design in SoC — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Subblock Design** is the systematic process of designing, optimizing, and integrating **functional hardware units (subblocks)** that together form the complete System-on-Chip (SoC). Each subblock is a self-contained RTL (Register Transfer Level) module performing a specific logical, arithmetic, control, or memory operation, characterized by defined interfaces, timing, area, and power budgets.

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, subblock design forms the **RTL Design Phase** of the SoC design flow, positioned between high-level architectural specification and physical synthesis. Subblocks include **datapath elements** (adders, multipliers, shifters, comparators), **control logic** (FSMs, decoders, arbiters), **storage elements** (registers, register files, FIFOs), and **interconnect components** (multiplexers, bus arbiters, clock-domain crossing logic).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Subblock design is graded on **Area–Delay–Power (ADP)** trade-offs, **modularity**, **reusability**, and adherence to **standard timing interfaces** (setup, hold, clock-to-Q). The design must be synthesizable using standard cell libraries and must conform to a defined **design specification (design spec)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Engineering Intuition

Imagine you are building a **modern automobile**. You do not manufacture the entire car as a single monolithic piece. Instead, you design and assemble independent functional modules: the **engine block**, the **transmission**, the **braking system**, the **electrical wiring harness**, and the **infotainment unit**. Each module is designed, tested, and validated independently, and they communicate through standardized interfaces (mechanical couplings, CAN bus, wiring connectors).

An **SoC is the automobile**. A **subblock is the engine block or the braking system**. Just as the engine block has well-defined input (fuel, air) and output (rotational torque) interfaces with documented performance (horsepower, RPM, fuel efficiency), each SoC subblock has:
- **Inputs** (operands, control signals, clock, reset)
- **Outputs** (results, status flags, valid signals)
- **Performance metrics** (latency, throughput, area in gate equivalents, power in µW/MHz)
- **Interface protocol** (handshaking, ready/valid, FIFO-style)

This **divide-and-conquer** philosophy is what makes SoC design tractable for chips containing **billions of transistors**.

## 1.3 Classification of Subblocks in SoC

Subblocks are broadly classified into the following categories, each with distinct design methodologies:

| Category | Examples | Primary Design Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Datapath Arithmetic** | Adders, Subtractors, Multipliers, Dividers, ALUs | Delay, Throughput |
| **Datapath Logic** | Multiplexers, Encoders, Decoders, Comparators | Area, Fan-out |
| **Storage** | Registers, Latches, Register Files, SRAM, ROM, CAM | Density, Access Time |
| **Control** | FSMs, Counters, Sequencers, Interrupt Controllers | Latency, State Encoding |
| **Interconnect** | Bus arbiters, Crossbars, Bridges, CDC circuits | Bandwidth, Skew |
| **Clocking** | PLLs, DLLs, Clock dividers, Clock gates | Jitter, Skew |
| **I/O** | Pad drivers, SerDes, PHY blocks | Bandwidth, Signal Integrity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics used throughout this module:**
> - **Delay (T):** measured in **nanoseconds (ns)** or **picoseconds (ps)**
> - **Area (A):** measured in **gate equivalents (GE)** or **µm²**
> - **Power (P):** measured in **µW** or **mW** at a given **operating frequency f (MHz or GHz)**
> - **Energy per operation (E):** measured in **pJ (picojoules)**
> - **Throughput:** operations per second, or **1/clock-cycle latency**

## 1.4 Why Subblock Design Matters in Modern SoC

The complexity of modern SoCs (e.g., smartphone processors with 10+ billion transistors) makes **monolithic design impossible**. Subblock design enables:

1. **Parallel design effort** — multiple engineering teams work concurrently
2. **Reusability (IP Cores)** — verified subblocks become reusable Intellectual Property
3. **Verification independence** — each subblock can be unit-tested before integration
4. **Technology portability** — a well-designed subblock can be retargeted to a new process node
5. **Power management** — individual subblocks can be **clock-gated** or **power-gated** independently

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Subblock Design Flow

The KTU 2024 scheme prescribes the following systematic design flow for any subblock. Each stage produces a verifiable artifact, ensuring quality at every level.

### Stage 1: Specification Capture
- Define **functional behavior** (truth table, algorithm)
- Define **interface signals** (ports, widths, polarity)
- Define **performance targets** (max delay, area budget, power cap)
- Define **clocking and reset strategy** (synchronous/asynchronous, active-high/low)

### Stage 2: Architectural Micro-architecture Selection
Choose the algorithm and architecture:
- **Ripple Carry Adder (RCA)** vs **Carry Lookahead Adder (CLA)** vs **Carry Select Adder (CSA)**
- **Array Multiplier** vs **Wallace Tree Multiplier** vs **Booth Multiplier**
- **Barrel Shifter** vs **Logarithmic Shifter**
- **Mealy** vs **Moore FSM**

### Stage 3: RTL Implementation
- Write synthesizable **Verilog/VHDL** code
- Use **non-blocking assignments** (`<=`) for sequential logic
- Use **blocking assignments** (`=`) for combinational logic
- Avoid **latch inference** by fully specifying all `if-else` and `case` branches

### Stage 4: Functional Verification
- Build **testbenches** with self-checking assertions
- Achieve **100% code coverage** and **functional coverage**
- Use **SystemVerilog assertions (SVA)** for protocol checks

### Stage 5: Logic Synthesis
- Map RTL to **standard cell library** using tools (Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus)
- Generate **gate-level netlist**
- Report **area, timing, and power**

### Stage 6: Timing Closure
- Resolve **setup violations** (slow path) by buffering, retiming, or pipelining
- Resolve **hold violations** (fast path) by inserting delay buffers

## 2.2 Critical Datapath Subblocks — Detailed Analysis

### 2.2.1 Adders

#### A. Half Adder (HA)
Simplest adder, computes 1-bit sum with **no carry-in**.

| Inputs | Outputs | Logic |
|---|---|---|
| A B | Sum (S) Carry (C) | S = A ⊕ B, C = A · B |

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Gates | 1 XOR + 1 AND |
| Delay | **T_XOR** (1 gate delay) |
| Area | **~5 GE** (Gate Equivalents) |

#### B. Full Adder (FA)
Adds 1 bit with **carry-in**, fundamental building block of all multi-bit adders.

$$S = A \oplus B \oplus C_{in}$$

$$C_{out} = (A \cdot B) + (C_{in} \cdot (A \oplus B))$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Alternative carry expression: $C_{out} = A \cdot B + B \cdot C_{in} + A \cdot C_{in}$ (majority function)

#### C. Ripple Carry Adder (RCA)
Chains N full adders in series. **Carry propagates** through every stage.

**Critical Path:** $C_{in} \rightarrow C_{out}$ of the last FA → output Sum

**Delay formula for an N-bit RCA:**

$$T_{RCA} = (N-1) \cdot T_{carry} + T_{sum}$$

Where $T_{carry}$ is the delay of one carry propagation stage and $T_{sum}$ is the final XOR delay.

**Area formula:**

$$A_{RCA} = N \cdot A_{FA}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** RCA is **area-optimal** but **delay-worst**. For N=32, delay grows linearly with N, making it unsuitable for high-speed designs beyond ~16 bits.

#### D. Carry Lookahead Adder (CLA)

The CLA solves the linear delay problem of RCA by **computing all carries in parallel** using **generate (G)** and **propagate (P)** signals.

$$G_i = A_i \cdot B_i \quad \text{(carry generated at bit i)}$$

$$P_i = A_i \oplus B_i \quad \text{(carry propagated through bit i)}$$

$$C_{i+1} = G_i + P_i \cdot C_i$$

Recursive expansion gives the lookahead equations:

$$C_1 = G_0 + P_0 \cdot C_0$$

$$C_2 = G_1 + P_1 \cdot G_0 + P_1 \cdot P_0 \cdot C_0$$

$$C_3 = G_2 + P_2 \cdot G_1 + P_2 \cdot P_1 \cdot G_0 + P_2 \cdot P_1 \cdot P_0 \cdot C_0$$

**General block CLA formula for 4-bit group:**

$$C_{i+4} = G_{i:3:0} + P_{i:3:0} \cdot C_i$$

Where the **block generate** and **block propagate** are:

$$G_{i:3:0} = G_3 + P_3 \cdot G_2 + P_3 \cdot P_2 \cdot G_1 + P_3 \cdot P_2 \cdot P_1 \cdot G_0$$

$$P_{i:3:0} = P_3 \cdot P_2 \cdot P_1 \cdot P_0$$

**Hierarchical CLA** applies lookahead both **within** blocks and **between** blocks, achieving **O(log N)** delay.

#### E. Carry Select Adder (CSelA)
Pre-computes sum for both $C_{in}=0$ and $C_{in}=1$, then **mux-selects** based on actual carry. Trades **area for speed**.

### 2.2.2 Multipliers

#### A. Array Multiplier (Braun Multiplier)
- Uses an **array of full adders** in a regular, repetitive structure
- Multiplies two unsigned N-bit numbers in **N clock cycles (iterative)** or in **combinational form with N² partial products**
- **Area:** $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ full adders
- **Delay:** $\mathcal{O}(N)$ levels of full adders

#### B. Wallace Tree Multiplier
- Reduces partial products using **parallel counters (3:2 compressors = full adders)**
- Achieves **logarithmic delay** $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ at the cost of irregular routing
- Used in **high-performance** DSP and CPU datapaths

#### C. Booth Multiplier
- Handles **signed multiplication** and reduces the number of partial products by recoding the multiplier using **Booth encoding** (examines pairs of bits)
- **Modified Booth (Radix-4)** reduces N-bit multiplier to N/2 partial products
- **Area:** roughly half of array multiplier for same throughput

#### D. Shift-and-Add Multiplier
- Simplest: shifts and adds in a loop, **N clock cycles**
- **Area-optimal**, **time-worst** — used in low-power microcontrollers

### 2.2.3 Shifters

#### A. Barrel Shifter
- Performs **any N-bit shift or rotation in a single cycle**
- Implemented as **log₂N stages of 2:1 muxes**
- **Area:** $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$ muxes
- **Delay:** $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ mux delays
- Used in **ALU** for fast bit manipulation

#### B. Logarithmic Shifter
- Decodes shift amount in **binary** and routes appropriate bit to each output
- Similar delay profile, different routing topology

### 2.2.4 Multiplexers (MUX)
- **2:1 MUX:** `Y = (S · A) + (\bar{S} · B)` — built from **3 gates** (1 NOT + 2 AND + 1 OR, or 1 transmission gate pair)
- **N:1 MUX:** tree of 2:1 MUXes, **log₂N levels**
- Critical for **datapath routing** and **register file read ports**

### 2.2.5 Comparators
- **Equality:** bitwise XNOR followed by AND-reduction
- **Magnitude:** bitwise comparison from MSB to LSB, can be **prefix-computed** for O(log N) delay

### 2.2.6 Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)
- **Combinational** core: MUX-tree selecting between adder output, logic output, shifter output
- Controlled by **opcode** from instruction decoder
- Outputs **flags**: Zero, Carry, Overflow, Negative

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Subblock | Delay (Critical Path) | Area (Gate Equivalents) | Power Relation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Half Adder (HA) | $T_{XOR}$ | $\approx 5$ GE | $\propto C \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f$ |
| 2 | Full Adder (FA) | $2T_{XOR}$ or $T_{XOR}+T_{AND/OR}$ | $\approx 9$–$13$ GE | Same as above |
| 3 | N-bit RCA | $(N-1) \cdot T_{carry} + T_{sum}$ | $N \cdot A_{FA}$ | Linear in N |
| 4 | N-bit CLA | $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ | $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$ | Higher than RCA |
| 5 | Carry Select Adder | $\approx T_{mux} + T_{FA}$ | $\approx 2N \cdot A_{FA}$ | $\approx 2 \times$ RCA |
| 6 | N-bit Array Multiplier | $\mathcal{O}(N)$ levels | $N^2 \cdot A_{FA}$ | $\propto N^2$ |
| 7 | Wallace Tree Multiplier | $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ | $\approx N^2 \cdot A_{FA}$ (irregular) | Lower delay, more routing |
| 8 | Booth Multiplier (Radix-4) | $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ | $\approx N^2/2 \cdot A_{FA}$ | Lower than array |
| 9 | N-bit Barrel Shifter | $\log_2 N \cdot T_{mux}$ | $N \cdot \log_2 N \cdot A_{mux}$ | $\propto N \log N$ |
| 10 | N:1 MUX (tree) | $\log_2 N \cdot T_{mux}$ | $(N-1) \cdot A_{mux}$ | $\propto N$ |
| 11 | N-bit Comparator | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ (parallel) or $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ (prefix) | $\mathcal{O}(N)$ | $\propto N$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Power Equation for CMOS subblocks:**
>
> $$P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f + I_{leak} \cdot V_{dd}$$
>
> Where $\alpha$ is the switching activity factor, $C_L$ is the load capacitance, $V_{dd}$ is the supply voltage, and $f$ is the clock frequency.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Subblock | Real-World Use |
|---|---|
| CLA / CSA | ALUs of RISC-V, ARM Cortex cores |
| Wallace Tree | Floating-point units in GPUs (NVIDIA, AMD) |
| Booth Multiplier | DSP kernels in Qualcomm Hexagon, Apple Neural Engine |
| Barrel Shifter | ARM barrel shifter (single-cycle shifts for any amount) |
| FSM Subblocks | Protocol controllers — USB, PCIe, DDR memory controller |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Critical Path Delay of an N-bit Ripple Carry Adder

We derive the worst-case propagation delay of an N-bit RCA combinational path.

### Step 1: Define the full adder carry propagation delay

A full adder has two outputs: Sum and $C_{out}$. The Sum equation is:
$$S = A \oplus B \oplus C_{in}$$

The carry equation is:
$$C_{out} = A \cdot B + C_{in} \cdot (A \oplus B) = A \cdot B + C_{in} \cdot P$$

The **carry propagation delay** $T_{carry}$ is the time taken for $C_{in}$ at stage $i$ to settle $C_{out}$ at stage $i$ — this is **one AND-OR gate delay** (or one AOI/OAI cell delay in a standard cell library).

### Step 2: Chain the full adders

For an N-bit RCA, the carries ripple as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= G_0 + P_0 \cdot C_0 \\
C_2 &= G_1 + P_1 \cdot C_1 = G_1 + P_1 \cdot G_0 + P_1 \cdot P_0 \cdot C_0 \\
C_3 &= G_2 + P_2 \cdot G_1 + P_2 \cdot P_1 \cdot G_0 + P_2 \cdot P_1 \cdot P_0 \cdot C_0 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
C_N &= G_{N-1} + P_{N-1} \cdot G_{N-2} + \cdots + \left(\prod_{k=0}^{N-1} P_k\right) \cdot C_0
\end{aligned}
$$

In the **ripple** structure, each stage waits for the previous $C_{out}$ before it can compute its own $C_{out}$.

### Step 3: Count the carry stages

The final carry $C_N$ must propagate through $N-1$ carry stages. The sum $S_{N-1}$ (most significant sum bit) is the **last** output and requires the carry $C_{N-1}$ to be stable.

### Step 4: Write the critical path delay equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{RCA,total} &= T_{C_0 \rightarrow C_1} + T_{C_1 \rightarrow C_2} + \cdots + T_{C_{N-1} \rightarrow S_{N-1}} \\
&= (N-1) \cdot T_{carry} + T_{sum}
\end{aligned}
$$

For typical 28nm CMOS standard cell libraries:
- $T_{carry} \approx 0.15$ ns
- $T_{sum} \approx 0.20$ ns

**Example:** For N=32, $T_{RCA} = 31 \times 0.15 + 0.20 = 4.85$ ns, which limits clock frequency to approximately $f_{max} \approx 1/T_{RCA} \approx 206$ MHz.

### Step 5: Identify the engineering implication

The linear dependence on N means that for **N > 16**, RCA is rarely acceptable in **high-performance** datapaths. CLA, CSA, or **pipelining** must be employed.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Carry Lookahead Adder (CLA) Group Equations

### Step 1: Define per-bit generate and propagate

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_i &= A_i \cdot B_i \\
P_i &= A_i \oplus B_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical meaning:** $G_i = 1$ means this bit position **definitely** generates a carry regardless of $C_{in}$. $P_i = 1$ means this bit position **passes through** the incoming carry.

### Step 2: Single-bit carry recurrence

$$C_{i+1} = G_i + P_i \cdot C_i$$

### Step 3: Unroll the recurrence for a 4-bit group (i to i+3)

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{i+1} &= G_i + P_i \cdot C_i \\
C_{i+2} &= G_{i+1} + P_{i+1} \cdot C_{i+1} \\
        &= G_{i+1} + P_{i+1} \cdot G_i + P_{i+1} \cdot P_i \cdot C_i \\
C_{i+3} &= G_{i+2} + P_{i+2} \cdot C_{i+2} \\
        &= G_{i+2} + P_{i+2} \cdot G_{i+1} + P_{i+2} \cdot P_{i+1} \cdot G_i + P_{i+2} \cdot P_{i+1} \cdot P_i \cdot C_i \\
C_{i+4} &= G_{i+3} + P_{i+3} \cdot C_{i+3} \\
        &= G_{i+3} + P_{i+3} \cdot G_{i+2} + P_{i+3} \cdot P_{i+2} \cdot G_{i+1} + P_{i+3} \cdot P_{i+2} \cdot P_{i+1} \cdot G_i \\
        &\quad + P_{i+3} \cdot P_{i+2} \cdot P_{i+1} \cdot P_i \cdot C_i
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Define block generate and block propagate

The pattern that emerges allows us to define for any contiguous group $[i : j]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{[i:j]} &= G_k + P_k \cdot G_{k-1} + P_k \cdot P_{k-1} \cdot G_{k-2} + \cdots + \left(\prod_{m=j}^{k} P_m\right) \cdot G_{j-1} \\
P_{[i:j]} &= \prod_{m=j}^{k} P_m
\end{aligned}
$$

For a 4-bit group $[i+3 : i]$:

$$G_{i:3:0} = G_3 + P_3 \cdot G_2 + P_3 \cdot P_2 \cdot G_1 + P_3 \cdot P_2 \cdot P_1 \cdot G_0$$

$$P_{i:3:0} = P_3 \cdot P_2 \cdot P_1 \cdot P_0$$

### Step 5: Combine blocks hierarchically

The block output carry is:
$$C_{i+4} = G_{i:3:0} + P_{i:3:0} \cdot C_i$$

This means **any group of 4 bits** can be reduced to a single block generate and block propagate in **constant time (2 gate levels)**, making the 16-bit CLA achievable in **4 gate levels** and the 64-bit CLA in **6 gate levels** (hierarchical).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Area and Delay of a Barrel Shifter

### Step 1: Define the operation

A barrel shifter can shift an N-bit input left or right by an **arbitrary amount S**, where $0 \le S \le N-1$, in a **single clock cycle**.

### Step 2: Decompose S in binary

Let S be encoded in $\log_2 N$ bits: $S = s_{k-1} 2^{k-1} + s_{k-2} 2^{k-2} + \cdots + s_0 2^0$ where $k = \log_2 N$.

### Step 3: Construct the shifter in $k$ stages

**Stage 0 (controlled by $s_0$, shift by 0 or 1):** Each output bit $Y_i$ is selected from $X_i$ or $X_{i-1}$ by a 2:1 MUX.

**Stage 1 (controlled by $s_1$, shift by 0 or 2):** Each output bit is selected from stage 0's output or shifted by 2 more positions.

**Stage j:** Shift by 0 or $2^j$.

### Step 4: Compute area

Each stage uses $N$ 2:1 muxes. There are $\log_2 N$ stages.

$$A_{barrel} = N \cdot \log_2 N \cdot A_{mux}$$

For $N=32$: $A_{barrel} = 32 \times 5 \times A_{mux} = 160 \cdot A_{mux}$.

### Step 5: Compute delay

The output of stage $j$ depends on stage $j-1$, which depends on stage $j-2$, ..., down to the input.

$$T_{barrel} = \log_2 N \cdot T_{mux}$$

For $N=32$: $T_{barrel} = 5 \cdot T_{mux}$.

### Step 6: Engineering verification

For typical 28nm CMOS: $T_{mux} \approx 0.10$ ns, so a 32-bit barrel shifter completes in $\approx 0.5$ ns, comfortably fitting a 1 GHz clock period.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Area and Delay of an N-bit Array Multiplier

### Step 1: Define partial products

For unsigned N×N multiplication, the multiplier B produces N partial products, each being A shifted left by the position of a 1-bit in B.

$$P_i = A \cdot b_i \quad \text{(shifted left by i positions)}$$

### Step 2: Accumulate partial products

The product is:
$$P = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} P_i \cdot 2^i$$

### Step 3: Count the adders required

To sum N partial products, we need $N-1$ rows of adders. Each row uses approximately N full adders. So total full adders:

$$A_{array} \approx N \cdot (N-1) \approx N^2 \text{ full adders}$$

### Step 4: Determine critical path

The critical path goes from the **top-right corner** of the array (MSB of the last partial product) through the **carry chain** of each adder row down to the **bottom-left corner** (LSB of the final product). The total number of full adders in the critical path is:

$$T_{array,delay} = (2N - 2) \cdot T_{FA} \approx 2N \cdot T_{FA}$$

This is the reason **Wallace Tree** and **Booth** multipliers are preferred for high-speed design.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Subblock | Area (GE, N=32) | Delay (gate levels) | Best Use Case | Worst Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **RCA** | $\approx 320$ | $\approx 64$ | Low-power, small N | High-speed, large N |
| **CLA** | $\approx 800$ | $\approx 6$ | CPU ALU, balanced | Area-constrained |
| **CSelA** | $\approx 640$ | $\approx 5$ | Mid-speed, mid-area | Very high speed |
| **Array Mult.** | $\approx 9000$ FA | $\approx 64$ | Low-cost DSP | Latency-critical |
| **Wallace Tree** | $\approx 9000$ FA | $\approx 18$ | GPU, FPU | Area-constrained |
| **Booth (Radix-4)** | $\approx 4500$ FA | $\approx 14$ | Signed DSP, ML | Very small N |
| **Barrel Shifter** | $\approx 160 \cdot A_{mux}$ | $\approx 5$ | Single-cycle shifts | Bit-serial designs |

> [!NOTE]
> **GE** = Gate Equivalent. 1 GE = area of a 2-input NAND gate in the target technology. The numbers above are **order-of-magnitude estimates** for typical 28nm CMOS and should be used for **relative comparison**, not absolute sizing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Subblock Design Flow — Top-Level View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Design Specification] --> B0[Architecture Selection]
    B0 --> C0[RTL Implementation]
    C0 --> D0[Functional Verification]
    D0 --> E0[Logic Synthesis]
    E0 --> F0[Gate Level Netlist]
    F0 --> G0[Static Timing Analysis]
    G0 --> H0{Timing Met?}
    H0 -- No --> I0[Optimization: Pipelining, Retiming]
    I0 --> E0
    H0 -- Yes --> J0[Subblock Sign Off]
    J0 --> K0[SoC Integration]
    D0 --> D1[Coverage 100%?]
    D1 -- No --> C0
    D1 -- Yes --> E0
```

## 4.2 N-bit Ripple Carry Adder (RCA) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0bit[A0] --> FA0[FA 0]
    B0bit[B0] --> FA0
    FA0 --> S0[S0]
    FA0 --> C1((C1))
    A1bit[A1] --> FA1[FA 1]
    B1bit[B1] --> FA1
    C1 --> FA1
    FA1 --> S1[S1]
    FA1 --> C2((C2))
    A2bit[A2] --> FA2[FA 2]
    B2bit[B2] --> FA2
    C2 --> FA2
    FA2 --> S2[S2]
    FA2 --> C3((C3))
    A3bit[A3] --> FA3[FA 3]
    B3bit[B3] --> FA3
    C3 --> FA3
    FA3 --> S3[S3]
    FA3 --> C4((C4))
    Cin[Cin] --> FA0
```

## 4.3 Carry Lookahead Adder (CLA) — 4-bit Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INA[A and B 0 to 3] --> GPGEN[Per Bit GP Generator]
    GPGEN --> GPout[G0 G1 G2 G3 P0 P1 P2 P3]
    GPout --> BLOOK[Block Lookahead Logic]
    CIN[Cin] --> BLOOK
    BLOOK --> COUTS[C1 C2 C3 C4 in parallel]
    COUTS --> SUMGEN[Sum XOR Stage]
    GPGEN --> SUMGEN
    SUMGEN --> SUMS[S0 S1 S2 S3]
```

## 4.4 Wallace Tree Multiplier — Reduction Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PP[Partial Products PP0 to PP7] --> SA1[Stage 1: Full Adders 3:2 Compress]
    SA1 --> SA2[Stage 2: Full Adders 3:2 Compress]
    SA2 --> SA3[Stage 3: Full Adders 3:2 Compress]
    SA3 --> SA4[Stage 4: Final CPA]
    SA4 --> OUT[Final Product 16 bit]
```

## 4.5 Barrel Shifter — Logarithmic Stage Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input 32 bit] --> STG0[Stage 0 Mux shift by 0 or 1]
    STG0 --> STG1[Stage 1 Mux shift by 0 or 2]
    STG1 --> STG2[Stage 2 Mux shift by 0 or 4]
    STG2 --> STG3[Stage 3 Mux shift by 0 or 8]
    STG3 --> STG4[Stage 4 Mux shift by 0 or 16]
    STG4 --> OUT[Shifted Output 32 bit]
    S0[s0] --> STG0
    S1[s1] --> STG1
    S2[s2] --> STG2
    S3[s3] --> STG3
    S4[s4] --> STG4
```

## 4.6 Generic ALU Subblock — Datapath Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OPIN[Operand A and B] --> ADDR[Adder Subblock]
    OPIN --> LOGIC[Logic Unit AND OR XOR]
    OPIN --> SHIFT[Barrel Shifter]
    ADDR --> MUX[Output Mux controlled by OPCODE]
    LOGIC --> MUX
    SHIFT --> MUX
    MUX --> RESULT[ALU Result]
    ADDR --> FLAGS[Flag Generator: Z C N V]
    MUX --> FLAGS
```

## 4.7 SoC Subblock Integration View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CPU[CPU Core] --> DEC[Instruction Decoder]
    DEC --> REGFILE[Register File]
    DEC --> ALU[ALU Subblock]
    DEC --> MUL[MULT Subblock]
    DEC --> SHFT[Barrel Shifter Subblock]
    DEC --> FCTRL[FSM Controller]
    ALU --> WB[Writeback Mux]
    MUL --> WB
    SHFT --> WB
    WB --> REGFILE
    FCTRL --> MEM[Memory Controller FSM]
    FCTRL --> IO[IO Controller FSM]
    MEM --> DRAM[External DRAM]
    IO --> PERIPH[Peripherals]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** All Mermaid block diagrams above represent **block-level functional architecture** rather than transistor-level schematics. Each rectangle is a subblock, each arrow is a data or control signal, and the labels on the arrows indicate signal names or widths. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to be able to **draw, label, and explain** such architecture diagrams in the ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a subblock in the context of SoC design. List any four categories of subblocks with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **subblock** is a self-contained, reusable hardware module that implements a specific function within a System-on-Chip, with well-defined input/output interfaces and characterized area, delay, and power.

Four categories:
1. **Datapath Arithmetic** — e.g., Carry Lookahead Adder
2. **Storage** — e.g., Register File
3. **Control** — e.g., FSM for UART controller
4. **Interconnect** — e.g., Bus Arbiter

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the list with examples)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Write the generate (G) and propagate (P) expressions used in a Carry Lookahead Adder. Why is the CLA faster than the RCA?**

**Model Answer:**

$$G_i = A_i \cdot B_i, \quad P_i = A_i \oplus B_i$$

$$C_{i+1} = G_i + P_i \cdot C_i$$

The **RCA** has a critical path of $(N-1) \cdot T_{carry}$ because the carry must ripple serially through every stage. The **CLA** computes all carries in parallel using lookahead logic, achieving a delay of $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$. Hence CLA is exponentially faster than RCA for large N.

*(3 marks: 1 for G and P, 1 for the recurrence, 1 for the speed comparison)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Design a 4-bit Carry Lookahead Adder. Derive the expressions for $C_1, C_2, C_3, C_4$ in terms of generate and propagate signals. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare Ripple Carry Adder, Carry Lookahead Adder, and Carry Select Adder in terms of area, delay, and power for an N-bit addition. State one application where each is most suitable. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — 4-bit CLA Derivation

**Step 1:** Per-bit generate and propagate:

$$G_0 = A_0 \cdot B_0, \quad P_0 = A_0 \oplus B_0$$
$$G_1 = A_1 \cdot B_1, \quad P_1 = A_1 \oplus B_1$$
$$G_2 = A_2 \cdot B_2, \quad P_2 = A_2 \oplus B_2$$
$$G_3 = A_3 \cdot B_3, \quad P_3 = A_3 \oplus B_3$$

**Step 2:** Apply the recurrence $C_{i+1} = G_i + P_i \cdot C_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= G_0 + P_0 \cdot C_0 \\
C_2 &= G_1 + P_1 \cdot C_1 = G_1 + P_1 \cdot G_0 + P_1 \cdot P_0 \cdot C_0 \\
C_3 &= G_2 + P_2 \cdot C_2 \\
    &= G_2 + P_2 \cdot G_1 + P_2 \cdot P_1 \cdot G_0 + P_2 \cdot P_1 \cdot P_0 \cdot C_0 \\
C_4 &= G_3 + P_3 \cdot C_3 \\
    &= G_3 + P_3 \cdot G_2 + P_3 \cdot P_2 \cdot G_1 + P_3 \cdot P_2 \cdot P_1 \cdot G_0 \\
    &\quad + P_3 \cdot P_2 \cdot P_1 \cdot P_0 \cdot C_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Sums computed as $S_i = P_i \oplus C_i$ once all carries are available.

**Step 4 (Block form):** Define block generate and propagate for 4-bit group:
$$G_{[3:0]} = G_3 + P_3 G_2 + P_3 P_2 G_1 + P_3 P_2 P_1 G_0$$
$$P_{[3:0]} = P_3 P_2 P_1 P_0$$
$$C_4 = G_{[3:0]} + P_{[3:0]} \cdot C_0$$

*(7 marks: 1 for G/P definitions, 3 for the four carry expressions, 1 for the sum expression, 2 for the block-level form)*

#### Part (b) — Comparison Table

| Parameter | Ripple Carry Adder (RCA) | Carry Lookahead Adder (CLA) | Carry Select Adder (CSelA) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Delay** | $\mathcal{O}(N)$ — linear, worst | $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ — best | $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{N})$ — moderate |
| **Area** | $\mathcal{O}(N)$ — smallest | $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$ — largest | $\mathcal{O}(2N)$ — moderate |
| **Power** | Lowest | Highest (more gates switching) | Moderate |
| **Wiring complexity** | Simple, regular | Complex, fan-in heavy | Moderate |
| **Best for N** | $N \le 8$ | $N \ge 16$, high speed | $N = 16$ to $32$, balanced |

**Applications:**
- **RCA:** Low-power IoT sensor nodes, simple microcontrollers (e.g., 8-bit 8051 ALU)
- **CLA:** Modern RISC-V / ARM Cortex-A series CPU ALUs
- **CSelA:** Mid-range DSP datapaths, address arithmetic in cache controllers

*(7 marks: 2 for the table, 2 for delay-area-power narrative, 1 for formulas, 2 for application reasoning)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the architecture and operation of a **Wallace Tree Multiplier**. Derive the area (in terms of full adders) and the critical path delay for an 8×8 multiplication. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **32-bit Barrel Shifter** that performs left-rotation by an amount specified in a 5-bit control signal. Show the stage-by-stage MUX decomposition and compute the total number of 2:1 muxes and the critical path delay. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — Wallace Tree Multiplier for 8×8

**Step 1: Generate partial products.** For 8-bit operands, there are 8 partial products, each of width up to 15 bits. Total partial product bits: 8 × 8 = 64.

**Step 2: First reduction stage.** Use **3:2 compressors** (full adders) — each takes 3 bits of the same column and outputs a sum bit and a carry bit to the next column.

In the first stage, the 8 rows are reduced to approximately $\lceil 8 \times 2/3 \rceil \approx 6$ rows.

**Step 3: Iterative reduction.** Each subsequent stage reduces the row count by a factor of 3/2 until only 2 rows remain.

**Step 4: Final CPA.** A **Carry Lookahead Adder** (or any fast adder) adds the final 2 rows to produce the 16-bit product.

**Step 5: Total full adders for 8×8 Wallace Tree.**

Each 3:2 compressor uses 1 full adder. Total reduction stages: $\lceil \log_{1.5}(8/2) \rceil = \lceil \log_{1.5} 4 \rceil \approx 4$ stages.

Approximate full adder count: $A_{WT,8\times8} \approx 64 - 16 = 48$ full adders (each FA reduces 3 bits to 2, saving 1 bit per column).

**Step 6: Critical path delay.**

Critical path = (delay through reduction tree) + (delay of final CLA)

$$T_{WT} \approx \log_{1.5}(8) \cdot T_{FA} + T_{CLA,16} \approx 6 \cdot T_{FA} + 4 \cdot T_{FA} = 10 \cdot T_{FA}$$

Compare to **Array Multiplier:** $T_{Array} = 14 \cdot T_{FA}$ (from $2N-2 = 14$ levels for 8×8).

**Wallace Tree is ~30% faster** for the 8×8 case.

*(7 marks: 2 for architecture, 2 for reduction stage analysis, 2 for area/delay derivation, 1 for comparison conclusion)*

#### Part (b) — 32-bit Barrel Shifter

**Step 1: Control signal width.** $k = \log_2 32 = 5$ bits: $S = s_4 s_3 s_2 s_1 s_0$.

**Step 2: Stage decomposition.**

| Stage | Shift controlled by | Shift amount | Muxes per stage |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $s_0$ | 0 or 1 | 32 |
| 1 | $s_1$ | 0 or 2 | 32 |
| 2 | $s_2$ | 0 or 4 | 32 |
| 3 | $s_3$ | 0 or 8 | 32 |
| 4 | $s_4$ | 0 or 16 | 32 |

**Step 3: Total MUX count.**

$$N_{mux} = 32 \times 5 = 160 \text{ muxes of 2:1}$$

**Step 4: Critical path delay.**

The output of stage 4 depends on stage 3, which depends on ... down to the input.

$$T_{barrel} = 5 \cdot T_{mux}$$

For typical 28nm CMOS, $T_{mux} \approx 0.10$ ns:
$$T_{barrel} \approx 0.5 \text{ ns} \implies f_{max} \approx 2 \text{ GHz}$$

**Step 5: Rotation handling.** For left-rotation by S, the input at stage $j$ is shifted by $2^j$ positions with **wrap-around** — bit at position $i$ in stage $j+1$ comes from bit at position $(i - 2^j) \mod 32$ in stage $j$.

*(7 marks: 1 for control width, 2 for stage table, 1 for total mux calculation, 2 for delay derivation, 1 for rotation wrap-around explanation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing generate with carry:** $G_i$ is **not** the carry output of bit $i$. It is a combinational signal derived directly from $A_i$ and $B_i$. Marks are deducted if you write $G_i = C_{i+1}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the carry-in term:** In the CLA expansion for $C_2, C_3, C_4$, the term involving $C_0$ must be present. Students often omit the $C_0$ product term, losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up Barrel Shifter with Logarithmic Shifter:** A barrel shifter uses **stages of 2:1 muxes** with cumulative shift. A logarithmic shifter uses **decoders** to directly select the input bit. These are **not** the same architecture.
> 4. **Wallace Tree area estimation:** Students often confuse **full adder count** with **gate count**. The question usually asks for full adders, not individual NAND/NOR gates. Specify the unit.
> 5. **Barrel shifter delay:** Writing $T_{barrel} = 5 \cdot T_{mux}$ without explicitly stating that this is for $N=32$ loses marks. Always specify the bit-width.
> 6. **ALU subblock:** Many students forget to include the **flag generation logic** (Zero, Carry, Negative, Overflow) as part of the ALU subblock. Marks are reserved for flag logic in the 14-mark question.
> 7. **Sign extension in multipliers:** For signed Booth multiplication, the **sign bit extension** of partial products must be properly handled. The examiner specifically checks for this.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute KTU Revision**

- **Subblock definition:** Self-contained RTL module with defined interface, area, delay, and power; part of the SoC divide-and-conquer design strategy.
- **Adders in increasing speed:** Half Adder → Full Adder → Ripple Carry → Carry Lookahead → Carry Select → Carry Skip → Prefix Adders (Kogge-Stone, Han-Carlson).
- **RCA delay:** $(N-1) \cdot T_{carry} + T_{sum}$ — **linear in N**, area-optimal, slowest.
- **CLA delay:** $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ — uses **generate (G)** and **propagate (P)** signals; block-level CLA further reduces delay hierarchically.
- **CLA key equation:** $C_{i+1} = G_i + P_i \cdot C_i$; block form: $C_{i+4} = G_{[i+3:i]} + P_{[i+3:i]} \cdot C_i$.
- **Carry Select Adder:** Pre-computes both $C_{in}=0$ and $C_{in}=1$ results in parallel, muxes to select — **doubles area, halves delay** vs RCA.
- **Multiplier types:** Array (regular, slow), Wallace Tree (irregular, fast), Booth / Modified Booth (signed, area-efficient).
- **Wallace Tree:** Uses 3:2 compressors iteratively; delay $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$, area $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$.
- **Array Multiplier:** $N^2$ full adders, delay $2N \cdot T_{FA}$.
- **Barrel Shifter:** $N \cdot \log_2 N$ muxes, delay $\log_2 N \cdot T_{mux}$ — performs any shift in **1 cycle**.
- **MUX:** Fundamental routing element; $N:1$ MUX built as tree of 2:1 MUXes with $\log_2 N$ levels.
- **Comparator:** Equality uses XNOR-AND; magnitude uses MSB-to-LSB priority or prefix computation.
- **ALU:** Combines adder, logic unit, shifter via output MUX controlled by opcode; produces flags (Z, C, N, V).
- **Power equation:** $P = \alpha C V_{dd}^2 f + I_{leak} V_{dd}$ — dynamic + static components.
- **Design trade-off triangle:** **Area ⇌ Delay ⇌ Power** — improving one typically worsens another.
- **Standard metrics:** GE (gate equivalent), ns/ps for delay, µW/mW for power, pJ for energy.
- **Verification:** Functional coverage + code coverage = 100% before synthesis sign-off.
- **Synthesis tools:** Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus — produce area/timing/power reports.
- **Static Timing Analysis:** Checks setup (max-delay) and hold (min-delay) on all timing paths.
- **Pipelining:** Insert registers along critical path to reduce $T_{critical}$ at cost of latency.
- **Retiming:** Move registers across combinational logic to balance slack without changing functionality.
- **Clock gating:** Disable clock to idle subblocks to reduce dynamic power.
- **Reusability:** Verified subblocks become **IP cores** for future SoCs.
- **KTU exam tip:** Always state **assumptions** (N-bit width, unsigned/signed, technology node) before deriving formulas.
- **KTU exam tip:** Use **block diagrams** liberally — they fetch easy marks and demonstrate understanding.
- **KTU exam tip:** Mention **real-world examples** (e.g., "CLA used in ARM Cortex-A53 ALU") for extra credit.
- **KTU exam tip:** For 14-mark questions, structure answer as: **Definition → Diagram → Derivation → Comparison Table → Application**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Macro Integration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT602 - SYSTEM ON CHIP DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Macro Integration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Macro Integration** is the systematic engineering process of assembling, interconnecting, and validating pre-designed, pre-verified Intellectual Property (IP) blocks — known as *macros* — into a unified, fully functional System-on-Chip (SoC). A *macro* in KTU 2024 SoC terminology refers to any reusable, functionally complete circuit block (digital, analog, mixed-signal, memory, or I/O) that has been independently designed, characterized, and verified, and is integrated at the chip top-level using a hierarchical design methodology.

Macros are formally classified by KTU 2024 SoC design taxonomy into three categories:

| Macro Type | Form Delivered | Flexibility | Characterization Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Soft Macro** | RTL (synthesizable HDL) | Highest (portable across processes) | Behavioral only |
| **Firm Macro** | Gate-level netlist | Medium (process-aware) | Logical + timing constraints |
| **Hard Macro** | GDSII / Layout (placed & routed) | Lowest (process-locked) | Fully characterized (PVT corners) |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of Macro Integration as Building a Modern City 🏙️**
>
> Imagine you are constructing a large city. You do not build every single house, road, power station, and school from scratch. Instead, you bring in **pre-fabricated, tested modules** (a residential block, a hospital, a stadium) and connect them using shared infrastructure — roads (buses), power grids (clock/power), water lines (data signals), and zoning rules (protocols).
>
> - A **Soft Macro** is like an architectural blueprint (you can build it any way you want, in any city).
> - A **Firm Macro** is like a pre-cut wooden frame (almost ready, just needs assembly).
> - A **Hard Macro** is like a pre-built concrete tower (just place and connect utilities).
>
> The **city planner (the SoC Integrator)** must ensure every module fits, shares power correctly, communicates via agreed roads, and obeys local laws (timing, power, protocol).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are used industry-wide in KTU-aligned SoC design:

- **Clock Tree Skew Target:** typically **< 50 ps** for high-performance cores
- **Power Domain Voltage Range:** typically **0.6 V – 1.2 V** for advanced nodes
- **Standard Cell Utilization Target:** **70% – 80%** of core area
- **Macro Placement Utilization:** typically **50% – 65%** (macros are blockage-heavy)
- **Bus Protocol Frequency:** AMBA AXI typically **up to 1 GHz** in modern SoCs
- **Hold Slack Margin:** typically **+50 ps** minimum across PVT corners

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Macro Integration is the **bridge between IP-level design and chip-level implementation**. The SoC integrator's job is *not* to redesign macros, but to ensure they coexist correctly with proper Power, Performance, and Area (PPA) trade-offs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Macro Integration Flow — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

### **Step 1: Macro Inventory & Specification Gathering**
The integrator first collects the **datasheets** of every macro. This includes:
- Power supply pins (VDD, VSS, VDD_A, VDD_D)
- Clock inputs and required frequencies
- Reset signals (POR, hard reset, soft reset)
- I/O interfaces (AXI, AHB, APB, custom)
- Physical dimensions (width × height in µm)
- Pin locations (for hard macros)

### **Step 2: SoC Architecture Planning**
- Define the **top-level bus matrix** (e.g., AXI interconnect)
- Plan **power domains** using UPF (Unified Power Format) or CPF
- Define **clock domains** and Clock Domain Crossing (CDC) strategy
- Identify **voltage domains** and the need for level shifters

### **Step 3: Floorplanning**
- Place macros based on data flow proximity
- Reserve **channels for global routing**
- Position I/O pads and bump arrays (for flip-chip)
- Set up **power grid (MESH/ring)** to deliver current uniformly

### **Step 4: Top-Level Routing & Integration**
- Connect macro pins to the SoC interconnect
- Insert **isolation cells** between power domains
- Insert **level shifters** at voltage domain crossings
- Insert **retention flops/registers** for sleep states
- Build the **clock tree** using CTS (Clock Tree Synthesis)

### **Step 5: Top-Level Verification**
- Run **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** across all corners
- Perform **Logical Equivalence Checking (LEC)** vs. RTL
- Execute **Power Intent verification** (UPF-aware simulation)
- Run **DFT (Design for Test) coverage** including macro-level BIST

### **Step 6: Sign-off & Tape-out**
- **DRC (Design Rule Check)** and **LVS (Layout vs. Schematic)**
- **IR drop and EM (Electromigration) analysis**
- Final **GDSII generation**

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Spec gathering | Prevents integration disasters (e.g., mismatched voltages) |
| Architecture planning | Avoids bus contention and bandwidth starvation |
| Floorplanning | Reduces wire length → lower dynamic power, better timing |
| Power management | Enables DVFS (Dynamic Voltage Frequency Scaling) |
| Top-level verification | Catches cross-macro bugs invisible at IP level |
| Sign-off | Ensures manufacturability and reliability |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_{total}$ | $P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static}$ | Watts (W) | Sum of all macro power |
| $P_{dynamic}$ | $P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | W | $\alpha$ = switching activity |
| $P_{static}$ | $P_{static} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$ | W | Dominant in advanced nodes |
| $t_{propagation}$ | $t_{prop} = t_{launch} + t_{ck2q} + t_{logic} + t_{setup}$ | seconds (s) | Setup path equation |
| $t_{skew}$ | $t_{skew} = \vert t_{ck,FF2} - t_{ck,FF1} \vert$ | seconds (s) | Clock skew between FFs |
| $f_{max}$ | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{clk} - t_{setup} - t_{ck2q,max} - t_{skew}}$ | Hertz (Hz) | Maximum operating frequency |
| $A_{utilization}$ | $A_{util} = \dfrac{A_{macros} + A_{stdcells}}{A_{core}} \times 100$ | percent (%) | Floorplan density |
| $I_{avg,domain}$ | $I_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} I_{total}(t)\,dt$ | Amperes (A) | Used for IR-drop analysis |
| $V_{drop}$ | $V_{drop} = I_{avg} \cdot R_{grid}$ | Volts (V) | Power grid IR-drop |
| $N_{level\ shifters}$ | $N_{ls} = \sum \text{(each voltage-domain crossing boundary)}$ | count | One per crossing signal |
| $N_{isolation\ cells}$ | $N_{iso} = \sum \text{(output signals of OFF domain)}$ | count | Placed at domain boundary |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember:** In all KTU exam tables, **never use** the literal `|` character for absolute value — always write `\vert` or `\mid`. This is critical for markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Macro Integration is the **backbone of modern VLSI industry**. Practically every smartphone SoC (Qualcomm Snapdragon, Apple M-series, MediaTek Dimensity, Samsung Exynos) is built by integrating **50–200+ macros** from multiple vendors. The technique:

- Reduces **time-to-market** by 60–80% versus full-custom design
- Enables **heterogeneous computing** (CPU + GPU + NPU + DSP on one die)
- Makes **chiplet-based design** possible (using advanced packaging)
- Is essential for **Automotive-grade ASIL-D** chips where macro reuse ensures pre-validated safety
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Operating Frequency of a Multi-Macro Data Path

Consider two macros $M_1$ and $M_2$ connected by a top-level interconnect. A flop in $M_1$ launches data at clock edge $t_0$, and a flop in $M_2$ must capture it. We need to find $f_{max}$.

### **Given Parameters**

- $t_{ck2q}$: clock-to-Q delay of the launching flop (from $M_1$)
- $t_{logic,intra,M1}$: internal combinational delay within $M_1$
- $t_{routing}$: delay of the top-level wire between macro pins
- $t_{logic,intra,M2}$: internal combinational delay within $M_2$
- $t_{setup,M2}$: setup time requirement of the capturing flop in $M_2$
- $t_{skew}$: clock skew between the two clock pins of the two macros

### **Derivation**

The data must arrive at the capture flop **at least $t_{setup}$ before the next clock edge**. The next clock edge occurs at $t_0 + t_{clk}$. The data path delay $t_{data}$ is:

$$
t_{data} = t_{ck2q} + t_{logic,intra,M1} + t_{routing} + t_{logic,intra,M2}
$$

The data arrival time at the capture flop (subtracting clock skew pessimism) is:

$$
t_{arrival} = t_0 + t_{data} + t_{skew}
$$

The setup constraint demands:

$$
t_{arrival} \leq t_0 + t_{clk} - t_{setup,M2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
t_0 + t_{ck2q} + t_{logic,intra,M1} + t_{routing} + t_{logic,intra,M2} + t_{skew} \leq t_0 + t_{clk} - t_{setup,M2}
$$

Cancel $t_0$ and rearrange to solve for $t_{clk}$:

$$
t_{clk} \geq t_{ck2q} + t_{logic,intra,M1} + t_{routing} + t_{logic,intra,M2} + t_{setup,M2} + t_{skew}
$$

The maximum operating frequency is therefore:

$$
\boxed{f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{ck2q} + t_{logic,intra,M1} + t_{routing} + t_{logic,intra,M2} + t_{setup,M2} + t_{skew}}}
$$

### **Numerical Evaluation Example**

Suppose:
- $t_{ck2q} = 100\ \text{ps}$
- $t_{logic,intra,M1} = 250\ \text{ps}$
- $t_{routing} = 180\ \text{ps}$
- $t_{logic,intra,M2} = 200\ \text{ps}$
- $t_{setup,M2} = 80\ \text{ps}$
- $t_{skew} = 30\ \text{ps}$

Substituting:

$$
t_{clk,\min} = 100 + 250 + 180 + 200 + 80 + 30
$$

$$
t_{clk,\min} = 840\ \text{ps}
$$

$$
f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{840 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.1905 \times 10^{9}\ \text{Hz}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{max} \approx 1.19\ \text{GHz}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: IR-Drop Across the Power Grid

The SoC's power grid is modeled as a distributed **RC mesh**. For a simplified 2×2 grid with horizontal resistance $R_h$ and vertical resistance $R_v$ per segment, with current $I$ drawn at the center node.

Using Kirchhoff's Current Law at the center node, the voltage drop to the nearest decap (decoupling capacitor) is:

$$
V_{drop} = I \cdot R_{eff}
$$

where the effective resistance from the center node to the supply pad is:

$$
R_{eff} = R_h \parallel R_v
$$

For a symmetric grid $R_h = R_v = R$:

$$
R_{eff} = \dfrac{R}{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{V_{drop} = \dfrac{I \cdot R}{2}}
$$

If 10 macros draw a combined average current of $I = 50\ \text{mA}$ and the grid segment resistance is $R = 0.2\ \Omega$:

$$
V_{drop} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.2}{2} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V} = 5\ \text{mV}
$$

If $V_{DD} = 0.8\ \text{V}$, the percentage drop is:

$$
\%V_{drop} = \dfrac{5\ \text{mV}}{800\ \text{mV}} \times 100\% = 0.625\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Industry rule of thumb: **IR-drop must stay below 2% of $V_{DD}$** to avoid timing failures.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Inter-Macro Communication Strategies

| Strategy | Bandwidth | Latency | Power | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Shared Bus (APB)** | Low | High | Low | Peripheral config (UART, GPIO) |
| **AXI Crossbar** | Very High | Low | Medium | CPU-to-DDR, CPU-to-GPU |
| **Network-on-Chip (NoC)** | Highest | Predictable | Medium-High | Many-core SoCs (>8 cores) |
| **Point-to-Point (custom)** | Highest | Lowest | High | Streaming DSP pipelines |
| **Mailbox / Register-based** | Low | High | Lowest | Inter-processor control (R5↔A53) |

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example: Level Shifter Count

Consider an SoC with **3 power domains**:
- **PD0 (Always-On, 0.8 V)** — contains a wake-up controller
- **PD1 (High-Performance, 0.9 V)** — contains the application processor
- **PD2 (Low-Power, 0.6 V)** — contains the sensor hub

The signals crossing domains are:
- PD0 → PD1: 12 control signals
- PD0 → PD2: 8 control signals
- PD1 → PD0: 4 status signals
- PD2 → PD0: 6 status signals

Total level shifters required (one per signal crossing):

$$
N_{ls} = 12 + 8 + 4 + 6 = 30
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The integrator must also budget for **isolation cells** on every output of an OFF-domain that connects to an ON-domain. Forgetting this is one of the most common KTU exam mistakes.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: SoC Macro Integration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IP Vendor Macros: Soft, Firm, Hard] --> B[Macro Library and Datasheets]
    B --> C[Architecture Planning: Bus, Power, Clock]
    C --> D[Floorplanning: Macro Placement and IO Pads]
    D --> E[Top Level Routing: Inter Macro Connections]
    E --> F[Insert Special Cells: Isolation, Level Shifter, Retention]
    F --> G[Clock Tree Synthesis: CTS]
    G --> H[Static Timing Analysis: STA]
    H --> I[Power Analysis: IR Drop and EM]
    I --> J[Design Rule Check: DRC and LVS]
    J --> K[Final GDSII Sign Off]
    K --> L[Tape Out to Foundry]
    L --> M[Fabrication and Package]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Multi-Domain SoC Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PD_AON[PD0 Always On Domain 0.8V]
        RTC[Real Time Clock RTC]
        WUC[Wake Up Controller WUC]
    end

    subgraph PD_HP[PD1 High Performance Domain 0.9V]
        CPU1[Application CPU Cortex A Class]
        CPU2[Application CPU Cortex A Class]
        GPU[GPU Engine]
        DDR_IF[DDR Controller]
    end

    subgraph PD_LP[PD2 Low Power Domain 0.6V]
        MCU[Sensor Hub MCU Cortex M Class]
        SENSOR[Analog Sensor Frontend]
    end

    ISO1[Isolation Cells and Level Shifters]
    ISO2[Isolation Cells and Level Shifters]
    ISO3[Isolation Cells and Level Shifters]

    RTC --- WUC
    WUC --- ISO1
    WUC --- ISO2
    ISO1 --- CPU1
    ISO1 --- CPU2
    ISO1 --- GPU
    ISO1 --- DDR_IF
    ISO2 --- MCU
    MCU --- SENSOR

    ISO3[Backup ISO for power gating] --- WUC
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Power Mode Transition

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SWPMU as Software PMU
    participant AON as Always On Controller
    participant HPCORE as High Perf Core
    participant LPCORE as Low Power Core
    participant ISO as Isolation Cells
    participant LS as Level Shifters

    Note over SWPMU,LPCORE: System in Active Mode
    SWPMU->>AON: Request Sleep Entry
    AON->>SWPMU: Acknowledge and Save State
    SWPMU->>HPCORE: Save Registers
    SWPMU->>AON: Ready for Power Gate
    AON->>ISO: Enable Isolation on HPCORE outputs
    AON->>HPCORE: Cut VDD Power Gate
    Note over HPCORE: HPCORE Powered Down
    SWPMU->>LPCORE: Wake Up Sensor Task
    LPCORE->>LS: Communicate at 0.6V to 0.8V
    Note over LPCORE,AON: System in Low Power Mode
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Top-Level Integration View

```mermaid

    subgraph TOP[SoC Top Level]
        PAD_RING[I/O Pad Ring]
        CLOCK_TREE[Global Clock Tree]

    end

    subgraph LEFT[Digital Macros]
        CPU[CPU Cluster]
        DSP[DSP Engine]
        CRYPTO[Hardware Crypto]
    end

    subgraph CENTER[Memory Macros]
        SRAM[SRAM Compiler Macro]

    end

    subgraph RIGHT[Analog and Mixed Signal]

        PHY[USB PHY Hard Macro]
    end

    PAD_RING --- INTERCONNECT

    CLOCK_TREE --- DSP
    CLOCK_TREE --- CRYPTO
    CLOCK_TREE --- SRAM

    POWER_GRID --- PLL

    INTERCONNECT --- SRAM
```
