
> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to draw an SoC block diagram, always show **three layers**: (1) Pad ring / I/O, (2) Power/Clock distribution, (3) Functional macro blocks. Examiners award marks for completeness of these layers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between Soft Macro, Firm Macro, and Hard Macro. Give one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Soft Macro (1 mark):** Delivered as synthesizable RTL (Verilog/VHDL). *Advantage:* Highest flexibility and process portability; can be re-synthesized to any technology node.
- **Firm Macro (1 mark):** Delivered as a gate-level netlist with timing constraints. *Advantage:* Better timing predictability than soft macros while still allowing placement optimization.
- **Hard Macro (1 mark):** Delivered as GDSII layout (fully placed and routed). *Advantage:* Fully characterized for PVT corners with guaranteed performance; saves significant implementation time.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**What are isolation cells and level shifters? Why are they necessary in a multi-power-domain SoC?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Isolation Cells (1.5 marks):** Special cells placed at the boundary of a power-gated (OFF) domain whose outputs connect to an active (ON) domain. They clamp the floating output to a known logic value (0 or 1) to prevent crowbar current and unpredictable behavior in the ON domain.
- **Level Shifters (1.5 marks):** Cells that translate signal voltage levels between two different voltage domains (e.g., 0.6 V ↔ 0.9 V). They are necessary because a low-voltage driver cannot reliably switch a high-voltage receiver's input, leading to metastability and excessive leakage.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Students often confuse **isolation cells** (prevent floating outputs of OFF domain) with **level shifters** (translate voltage levels). They are *different cells for different problems* — do not interchange them in answers.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Architecture & Design

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the complete SoC Macro Integration flow from IP selection to GDSII tape-out. Highlight the role of the floorplanning step in macro integration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A multi-domain SoC operates with three voltage domains: $V_{DD1} = 0.9\ \text{V}$ (application processor), $V_{DD2} = 0.6\ \text{V}$ (sensor hub), and $V_{DD3} = 0.8\ \text{V}$ (always-on logic). The number of inter-domain signals are:
- Domain 1 → Domain 3: **18 signals**
- Domain 2 → Domain 3: **10 signals**
- Domain 3 → Domain 1: **6 signals**
- Domain 1 → Domain 2: **4 signals**

Calculate the total number of level shifters required. Also explain, with a diagram, the difference between **isolation cells** and **retention flops**, and state when each is used. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Macro Integration Flow (7 marks):**
1. **IP/Macro selection (1 mark):** Identify soft, firm, and hard macros from internal libraries or third-party vendors; verify licensing.
2. **Architecture planning (1 mark):** Define top-level bus topology (AXI/APB), power domains (UPF), clock domains, and reset distribution.
3. **Floorplanning (1.5 marks):** Place macros considering data-flow proximity, route channels, and I/O pad placement. This step determines routing congestion, wire delay, and overall chip area.
4. **Top-level integration (1 mark):** Connect macros to the bus interconnect; insert isolation cells, level shifters, and retention cells at power-domain boundaries.
5. **Clock Tree Synthesis and Routing (1 mark):** Build balanced clock trees; complete signal and power routing.
6. **Verification and Sign-off (1.5 marks):** STA, DRC, LVS, IR-drop, EM, and LEC across all corners. Final GDSII release.

**Role of floorplanning:** Determines timing closure feasibility, area utilization, and routing congestion. A poor macro placement leads to long critical paths, IR-drop hotspots, and impossible timing closure.

**(b) Level Shifter Count (4 marks):**

$$
N_{ls} = 18 + 10 + 6 + 4 = 38\ \text{level shifters}
$$

**Isolation vs. Retention (3 marks):**

| Feature | Isolation Cell | Retention Flop |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Clamp output of OFF domain to known value | Preserve FF state during power-down |
| **Used when** | Domain is power-gated and its outputs connect to an ON domain | Domain is power-gated but must restore state on wake-up |
| **Power during OFF** | Zero (downstream protected) | Requires tiny always-on supply to retain state |
| **Output during OFF** | Fixed logic 0 or 1 | High-impedance (no driving) |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Analytical & Verification

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Alternative Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a typical multi-power-domain SoC. Explain the function of the **Power Management Unit (PMU)** in coordinating the power states of the domains. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An SoC's power grid has 4 voltage islands. The power drawn by each island during active mode is measured as: Island A = 250 mW, Island B = 400 mW, Island C = 180 mW, Island D = 320 mW. The power grid segment resistance from the supply pad to each island is 0.15 Ω. The supply voltage is 0.85 V. Calculate:
- (i) The current drawn by each island.
- (ii) The IR-drop for each island.
- (iii) Verify whether each island satisfies the 2% IR-drop rule. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Multi-Power-Domain SoC Architecture (7 marks):**

A typical SoC contains multiple **Voltage Islands (VIs)** powered by independent supply rails. The **Power Management Unit (PMU)** is a dedicated always-on controller that:
- Sequences power-up and power-down of each domain (1 mark)
- Coordinates with the **Clock Controller** to gate clocks before cutting power (1 mark)
- Manages **retention mode** saving context of FFs in powered-down domains (1 mark)
- Handles **wake-up events** from always-on sources (RTC, GPIO interrupts) (1 mark)
- Communicates DVFS targets to the on-chip **Voltage Regulator (PMIC interface)** (1 mark)
- Ensures **isolation** and **level shifting** are activated before power-down (1 mark)
- Block diagram showing PMU at center, connected to all VIs via power-gate control signals (1 mark)

**(b) IR-Drop Calculation (7 marks):**

Using $P = V \cdot I$:

**(i) Currents (2 marks):**

$$
I_A = \dfrac{250\ \text{mW}}{0.85\ \text{V}} = 294.12\ \text{mA}
$$

$$
I_B = \dfrac{400\ \text{mW}}{0.85\ \text{V}} = 470.59\ \text{mA}
$$

$$
I_C = \dfrac{180\ \text{mW}}{0.85\ \text{V}} = 211.76\ \text{mA}
$$

$$
I_D = \dfrac{320\ \text{mW}}{0.85\ \text{V}} = 376.47\ \text{mA}
$$

**(ii) IR-Drop (using $V_{drop} = I \cdot R$):** (2 marks)

$$
V_{drop,A} = 294.12 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.15 = 44.12\ \text{mV}
$$

$$
V_{drop,B} = 470.59 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.15 = 70.59\ \text{mV}
$$

$$
V_{drop,C} = 211.76 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.15 = 31.76\ \text{mV
$$

$$
V_{drop,D} = 376.47 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.15 = 56.47\ \text{mV}
$$

**(iii) Verification against 2% rule** ($V_{drop,allow} = 0.02 \times 0.85 = 17\ \text{mV}$): (3 marks)

| Island | $V_{drop}$ (mV) | Limit (mV) | Pass? |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 44.12 | 17.00 | ❌ FAIL |
| B | 70.59 | 17.00 | ❌ FAIL |
| C | 31.76 | 17.00 | ❌ FAIL |
| D | 56.47 | 17.00 | ❌ FAIL |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** Many students incorrectly use $V_{drop} = I^2 \cdot R$ (which gives power loss, not voltage drop). The correct form is **Ohm's law: $V = I \cdot R$**. Also, do not confuse the **2% of $V_{DD}$** rule with 2% of the voltage drop.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Macro types:** Soft = RTL, Firm = Netlist, Hard = GDSII (memorize flexibility order)
- **Integration flow:** Selection → Architecture → Floorplan → Routing → CTS → STA → Sign-off
- **Special cells:** Isolation (prevent floating), Level Shifter (voltage translation), Retention (state preservation)
- **Bus protocols:** APB (slow peripherals), AHB (mid-speed), AXI (high-speed), NoC (many-core)
- **Power formula:** $P_{total} = \alpha C V^2 f + I_{leak} V$
- **$f_{max}$ formula:** $f_{max} = 1 / (t_{ck2q} + t_{logic} + t_{routing} + t_{setup} + t_{skew})$
- **IR-drop rule:** Must be **< 2% of $V_{DD}$**
- **Floorplan utilization target:** ~50–65% for macro-heavy designs
- **CDC (Clock Domain Crossing):** Always need synchronizers; KTU loves this topic
- **UPF/CPF:** Used for power intent specification — read the file for sign-off
- **Bump array / Pad ring:** Hard macros must connect to power grid through dedicated straps
- **Hold slack:** Must be **positive** across all PVT corners
- **Macro datasheet:** The integrators' "bible" — every pin, voltage, frequency, and timing arc is listed
- **DFT integration:** Macros may have BIST; integrator must chain them into top-level scan

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When a question says "explain macro integration," **always** structure your answer in this order: (1) Define macros and types, (2) Flow diagram, (3) Special cells, (4) Verification, (5) Sign-off. This covers all COs mapped to the topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soft Macro Productization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT602 - SYSTEM ON CHIP DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Soft Macro Productization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Soft Macro** is a pre-designed, pre-verified Intellectual Property (IP) core that is delivered to the integrator in the form of a **synthesizable Hardware Description Language (HDL)** — typically **RTL (Register Transfer Level) Verilog, SystemVerilog, or VHDL** — along with the corresponding **synthesis constraints, verification testbench, documentation, and a reusable design flow**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A soft macro is a *technology-independent, behaviorally described, synthesizable IP block* whose final gate-level netlist, physical layout, timing, area, and power characteristics are *not yet fixed* at the time of delivery. These parameters are determined *post-synthesis* at the integrator's target fabrication node.

**Soft Macro Productization** is the systematic engineering process of converting an internal, project-specific RTL block into a **reusable, robust, integrator-friendly, commercially-validated IP** that can be dropped into any System-on-Chip (SoC) design with minimum effort, predictable behaviour, and guaranteed deliverables.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Card" View

Imagine you are a chef who has perfected a *Biriyani recipe* in your own kitchen. If you want to sell this recipe to other chefs worldwide, you cannot just hand them your cooked pot of rice. You must give them a **printed recipe card** that lists:
- The exact ingredients (functional inputs/outputs)
- The cooking steps in order (HDL RTL)
- The cooking temperature and time for any oven (synthesis constraints)
- A list of "this works in gas, electric, wood-fire ovens" notes (technology independence)
- A troubleshooting guide (verification suite)
- A sample dish to compare taste (testbench with golden vectors)

The **recipe card** is your **soft macro**, and the act of preparing, packaging, validating, and shipping it is **productization**.

> [!NOTE]
> In contrast, a **Hard Macro** is like shipping a *pre-cooked, frozen, ready-to-reheat meal* — the layout is fixed for a specific foundry node, and the integrator cannot alter it.

## 1.3 Key Characteristics of a Soft Macro

| Parameter | Soft Macro Behaviour |
|---|---|
| **Form of Delivery** | **Synthesizable RTL (HDL code)** + scripts |
| **Technology Dependency** | **Technology-independent** (retargetable) |
| **Timing Closure** | Done by **integrator** at SoC level |
| **Area / Power Predictability** | **Estimated**, not guaranteed |
| **Flexibility** | **High** (can be resynthesized, retimed) |
| **IP Protection** | Low (RTL is human-readable) |
| **Time to Silicon** | Shorter IP development, longer SoC integration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard metric for IP quality in KTU context:** A productized soft macro is judged by its **Ease of Integration (EOI) score**, the breadth of its **Verification Coverage (functional code coverage > 95%)**, and the robustness of its **Synthesis Script (Synopsys DC / Cadence Genus compatible)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Soft Macro Productization Flow — A 7-Phase Lifecycle

The transformation from "an RTL block on an engineer's laptop" to "a shipping IP product" follows a strict, auditable flow. KTU examiners frequently ask students to **list and explain these phases**.

### Phase 1 — **Specification Freeze**
- Authoring the **IP Specification Document (IP-XACT / IEEE 1685 compatible)**.
- Defining **micro-architecture**, **interface protocols** (e.g., AMBA AXI4, AHB, APB), **clocking strategy**, and **performance targets**.
- The **"Why"**: Without a frozen spec, downstream productization is meaningless.

### Phase 2 — **RTL Authoring & Coding Standards**
- HDL coding must follow **reusability rules**: no hard-coded constants, parameterised widths, synchronous resets, clean clock-domain crossing (CDC) handling.
- **"How"**: Use `localparam`, `generate` blocks, and **synthesis-friendly** constructs.

### Phase 3 — **Verification & Validation**
- Build a **Universal Verification Methodology (UVM)** or constrained-random testbench.
- Achieve **functional code coverage > 95%** and **functional coverage > 98%** before sign-off.
- Run **formal verification** (e.g., Synopsys Formality, Cadence JasperGold) for corner cases.

### Phase 4 — **Synthesis Script Generation**
- Deliver a **technology-independent synthesis script** (`.tcl` for Design Compiler / Genus).
- Provide **multi-corner, multi-mode (MCMM)** constraints.
- The integrator simply runs the script — gate-level netlist is auto-generated for their target node (e.g., TSMC 28 nm, GF 12 nm).

### Phase 5 — **DFT (Design for Test) Integration**
- Insert **scan chains**, **BIST (Built-In Self-Test)** blocks, and **boundary scan (JTAG 1149.1)** wrappers.
- Deliver **ATPG patterns** and **stuck-at / transition fault coverage reports** (target **> 99%**).

### Phase 6 — **Power & Performance Characterization**
- Provide **power estimation reports** at typical and worst-case corners.
- Use **vector-based dynamic power** simulation and **static power** extraction at gate level.
- Document **Performance per MHz** and **Energy per Operation** metrics.

### Phase 7 — **Documentation & Deliverable Packaging**
- Ship the **IP Deliverable Bundle** containing:
  1. RTL source (encrypted or clear-text)
  2. Synthesis / LEC / STA scripts
  3. UVM verification testbench + regression suite
  4. Integration guide & user manual
  5. Datasheet with area/timing/power projections
  6. Reference driver software (for processor-based macros)
  7. Errata sheet & revision history

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Productized soft macros form the **backbone of modern SoC design reuse**. A 5 nm mobile SoC may integrate **> 200 soft macros** (CPU cores, GPUs, DSPs, video codecs, modems). Without rigorous productization, the SoC integration team would drown in verification and timing closure issues.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Parameter | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Code Coverage** | $C_{code} = \dfrac{L_{hit}}{L_{total}} \times 100$ | Lines/branches executed by tests | **%** |
| **Functional Coverage** | $C_{func} = \dfrac{B_{hit}}{B_{total}} \times 100$ | Cross-coverage of features and corner cases | **%** |
| **Fault Coverage (DFT)** | $F_{cov} = \dfrac{D_{det}}{D_{total}} \times 100$ | Defects detected by ATPG | **%** |
| **Dynamic Power (CMOS)** | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | Switching activity, load cap, voltage, frequency | **Watts (W)** |
| **Static Power (Leakage)** | $P_{stat} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Leakage current at the technology node | **Watts (W)** |
| **Total Power** | $P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{stat} + P_{short}$ | Sum of switching, leakage, and short-circuit | **Watts (W)** |
| **Area (Gate Equivalent)** | $A_{GE} = \dfrac{N_{gates}}{N_{ref}}$ | Normalized to a 2-input NAND gate | **GE** |
| **Energy per Operation** | $E_{op} = \dfrac{P_{dyn}}{f}$ | Energy consumed per clock cycle | **Joules (J)** |
| **Timing Slack (Setup)** | $T_{slack} = T_{clk} - (T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{setup})$ | Positive slack $\Rightarrow$ timing met | **seconds (s)** |
| **Maximum Operating Frequency** | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{setup}}$ | Theoretical upper bound | **Hertz (Hz)** |

> [!TIP]
> In any KTU numerical, always carry **units** through every algebraic step. The board examiner awards the "unit mark" in the final step of calculation.

## 2.3 Soft Macro vs. Hard Macro vs. Firm Macro — The Comparison Trifecta

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **favourite 7-mark KTU question**. Memorize the comparison table below.

| Aspect | **Soft Macro** | **Firm Macro** | **Hard Macro** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Delivery form** | Synthesizable RTL | Gate-level netlist | Full GDSII layout |
| **Technology node** | Independent | Partially mapped (P&R ready) | Fixed to one node |
| **Timing predictability** | Low | Medium | Very high |
| **Area predictability** | Low | Medium | Very high |
| **Power predictability** | Low | Medium | Very high |
| **Integration flexibility** | Highest | Medium | Lowest |
| **IP Protection** | Weakest (RTL visible) | Medium (netlist obfuscated) | Strongest (GDSII is binary) |
| **Time-to-market (IP dev)** | Fastest | Medium | Slowest |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Examples** | CPU RTL cores, peripheral RTL | Synthesized netlist with floorplan | Hard SRAM compilers, analog PLLs |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Estimating the Energy-Per-Operation of a Productized Soft Macro

The following derivation is a **classic KTU Part B problem**. You are often given a soft macro's switching activity, load capacitance, supply voltage, and clock frequency, and asked to derive its energy budget.

### Given Data (Standard KTU-style problem)
- Supply voltage: $V_{DD} = 1.0 \text{ V}$
- Operating frequency: $f = 500 \text{ MHz} = 5 \times 10^{8} \text{ Hz}$
- Average switching activity factor: $\alpha = 0.15$
- Total load capacitance (per cycle): $C_{L} = 25 \text{ pF} = 25 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}$
- Static leakage current: $I_{leak} = 10 \text{ }\mu\text{A} = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A}$

### Step 1 — State the Standard Dynamic Power Formula

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$$

This is the canonical CMOS dynamic power equation, valid for the **entire soft macro block** when $\alpha$ is the average probability that a node toggles in a given clock cycle.

### Step 2 — Substitute the Numerical Values

We substitute each quantity, **converting all units to SI base units** so the result emerges in Watts.

$$P_{dyn} = (0.15) \cdot (25 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}) \cdot (1.0 \text{ V})^{2} \cdot (5 \times 10^{8} \text{ Hz})$$

### Step 3 — Simplify the Square Term

The supply voltage is at unity, so its square contributes nothing additional.

$$P_{dyn} = 0.15 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 1.0 \cdot 5 \times 10^{8} \text{ W}$$

### Step 4 — Multiply Coefficients and Powers of Ten Separately

We split the multiplication into two parts to keep the arithmetic transparent.

First, multiply the scalar coefficients:

$$0.15 \cdot 25 \cdot 5 = 18.75$$

Then, multiply the powers of ten:

$$10^{-12} \cdot 10^{8} = 10^{-4}$$

### Step 5 — Combine the Two Parts

$$P_{dyn} = 18.75 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W} = 1.875 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W}$$

Hence, the **dynamic power** of the soft macro is:

$$P_{dyn} = 1.875 \text{ mW}$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Static (Leakage) Power

We apply the static power formula:

$$P_{stat} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$$

$$P_{stat} = (1.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A}) \cdot (1.0 \text{ V})$$

$$P_{stat} = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W} = 10 \text{ }\mu\text{W}$$

### Step 7 — Compute the Total Power

$$P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{stat}$$

$$P_{total} = 1.875 \text{ mW} + 0.010 \text{ mW}$$

$$P_{total} = 1.885 \text{ mW}$$

### Step 8 — Compute the Energy Per Operation

Energy per operation is **power per clock cycle**, which mathematically equals **dynamic power divided by frequency** (the static power is a DC bias and is not cycle-scaled).

$$E_{op} = \dfrac{P_{dyn}}{f}$$

$$E_{op} = \dfrac{1.875 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W}}{5 \times 10^{8} \text{ Hz}}$$

$$E_{op} = 3.75 \times 10^{-12} \text{ J} = 3.75 \text{ pJ}$$

### Final Result

> **The productized soft macro consumes $1.885 \text{ mW}$ of total power and $3.75 \text{ pJ}$ of energy per operation.**

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: The Productization Readiness Scorecard

A useful framework KTU examiners love to test is the **"Six Cs" of productization readiness**. A soft macro is *shippable* only when it satisfies all six criteria.

| **"C"** | **Question to ask** | **Pass Criterion** |
|---|---|---|
| **Correctness** | Is the RTL functionally verified? | **Functional coverage > 98%** |
| **Completeness** | Are all deliverables present? | **All 7 deliverable items shipped** |
| **Compliance** | Does it follow interface standards? | **AMBA AXI4 / AHB / APB conformance** |
| **Constrainability** | Is the synthesis script retargetable? | **Single-command re-synthesis for any node** |
| **Coverability** | Is DFT coverage high? | **Stuck-at fault coverage > 99%** |
| **Communicability** | Is the documentation integrator-grade? | **Datasheet + User Guide + Errata present** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A soft macro failing even *one* of the Six Cs is considered **non-productized** and cannot be sold or reused across projects.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Timing Slack Calculation for a Soft Macro Path

### Given
- Clock period: $T_{clk} = 10 \text{ ns}$ (i.e., $f = 100 \text{ MHz}$)
- Clock-to-Q delay of source flop: $T_{cq} = 0.4 \text{ ns}$
- Combinational logic delay: $T_{comb} = 7.8 \text{ ns}$
- Setup time of destination flop: $T_{setup} = 0.3 \text{ ns}$

### Step 1 — State the Setup Slack Equation

$$T_{slack} = T_{clk} - (T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{setup})$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Sum of Internal Delays

$$T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{setup} = 0.4 + 7.8 + 0.3 = 8.5 \text{ ns}$$

### Step 3 — Subtract from the Clock Period

$$T_{slack} = 10 - 8.5 = 1.5 \text{ ns}$$

### Step 4 — Interpret the Result

A **positive slack of 1.5 ns** means the soft macro meets timing with **1.5 ns of design margin** — a healthy figure indicating a **productization-ready** block.

$$f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{setup}} = \dfrac{1}{8.5 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 117.6 \text{ MHz}$$

> [!TIP]
> The macro could theoretically run at **117.6 MHz**, but it is *productized* at the **100 MHz target** with **15% timing headroom** — a typical industrial safety margin.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Soft Macro Productization Workflow — Block-Level Functional Flow

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **end-to-end productization flow** as a sequence of engineering phases, the deliverables produced, and the gating sign-off criteria. This is the visual answer that KTU examiners expect when they ask "Draw/Explain the productization flow."

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 1: Spec Freeze] --> B[Phase 2: RTL Authoring]
    B --> C[Phase 3: Verification UVM/Formal]
    C --> D{All Coverage Met?}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E[Phase 4: Synthesis Scripts]
    E --> F[Phase 5: DFT Integration]
    F --> G[Phase 6: Power and Timing Char]
    G --> H{All Six Cs Pass?}
    H -- No --> I[Engineering Fix-up Loop]
    I --> E
    H -- Yes --> J[Phase 7: Documentation and Packaging]
    J --> K[Shipped Soft Macro IP Product]
    K --> L[Integrator: SoC Level P and R]
    L --> M[Tapeout Ready GDSII]
```

**Reading the flow:**
- Phases A, B, C, E, F, G, J are the seven productization stages.
- Nodes D and H are **gates** — sign-off checkpoints with measurable pass criteria.
- Node I is the **rework loop** — if the readiness scorecard fails, the macro returns to synthesis/scripts.
- Node K is the **deliverable bundle** that leaves the IP vendor.
- Nodes L and M occur *outside* the IP vendor — at the SoC integrator's site, where the soft macro is finally realized as physical silicon.

## 4.2 Soft Macro Architecture — Internal Block View

A productized soft macro is never a single monolithic RTL blob. Internally, it follows a **layered micro-architecture** to maximize reuse. The diagram below shows the **canonical internal structure** of a generic productized soft macro (e.g., a UART controller, SPI master, or custom DSP).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INT[Soft Macro Boundary]
        IF[Standard Interface Layer AMBA AXI4 or AHB or APB]
        CFG[CSR and Configuration Registers]
        FIFO[Data Path Buffers and FIFOs]
        CORE[Datapath and FSM Engine]
        CDC[Clock Domain Crossing Logic]
        DFTW[DFT Scan and BIST Wrapper]
    end
    INT --> SOC[Integrator SoC]
    IF --- CFG
    CFG --- CORE
    CORE --- FIFO
    CORE --- CDC
    DFTW --- CORE
```

**Block-by-block role explanation:**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **Standard Interface Layer** | Decodes the bus protocol; the *only* point of contact with the SoC. |
| **CSR & Configuration Registers** | Address-mapped control/status registers — the macro's "knobs". |
| **Data Path Buffers / FIFOs** | Decouple the macro's internal timing from the SoC bus. |
| **Datapath & FSM Engine** | The actual computation engine — what the macro *does*. |
| **Clock Domain Crossing Logic** | Handles metastability safely (two-flop synchronizers, async FIFOs). |
| **DFT Scan & BIST Wrapper** | Testability infrastructure for production screening. |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test the student's ability to identify **which block ensures technology independence**. The answer is the **Interface Layer + CSR + Datapath** — all three are *purely behavioural* and have no foundry-specific cells. Only the **DFT wrapper** and the **FIFO memory macros** are *technology-mapped* during synthesis, and even those are mapped *by the integrator*, not by the IP vendor.

## 4.3 Productization Reuse Model — The IP Value Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IP Vendor: Designs Soft Macro] --> B[Productized IP Bundle]
    B --> C[SoC Integrator Site 1]
    B --> D[SoC Integrator Site 2]
    B --> E[SoC Integrator Site 3]
    C --> F[Mobile SoC]
    D --> G[IoT Edge SoC]
    E --> H[Automotive SoC]
```

This diagram illustrates the **economic rationale** of productization: *one productized soft macro* can be re-synthesized and dropped into dozens of SoCs across multiple market segments, multiplying the ROI of the original design effort.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a soft macro. Why is it called "soft"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A soft macro is a pre-designed, pre-verified Intellectual Property (IP) core delivered as a **synthesizable Hardware Description Language (RTL) source** along with constraints, scripts, testbenches, and documentation. It is called "soft" because **none of its physical characteristics — gate count, timing, area, or power — are fixed at delivery time**. These parameters only become concrete *after* the integrator synthesizes the RTL onto a specific target fabrication technology (e.g., TSMC 28 nm, GF 12 nm). The macro is therefore *soft* in the sense of being *pliable* and *retargetable*, in contrast to a *hard* macro whose GDSII layout is frozen at one specific technology node.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any four deliverables in a productized soft macro IP bundle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **RTL source code** (Verilog/SystemVerilog/VHDL) with synthesizable constructs.
2. **Synthesis script** (TCL-based) for multi-corner, multi-mode constraint application.
3. **Verification testbench** (UVM) with regression suite and coverage reports.
4. **Integration guide / User manual** explaining pin-out, clock/reset requirements, and configuration register map.

*(A fifth and sixth deliverable that may be credited: ATPG patterns with fault coverage report, and a power/timing datasheet.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Weight Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** When asked to "explain soft macro productization", many students *only* list the phases without explaining the **gating criteria** (coverage %, sign-off checks). You lose **2 to 3 marks** for skipping the pass-criteria columns. Always pair every phase with a measurable checkpoint.
> 
> **Pitfall #2:** Many students confuse **soft macro** with **firm macro**. Recall: *soft* = RTL, *firm* = gate-level netlist with a floorplan, *hard* = GDSII. Mixing these up costs you the definition marks in any 7-marker.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 Choice A]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **seven phases of soft macro productization**. For each phase, state the **gating sign-off criterion** that determines whether the macro can proceed to the next phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A productized soft macro operates at $V_{DD} = 1.2 \text{ V}$ and $f = 250 \text{ MHz}$. Its average switching activity is $\alpha = 0.20$, load capacitance is $C_{L} = 40 \text{ pF}$, and leakage current is $I_{leak} = 5 \text{ }\mu\text{A}$. Compute the **dynamic power, static power, total power, and energy per operation** of the macro. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

The seven phases with their **gating criteria** are:

| Phase | Activity | Gating Criterion |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Spec Freeze | Author IP-XACT specification | **Signed-off by IP steering committee** |
| 2. RTL Authoring | Write clean, parameterised HDL | **Lint-clean (zero Synopsys VCS / Cadence Lint errors)** |
| 3. Verification | UVM regression, formal proofs | **Code coverage > 95%, functional coverage > 98%** |
| 4. Synthesis Scripts | Generate technology-independent TCL | **Clean synthesis for ≥ 2 reference nodes** |
| 5. DFT Integration | Insert scan chains, BIST, JTAG | **Stuck-at fault coverage > 99%** |
| 6. Power / Timing Char | Run vector-based power sim, STA | **Slack > 10% of $T_{clk}$, $P_{total}$ within budget** |
| 7. Documentation | Author datasheet, user guide, errata | **Reviewer sign-off, traceability matrix complete** |

*(Block diagram: refer to the Mermaid flow in Section 4.1 of these notes — the student is expected to redraw it on the answer script.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Dynamic Power:**

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$$

$$P_{dyn} = 0.20 \cdot 40 \times 10^{-12} \cdot (1.2)^{2} \cdot 250 \times 10^{6}$$

Compute $V_{DD}^{2} = 1.44 \text{ V}^{2}$:

$$P_{dyn} = 0.20 \cdot 40 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 1.44 \cdot 250 \times 10^{6}$$

$$P_{dyn} = 0.20 \cdot 40 \cdot 1.44 \cdot 250 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$P_{dyn} = 2880 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} = 2.88 \text{ mW}$$

**Step 2 — Static Power:**

$$P_{stat} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1.2 = 6.0 \text{ }\mu\text{W}$$

**Step 3 — Total Power:**

$$P_{total} = 2.88 \text{ mW} + 0.006 \text{ mW} = 2.886 \text{ mW}$$

**Step 4 — Energy per Operation:**

$$E_{op} = \dfrac{P_{dyn}}{f} = \dfrac{2.88 \times 10^{-3}}{250 \times 10^{6}} = 11.52 \times 10^{-12} \text{ J} = 11.52 \text{ pJ}$$

> **Final Answer:** $P_{dyn} = 2.88 \text{ mW}$, $P_{stat} = 6.0 \text{ }\mu\text{W}$, $P_{total} = 2.886 \text{ mW}$, $E_{op} = 11.52 \text{ pJ}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice B]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast **soft, firm, and hard macros** across any **seven** evaluation parameters. Why is a soft macro preferred for **technology-independent IP reuse**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a soft macro timing path, $T_{clk} = 8 \text{ ns}$, $T_{cq} = 0.5 \text{ ns}$, $T_{comb} = 6.2 \text{ ns}$, and $T_{setup} = 0.3 \text{ ns}$. Compute the **setup slack, maximum operating frequency, and the percentage timing headroom** with respect to the 8 ns target clock. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

| # | Parameter | Soft Macro | Firm Macro | Hard Macro |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Delivery form | Synthesizable RTL | Gate-level netlist | GDSII layout |
| 2 | Technology node | Independent | Partially mapped | Fixed |
| 3 | Timing predictability | Low | Medium | Very high |
| 4 | Area predictability | Low | Medium | Very high |
| 5 | IP protection strength | Weak | Medium | Strong |
| 6 | Integration flexibility | Highest | Medium | Lowest |
| 7 | Time-to-market (IP dev) | Fastest | Medium | Slowest |

**Why soft macro is preferred for technology-independent IP reuse (3 additional marks absorbed into the 7):**
- The RTL can be re-synthesized for **any foundry node** (180 nm, 65 nm, 7 nm, etc.) by simply rerunning the synthesis scripts.
- The IP vendor is **not locked** to a specific fab or PDK.
- The integrator retains full control over PPA (Power, Performance, Area) trade-offs.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute the Setup Slack:**

$$T_{slack} = T_{clk} - (T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{setup})$$

$$T_{slack} = 8 - (0.5 + 6.2 + 0.3) = 8 - 7.0 = 1.0 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Maximum Operating Frequency:**

$$f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{setup}} = \dfrac{1}{7.0 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$f_{max} \approx 142.86 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Percentage Timing Headroom:**

$$\text{Headroom \%} = \dfrac{T_{slack}}{T_{clk}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1.0}{8.0} \times 100 = 12.5\%$$

> **Final Answer:** $T_{slack} = 1.0 \text{ ns}$ (positive, so timing met), $f_{max} \approx 142.86 \text{ MHz}$, headroom = **12.5%**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Soft Macro Productization**

- **Definition:** Soft macro = synthesizable HDL + scripts + verification IP, delivered technology-independent.
- **Why "soft"?** Because all physical attributes (area, timing, power) are *soft* / *unfixed* at delivery.
- **Seven Phases:** Spec Freeze → RTL → Verification → Synthesis Scripts → DFT → Power/Timing Char → Documentation.
- **Six Cs:** Correctness, Completeness, Compliance, Constrainability, Coverability, Communicability.
- **Deliverables Bundle:** RTL, synthesis TCL, UVM testbench, integration guide, datasheet, ATPG patterns, errata.
- **Dynamic Power Formula:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ — *the most tested formula in this module*.
- **Static Power Formula:** $P_{stat} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$.
- **Energy per Operation:** $E_{op} = P_{dyn} / f$.
- **Setup Slack:** $T_{slack} = T_{clk} - (T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{setup})$; positive slack $\Rightarrow$ timing closure achieved.
- **Maximum Frequency:** $f_{max} = 1 / (T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{setup})$.
- **Macro Comparison Trinity:** Soft = RTL, Firm = Netlist, Hard = GDSII — *memorize the three-way contrast*.
- **Functional Coverage Target:** > 98% for productization sign-off.
- **DFT Coverage Target:** Stuck-at fault coverage > 99% for production-readiness.
- **Common Trap:** Forgetting to *convert units to SI* before plugging into power formulas — KTU examiners deduct 1 full mark for unit errors.
- **Industrial Reality:** Productization transforms an *internal design asset* into a *reusable IP product* — the entire modern SoC economy depends on it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Coding Guidelines
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT602 - SYSTEM ON CHIP DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Overview of Coding Guidelines

# Overview of Coding Guidelines — SoC Design (KTU 2024)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Coding Guidelines** in the context of System on Chip (SoC) design refer to a *standardized, rule-based set of conventions and best practices* governing the writing of Hardware Description Language (HDL) code — predominantly **Verilog**, **SystemVerilog**, and **VHDL** — for the Register Transfer Level (RTL) abstraction. These guidelines ensure that the code is **synthesizable, portable, simulation-efficient, lint-clean, reusable, and unambiguously interpretable** across the entire VLSI design flow, from specification and RTL coding to synthesis, place-and-route, and silicon fabrication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Coding guidelines are the engineering discipline of imposing *consistent structure, naming, synchronization, reset, and abstraction rules* on HDL source code to minimize human-introduced design bugs, ensure design-for-test (DFT) friendliness, achieve predictable synthesis results, and enable smooth team-based collaborative IP/SoC development.

### 1.2 Why Coding Guidelines Matter in SoC Design

A modern SoC may contain **hundreds of IP blocks** (CPU cores, GPUs, memory controllers, peripherals, interconnect fabrics) authored by distributed design teams across geographies. Without enforced coding standards, the SoC integration effort collapses into a debugging nightmare of mismatched interpretations, race conditions, and unreproducible synthesis results.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fun Fact for Intuition:** A single unsynchronized asynchronous reset crossing two clock domains in an SoC of 5 billion transistors can cause **metastability propagation** that manifests as a rare field failure every 10^9 clock cycles — translating to a *1-in-31-years* silent data corruption in a 1 GHz SoC. Coding guidelines prevent exactly this.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building Code" Analogy

Imagine constructing a 100-storey skyscraper:

- **HDL code without guidelines** is like 50 different contractors each building floors with their own materials, wiring colors, plumbing dimensions, and stairwell placement. The building "compiles" (stands), but the electrician cannot find a wire, the plumber cannot connect pipes, and the fire inspector rejects occupancy.
- **HDL code with strict guidelines** is like having a *uniform building code* (IS 456, NBC): every contractor uses the same wire gauge, the same pipe diameter, the same rebar spacing. The structure is **inspectable, maintainable, and safe**.

SoC coding guidelines are the *National Building Code of silicon design*.

### 1.4 Categories of Coding Guidelines (KTU Module 3 Map)

The KTU 2024 scheme groups coding guidelines into the following sub-domains:

| # | Category | Focus Area |
|---|----------|------------|
| 1 | **Naming Conventions** | Signals, modules, parameters, files |
| 2 | **Indentation & Style** | Readability, whitespace, comment density |
| 3 | **Reset Strategy** | Synchronous, asynchronous, partial, soft |
| 4 | **Clock Domain Crossing (CDC)** | Synchronizers, FIFOs, handshake protocols |
| 5 | **Latch Avoidance** | Complete `if/else`, `case` with `default` |
| 6 | **Sensitivity List Discipline** | Combinational vs sequential constructs |
| 7 | **Blocking vs Non-Blocking** | Verilog race-condition avoidance |
| 8 | **Resource Sharing & Inference** | Mux/adder inference patterns |
| 9 | **Parameterization & Reuse** | `parameter`, `localparam`, `generate` |
| 10 | **Synthesis–Simulation Mismatch** | `initial` blocks, `delays`, `real` types |

### 1.5 Key Design Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Golden Rules of SoC RTL Coding (Industry Standard):**
> 1. **Synchronous design only** — every flip-flop clocked by the same domain boundary.
> 2. **One clock per `always` block** — never mix clocks inside a single process.
> 3. **Reset everything or document why not** — eliminate X-propagation.
> 4. **Never infer a latch in synthesis** — complete all conditional branches.
> 5. **Code must be lint-clean** — pass Synopsys VCS Lint, Cadence HAL, or Verilator with zero warnings.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Naming Convention Rules (Industry-Standard Pattern)

A well-engineered naming scheme uses *case prefixes* to identify signal *role* rather than its *function*:

| Prefix | Meaning | Example | Usage Domain |
|--------|---------|---------|--------------|
| `clk_` | Clock signal | `clk_sys`, `clk_ddr` | All clock nets |
| `rst_` | Reset (active low) | `rst_sys_n` | Reset distribution |
| `i_` | Input port | `i_data` | Top-level boundary |
| `o_` | Output port | `o_valid` | Top-level boundary |
| `r_` | Registered (flopped) | `r_state` | Internal registers |
| `w_` | Wire (combinational) | `w_mux_out` | Internal nets |
| `_n` | Active-low signal suffix | `cs_n`, `wr_n` | Polarity indication |
| `_reg` | Register suffix | `count_reg` | Sequential elements |
| `_next` | Next-state logic | `state_next` | FSM transitions |
| `p_` | Parameter/localparam | `p_WIDTH` | Compile-time constants |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Many SoC companies (Intel, AMD, NVIDIA, Qualcomm) enforce *role-based prefixes* through linting tools. A `clk_` signal is forbidden from being driven by combinational logic, and lint rules enforce this contract.

### 2.2 Reset Strategy — Deep Dive

#### 2.2.1 Types of Resets

| Reset Type | Trigger | Removal | SoC Usage |
|------------|---------|---------|-----------|
| **Hard Reset (Power-on)** | External `POR` pin | Asynchronous | Initializes all flops at silicon power-up |
| **Soft Reset** | Software register write | Synchronous | Module-level warm reset |
| **Synchronous Reset** | Clocked assertion | Clocked removal | DFT-friendly, lower area |
| **Asynchronous Reset** | Independent of clock | Asynchronous | Robust against clock glitches |
| **Asynchronous Assert / Synchronous De-assert (AASD)** | Async assert, sync deassert | Synchronous deassert | Industry-recommended best practice |

#### 2.2.2 AASD Reset — The Gold Standard

The recommended SoC industry pattern is **Asynchronous Assertion, Synchronous De-assertion (AASD)**. It guarantees that:
- The reset takes effect immediately (no clock dependency on assertion).
- The reset release is *synchronous* to the clock — eliminating reset recovery and removal timing violations.

### 2.3 Clock Domain Crossing (CDC) Theory

When a signal `src_data` from clock domain `clk_A` is consumed by a flop in clock domain `clk_B`, the destination flop may enter **metastability** — an unstable voltage level that resolves to either 0 or 1 in undefined time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) Equation (KTU High-Yield Formula):**

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{e^{t_r / \tau}}{T_0 \cdot f_{clk_A} \cdot f_{clk_{data}}}
$$

Where:
- $t_r$ = available resolution time after clock-to-Q delay (in seconds)
- $\tau$ = metastability time constant of the flip-flop (typically 10–50 ps)
- $T_0$ = process-dependent constant (typically 0.1–10 seconds)
- $f_{clk_A}$ = sampling clock frequency
- $f_{clk_{data}}$ = data toggle frequency

**Interpretation:** Larger $t_r$ (slower destination clock) and smaller data-toggle rate dramatically increase MTBF. A two-flop synchronizer pushes MTBF into the **millions of years** range for typical SoC clock frequencies.

### 2.4 Two-Flop Synchronizer (Canonical CDC Pattern)

For single-bit control signals crossing clock domains, the standard pattern is a **two-flip-flop shift register** in the destination domain:

| Stage | Function |
|-------|----------|
| `FF1` | Samples asynchronous input — may go metastable |
| `FF2` | Re-samples after one destination-clock period — output is *guaranteed stable* |

> A third flop is added for *very high reliability* (MTBF > 10^12 years) in automotive ASIL-D and aerospace designs.

### 2.5 Latch Inference — Forbidden Pattern

A latch is inferred when an `always @(*)` or `always @ (combinational list)` block fails to assign a value to a signal in *all* execution paths.

> [!WARNING]
> **Latch Inference Triggers (Auto-detect by Synthesis Tools):**
> - `if` without matching `else` where the LHS is not assigned outside the `if`.
> - `case` without `default` clause.
> - Self-assignment of a variable inside the same always block.
> - Reading a variable before assignment in a procedural block.

### 2.6 Blocking vs Non-Blocking — The IEEE 1364 Rule

| Construct | Recommended Assignment | Reason |
|-----------|----------------------|--------|
| `always @(posedge clk)` — Sequential | **Non-blocking** (`<=`) | Models parallel flip-flop update — race-free |
| `always @(*)` — Combinational | **Blocking** (`=`) | Models immediate dataflow — no race |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sunburst Design Rule (Verilog/SystemVerilog):** "Use non-blocking for sequential logic, blocking for combinational logic, and never mix them in the same `always` block." This is the single most-tested KTU question on coding guidelines.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Typical SoC Value |
|---------|----------------|-------------------|
| Metastability MTBF | $\text{MTBF} = \frac{e^{t_r/\tau}}{T_0 f_1 f_2}$ | $10^9$ to $10^{15}$ seconds |
| Two-flop synchronizer resolution | $t_r = T_{clk} - t_{cq} - t_{setup}$ | ~1 ns in 1 GHz SoC |
| Setup margin (metastability) | $t_{margin} = T_{clk} - t_{cq} - t_{setup} - t_{skew}$ | ≥ 0 |
| Reset recovery time | $t_{rec} \le t_{cq}$ (reset) | Vendor-specific |
| Reset removal time | $t_{rem} \le T_{clk} - t_{rec}$ | Vendor-specific |
| FIFO depth (async CDC) | $D = 2^{\lceil \log_2 N \rceil}$ where $N = $ burst size | Power-of-2 |
| Gray-code transition | $G_{n+1} = G_n \oplus (G_n \gg 1)$ | Hamming distance = 1 |
| Latch check | Every LHS assigned in *all* paths | — |
| Clock skew budget | $t_{skew} < 0.1 \times T_{clk}$ | ~100 ps in 1 GHz |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DFT (Design for Test):** Synchronous reset enables clean scan-chain stitching. A latch in scan mode is *untestable* and causes ATPG pattern explosion.
- **Low-Power Design:** Coding guidelines enforce clean clock-gating boundaries, enabling synthesis tools (Synopsys Power Compiler, Cadence Genus) to insert ICG (Integrated Clock Gating) cells with accurate activity vectors.
- **Formal Verification:** Lint-clean, single-clock-domain code is *formally tractable*. Latches and CDC bugs explode the state space beyond solver capacity.
- **FPGA → ASIC Portability:** Coding guidelines ensure that a design validated on Xilinx/Intel FPGA ports to TSMC/Samsung foundry with *zero RTL rewrite* — saving 3–6 months of engineering effort.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: MTBF of a Two-Flop Synchronizer

**Goal:** Quantify how a two-flop synchronizer achieves >10^9 year MTBF.

**Given (typical 28nm SoC):**
- Metastability time constant: $\tau = 20$ ps
- Process constant: $T_0 = 1$ s
- Source clock: $f_{clk_A} = 1$ GHz
- Data toggle rate: $f_{data} = 100$ MHz
- Available resolution time: $t_r = 1$ ns (destination clock at 1 GHz, with $t_{cq} = 50$ ps, $t_{setup} = 50$ ps)

**Step 1 — Compute the exponent:**

$$
\frac{t_r}{\tau} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-9}}{20 \times 10^{-12}} = 50
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the exponential:**

$$
e^{t_r/\tau} = e^{50} \approx 5.18 \times 10^{21}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the product of frequencies:**

$$
f_{clk_A} \cdot f_{data} = 10^9 \times 10^8 = 10^{17}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into MTBF formula:**

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{5.18 \times 10^{21}}{1 \times 10^{17}} = 5.18 \times 10^{4} \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to years:**

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{5.18 \times 10^4}{3.154 \times 10^7} \approx 1.64 \times 10^{-3} \text{ years}
$$

**Step 6 — Add second synchronizer flop:**

The second flop reduces the effective probability by another factor of $e^{t_r/\tau} = e^{50}$, giving:

$$
\text{MTBF}_{\text{2-flop}} = 1.64 \times 10^{-3} \times 5.18 \times 10^{21} \approx 8.5 \times 10^{18} \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
\text{MTBF}_{\text{2-flop}} \approx 2.7 \times 10^{11} \text{ years}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A two-flop synchronizer converts a 1.6 ms failure probability into a 270-billion-year failure probability — effectively **zero** for any realistic product lifetime. This is why every SoC ASIC library cell for CDC *guarantees* the two-flop primitive.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Gray Code Property for Multi-bit CDC

**Problem:** A 4-bit binary counter increments from `0111` to `1000`. In binary, **all 4 bits change** simultaneously, sampling skew across flip-flops can capture an intermediate value (e.g., `0000` or `1111`), causing a *catastrophic* misread in the destination FIFO pointer.

**Step 1 — Convert 4-bit binary to Gray code** using the standard recurrence:

$$
G_i = B_i \oplus B_{i+1}
$$

where $B_i$ is the $i$-th binary bit and $G_i$ is the $i$-th Gray bit.

**Step 2 — Apply to count values 7 and 8:**

For $B = 0111$ (decimal 7):
$$
G_3 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1, \quad G_2 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0, \quad G_1 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0, \quad G_0 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1
$$
$$
G(7) = 0100
$$

For $B = 1000$ (decimal 8):
$$
G_3 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1, \quad G_2 = 0 \oplus 0 = 0, \quad G_1 = 0 \oplus 0 = 0, \quad G_0 = 0 \oplus 0 = 0
$$
$$
G(8) = 1100
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Hamming distance:**

$$
H(G(7), G(8)) = H(0100, 1100) = 1
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Only **one bit** transitions between consecutive Gray-coded values. Regardless of metastability on a single bit, the destination domain reads either the *old* or *new* pointer value — never a *garbled* intermediate. This is the foundation of **asynchronous FIFO pointer design**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Latch Inference from Incomplete `if`

**Verilog Code (BAD — latches inferred):**

```verilog
always @(*) begin
    if (sel == 1'b1)
        y = a;
end
```

**Synthesis Output:** Tool infers a **transparent latch** for `y` because when `sel == 0`, the code does not assign `y` — but the synthesis tool must preserve the previous value, which is *exactly* the function of a latch.

**Corrected Code (GOOD — no latch):**

```verilog
always @(*) begin
    if (sel == 1'b1)
        y = a;
    else
        y = b;       // Default assignment covers all paths
end
```

**Decision Matrix for Latch Inference:**

| `if`/`else` Presence | All LHS Assigned in Every Branch? | Synthesis Result | KTU Verdict |
|----------------------|----------------------------------|------------------|-------------|
| Yes (with default) | Yes | Pure combinational mux | ✅ Pass |
| `if` only, no `else` | No | **Latch inferred** | ❌ Fail |
| `case` with `default` | Yes | One-hot mux | ✅ Pass |
| `case` no `default` | No | **Latch inferred** for unspecified codes | ❌ Fail |
| `if` inside `always @(posedge clk)` | N/A (sequential context) | Flip-flop + mux | ✅ Pass |

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Reset Strategies

| Property | Async Reset | Sync Reset | AASD Reset |
|----------|------------|-----------|-----------|
| Recovery timing required | Yes (critical) | No | Yes (for de-assertion only) |
| Removal timing required | Yes (critical) | No | Yes (for de-assertion only) |
| DFT scan friendliness | Moderate | Excellent | Excellent |
| Clock-gating compatibility | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| Power-on behavior | Deterministic | Clock-dependent | Deterministic |
| Area overhead | Lowest | Lowest | +2 flops per reset domain |
| Used in modern SoC | Legacy only | High-frequency cores | **Industry standard** |

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — CDC Strategies

| Method | Data Width | Latency | Bandwidth | Use Case |
|--------|-----------|---------|-----------|----------|
| Two-flop synchronizer | 1 bit | 2 cycles | Low | Single-bit control flags |
| Handshake (req/ack) | Any | Variable | Moderate | Block-level data transfer |
| Async FIFO (Gray-coded ptrs) | Any (burst) | N cycles | High | Stream/block DMA |
| Async Gearbox | Power-of-2 ratio | 1–2 cycles | High | Clock-ratio conversion |
| Pulse synchronizer | 1 bit | 2–3 cycles | Moderate | Single-event interrupts |

### 3.6 Comprehensive Synthesis–Simulation Mismatch Table

| Construct | Simulation Behavior | Synthesis Behavior | Guideline |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|-----------|
| `initial` block | Executes once at time 0 | **Ignored** (FPGA), partially supported (ASIC) | Avoid for logic; use reset |
| `#5 delay` | Delays event scheduling | **Ignored** (treated as comment) | Never use in synthesizable code |
| `real` variable | Floating-point arithmetic | **Not supported** | Use `integer` or fixed-point |
| `force` / `release` | Overrides signal | **Not supported** | Forbidden in RTL |
| `fork`/`join` (Verilog) | Parallel threads | **Partially supported** (SystemVerilog `fork...join_any`) | Use with caution |
| `$display` | Prints to console | **Ignored** | Simulation only |
| `casez` / `casex` | Wildcard match | Supported but **lint warnings** | Avoid in synthesized code |
| `wait` statement | Level-sensitive pause | **Not supported** | Forbidden |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any six essential RTL coding guidelines that must be followed to make a Verilog design fully synthesizable and lint-clean.*

**Model Answer:**

The six essential RTL coding guidelines are:

1. **Use synchronous design** — clock all flip-flops with a single rising edge of a known clock; avoid gated clocks.
2. **Avoid latches** — every `always @(*)` block must assign all LHS signals in all execution paths (complete `if/else`, `case` with `default`).
3. **Use non-blocking assignments (`<=`) for sequential logic** — prevents race conditions in simulation.
4. **Use blocking assignments (`=`) for combinational logic** — models immediate dataflow.
5. **Provide a complete reset strategy** — every flop must be initialized via async, sync, or AASD reset; never leave flops at `X`.
6. **Do not use `initial` blocks, `#` delays, or `real` types in synthesizable RTL** — these are simulation-only constructs and cause synthesis–simulation mismatches.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the "two-flop synchronizer" pattern used for single-bit CDC with a neat block diagram description.*

**Model Answer:**

A **two-flop synchronizer** is the standard CDC primitive used to safely transfer a single-bit asynchronous signal from source clock domain `clk_A` to destination clock domain `clk_B`. The signal is sampled by **Flip-Flop 1 (FF1)**, which is clocked by `clk_B`. FF1 may enter metastability if the source signal transitions near the rising edge of `clk_B`. After one destination-clock period, **Flip-Flop 2 (FF2)** re-samples the (now-resolved) FF1 output. The FF2 output is *guaranteed* to be a stable logic level (0 or 1) with probability `1 - e^{-t_r/\tau}`, where $t_r$ is the available resolution time. The MTBF of a two-flop synchronizer in a 1 GHz SoC exceeds **10^11 years**, making it effectively zero-failure for any product lifetime. The block-level structure is: `src_data → FF1 (clk_B) → FF2 (clk_B) → dst_data`.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **naming convention, reset strategy, and clock domain crossing (CDC)** guidelines that must be enforced in a modern SoC RTL design. Mention at least 4 industry-standard rules for each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain **latch inference in Verilog**. Show how an incomplete `if` statement inside an `always @(*)` block synthesizes a latch, and write the corrected latch-free code. Also list the two IEEE 1364 golden rules for **blocking vs non-blocking assignments** in Verilog. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Naming Convention, Reset Strategy, and CDC Guidelines (7 marks):**

**Naming Convention (4 rules):**
1. Use **role-based prefixes**: `clk_` for clocks, `rst_` for resets, `r_` for registered signals, `w_` for wires, `i_`/`o_` for input/output ports.
2. **Suffix active-low signals with `_n`**: e.g., `cs_n`, `wr_n` — clarity of polarity at first glance.
3. **Module and file names must match exactly**: file `alu_core.v` contains `module alu_core (...)`.
4. **Parameters in UPPERCASE with `p_` prefix**: e.g., `parameter p_DATA_WIDTH = 32`.

**Reset Strategy (4 rules):**
1. **Industry-recommended pattern is AASD (Asynchronous Assertion, Synchronous De-assertion)** — eliminates reset recovery and removal timing violations.
2. **All flip-flops must be reset** to eliminate `X` propagation in simulation.
3. **Use synchronous reset for DFT-friendly scan stitching** — no need for reset recovery logic in scan mode.
4. **Reset tree must be balanced** using a reset buffer tree (e.g., Synopsys `reset_tree_synth`); skew on reset net must be <10% of clock period.

**CDC Guidelines (4 rules):**
1. **Use a two-flop synchronizer** for single-bit control signals crossing clock domains.
2. **Use Gray-coded pointers** in asynchronous FIFOs to ensure only one bit changes per increment — eliminates multi-bit CDC errors.
3. **Never sample asynchronous data with a single flop** in the destination domain — guarantees metastability resolution.
4. **Tag every CDC signal with a comment or attribute** (e.g., `(* CDC = "TO_CLK_B" *)`) and verify with formal CDC tools (Cadence JasperGold CDC, Synopsys SpyGlass CDC).

**(b) Latch Inference and Blocking vs Non-Blocking (7 marks):**

**Latch-Inferring Code (BAD):**

```verilog
module bad_latch (input wire a, b, sel, output reg y);
    always @(*) begin
        if (sel == 1'b1)
            y = a;     // When sel = 0, y is NOT assigned → LATCH inferred
    end
endmodule
```

**Synthesis Tool Output:** The synthesis tool detects that `y` is not assigned when `sel == 0`, so it must *retain* its previous value. This is precisely the definition of a **transparent latch** (level-sensitive storage element). Latches are forbidden in synchronous SoC designs because:
- They break timing analysis (no clear setup/hold).
- They are not scan-insertable.
- They create glitches in synthesized combinational paths.

**Corrected Latch-Free Code (GOOD):**

```verilog
module good_mux (input wire a, b, sel, output reg y);
    always @(*) begin
        if (sel == 1'b1)
            y = a;
        else
            y = b;     // Default assignment — y is assigned in ALL paths
    end
endmodule
```

The synthesis tool now infers a **clean 2:1 multiplexer** with no storage element.

**IEEE 1364 Golden Rules for Blocking vs Non-Blocking:**

1. **Always use non-blocking assignments (`<=`) for sequential logic** — i.e., inside `always @(posedge clk)`. This models the *parallel update* of all flip-flops at the clock edge, eliminating simulation race conditions.

2. **Always use blocking assignments (`=`) for combinational logic** — i.e., inside `always @(*)`. This models the *immediate dataflow* evaluation of combinational expressions, mimicking hardware propagation delay.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly lose marks by:
> - Mixing blocking and non-blocking in the same `always` block (e.g., `=` and `<=` for two different signals in a sequential block) — this is a **linter error** and breaks simulation determinism.
> - Writing `if (sel) y = a;` with no `else` and claiming "it's a combinational mux" — synthesis will infer a **latch**, and the examiner deducts 2–3 marks.
> - Using `#5` delays in synthesizable code — the examiner treats this as a **fatal design error** (1–2 marks deduction).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **MTBF equation for metastability** in a synchronizer. A 28nm SoC uses a two-flop synchronizer at 1 GHz destination clock. Given $\tau = 20$ ps, $T_0 = 1$ s, $f_{clk_A} = 1$ GHz, $f_{data} = 100$ MHz, $t_r = 1$ ns, compute the MTBF of the **single-flop** and **two-flop** synchronizers. Comment on the safety margin. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a diagram, explain the **Gray code pointer scheme** used in an asynchronous FIFO for clock domain crossing of multi-bit signals. Why is binary-coded pointer unsafe for CDC? Show that the Gray code of 4-bit values 7 and 8 differ in only one bit. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) MTBF Computation (7 marks):**

**MTBF Formula:**

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{e^{t_r / \tau}}{T_0 \cdot f_{clk_A} \cdot f_{data}}
$$

**Given:**
- $t_r = 1 \text{ ns} = 10^{-9}$ s
- $\tau = 20 \text{ ps} = 20 \times 10^{-12}$ s
- $T_0 = 1$ s
- $f_{clk_A} = 10^9$ Hz
- $f_{data} = 10^8$ Hz

**Step 1 — Compute exponent:**

$$
\frac{t_r}{\tau} = \frac{10^{-9}}{20 \times 10^{-12}} = 50
$$

**Step 2 — Compute exponential:**

$$
e^{50} = 5.1847 \times 10^{21}
$$

**Step 3 — Single-flop MTBF:**

$$
\text{MTBF}_{\text{1-flop}} = \frac{5.1847 \times 10^{21}}{1 \times 10^9 \times 10^8} = 5.1847 \times 10^{4} \text{ s}
$$

$$
\text{MTBF}_{\text{1-flop}} = \frac{5.1847 \times 10^4}{3.154 \times 10^7} \approx 1.64 \times 10^{-3} \text{ years} \approx 14 \text{ hours}
$$

> **Comment:** A single-flop synchronizer fails every ~14 hours — **unacceptable** for any product.

**Step 4 — Two-flop MTBF:**

The second flop multiplies MTBF by another factor of $e^{t_r/\tau} = 5.1847 \times 10^{21}$:

$$
\text{MTBF}_{\text{2-flop}} = 5.1847 \times 10^4 \times 5.1847 \times 10^{21} = 2.69 \times 10^{26} \text{ s}
$$

$$
\text{MTBF}_{\text{2-flop}} = \frac{2.69 \times 10^{26}}{3.154 \times 10^7} \approx 8.5 \times 10^{18} \text{ years}
$$

**Safety Margin:** MTBF of 8.5 × 10^18 years is **8.5 trillion times** the age of the universe (~1.38 × 10^10 years). The two-flop synchronizer is **provably safe** for any commercial SoC.

**(b) Gray Code Pointer for Async FIFO (7 marks):**

**Why Binary is Unsafe for CDC:**

Consider a 4-bit write pointer crossing from `clk_wr` to `clk_rd` domain. As the pointer increments from `0111` (7) to `1000` (8), **all 4 bits change simultaneously**. Due to clock-skew and metastability, the destination domain may capture any of the 2^4 = 16 possible intermediate values (e.g., `0000`, `1111`, `0101`). This produces a *catastrophic* FIFO underflow/overflow and silent data corruption.

**Gray Code Property:**

Gray code ensures that **exactly one bit transitions** between consecutive values. The recurrence is:

$$
G_i = B_i \oplus B_{i+1}
$$

**For B = 0111 (decimal 7):**
- $G_3 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1$
- $G_2 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0$
- $G_1 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0$
- $G_0 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1$
- **G(7) = 0100**

**For B = 1000 (decimal 8):**
- $G_3 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1$
- $G_2 = 0 \oplus 0 = 0$
- $G_1 = 0 \oplus 0 = 0$
- $G_0 = 0 \oplus 0 = 0$
- **G(8) = 1100**

**Hamming Distance:**

$$
H(G(7), G(8)) = H(0100, 1100) = 1
$$

Only **one bit** changes — the destination domain samples either the old (`0100`) or new (`1100`) pointer, **never** an intermediate garbled value. This is the foundation of every asynchronous FIFO in SoC designs.

**Block Diagram:**

```
   Write Domain (clk_wr)           Read Domain (clk_rd)
  +------------------+             +------------------+
  | Write Counter    |   Gray      | Sync FF1, FF2    |
  | (Binary)         | --> Code -->| (2-flop per ptr) |
  +------------------+   Pointer   +------------------+
                                   |
                                   v
                              Empty Flag Gen
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (for Q.B):** Students commonly lose marks on this question by:
> - Forgetting to **state the Hamming distance of 1** explicitly (0.5–1 mark deduction).
> - Failing to mention that **synchronization of the Gray pointer is still required** (2-flop synchronizer on each pointer bit).
> - Confusing **write pointer depth** with **Gray code bit width** — Gray code needs $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil + 1$ bits for an N-deep FIFO (e.g., 16-deep → 5-bit Gray).
> - Not connecting Gray code to **FIFO full/empty flag generation** logic.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Coding Guidelines**

- ✅ **Synchronous design only** — every flop clocked by one rising edge.
- ✅ **Naming conventions** — use role-based prefixes (`clk_`, `rst_`, `r_`, `w_`, `_n` suffix for active-low).
- ✅ **Reset strategy** — prefer AASD (Async assert, Sync de-assert); initialize all flops.
- ✅ **Clock Domain Crossing (CDC)** — use **two-flop synchronizer** for 1-bit; **Gray-coded async FIFO** for multi-bit.
- ✅ **MTBF** — single flop = hours (unsafe); two-flop = 10^18 years (safe).
- ✅ **Latch avoidance** — every `always @(*)` must have complete `if/else` and `case default`.
- ✅ **Blocking (`=`) for combinational**; **Non-blocking (`<=`) for sequential**. Never mix.
- ✅ **Sensitivity list** — use `always @(*)` for combinational, not explicit port lists.
- ✅ **No `initial`, no `#` delays, no `real` types** in synthesizable RTL.
- ✅ **Every CDC signal must be tagged** and verified with formal CDC tools.
- ✅ **Reset recovery and removal times** must be < $T_{clk}$ — use AASD to avoid this.
- ✅ **Gray code** — Hamming distance = 1, used in FIFO pointers, one bit changes at a time.
- ✅ **Code must be lint-clean** (zero warnings) before synthesis sign-off.
- ✅ **Synthesis–simulation mismatch sources** — `initial`, `force`, `real`, `#` delays, `fork/join`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Guidelines for Clocks and Resets - Mixed Clock Edges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT602 - SYSTEM ON CHIP DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Guidelines for Clocks and Resets - Mixed Clock Edges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **System on Chip (SoC) Design** under the synchronous design methodology prescribed for digital VLSI, **Mixed Clock Edges** (also termed *Dual-Edge Clocking* or *Mixed-Edge Triggering*) refers to a clocking style in which a single clock signal is used to trigger sequential elements (flip-flops or latches) on **both its rising (posedge) and falling (negedge) edges**. Formally, if a clock signal is denoted as `CLK`, the design employs both sampling instants `t = T/4` (rising edge at mid-supply crossing) and `t = 3T/4` (falling edge), where `T` is the nominal clock period.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Mixed-edge clocking is a *specialized clocking style*. KTU examiners specifically test whether the student can identify its **trade-offs** between throughput/density vs. timing closure complexity. A correct answer MUST mention both the advantage (halving the clock frequency for a given data rate, or doubling throughput) and the disadvantage (half-cycle timing paths, glitch sensitivity, complex reset alignment).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive View)

Imagine a **revolving door at a hotel entrance**:

- **Single-edge clocking (conventional):** People only enter when the door is at the *12 o'clock position* (rising edge). Each rotation lets in exactly one batch. The system is simple to control but throughput is limited to one batch per rotation.
- **Mixed-edge clocking:** People enter at both the *12 o'clock* (rising) AND *6 o'clock* (falling) positions. The door still rotates at the same speed, but **double the people** get through per revolution. However, the door operator (timing analyzer / static timing tool) must now precisely know *which half* of the rotation each person belongs to — making control much more delicate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Mixed-edge clocking effectively gives the *appearance* of operating at `2f` while the physical clock still runs at `f`. This is the primary reason designers are tempted to use it, especially in low-power SoC blocks like DDR PHY interfaces, serializer/deserializer (SerDes) pre-divider stages, and certain DSP datapaths.

## 1.3 Physical and Standard Metrics

- **Setup Time ($t_{su}$):** Typically **0.1 ns to 0.5 ns** in modern 28nm/16nm SoC nodes.
- **Hold Time ($t_{h}$):** Typically **0.05 ns to 0.2 ns**.
- **Clock-to-Q Delay ($t_{cq}$):** **0.05 ns to 0.3 ns**.
- **Clock Duty Cycle Target:** **50% ± 2%** — *absolutely critical* for mixed-edge designs (asymmetry directly translates to timing skew).
- **Jitter Tolerance:** **±5%** of clock period for synchronous digital blocks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Why 50% duty cycle is non-negotiable in mixed-edge design:** If `CLK_HIGH ≠ CLK_LOW`, the two edges occur at unequal intervals, breaking the symmetry assumption underlying all half-cycle timing analysis. This is a top-3 reason for silicon re-spins in mixed-edge designs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown: How Mixed-Edge Clocking Works

The mechanism can be decomposed into the following structured logic steps:

1. **Clock Distribution:** A single master clock `CLK` is routed through a balanced clock tree (H-tree or mesh) to all sequential elements. The clock tree is engineered to deliver a *matched duty cycle* (50:50) at every leaf pin.

2. **Edge Selection per FF:** Each flip-flop is intentionally configured to trigger on either `posedge CLK` OR `negedge CLK`. In RTL (Verilog), this is expressed as:
   - `always @(posedge clk) q <= d;` → rising-edge FF
   - `always @(negedge clk) q <= d;` → falling-edge FF

3. **Data Transfer Mechanics:**
   - A rising-edge FF launches data at time `t = 0`.
   - A falling-edge FF must capture that data at time `t = T/2` (half a clock period later).
   - Conversely, a falling-edge FF launches data at `t = T/2`, which a rising-edge FF captures at `t = T`.

4. **Timing Path Classification:** Three path types emerge:
   - **Half-cycle path (pos-to-neg or neg-to-pos):** Available slack is `T/2` instead of `T`.
   - **Full-cycle path (pos-to-pos or neg-to-neg):** Available slack is `T`.
   - **Mixed paths** crossing between the two — the most error-prone.

5. **Reset Considerations:** Reset deassertion must be synchronized to the *correct* edge for each FF type, otherwise some flip-flops exit reset one half-cycle earlier/later than others → functional bugs.

6. **STA Tool Implications:** Static Timing Analysis (STA) tools (e.g., Synopsys PrimeTime, Cadence Tempus) must be explicitly told to analyze *both* posedge and negedge constraints, with the correct `-waveform` argument in the SDC.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Explained

- **WHY use mixed edges?** To achieve *double data rate (DDR)* without doubling the clock frequency, saving power and avoiding PLL frequency-multiplication overhead. It is also used in **source-synchronous interfaces** (DDR memory, LVDS) where data is center-aligned and sampled on both edges.

- **HOW does it work physically?** The clock signal has two usable edges per period. By triggering some FFs on one edge and others on the opposite, the data throughput is effectively doubled. The flip-flop simply uses an *edge-detector circuit* internally — either an inverter-delay-based rising detector or a falling-edge detector built from clock-bar.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Concept | Formula / Condition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Effective Throughput | $f_{eff} = 2 \cdot f_{clk}$ | Samples/second |
| Half-cycle path slack | $T_{HC} = \frac{T_{clk}}{2} - t_{cq} - t_{logic} - t_{su}$ | seconds |
| Full-cycle path slack | $T_{FC} = T_{clk} - t_{cq} - t_{logic} - t_{su}$ | seconds |
| Maximum logic delay (half-cycle) | $t_{logic,max} = \frac{T_{clk}}{2} - t_{cq} - t_{su} - t_{skew}$ | seconds |
| Maximum logic delay (full-cycle) | $t_{logic,max} = T_{clk} - t_{cq} - t_{su} - t_{skew}$ | seconds |
| Maximum operating frequency (mixed) | $f_{max} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot (t_{cq} + t_{logic,max} + t_{su})}$ | Hz |
| Duty cycle tolerance | $\Delta_{DC} = \vert 0.5 - DC_{actual} \vert \leq 0.02$ | dimensionless |
| Reset skew budget | $t_{rst,skew} \leq \frac{T_{clk}}{4}$ | seconds |
| Hold margin (half-cycle) | $t_{hold,margin} = t_{cq} + t_{skew} - t_{h}$ | must be $\geq 0$ |
| SDC waveform specification | `create_clock -name CLK -period 10 -waveform {0 5}` | ns |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** In a mixed-edge design, the hold-time check is often *harder to meet* than the setup-time check, because the data launch-to-capture window is only `T/2`. The `t_{cq} + t_{skew} > t_h` inequality can easily be violated by slow clock buffers.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Mixed-edge clocking is foundational in:

- **DDR SDRAM Controllers** (DDR3/4/5) — data is sampled on both edges to achieve 2× the per-pin bandwidth.
- **LVDS / SerDes Deserializers** — incoming serial bit stream is captured on both edges at half the bit-rate clock.
- **High-speed ADC interfaces** — sub-sampling on both edges doubles effective sample rate.
- **Display controllers (MIPI-DSI, HDMI)** — pixel data is latched on both clock edges.
- **RF baseband processors** — for I/Q channel multiplexing at reduced clock frequencies (saves power).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Operating Frequency for a Mixed-Edge Half-Cycle Path

We will exhaustively derive the maximum frequency of operation for a pipeline stage where data is launched by a rising-edge FF and captured by a falling-edge FF (the worst-case scenario in mixed-edge design).

**Step 1: Identify the timing arc.**
A rising-edge FF `FF1` launches data at time `t = 0` (rising edge of `CLK`). The next falling-edge FF `FF2` is scheduled to capture at `t = T/2` (falling edge). The combinational logic delay between them is `t_logic`.

**Step 2: Express the data arrival time at FF2.**
The data arrives at the D-pin of FF2 at:

$$
t_{arrival} = t_{cq,FF1} + t_{logic} + t_{skew}
$$

where `t_cq,FF1` is the clock-to-Q delay of the launching flip-flop, and `t_skew` accounts for any timing difference between the two clock pins.

**Step 3: Express the data required time at FF2.**
The data is required to be stable at FF2 *before* the falling-edge capture instant, specifically by at least the setup time `t_su`:

$$
t_{required} = \frac{T_{clk}}{2} - t_{su}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the setup-time inequality.**
For correct capture, we must have:

$$
t_{arrival} \leq t_{required}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute the expressions.**

$$
t_{cq,FF1} + t_{logic} + t_{skew} \leq \frac{T_{clk}}{2} - t_{su}
$$

**Step 6: Isolate $t_{logic}$ to find the maximum allowed logic delay.**

$$
t_{logic} \leq \frac{T_{clk}}{2} - t_{su} - t_{cq,FF1} - t_{skew}
$$

**Step 7: Solve for $T_{clk}$ to find the minimum clock period.**

$$
T_{clk,min} = 2 \cdot (t_{cq,FF1} + t_{logic} + t_{su} + t_{skew})
$$

**Step 8: Convert to maximum frequency.**

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{clk,min}} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot (t_{cq,FF1} + t_{logic} + t_{su} + t_{skew})}
$$

**Step 9: Compare to single-edge (full-cycle) design.**
For a conventional full-cycle path:

$$
f_{max,full} = \frac{1}{t_{cq} + t_{logic} + t_{su} + t_{skew}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
f_{max,mixed} = \frac{f_{max,full}}{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** This proves a fundamental KTU result — a mixed-edge design running at frequency `f` is *timing-equivalent* to a single-edge design running at `2f`. The mixed-edge approach *does not magically provide more timing margin*; it merely shifts the timing budget to operate at half the clock frequency, which can be beneficial for low-power applications.

## 3.2 Derivation: Hold-Time Margin for Mixed-Edge Half-Cycle Paths

The hold-time check ensures that the data launched by FF1 does not change *too quickly* and corrupt the value being captured at FF2.

**Step 1: Define the data-change time at FF2.**
The data at the D-pin of FF2 begins to change at:

$$
t_{change} = t_{cq,FF1} + t_{logic} + t_{skew}
$$

**Step 2: Define the hold-time requirement window at FF2.**
The data must remain stable until:

$$
t_{hold,end} = \frac{T_{clk}}{2} + t_{h}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the hold-time inequality.**

$$
t_{change} \geq t_{hold,end}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute.**

$$
t_{cq,FF1} + t_{logic} + t_{skew} \geq \frac{T_{clk}}{2} + t_{h}
$$

**Step 5: Rearrange to isolate the hold margin.**

$$
Margin_{hold} = t_{cq,FF1} + t_{logic} + t_{skew} - \frac{T_{clk}}{2} - t_{h} \geq 0
$$

**Step 6: Identify the problematic case.**
When $T_{clk}$ is large (low-frequency operation) and $t_{logic}$ is small (e.g., a simple wire or buffer), the inequality can be violated. This is a *classic* mixed-edge design failure mode.

**Step 7: Mitigations.**
- Insert *delay buffers* deliberately along the data path to enlarge $t_{logic}$.
- Use *clock skew* engineering (deliberately delaying the capture clock) to increase $t_{skew}$.
- Avoid *short paths* between posedge and negedge flip-flops — this is the **golden rule** of mixed-edge design.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often incorrectly assume hold-time checks are easier at lower frequencies. In *mixed-edge* designs, the opposite is true for short combinational paths. This is a frequently-tested concept.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A mixed-edge SoC block has $t_{cq} = 0.2$ ns, $t_{su} = 0.15$ ns, $t_{h} = 0.1$ ns, and $t_{skew} = 0.05$ ns. The combinational logic delay is $t_{logic} = 0.8$ ns. The clock period is $T_{clk} = 2$ ns with 50% duty cycle. A rising-edge FF launches data into a falling-edge FF.

**Step 1: Check setup-time slack.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Slack_{setup} &= \frac{T_{clk}}{2} - t_{su} - t_{cq} - t_{logic} - t_{skew} \\
&= \frac{2}{2} - 0.15 - 0.2 - 0.8 - 0.05 \\
&= 1.0 - 1.20 \\
&= -0.20 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Interpret the result.**
A *negative* slack of `-0.20 ns` indicates a **setup-time violation**. The design will not work at this clock frequency.

**Step 3: Find the minimum allowable clock period.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{clk,min} &= 2 \cdot (t_{cq} + t_{logic} + t_{su} + t_{skew}) \\
&= 2 \cdot (0.2 + 0.8 + 0.15 + 0.05) \\
&= 2 \cdot 1.20 \\
&= 2.40 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the maximum operating frequency.**

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{2.40 \text{ ns}} = 416.67 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 5: Check hold-time margin.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Margin_{hold} &= t_{cq} + t_{logic} + t_{skew} - \frac{T_{clk}}{2} - t_{h} \\
&= 0.2 + 0.8 + 0.05 - 1.0 - 0.1 \\
&= 1.05 - 1.10 \\
&= -0.05 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

A negative hold margin — the design has a hold violation too! This necessitates inserting a delay buffer of at least 0.05 ns in the data path.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** This example demonstrates that mixed-edge designs are *inherently timing-sensitive* on both setup and hold, making them challenging to close in SoC integration.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Mixed-Edge Clocking Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Oscillator PLL 416MHz] --> nodeB[Clock Tree Buffer H-Tree]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Clock Net 50 percent Duty Cycle]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Posedge FF1 Register A]
```
