```mermaid
    nodeC --> nodeE[Negedge FF2 Register B]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Posedge FF3 Register C]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Negedge FF4 Register D]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Combinational Logic Block MUX Adder]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Combinational Logic Block Comparator]
    nodeF --> nodeJ[Combinational Logic Block Shifter]
    nodeG --> nodeK[Combinational Logic Block Accumulator]
    nodeH --> nodeE
    nodeI --> nodeF
    nodeJ --> nodeG
    nodeK --> nodeL[Output Stage Serializer]
    nodeL --> nodeM[SoC Output Pin DDR Interface]
    nodeN[Reset Synchronizer Block] --> nodeD
    nodeN --> nodeE
    nodeN --> nodeF
    nodeN --> nodeG
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Timing Diagram: Mixed-Edge Sampling Behavior

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Clock CLK] --> nodeB[Rising Edge at t=0 FF1 launches Data_A]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Falling Edge at t=T/2 FF2 captures Data_A]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Next Rising Edge at t=T FF3 captures Data_B from FF2]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Next Falling Edge at t=3T/2 FF4 captures Data_C from FF3]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Data Path A to FF2 Half-Cycle Path]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Data Path B to FF3 Full-Cycle Path]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Data Path C to FF4 Half-Cycle Path]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Flow: When to Use Mixed-Edge Clocking

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Design Requirement: 2x Data Rate Needed] --> nodeB{Is Source Synchronous Interface}
    nodeB --> nodeYes[Yes DDR LVDS MIPI]
    nodeB --> nodeNo[No Internal SoC Logic]
    nodeYes --> nodeC[Recommended: Mixed Edge Clocking]
    nodeNo --> nodeD{Is Power Critical and Frequency Headroom Available}
    nodeD --> nodeYes2[Yes Low Power IoT Wearable]
    nodeD --> nodeNo2[No Performance Critical]
    nodeYes2 --> nodeC
    nodeNo2 --> nodeE[Recommended: Single Edge Standard Clocking]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Implement Duty Cycle Correction PLL]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Apply Reset Synchronization Per Edge]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Run STA with Half-Cycle Path Constraints]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Final Mixed Edge SoC Block]
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeE --> nodeJ[Standard Single Edge SoC Block]
```

## 4.4 Architecture Block Diagram: Reset Distribution in Mixed-Edge Design

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Power-On Reset POR Source] --> nodeB[Reset Synchronizer FF0 Posedge]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Reset Tree Balanced Skew Network]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Posedge FF Group A Reset on Clock High]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Negedge FF Group B Reset on Clock Low]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Posedge FF Group C Reset on Clock High]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Negedge FF Group D Reset on Clock Low]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Logic Operates in Phase 0 to T/2]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Logic Operates in Phase T/2 to T]
    nodeF --> nodeJ[Logic Operates in Phase 0 to T/2]
    nodeG --> nodeK[Logic Operates in Phase T/2 to T]
    nodeH --> nodeL[Re-synchronize Outputs at Domain Crossing]
    nodeI --> nodeL
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeL
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define mixed-edge clocking. Mention any two situations where it is preferred in SoC design.**

**Model Answer:**
Mixed-edge clocking is a clocking methodology in which sequential elements (flip-flops) within a single clock domain are triggered on *both* the rising and falling edges of the same clock signal, effectively doubling the data throughput without increasing the clock frequency.

**Two preferred situations:**
1. **Source-synchronous interfaces** such as DDR SDRAM, LVDS, and MIPI-DSI, where the incoming data is center-aligned and the receiver samples on both clock edges to achieve double data rate.
2. **Low-power SoC blocks** (e.g., IoT sensor hubs, wearable processors) where power reduction is prioritized; operating at half the clock frequency with mixed edges consumes less dynamic power than operating at double the frequency with single edges.

*(Examiner's note: A complete answer should also mention the *2× throughput per Hz* property. Award full 3 marks only if both situations are technically justified, not just listed.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List any three guidelines / precautions that a designer must follow when implementing a mixed-edge clocking scheme in an SoC.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Strict 50% duty cycle maintenance:** The clock tree must be balanced and a duty-cycle correction (DCC) circuit should be employed, because any asymmetry between the high and low phases directly translates into half-cycle path timing skew.
2. **Careful reset synchronization:** The reset deassertion edge must be aligned with the active edge of each flip-flop; otherwise, some flip-flops exit reset one half-cycle earlier than others, leading to functional errors.
3. **Avoid short combinational paths between posedge and negedge flip-flops:** Such paths frequently fail hold-time checks in mixed-edge designs, requiring deliberate insertion of delay buffers to enlarge the data path delay.

*(Examiner's note: Half-page answer expected. A 4th point about STA constraints is also acceptable.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**a)** Explain the concept of mixed-edge clocking with a clear architectural block diagram. Compare it with conventional single-edge clocking in terms of throughput, power, and timing complexity. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A mixed-edge SoC datapath has the following parameters: $t_{cq} = 0.25$ ns, $t_{su} = 0.2$ ns, $t_{h} = 0.08$ ns, combinational logic delay $t_{logic} = 0.6$ ns, and clock-tree skew $t_{skew} = 0.05$ ns. The clock has a period of $T_{clk} = 2$ ns with a 50% duty cycle. Data flows from a rising-edge FF to a falling-edge FF.
   (i) Determine whether the design meets the setup-time constraint.
   (ii) If not, calculate the minimum clock period and the corresponding maximum operating frequency.
   (iii) Check the hold-time margin and recommend a remedy if violated. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Concept and Comparison (7 marks):**

**Concept (3 marks):**
Mixed-edge clocking is a digital design technique in which flip-flops within a single clock domain are triggered on *both* the rising edge (posedge) and falling edge (negedge) of the same clock `CLK`. This effectively doubles the number of data transactions per clock cycle without requiring a higher-frequency clock source. A block diagram is shown below in conceptual form:

```
CLK ---> [Posedge FF] ---> [Combinational Logic] ---> [Negedge FF] ---> next stage
CLK ---> [Negedge FF] ---> [Combinational Logic] ---> [Posedge FF] ---> next stage
```

**Comparison (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Single-Edge Clocking | Mixed-Edge Clocking |
|---|---|---|
| Throughput per Hz | 1 sample/cycle | 2 samples/cycle |
| Clock Frequency Required | $f$ for rate $R$ | $f/2$ for rate $R$ |
| Dynamic Power | Higher (more toggling at high $f$) | Lower (operates at $f/2$) |
| Timing Paths | All full-cycle | Mix of full-cycle and half-cycle |
| Hold-Time Closure | Easier (long $T$) | Harder (short $T/2$ window) |
| Reset Complexity | Standard | Edge-aware, per-FF |
| STA Complexity | Simpler | Requires explicit edge constraints |
| Area | Larger (more pipeline stages for same throughput) | Smaller (fewer pipeline stages) |

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Analysis (7 marks):**

**(i) Setup-time check (2 marks):**

For a rising-edge FF launching into a falling-edge FF, the available timing window is $T_{clk}/2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{clk}/2 &= 2.0 / 2 = 1.0 \text{ ns} \\
\text{Required slack} &= t_{cq} + t_{logic} + t_{skew} + t_{su} \\
&= 0.25 + 0.6 + 0.05 + 0.20 \\
&= 1.10 \text{ ns} \\
\text{Available time} &= 1.0 \text{ ns} \\
\text{Slack} &= 1.0 - 1.10 = -0.10 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result: Setup-time VIOLATION of 0.10 ns.** ❌

**(ii) Minimum clock period and maximum frequency (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{clk,min} &= 2 \cdot (t_{cq} + t_{logic} + t_{su} + t_{skew}) \\
&= 2 \cdot (0.25 + 0.6 + 0.20 + 0.05) \\
&= 2 \cdot 1.10 \\
&= 2.20 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{2.20 \text{ ns}} = 454.5 \text{ MHz}
$$

**(iii) Hold-time check and remedy (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Margin_{hold} &= t_{cq} + t_{logic} + t_{skew} - \frac{T_{clk}}{2} - t_{h} \\
&= 0.25 + 0.60 + 0.05 - 1.0 - 0.08 \\
&= 0.90 - 1.08 \\
&= -0.18 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result: Hold-time VIOLATION of 0.18 ns.** ❌

**Remedy:** Insert a delay buffer of at least **0.18 ns** (plus a 0.02 ns margin → recommend 0.2 ns) in the data path between the rising-edge FF and the falling-edge FF. This increases the effective $t_{logic}$ to $0.6 + 0.2 = 0.8$ ns, which satisfies:

$$
Margin_{hold} = 0.25 + 0.80 + 0.05 - 1.0 - 0.08 = 0.02 \text{ ns} \geq 0 \checkmark
$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**a)** With the help of a timing diagram, explain how a half-cycle timing path arises in a mixed-edge clocking scheme. Why is the hold-time check more critical in mixed-edge designs compared to single-edge designs? **(7 marks)**

**b)** An SoC block uses mixed-edge clocking with a 200 MHz clock. The flip-flops have $t_{cq,max} = 0.3$ ns and $t_{su} = 0.18$ ns. The clock tree has a maximum skew of 0.08 ns. Calculate:
   (i) The maximum combinational logic delay permissible in a half-cycle path.
   (ii) The maximum combinational logic delay permissible in a full-cycle path within the same block.
   (iii) If the actual logic delay is 0.7 ns, will the half-cycle path work? If not, suggest a clock-frequency adjustment. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Half-Cycle Path & Hold-Time Criticality (7 marks):**

**Half-cycle timing path (3 marks):**
A half-cycle timing path arises when a rising-edge FF launches data and a falling-edge FF captures it (or vice versa) within the *same* clock domain. The data must traverse the combinational logic and arrive at the capture FF in only **half a clock period** ($T/2$), as opposed to a full period in conventional designs.

**Textual timing diagram:**

| Time | t=0 | t=T/2 | t=T | t=3T/2 | t=2T |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CLK | ↑ rising | ↓ falling | ↑ rising | ↓ falling | ↑ rising |
| FF1 (posedge) Q | data launched | holds | new data | holds | new data |
| FF2 (negedge) Q | previous | captures FF1 data | holds | captures new | holds |
| D-pin of FF2 | unstable | must be stable by t_su before T/2 | — | — | — |

The data path from FF1 → logic → FF2 must complete in `T/2 - t_su`.

**Hold-time criticality (4 marks):**
In single-edge designs, the hold-time check window is `t_cq + t_skew > t_h` and the *capturing edge* is a full `T` away from the *launching edge*. In mixed-edge designs, the capture happens only `T/2` later. For *short* data paths (small $t_{logic}$), the new data from the *next* launching edge can race in and corrupt the currently-capturing data, creating a hold violation. The shorter the available window, the *more* vulnerable the design is to hold failures. Mitigation requires buffer insertion, careful clock skew engineering, and avoiding direct posedge-to-negedge FF connections on short paths.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computations (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $f = 200$ MHz → $T_{clk} = 1/200\text{ MHz} = 5$ ns
- $t_{cq,max} = 0.3$ ns
- $t_{su} = 0.18$ ns
- $t_{skew} = 0.08$ ns

**(i) Max logic delay for half-cycle path (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{logic,max,HC} &= \frac{T_{clk}}{2} - t_{su} - t_{cq,max} - t_{skew} \\
&= \frac{5.0}{2} - 0.18 - 0.30 - 0.08 \\
&= 2.5 - 0.56 \\
&= 1.94 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Max logic delay for full-cycle path (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{logic,max,FC} &= T_{clk} - t_{su} - t_{cq,max} - t_{skew} \\
&= 5.0 - 0.18 - 0.30 - 0.08 \\
&= 4.44 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Feasibility check at 200 MHz and frequency adjustment (2 marks):**

The actual logic delay is 0.7 ns, which is **less than** 1.94 ns → setup-time check **PASSES**. ✅

However, checking the hold-time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Margin_{hold} &= t_{cq,max} + t_{logic} + t_{skew} - \frac{T_{clk}}{2} - t_{h} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Without a specified $t_h$, the textbook estimate $t_h \approx 0.1 \cdot t_{su} = 0.018$ ns yields:

$$
Margin_{hold} = 0.30 + 0.70 + 0.08 - 2.5 - 0.018 = -1.438 \text{ ns}
$$

**Huge hold violation.** This is the typical *mixed-edge trap*: setup is fine, but hold is severely violated.

**Recommended adjustment:** Since hold-time is independent of clock frequency, *reducing the clock frequency does NOT fix the hold violation*. The correct remedy is:
1. Insert a delay buffer of ≥ 1.44 ns in the half-cycle data path, OR
2. Add a 1.44 ns delay-skew cell on the clock to skew the capture, OR
3. Restructure the path as a *full-cycle* path by replacing the falling-edge FF with a rising-edge FF and adding an extra pipeline stage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Most common mistake (lose 2-3 marks):** Students answer the "mixed-edge clocking definition" question as merely "using posedge and negedge" without explaining the *consequence* (half-cycle paths, throughput doubling, hold-time risk). Always quantify the throughput.
> 2. **Pitfall #2 (lose 2 marks):** In numerical problems, students forget the factor of `2` in the half-cycle timing formula. The window is $T/2$, NOT $T$.
> 3. **Pitfall #3 (lose 2 marks):** Students assume that reducing clock frequency fixes hold-time violations. **It does not.** Hold-time is frequency-independent.
> 4. **Pitfall #4 (lose 1-2 marks):** In reset-related mixed-edge questions, students forget that asynchronous reset deassertion must be synchronized *separately* to each clock-edge group.
> 5. **Pitfall #5 (lose 1 mark):** Failure to mention duty-cycle sensitivity in the answer — KTU examiners specifically test this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — Print This Section Before Exam**

- ✅ **Mixed-edge clocking** = triggering FFs on *both* rising and falling edges of the same `CLK`.
- ✅ Doubles *effective data throughput* without increasing clock frequency.
- ✅ **Half-cycle path** = posedge FF → negedge FF (or vice versa); timing budget = $T/2$.
- ✅ **Full-cycle path** = posedge-to-posedge (or negedge-to-negedge); timing budget = $T$.
- ✅ Maximum frequency formula: $f_{max} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot (t_{cq} + t_{logic} + t_{su} + t_{skew})}$.
- ✅ **50% duty cycle is MANDATORY** — any skew between high/low phases directly violates timing.
- ✅ **Hold-time check is the #1 failure mode** in mixed-edge designs, especially for short data paths.
- ✅ **Reset must be edge-synchronized** — posedge FFs need posedge-aligned reset deassertion; negedge FFs need negedge-aligned deassertion.
- ✅ STA tool must be configured with explicit `-waveform` and half-cycle path constraints in the SDC file.
- ✅ Common applications: **DDR SDRAM, LVDS, MIPI-DSI, SerDes, low-power IoT SoCs**.
- ✅ Trade-off: saves power & area ↔ adds timing complexity & reset synchronization burden.
- ✅ Buffer insertion is the standard fix for hold-time violations in mixed-edge designs.
- ✅ Reducing clock frequency does NOT fix hold-time violations — it only helps setup-time.
- ✅ Mixed-edge clocking is *not* the same as *multi-clock domain* design — they are distinct concepts often confused by students.
- ✅ In KTU exams, always state both the *advantage* (throughput/power) AND the *disadvantage* (timing/reset complexity) for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clock Buffers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT602 - SYSTEM ON CHIP DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Clock Buffers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Clock Buffer** is a specialized high-drive-strength amplifier circuit inserted in the clock distribution network of a synchronous VLSI system-on-chip (SoC) to regenerate, isolate, and fan-out the global clock signal from a phase-locked loop (PLL) or clock generator to thousands of sequential elements (flip-flops, latches, SRAMs) with controlled **skew**, **jitter**, and **insertion delay**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 3 – Clock Distribution):**
> A clock buffer is a matched, tapered repeater inserted at strategic nodes of a clock tree to (i) restore logic levels degraded by RC interconnect loading, (ii) provide large fan-out (typ. $\ge 32$), (iii) equalize path delays (minimize skew), and (iv) isolate the clock source from load variations. They form the building blocks of **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)** and **Clock Distribution Networks (CDN)**.

Mathematically, a clock buffer can be modeled as a tapered chain of inverters (or non-inverting buffers) where each stage is sized up by a factor $f$ to drive a target load $C_L$:

$$
f = \left(\frac{C_L}{C_{in}}\right)^{1/N}
$$

where $C_{in}$ is the input capacitance of the first stage and $N$ is the number of buffer stages.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Distribution Network"

Imagine a city water supply system:
- The **reservoir (dam)** is the **PLL / clock source** — produces the clean, low-power "wave" (clock edge).
- **Small pipes** leading from the dam can only carry water to a few houses before pressure drops.
- A **clock buffer** is like a **pumping station** placed at every junction. It re-pressurizes the flow so that every household (flip-flop), no matter how far, receives water (clock edges) at almost the same time.
- The **equalizing tank at the end** is like a **zero-delay buffer** — it ensures simultaneous delivery to all end points.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use one giant wire from the source?**
> In deep-submicron SoCs (e.g., 28 nm, 7 nm), a single global wire from PLL to all flip-flops would have a resistance of several kΩ, an RC delay of nanoseconds, and severe **electromigration**. Buffers break this into short, manageable segments — just like relay pumps along a pipeline.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Must Know for KTU)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Clock Skew** | $t_{skew}$ | **20 – 100 ps** | Max – min arrival time of clock edge across sinks |
| **Clock Jitter** | $t_{jitter}$ | **5 – 50 ps rms** | Cycle-to-cycle temporal uncertainty of the edge |
| **Insertion Delay** | $t_d$ | **100 – 500 ps** | Latency from source to a representative sink |
| **Duty Cycle** | $D$ | **50 % $\pm$ 2 %** | Ratio of high time to total period |
| **Fan-out** | $FO$ | **$\ge$ 32** | Number of loads a buffer can drive at rated speed |
| **Power** | $P_{clk}$ | **30 – 40 % of $P_{total}$** | Power dissipated by the entire clock network |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles — The "Why" Behind Clock Buffers

A clock buffer must satisfy four simultaneous design objectives:

1. **Signal Restoration (Level Repair):** Long interconnects attenuate the clock waveform due to RC low-pass filtering. The buffer re-sharpens edges, restoring full-swing $V_{DD}$ to $0$ transition.
2. **Fan-out Amplification:** A minimum-sized inverter from the PLL can drive only a few fF of load. Buffers taper this drive to drive pF-level wire+gate loads.
3. **Skew Minimization:** Buffers are placed symmetrically (H-tree, X-tree, fishbone) to make every path from source to flip-flop have **equal electrical length**.
4. **Isolation (Decoupling):** Buffers prevent load-side switching noise from propagating back into the PLL (which would cause jitter and frequency drift).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners love asking *"Why is a clock buffer placed at every branch point of the clock tree?"* — answer: to *isolate*, *re-buffer*, and *equalize* delays.

## 2.2 Hierarchy of the Clock Distribution Network

Modern SoCs use a **multi-level buffer hierarchy**:

```
Level-0  : Source (PLL)                 — 1 driver
Level-1  : Pre-buffer (Local PLL driver)  — 1–4 outputs
Level-2  : Global buffer                 — drives H/V spines
Level-3  : Regional/Leaf buffer          — drives clock grids
Level-4  : Local buffer / gaters         — final fan-out to FFs
```

Each upward level increases drive strength and current capacity by 2× to 4× per stage.

## 2.3 Types of Clock Buffers (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| Type | Schematic Symbol | Function | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Non-Inverting Buffer** | `BUF` | $A \rightarrow A$ | Most common leaf buffer |
| **Inverting Buffer** | `INV` | $A \rightarrow \overline{A}$ | Used in pair to form non-inverting |
| **Differential Buffer** | `BUFx2_DIFF` | LVDS / CML levels | High-noise analog blocks, SerDes |
| **Clock Gating Buffer (ICG)** | `BUFx + AND/LATCH` | Enables/disables clock | Power gating, low-power modes |
| **Zero-Delay Buffer (ZDB)** | `BUFx_ZD` | Source-synchronous output | FPGA, DDR memory interfaces |
| **Balanced Buffer** | `BUF_BAL` | Matched rise/fall | Duty-cycle correction |
| **Level Shifter Buffer** | `LS_BUF` | $V_{DDL} \leftrightarrow V_{DDH}$ | Multi-rail SoCs |

## 2.4 Clock Tree Topologies

| Topology | Skew | Power | Area | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **H-Tree** | Very Low | High | High | CPU cores, GPU |
| **X-Tree** | Low | Med | Med | Network SoC |
| **Fishbone (spine)** | Med | Med | Low | ASIC, MCU |
| **Clock Grid (Mesh)** | Ultra-Low | Very High | Very High | High-perf CPUs |
| **Multi-source CTS** | Very Low | High | Med | Modern ASICs |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU-board-favorite. Memorize the symbol mapping — they often swap variables in problems.**

### A. Buffer Delay (Logical Effort Method)

The delay of a single inverter/buffer stage:

$$
t_{pd} = \tau \cdot (g \cdot h + p)
$$

where $\tau$ = process technology constant, $g$ = logical effort (= 1 for inverter), $h = C_{out}/C_{in}$, $p$ = parasitic delay.

### B. Optimal Stage Ratio (Tapered Buffer)

$$
f_{opt} = e \approx 2.718 \quad \text{(for minimum delay chain)}
$$

Number of stages for minimum delay:

$$
N_{opt} = \frac{\ln(C_L / C_{in})}{\ln(f_{opt})} = \frac{\ln(C_L / C_{in})}{1}
$$

### C. Skew and Jitter

$$
t_{skew} = \max_{i,j} \mid t_{d,i} - t_{d,j} \mid
$$

$$
\sigma_{skew} = \sqrt{\sum_{k} \sigma_{k}^{2}} \quad \text{(root-sum-square along path)}
$$

### D. Power Dissipation of Clock Network

$$
P_{clk} = \alpha \cdot C_{total} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}
$$

Typical $\alpha$ (activity factor) for clock = **1** (toggles every cycle).

### E. Hold-Time Margin Equation (Buffer Insertion for Hold Fixing)

$$
t_{hold,slack} = t_{cd,q} + t_{logic} - (t_{skew} + t_{hold,FF})
$$

A buffer with delay $t_{buf}$ inserted on the fast path satisfies hold if:

$$
t_{buf} \ge t_{hold,FF} - t_{cd,q} - t_{logic} + t_{skew}
$$

### F. Useful-Skew Bound (Setup Constraint)

$$
t_{skew} \le T_{clk} - (t_{cq} + t_{logic,max} + t_{su})
$$

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Clock buffers underpin every synchronous digital system:
- **Microprocessors (Intel/AMD):** Use grid distributions with hundreds of thousands of buffers.
- **Mobile SoCs (Snapphone, Dimensity):** Use clock gating with ICG cells to save power.
- **FPGA (Xilinx/Intel):** Use dedicated global clock buffers (`BUFG`, `BUFH`, `BUFR`) with hardwired routes.
- **DDR Memory Interfaces:** Use zero-delay buffers to phase-align external DRAM clock.
- **SerDes (PCIe, USB):** Use differential clock buffers with low-jitter PLLs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimal Buffer Sizing for a Long Wire (Bakoglu's Method)

**Problem Setup:**
A clock wire of length $L$ has total capacitance $C_w = c \cdot L$ and total resistance $R_w = r \cdot L$, where $c$ and $r$ are per-unit-length parameters. The wire is driven by a chain of $N$ identical inverters each of size $s_i$ (relative to minimum).

**Step 1:** Treat one inverter + the next wire segment as a repeating $\pi$-model. The Elmore delay from input of $k$-th stage to output of $(k+1)$-th stage is:

$$
t_{d,k} = R_{tr,k} \cdot C_{w,k} + R_{tr,k} \cdot C_{g,k+1} + R_{w,k} \cdot C_{g,k+1}
$$

**Step 2:** Assuming the wire is divided into $N$ equal segments, and each stage scales by factor $f$:

$$
C_{w,k} = \frac{c L}{N}, \quad C_{g,k+1} = C_{in} \cdot f^{k+1}, \quad R_{tr,k} = \frac{R_{tr,min}}{s_k} = \frac{R_{tr,min}}{f^k}
$$

**Step 3:** Sum total delay over all $N$ stages:

$$
t_d = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \left[\frac{R_{tr,min}}{f^k} \cdot \frac{cL}{N} + \frac{R_{tr,min}}{f^k} \cdot C_{in} f^{k+1} + \frac{rL}{N} \cdot C_{in} f^{k+1}\right]
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify term by term:

$$
t_d = N \cdot R_{tr,min} \cdot C_{in} \cdot f + N \cdot \frac{r L \cdot C_{in} \cdot f}{N} + N \cdot \frac{R_{tr,min} \cdot c L}{N \cdot f^k}
$$

(Detailed summation after factoring the geometric series gives the closed-form Bakoglu result.)

**Step 5:** Closed-form total delay:

$$
t_d = R_{tr,min} \cdot C_{in} \cdot N f + r L \cdot C_{in} \cdot f + R_{tr,min} \cdot c L \cdot \frac{1 - f^{-N}}{f - 1}
$$

**Step 6:** Differentiate with respect to $f$ and set $\partial t_d / \partial f = 0$ to find optimal taper factor. For large $N$, the optimum satisfies:

$$
f_{opt} = 1 + \sqrt{\frac{R_{tr,min} \cdot c}{r \cdot C_{in}}}
$$

**Step 7:** Interpretation: the optimal $f$ balances the **intrinsic capacitance of the driver** against the **wire capacitance**. In typical 28 nm CMOS, $f_{opt} \approx 3$ to $4$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** $f_{opt}$ is the **size ratio between successive inverters** in a tapered clock buffer chain. Choosing $f = e \approx 2.718$ minimizes *logic delay alone*; with wire loading, $f$ grows to 3–4.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Skew from a Symmetric H-Tree

**Setup:** H-tree with 4 levels, $L$ = die dimension. Each level has 4 branches of equal length $L/2$, $L/4$, $L/8$, … and each branch is buffered with an identical clock buffer of intrinsic delay $t_b$ and drives identical wire load $C_w$.

**Step 1:** Total wire length from source to any leaf in an H-tree of $k$ levels:

$$
\ell_{total} = L \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{2}{2^{i-1}} = 2L \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \frac{1}{2^{i}} < 4L
$$

**Step 2:** Per-branch RC delay of level-$i$ segment:

$$
t_{RC,i} = 0.5 \cdot r \cdot \frac{L}{2^{i}} \cdot c \cdot \frac{L}{2^{i}} = \frac{0.5 \, r c L^{2}}{4^{i}}
$$

**Step 3:** Elmore delay from root to any leaf (sum of per-level delays, weighted by downstream capacitance):

$$
t_{d,leaf} = \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} t_{RC,i} + k \cdot t_b = 0.5 \, r c L^{2} \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \frac{1}{4^{i}} + k \cdot t_b
$$

**Step 4:** Use the geometric series identity $\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} (1/4)^i = \frac{1 - (1/4)^k}{1 - 1/4} = \frac{4}{3}\left[1 - 4^{-k}\right]$:

$$
t_{d,leaf} = \frac{2}{3} \, r c L^{2} \left[1 - 4^{-k}\right] + k \cdot t_b
$$

**Step 5:** Since every leaf experiences **identical** electrical path, the **deterministic skew is zero** in an ideal H-tree:

$$
t_{skew} = t_{d,leaf}^{max} - t_{d,leaf}^{min} = 0
$$

**Step 6:** But *process variations* introduce a residual random skew. With buffer delay variance $\sigma_{buf}^2$ per stage and wire delay variance $\sigma_{w}^2$ per level, total skew standard deviation:

$$
\sigma_{skew} = \sqrt{k \cdot \sigma_{buf}^{2} + k \cdot \sigma_{w}^{2}}
$$

**Step 7:** Plug in $k = 4$ (4-level H-tree), $\sigma_{buf} = 5$ ps, $\sigma_w = 3$ ps:

$$
\sigma_{skew} = \sqrt{4 \cdot 25 + 4 \cdot 9} \text{ ps} = \sqrt{136} \approx 11.66 \text{ ps}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** Buffer-induced skew dominates wire-induced skew because buffers are more sensitive to $V_T$ mismatch. KTU exam question: *"Why is clock skew non-zero in a real H-tree despite perfect symmetry?"* — Answer: process, voltage, and temperature (PVT) variations.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hold-Time Fixing via Deliberate Buffer Insertion

**Problem:** Two flip-flops FF1 → FF2 with combinational delay $t_{logic} = 0.4$ ns, clock-Q delay $t_{cq} = 0.1$ ns, hold time of FF2 is $t_{hold} = 0.05$ ns, clock skew $t_{skew} = 0.08$ ns (FF2 clock arrives earlier).

**Step 1:** Compute hold slack without any fix:

$$
t_{slack} = t_{cq} + t_{logic} - t_{skew} - t_{hold}
$$

$$
t_{slack} = 0.1 + 0.4 - 0.08 - 0.05 = 0.37 \text{ ns} > 0 \;\; \text{(HOLD OK)}
$$

**Counter-example:** If $t_{logic} = 0.05$ ns (very short path), $t_{cq} = 0.08$ ns, $t_{skew} = 0.12$ ns, $t_{hold} = 0.05$ ns:

$$
t_{slack} = 0.08 + 0.05 - 0.12 - 0.05 = -0.04 \text{ ns}
$$

Hold violation of 40 ps.

**Step 2:** Required buffer delay on FF1 clock path:

$$
t_{buf,req} = t_{hold} - t_{cq} - t_{logic} + t_{skew} = 0.05 - 0.08 - 0.05 + 0.12 = 0.04 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3:** Select a standard-cell buffer `BUFx2` with $t_{buf} = 35$ ps — insufficient. Choose `BUFx4` with $t_{buf} = 55$ ps → satisfies.

**Step 4:** Re-verify hold slack:

$$
t_{slack}^{new} = 0.08 + 0.05 - (0.12 - 0.055) - 0.05 = 0.015 \text{ ns} > 0
$$

(We *delayed* FF1's clock by 55 ps, effectively reducing the skew by 55 ps.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Inserting a hold-fixing buffer makes **setup worse** by 55 ps. Always check **both** setup and hold after buffer insertion.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Clock Buffer Internal Tapering — Schematic View

```mermaid
graph LR
    src[Clock Source PLL] --> b1[BUF x1 Inverter]
    b1 --> b2[BUF x4 Inverter]
    b2 --> b3[BUF x16 Inverter]
    b3 --> b4[BUF x64 Inverter]
    b4 --> load1[Flip Flop A]
    b4 --> load2[Flip Flop B]
    b4 --> load3[Flip Flop C]
    b4 --> load4[Flip Flop D]
```

**Explanation:** A 4-stage tapered clock buffer chain with sizing ratio $f = 4$ per stage. The fan-out at the leaf is 4 (or 32 in real designs).

## 4.2 Clock Tree Architecture — H-Tree (4 Sinks Shown)

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[PLL Source] --> n1A[Buffer A]
    root --> n1B[Buffer B]
    n1A --> n2A[Buffer A1]
    n1A --> n2B[Buffer A2]
    n1B --> n2C[Buffer B1]
    n1B --> n2D[Buffer B2]
    n2A --> ff1[FF Region 1]
    n2B --> ff2[FF Region 2]
    n2C --> ff3[FF Region 3]
    n2D --> ff4[FF Region 4]
```

**Explanation:** H-tree with 2 levels. The root-to-leaf path is symmetric in *electrical length*, ensuring near-zero deterministic skew.

## 4.3 Integrated Clock Gating (ICG) Cell — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    clk[Global Clock] --> l[Latch EN sampled on neg edge]
    en[Enable Signal] --> l
    l --> and1[AND gate]
    clk --> and1
    and1 --> gatedclk[Gated Clock to FF Bank]
```

**Explanation:** This is the standard "ICG" cell used in low-power SoCs. The latch prevents glitches on the enable from corrupting the clock.

## 4.4 Hold-Fixing Buffer Insertion Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Static Timing Analysis] --> detect{Hold Violation on Path?}
    detect -->|Yes| insert[Insert Delay Buffer on Fast Path]
    detect -->|No| ok[No Action Required]
    insert --> reverify[Re-run STA Setup and Hold]
    reverify --> ok2{Both Met?}
    ok2 -->|No| upsize[Upsize Buffer or Add Stage]
    ok2 -->|Yes| done[Sign-off]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCEVT602]**
**Define a clock buffer. List any four design requirements it must satisfy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A clock buffer is a high-drive-strength, matched amplifier circuit used in the clock distribution network of an SoC to regenerate, fan-out, and equalize delays of the global clock signal feeding sequential elements.

Four design requirements:
1. **High fan-out drive capability** (typ. $\ge 32$).
2. **Low and balanced rise/fall times** to preserve 50 % duty cycle.
3. **Low skew contribution** (matched symmetric layout).
4. **Isolation from load variations** (input/output decoupling).
5. *(bonus – not for mark)* Low jitter, low power.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCEVT602]**
**What is clock skew? How does it differ from clock jitter?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Clock Skew** is the *spatial* (inter-flop) difference in clock arrival times across the chip, defined as:

$$
t_{skew} = t_{d,max} - t_{d,min}
$$

**Clock Jitter** is the *temporal* (cycle-to-cycle) uncertainty of a single clock edge at a given flip-flop, measured as the standard deviation $\sigma_{jitter}$ of the edge arrival time.

| Aspect | Skew | Jitter |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Spatial, deterministic + random | Temporal, random |
| Cause | Path-length mismatch, PVT | PLL noise, supply noise, crosstalk |
| Fix | Symmetric tree, buffer insertion | Better PLL, decoupling, shielding |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCEVT602, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **logical-effort method** for sizing a tapered clock buffer chain driving a load of $C_L = 8$ pF. The minimum input gate capacitance is $C_{in} = 10$ fF. Compute the optimal number of stages $N_{opt}$ and per-stage sizing factor $f$ if the chain must achieve minimum delay.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the operation of an **Integrated Clock Gating (ICG) cell**. Why is the negative-edge-sampled latch essential in an ICG? What happens if it is replaced by a simple AND gate?

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (a) — Tapered Buffer Sizing (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Recap the logical effort delay formula [1 mark]:**

$$
t_{pd} = \tau \cdot N \cdot (g \cdot f + p) \quad \text{with } f = (C_L/C_{in})^{1/N}
$$

**Step 2 — Set up the optimization [1 mark]:**
To minimize delay, we differentiate w.r.t. $N$. For an inverter, logical effort $g = 1$ and parasitic $p = 1$. The optimum per-stage ratio is $f_{opt} = e \approx 2.718$, achieved for a *self-loading* minimum-size inverter.

**Step 3 — Compute the load ratio [1 mark]:**

$$
\frac{C_L}{C_{in}} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-12}}{10 \times 10^{-15}} = 800
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $N_{opt}$ [2 marks]:**

$$
N_{opt} = \frac{\ln(C_L/C_{in})}{\ln(e)} = \ln(800) = 6.685
$$

Round up to the next integer: $\boxed{N_{opt} = 7}$ stages. (If $N = 6$: $f = 800^{1/6} = 2.985$ — slightly above $e$, suboptimal; if $N = 7$: $f = 800^{1/7} = 2.844$, closer to optimum.)

**Step 5 — Sizing factor [1 mark]:**

$$
\boxed{f = (C_L/C_{in})^{1/7} = 800^{1/7} = 2.84}
$$

**Step 6 — Stage sizes [1 mark]:**
Each stage is 2.84× larger than the previous. Final stage size relative to minimum:

$$
s_7 = f^7 = (2.84)^7 \approx 800 \;\; \text{(matches } C_L/C_{in}\text{)}
$$

**Verification of delay [bonus]:** With $N = 7$, $f = 2.84$, $g = 1$, $p = 1$:

$$
t_{pd} = 7 \cdot \tau \cdot (1 \cdot 2.84 + 1) = 7 \cdot 3.84 \cdot \tau = 26.88 \tau
$$

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (b) — ICG Cell (7 marks)**

**Block diagram [3 marks]:**

```
        EN  ─────┐
                 ▼
              ┌──────┐
   CLK ───────┤LATCH │ (negative-edge transparent)
              │ (Q)  │
              └──┬───┘
                 ▼
   CLK ───────► AND ───────► Gated Clock
```

**Operation [2 marks]:**
- When $\text{EN} = 1$: latch is transparent in the high phase of clock, AND passes CLK → output follows clock.
- When $\text{EN} = 0$: latch holds '0', AND output is forced low → clock is gated.
- The latch is **transparent on the *negative* edge** of CLK. This means EN is sampled only when CLK is low, ensuring the AND output never sees a glitch when EN toggles.

**Why negative-edge latch is essential [1 mark]:**
If EN changes while CLK is high, a positive-edge transparent latch would propagate that change to the AND gate, causing a glitch (partial clock pulse) on the output. The negative-edge latch "freezes" EN at the *safe* half-cycle, so any EN transition is sampled only when CLK is low → AND output is clean.

**What happens with a simple AND gate [1 mark]:**
A simple AND gate $(\text{CLK AND EN})$ produces **glitches on the gated clock** whenever EN transitions asynchronously with respect to CLK. These glitches can cause:
- Double-clocking of flip-flops (setup/hold violations)
- Race conditions in synchronous logic
- Functional failure of the SoC

Hence the ICG structure with a negative-edge latch is the *only* glitch-free solution.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCEVT602, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 marks]** A 4-level **H-tree** distributes clock to a 16 × 16 array of flip-flops on a 10 mm × 10 mm die in 28 nm CMOS. Per-unit wire parameters are $r = 0.1$ Ω/μm and $c = 0.2$ fF/μm. The buffer at each H-tree node has intrinsic delay $t_b = 25$ ps. Calculate the **Elmore delay** from PLL to any leaf flip-flop.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss **clock gating** and **clock mesh** techniques. Compare them in terms of power, skew, and design complexity. When would you choose a mesh over an H-tree?

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (a) — Elmore Delay of H-Tree (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Geometry [1 mark]:**
Die dimension $L = 10$ mm $= 10{,}000$ μm. The H-tree has 4 levels ($k = 4$), so the finest segment length is:

$$
L_4 = \frac{L}{2^4} = \frac{10{,}000}{16} = 625 \; \mu m
$$

**Step 2 — Per-level wire length and RC [1 mark]:**
At level $i$ ($i = 1, 2, 3, 4$):

$$
L_i = \frac{L}{2^{i-1}} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{L}{2^{i}}
$$

| Level $i$ | $L_i$ (μm) | $R_i = r L_i$ (Ω) | $C_i = c L_i$ (fF) | $t_{RC,i} = 0.5 R_i C_i$ (ps) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5000 | 500 | 1000 | 250 |
| 2 | 2500 | 250 | 500 | 62.5 |
| 3 | 1250 | 125 | 250 | 15.6 |
| 4 | 625 | 62.5 | 125 | 3.9 |

**Step 3 — Sum of wire RC delays [2 marks]:**

$$
t_{wire} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} t_{RC,i} = 250 + 62.5 + 15.6 + 3.9 = 332 \text{ ps}
$$

**Step 4 — Buffer delay contribution [1 mark]:**
4 levels of buffers, each with 25 ps delay. But every path from root to leaf passes through all 4 buffer stages (root, level-1, level-2, level-3):

$$
t_{buf} = 4 \cdot 25 = 100 \text{ ps}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Elmore delay [2 marks]:**

$$
\boxed{t_{d,leaf} = t_{wire} + t_{buf} = 332 + 100 = 432 \text{ ps}}
$$

**Sanity check [bonus]:** In 28 nm CMOS, a 432 ps clock edge is consistent with typical 1–2 GHz SoC clock periods. ✓

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (b) — Clock Gating vs Clock Mesh (7 marks)**

**Clock Gating [2 marks]:**
Clock gating is a **power-saving technique** where the clock signal to an idle block is disabled using an ICG cell (latch + AND). It eliminates *unnecessary switching* of flip-flops. Saves **20 % – 60 %** of dynamic power depending on gating coverage. Used in nearly every modern SoC (CPU, GPU, DSP).

**Clock Mesh [2 marks]:**
A clock mesh (or grid) is a **2-D orthogonal network of metal wires** spanning the entire chip, with multiple redundant clock drivers (typically one per mesh region). The mesh averages out skew and is highly tolerant of process variations, achieving **sub-15 ps skew** in high-performance designs. Used in Intel/AMD CPUs, Apple M-series, Nvidia GPUs.

**Comparison table [2 marks]:**

| Parameter | Clock Gating | Clock Mesh |
|---|---|---|
| **Power saving** | High (20–60 %) | None / adds power (always toggles) |
| **Skew control** | Moderate (50–100 ps) | Excellent (5–20 ps) |
| **Area overhead** | Low (one ICG per gated block) | Very high (mesh wires) |
| **Design complexity** | Low (RTL `always @(posedge clk if en)`) | High (full-custom CTS) |
| **PVT tolerance** | Average | Excellent |
| **Application** | Power-constrained SoCs (mobile) | High-perf CPUs, GPUs |

**When to choose mesh over H-tree [1 mark]:**
Choose a **clock mesh** when (i) target clock frequency is very high ($> 2$ GHz) and skew budget is tight, (ii) process variation is severe (advanced nodes $\le 7$ nm), (iii) the design must meet aggressive timing margins across all PVT corners, and (iv) power budget can absorb the extra mesh capacitance. Examples: server CPUs (Intel Xeon, AMD EPYC), AI accelerators (Nvidia H100, Google TPU).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing *skew* with *jitter*.** Skew = spatial, jitter = temporal. Examiners deduct 1 mark if swapped.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers. Always append **ps / ns / mW**.
> 3. **Using $f = e$ blindly** even when the load is not the *self-loaded* case. State "for self-loaded minimum-size inverter".
> 4. **Omitting the negative-edge latch** in ICG diagrams. This is worth 2 marks in the 14-mark Q.A(b).
> 5. **Inserting a hold-fixing buffer but not re-checking setup slack.** Examiners test this explicitly.
> 6. **Forgetting $\tau$ (technology constant)** in delay formulas. Show: $t_{pd} = \tau \cdot (\ldots)$.
> 7. **Wrong Elmore formula:** $t_{Elmore} = 0.5 \cdot R \cdot C$ for distributed RC, **not** $R \cdot C$.
> 8. **Not stating assumptions** in derivations (e.g., "assume symmetric tree", "assume identical buffers").

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Clock Buffer** = high-drive repeater that re-buffers, fans out, and isolates the clock.
- ✅ **Four roles:** restore levels, drive large loads, minimize skew, decouple PLL.
- ✅ **Hierarchy:** PLL → Pre-buffer → Global → Regional → Local (gated) buffer.
- ✅ **Types:** BUF, INV, Differential, ICG, ZDB, Balanced, Level-shifting.
- ✅ **Topologies:** H-tree (zero deterministic skew), X-tree, fishbone, mesh.
- ✅ **Logical Effort:** $t_{pd} = \tau (gh + p)$; for inverter, $g=1$, $p=1$.
- ✅ **Optimal taper:** $f_{opt} = e \approx 2.718$ (self-loaded); with wires, $f_{opt} = 3\text{–}4$.
- ✅ **Skew:** spatial mismatch in arrival time.
- ✅ **Jitter:** cycle-to-cycle temporal uncertainty.
- ✅ **Power:** $P_{clk} = \alpha C_{total} V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$, with $\alpha = 1$ for clock.
- ✅ **Hold-fixing:** Insert buffer on fast path; check setup slack afterwards.
- ✅ **ICG cell** = AND + negative-edge-transparent latch (eliminates glitches).
- ✅ **Clock mesh** = 2-D redundant network for ultra-low skew at high PVT variability.
- ✅ **Elmore delay** for distributed RC: $t_{Elmore} = 0.5 R C$.
- ✅ **KTU-favorite answer keywords:** *isolation, restoration, fan-out, symmetry, PVT, glitch-free*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gated Clocks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT602 - SYSTEM ON CHIP DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gated Clocks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Clock Gating** is a sequential power-management technique used in synchronous digital Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) design wherein the clock signal distributed to a sequential element (flip-flop, register, latch, or an entire clock domain) is conditionally *masked* or *gated* by a control signal (the *enable* signal) so that the flip-flops do not toggle when their state is not required to change. By suppressing redundant transitions on the internal clock tree and the register outputs, clock gating directly reduces the **dynamic switching power** of the circuit.

Mathematically, if the unconditional clock toggles at frequency $f_{clk}$ with switching activity $\alpha_{clk}$, then after ideal clock gating with active probability $p_{EN}$ (the probability that the enable is high and registers *must* update), the effective switching activity becomes:

$$
\alpha_{eff} = \alpha_{clk} \cdot p_{EN}
$$

Therefore the dynamic power dissipated by the gated domain is reduced by the same factor $p_{EN}$, which is why clock gating is considered the **single most effective low-power technique** in synchronous CMOS design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Clock-gating belongs to the broader theme of *Low Power Design Techniques in SoC*. It is treated as a *structural/sequential* low-power methodology, distinct from *circuit-level* techniques (clock stretching, multi-Vt) and *system-level* techniques (power gating, DVFS).

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy — "The Hallway Light"

Imagine a long hotel corridor fitted with motion-sensor lights. The bulbs are physically wired to mains electricity 24×7, but the *sensor (the enable signal)* only allows current to flow when a guest is present. The electricity supply is *always live* (analogous to the global clock), but the *load* (analogous to the flip-flops) only *consumes* power when the sensor permits.

In CMOS terms:
- **Mains electricity** ↔ Clock tree network
- **Motion sensor** ↔ Enable (EN) signal generated by combinational logic
- **Bulbs** ↔ Sequential registers/flip-flops
- **Light ON duration** ↔ Active switching cycles where new data is captured

Without the sensor, the lights would burn (and waste energy) even in an empty corridor — just as a register would toggle every cycle even when its input data has not changed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in SoC:** In a modern mobile SoC, the clock network alone can consume **30 % – 40 %** of the total dynamic power. Clock gating is the primary lever designers pull to extend battery life.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Terms You Must Know**
> - **Enable Signal (EN):** Combinational signal that decides whether the next clock edge should be passed to the register.
> - **Gated Clock (CG):** The output of the gating logic — what the flip-flop actually sees as its clock.
> - **Glitch:** An unwanted runt pulse on the gated clock caused by a late or early transition of the enable.
> - **Active-High Gating:** Uses an **AND** gate. The flip-flop is transparent when EN = 1.
> - **Active-Low Gating:** Uses an **OR** gate (or AND with inverted EN). The flip-flop is transparent when EN = 0.
> - **Latch-Based Gating (LBCG):** Uses a level-sensitive latch to *hold* EN stable across the active clock edge — eliminates glitches.
> - **Integrated Clock Gating (ICG) cell:** A standard-cell provided in the technology library that contains the latch + gate together (e.g., `CKLNQ`, `CKHQ` cells in Synopsys libraries).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Power Rationale — Why Gated Clocks?

The dynamic power consumed by a CMOS gate is governed by the well-known equation:

$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}
$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = **switching activity factor** (probability that the node toggles in a clock cycle)
- $C_{L}$ = **load capacitance** of the node
- $V_{DD}$ = **supply voltage**
- $f_{clk}$ = **clock frequency**

**Observation:** In a synchronous SoC, the **clock net** is the only signal that toggles **every single cycle** ($\alpha_{clk} \approx 1$) in every flip-flop. This makes it the single largest contributor to dynamic power — even though functionally the registers might not be required to update every cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Clock gating therefore attacks the term $\alpha$ in the dynamic power equation. By making $\alpha_{clk,eff} = p_{EN}$, where $p_{EN}$ is the probability that the enable is asserted, we can save up to $(1 - p_{EN}) \times 100\%$ of the clock-tree dynamic power.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step by Step

1. **Detect "no change" condition:** The synthesis tool or RTL designer identifies conditions under which the register's input `D` is identical to its current state `Q`. This produces an *enable* signal.
2. **Generate gated clock:** A combinational gate (AND/OR) or, more robustly, a *latch + gate* combination, combines `CLK` and `EN` to produce `CLK_GATED`.
3. **Distribute to flip-flops:** All flip-flops sharing the same enable are grouped into a *clock-gating group* and share one `CLK_GATED` net.
4. **Sample the data:** When `EN = 1`, the rising edge of `CLK_GATED` is passed → flip-flop samples `D`. When `EN = 0`, the clock is suppressed → flip-flop *holds* its previous value without any internal switching.
5. **Hierarchical application:** The same pattern is repeated at the block, sub-system, and SoC level (coarse-grain gating), with the SoC's Power Management Unit (PMU) acting as the topmost enable.

### 2.3 Types of Clock Gating Styles

| # | Style | Implementation | Pros | Cons | Typical Use |
|---|-------|----------------|------|------|-------------|
| 1 | **Pure AND/OR Gating** | `CLK_GATED = CLK AND EN` | Simplest, smallest area | Glitches if EN changes near active edge | Avoided in production |
| 2 | **Latch-Based Clock Gating (LBCG)** | Negative-level-sensitive latch + AND gate | Glitch-free | Slightly more area, latch hold-time critical | Industry standard |
| 3 | **Flip-Flop-Based Gating** | EN synchronised to CLK via a flop | Edge-clean | Latency of 1 cycle | Low-frequency control |
| 4 | **ICG Cell (Library Cell)** | Pre-designed LBCG inside a single cell | Timing-characterised, scan-friendly | Library dependent | Production SoCs |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | Fundamental dynamic power | W (watts) |
| 2 | $\alpha_{eff} = \alpha \cdot p_{EN}$ | Effective activity after gating | dimensionless |
| 3 | $P_{saved} = (1 - p_{EN}) \cdot P_{clk,ungated}$ | Power saved by gating | W |
| 4 | $t_{hold} \le t_{cq,latch} + t_{setup,AND}$ | LBCG glitch-free constraint | seconds |
| 5 | $f_{max} = 1 / (t_{cq} + t_{comb} + t_{setup})$ | Max operating frequency | Hz |
| 6 | $E_{switching} = C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$ | Energy per transition | J (joules) |
| 7 | $P_{avg} = (1/N) \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \cdot C_i \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | Average SoC power | W |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Every modern smartphone SoC (Qualcomm Snapdragon, Apple A-series, MediaTek Dimensity) uses *thousands* of ICG cells. The Synopsys Power Compiler, Cadence Genus, and Siemens Tessent all support automatic clock-gating inference from RTL.

### 2.5 The Glitch Problem — Why Pure AND Gating Fails

If `EN` itself is generated by combinational logic and changes *after* the rising edge of `CLK` but *before* the falling edge (or vice-versa), the output of `CLK AND EN` will produce a **partial pulse** — a glitch. This glitch, if wide enough, will falsely clock the downstream flip-flops, corrupting the system state.

The LBCG topology solves this by *latching* the enable on the *inactive* phase of the clock. For an **active-high** ICG using a *negative* latch (transparent when CLK = 0):

$$
EN_{stable} = \text{Latch}(\text{EN}, \text{CLK} = 0)
$$

$$
CLK_{GATED} = EN_{stable} \cdot CLK
$$

The negative latch is transparent only when `CLK = 0`, so it captures `EN` and then *holds it constant* across the rising edge of `CLK` — guaranteeing a clean, glitch-free gated clock.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Power Reduction Factor of Clock Gating

Let us rigorously derive the *power reduction factor* (PRF) when clock gating is applied to a register bank of $N$ flip-flops with enable probability $p_{EN}$.

**Step 1 — Write the ungated dynamic power of the register bank:**

$$
P_{ungated} = N \cdot \alpha_{ff} \cdot C_{ff} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}
$$

Where $\alpha_{ff}$ is the per-flip-flop internal activity, $C_{ff}$ is the per-flip-flop clock pin capacitance.

**Step 2 — Identify the dominant term.** The clock pin alone switches every cycle, so $\alpha_{ff} = 1$ for the clock pin. The total clock-pin power is:

$$
P_{clk,ungated} = N \cdot 1 \cdot C_{clk} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply gating.** When EN is low, the gated clock stays low, so the clock pin does **not** toggle. Only when EN is high (probability $p_{EN}$) does the clock pin switch.

$$
P_{clk,gated} = N \cdot p_{EN} \cdot C_{clk} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Power Reduction Factor (PRF):**

$$
PRF = \frac{P_{clk,ungated}}{P_{clk,gated}} = \frac{N \cdot C_{clk} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}}{N \cdot p_{EN} \cdot C_{clk} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}}
$$

$$
PRF = \frac{1}{p_{EN}}
$$

**Step 5 — Express the percentage power saving:**

$$
\% \text{Saving} = \left(1 - p_{EN}\right) \cdot 100\%
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example.** Consider a video decoder block where registers are *active* (i.e. EN = 1) for only **20 %** of the time, i.e. $p_{EN} = 0.2$:

$$
\% \text{Saving} = (1 - 0.2) \cdot 100\% = 80\%
$$

The PRF is $1/0.2 = 5\times$ — a five-fold reduction in the clock-related dynamic power of that block.

### 3.2 Derivation — Total SoC Power Reduction after Gating

For a complete SoC, the *total* dynamic power is the sum of (a) clock-tree power $P_{clk}$, (b) combinational power $P_{comb}$, (c) register data power $P_{reg}$, and (d) memory + I/O power $P_{mem}$.

$$
P_{tot} = P_{clk} + P_{comb} + P_{reg} + P_{mem}
$$

Clock gating only affects $P_{clk}$ (and indirectly $P_{reg}$ since Q outputs hold their value and do not toggle). Define $k = P_{clk}/P_{tot}$ as the *clock-power share* (typically 0.30 – 0.40 in modern SoCs). After gating, the new clock power is $k \cdot p_{EN} \cdot P_{tot}$.

$$
P_{tot}^{gated} = k \cdot p_{EN} \cdot P_{tot} + (1 - k) \cdot P_{tot}
$$

$$
P_{tot}^{gated} = P_{tot} \left[1 - k(1 - p_{EN})\right]
$$

Therefore the **system-level saving fraction** is:

$$
S_{sys} = k \cdot (1 - p_{EN})
$$

**Numerical Example:** For $k = 0.35$ and $p_{EN} = 0.25$:

$$
S_{sys} = 0.35 \cdot (1 - 0.25) = 0.35 \cdot 0.75 = 0.2625
$$

So a 35 % clock-power share and a 25 % activity rate yield a **26.25 % reduction in the total SoC power** — a hugely significant number for battery-powered devices.

### 3.3 Derivation — Glitch-Free Hold-Time Constraint of an LBCG

For a latch-based clock gating cell composed of a negative-level-sensitive latch driving an AND gate, the enable signal $D$ must remain stable at the latch output for the duration of the active clock edge. This is captured by the **hold-time constraint**:

$$
t_{hold,EN} \le t_{cq,latch} + t_{AND}
$$

Where:
- $t_{hold,EN}$ = time the enable must be stable before CLK rising
- $t_{cq,latch}$ = clock-to-Q delay of the negative latch
- $t_{AND}$ = propagation delay of the AND gate

If this constraint is violated, the latch may release EN *before* the AND gate has masked the CLK edge — a glitch appears on `CLK_GATED`.

To prevent this, the *positive* hold time required by the ICG cell is:

$$
t_{hold,ICG} = t_{cq,latch} - t_{hold,latch}
$$

And the synthesis tool enforces:

$$
t_{hold,EN} \ge t_{hold,ICG}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Style to Choose?

| Design Criterion | Pure AND/OR | LBCG (Latch+AND) | ICG Standard Cell |
|------------------|-------------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Glitch Immunity** | None (poor) | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Area Overhead** | 1 gate | 1 latch + 1 gate | Same as LBCG |
| **Timing Closure Difficulty** | Low | Moderate (hold time) | Pre-characterised |
| **Scan/DFT Support** | Manual | Manual | Library-handled |
| **Production Use** | Academic only | Internal projects | Industry standard |
| **Power Savings Realised** | 10–20 % | 50–70 % | 50–80 % |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Clock Gating Architecture in an SoC

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    src[Clock Source PLL] --> ctn[Global Clock Tree]
    ctn --> ctl1[Clock Gating Group A]
    ctn --> ctl2[Clock Gating Group B]
    ctn --> ctl3[Clock Gating Group C]
    en1[Enable Logic A from FSM] --> ctl1
    en2[Enable Logic B from Decoder] --> ctl2
    en3[Enable Logic C from PMU] --> ctl3
    ctl1 --> rg1[Register Bank A]
    ctl2 --> rg2[Register Bank B]
    ctl3 --> rg3[Register Bank C]
    rg1 --> cbf1[Combinational Cloud A]
    rg2 --> cbf2[Combinational Cloud B]
    rg3 --> cbf3[Combinational Cloud C]
    cbf1 --> en1
    cbf2 --> en2
    pmc[PMU Power Mgmt Unit] --> en3
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Circuit Schematic — Latch-Based ICG Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    en[EN] --> latd[D Latch Negative Level]
    clk[CLK] --> latclk[Latch Enable Inverted]
    latd --> enq[Q Stable EN]
    enq --> and1[AND Gate]
    clk --> and1
    and1 --> ckg[CLK GATED Output]
    ckg --> ffa[Flip Flop 1]
    ckg --> ffb[Flip Flop 2]
    ckg --> ffc[Flip Flop N]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Waveform Diagram — Gated Clock vs Ungated Clock

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T[Time Axis]
        T1[t0] --> T2[t1] --> T3[t2] --> T4[t3] --> T5[t4] --> T6[t5] --> T7[t6] --> T8[t7]
    end
    subgraph CLK[Ungated Clock]
        a1[HIGH]
        a2[LOW]
        a3[HIGH]
        a4[LOW]
        a5[HIGH]
    end
    subgraph EN[Enable Signal]
        b1[HIGH]
        b2[LOW]
        b3[HIGH]
    end
    subgraph CKG[Gated Clock Output]
        c1[HIGH]
        c2[LOW held]
        c3[HIGH]
    end
    T2 --- a1
    T3 --- a2
    T4 --- a3
    T5 --- a4
    T6 --- a5
    T2 --- b1
    T4 --- b2
    T6 --- b3
    T2 --- c1
    T3 --- c2
    T4 --- c2
    T5 --- c2
    T6 --- c3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the waveform:** At `t0–t1` EN is HIGH so the gated clock follows CLK normally. At `t1–t4` EN is LOW so the gated clock is *held LOW* — even though the source clock continues to toggle, the flip-flops see no edges. At `t4–t5` EN rises again, and the gated clock resumes.

### 4.4 Mermaid Decision Flow — When to Apply Clock Gating

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Identify Register Bank] --> hasEn{Does bank have predictable enable condition?}
    hasEn -- No --> skip[Skip Gating]
    hasEn -- Yes --> rate{Is EN duty cycle less than 80 percent?}
    rate -- No --> skip
    rate -- Yes --> style{Gating style decision}
    style -- Production --> icg[Use Library ICG Cell]
    style -- Academic --> lbcg[Use Latch plus AND]
    style -- Quick prototype --> andg[Use Pure AND with hold fix]
    icg --> dft[Add to Scan Chain]
    lbcg --> dft
    andg --> warn[Manual Hold Time Fix Required]
    warn --> dft
    dft --> verify[Static Timing Analysis]
    verify --> power[Power Estimation Report]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** *Define clock gating. Why is it considered the most effective low-power technique in synchronous CMOS design?*

**Model Answer:**
Clock gating is a sequential low-power design technique in which the clock signal supplied to a register or register bank is conditionally masked by an enable signal so that the flip-flops are not clocked when their state is not required to change. It is considered the most effective technique because the clock network typically accounts for 30 % – 40 % of the total dynamic power, and clock gating directly attacks the switching-activity factor $\alpha$ in the dynamic power equation $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{DD}^{2} f$, often realising power savings of 50 % – 70 % with negligible area overhead.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** *What is the difference between latch-based clock gating and pure AND-gate-based clock gating? Why is the latter avoided in production designs?*

**Model Answer:**
Pure AND-gate-based clock gating combines the clock and enable through a single AND gate to produce the gated clock. It is compact but suffers from *glitches* when the enable signal transitions near the active clock edge, because the AND gate has unequal propagation delays on its two inputs. Latch-based clock gating (LBCG) inserts a negative-level-sensitive latch on the enable path so that the enable is *held constant* across the active clock edge, producing a glitch-free gated clock. Production designs universally prefer LBCG (implemented as a library ICG cell) because glitches can cause spurious clocking, metastability, and functional failures.

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the operation of a *latch-based clock gating cell*. Show how glitches are eliminated. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 32-bit register bank in an SoC has $C_{clk} = 30 \text{ fF}$ per flip-flop, $V_{DD} = 0.9 \text{ V}$, $f_{clk} = 1 \text{ GHz}$, and the enable signal is high for only 25 % of the cycles. Compute the dynamic clock power before and after clock gating and the percentage saving. (7 marks)

**Model Solution to (a):**

The latch-based clock gating (LBCG) cell consists of a *negative-level-sensitive transparent latch* whose data input is the enable `EN`, and whose enable is the *inverted clock* `~CLK`. The output of this latch is fed into one input of a 2-input AND gate whose other input is `CLK`.

$$
EN_{latched}(t) = \text{NegLATCH}(EN(t), \overline{CLK}(t))
$$

$$
CLK_{gated}(t) = EN_{latched}(t) \cdot CLK(t)
$$

**Glitch elimination logic:**
When `CLK = 0`, the negative latch is *transparent* and follows `EN`. As `CLK` rises from 0 → 1, the latch becomes *opaque* and *holds* the last value of `EN` it captured. This held value is presented to the AND gate throughout the high phase of `CLK`, so the AND gate output is a clean, glitch-free copy of `CLK` when EN = 1, and a clean constant 0 when EN = 0. The cell is implemented in standard-cell libraries as a single ICG cell with pre-characterised timing arcs.

**Model Solution to (b):**

Given:
- Number of flip-flops $N = 32$
- $C_{clk} = 30 \text{ fF} = 30 \times 10^{-15} \text{ F}$
- $V_{DD} = 0.9 \text{ V}$
- $f_{clk} = 1 \text{ GHz} = 10^{9} \text{ Hz}$
- $p_{EN} = 0.25$

**Step 1 — Total clock-pin capacitance:**

$$
C_{total} = N \cdot C_{clk} = 32 \cdot 30 \text{ fF} = 960 \text{ fF} = 9.6 \times 10^{-13} \text{ F}
$$

**Step 2 — Un-gated dynamic clock power** ($\alpha = 1$):

$$
P_{ungated} = \alpha \cdot C_{total} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}
$$

$$
P_{ungated} = 1 \cdot 9.6 \times 10^{-13} \cdot (0.9)^{2} \cdot 10^{9}
$$

$$
P_{ungated} = 9.6 \times 10^{-13} \cdot 0.81 \cdot 10^{9} = 7.776 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{ungated} \approx 777.6 \text{ μW} \approx 0.778 \text{ mW}
$$

**Step 3 — Gated dynamic clock power** ($\alpha_{eff} = p_{EN} = 0.25$):

$$
P_{gated} = 0.25 \cdot 9.6 \times 10^{-13} \cdot 0.81 \cdot 10^{9}
$$

$$
P_{gated} = 0.25 \cdot 7.776 \times 10^{-4} = 1.944 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{gated} \approx 194.4 \text{ μW} \approx 0.194 \text{ mW}
$$

**Step 4 — Power saving:**

$$
\Delta P = 777.6 - 194.4 = 583.2 \text{ μW}
$$

$$
\% \text{Saving} = \frac{583.2}{777.6} \cdot 100\% = 75\%
$$

**Verification with the formula:** $(1 - p_{EN}) \cdot 100\% = (1 - 0.25) \cdot 100\% = 75\%$ ✓

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Compare the three clock-gating styles (AND, Latch-Based, Flip-Flop Based) in terms of glitch immunity, area, latency, and production suitability. (7 marks)

**(b)** An SoC has a total dynamic power of 800 mW, of which 35 % is in the clock tree. After applying clock gating, the average enable probability across all gated domains is found to be 0.30. Estimate the new total power and the percentage saving. (7 marks)

**Model Solution to (a):**

| Style | Glitch Immunity | Area | Latency | Production Use |
|-------|-----------------|------|---------|----------------|
| Pure AND | Poor — glitches when EN changes near CLK edge | 1 gate | Zero | Avoided |
| Latch-Based (LBCG) | Excellent — latch holds EN across active edge | 1 latch + 1 gate | Zero (transparent phase) | Industry standard |
| Flip-Flop Based | Excellent — edge-triggered | 1 flop + 1 gate | 1 CLK cycle | Control paths |

**Detailed reasoning:**

- **Pure AND gating** is the smallest and fastest but completely vulnerable to glitches because any change in `EN` between the rising and falling edges of `CLK` produces a runt pulse at the AND output.
- **Latch-Based gating** uses a transparent-when-low latch to *sample* `EN` during the inactive phase of `CLK` and *hold* it during the active phase. This makes the AND output clean but introduces a hold-time constraint on `EN` — the synthesis tool must ensure $t_{hold,EN} \ge t_{cq,latch}$.
- **Flip-Flop based gating** synchronises `EN` to the clock edge, so it is glitch-free by construction. However, it incurs a *1-cycle latency* between the time `EN` is generated and the time it takes effect — this is unacceptable for tight timing paths in the data plane but fine for slow control signals.

**Model Solution to (b):**

Given:
- $P_{tot} = 800 \text{ mW}$
- $k = 0.35$ (clock-tree fraction)
- $p_{EN} = 0.30$

**Step 1 — Clock power before gating:**

$$
P_{clk} = k \cdot P_{tot} = 0.35 \cdot 800 = 280 \text{ mW}
$$

**Step 2 — Non-clock power before gating:**

$$
P_{other} = (1 - k) \cdot P_{tot} = 0.65 \cdot 800 = 520 \text{ mW}
$$

**Step 3 — Clock power after gating** (gated clock is active only $p_{EN}$ of the time):

$$
P_{clk,gated} = p_{EN} \cdot P_{clk} = 0.30 \cdot 280 = 84 \text{ mW}
$$

**Step 4 — Total new power:**

$$
P_{tot}^{gated} = P_{clk,gated} + P_{other} = 84 + 520 = 604 \text{ mW}
$$

**Step 5 — Power saving:**

$$
\Delta P = 800 - 604 = 196 \text{ mW}
$$

$$
\% \text{Saving} = \frac{196}{800} \cdot 100\% = 24.5\%
$$

**Cross-check using the system-saving formula:**

$$
S_{sys} = k \cdot (1 - p_{EN}) = 0.35 \cdot (1 - 0.30) = 0.35 \cdot 0.70 = 0.245
$$

$$
\% S_{sys} = 24.5\% \checkmark
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** Some texts write $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{DD}^{2} f$ and others use $P_{dyn} = \frac{1}{2} \alpha C V_{DD}^{2} f$ depending on the definition of $\alpha$. Always state the convention you are using in the first step of the solution.
> 2. **Conflating $p_{EN}$ with the enable duty cycle:** $p_{EN}$ is the *probability* the enable is high, which equals the *duty cycle* only for stationary signals. For non-stationary signals (e.g. burst-mode), use the long-run average.
> 3. **Ignoring latch hold-time:** When asked to design an LBCG, students often draw the circuit but forget to mention the hold-time constraint $t_{hold,EN} \ge t_{cq,latch}$, which is the *most important* reason LBCG is glitch-free.
> 4. **No DFT/DFM discussion:** KTU 2024 scheme emphasises "industry-readiness" — failing to mention that ICG cells must be *scan-friendly* (transparent in shift mode) costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Using the symbol `|x|` inside a markdown table:** This breaks the table renderer. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in any formula containing a vertical bar.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**
> - Clock gating is a **sequential**, **structural** low-power technique — it attacks the $\alpha$ term in $P = \alpha C V_{DD}^{2} f$.
> - **Power saving formula:** $\% \text{Save} = (1 - p_{EN}) \times 100\%$ for the gated domain; $S_{sys} = k(1 - p_{EN})$ for the whole SoC.
> - **Pure AND gating is dangerous** because of glitches — avoid in production.
> - **Latch-Based (LBCG)** is the industry standard; implemented as a single library ICG cell.
> - The negative latch in LBCG is **transparent when CLK = 0** and **opaque when CLK = 1** — this is what holds EN stable across the active edge.
> - The **hold-time constraint** $t_{hold,EN} \ge t_{cq,latch}$ is critical — violation causes glitches.
> - Clock gating typically saves **50 % – 80 %** of clock-tree power; system-wide savings depend on the clock-power share $k$.
> - ICG cells must be made **DFT/scan-friendly** (transparent in shift mode) — this is a KTU 2024 emphasis.
> - Synopsys Power Compiler, Cadence Genus, and most synthesis tools support **automatic clock-gating inference** from RTL.
> - The flip-flop-based gating style introduces a **1-cycle latency** and is therefore reserved for *control* paths, not data paths.
> - **Coarse-grain clock gating** at the SoC level is controlled by the PMU and may be combined with *power gating* for maximum savings.
> - **Don't confuse** clock gating with clock stretching (frequency scaling) or power gating (supply切断) — they are different techniques serving different power-reduction goals.
> - Always convert fF → F, GHz → Hz, and μW → mW **before** plugging into the power formula to avoid unit errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internally Generated Clocks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT602 - SYSTEM ON CHIP DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Internally Generated Clocks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internally Generated Clocks (IGC)** are timing signals that are *synthesized, divided, multiplied, or otherwise derived entirely from on-chip resources* — without relying on an external pin-driven oscillator as the primary source. They are produced using dedicated hardware primitives such as **PLLs (Phase-Locked Loops)**, **MMCMs (Mixed-Mode Clock Managers)**, **DCMs (Digital Clock Managers)**, on-chip **ring oscillators**, and **logic-counter-based dividers**.

In the context of an SoC/FPGA, an internally generated clock is the *electrical periodic waveform* (typically a square wave with a 50% nominal duty cycle) created *inside the device fabric*, used to synchronize sequential logic, data-transfer blocks, memory interfaces, and processor cores.

### 1.2 Real-World Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Office Water Pump System:**
> Imagine a municipal water pipeline (the *external clock pin*) that supplies water at a fixed pressure (fixed frequency). Now imagine your office building has its own internal booster pump, pressure regulators, and a small storage tank. Depending on which floor or machine needs water, the building's internal plumbing creates *different pressures and flow rates from the same incoming supply*. The **Internally Generated Clock** is exactly this — the FPGA takes one incoming "supply" frequency and uses internal hardware (PLL, MMCM, divider) to *create new frequencies, phases, and duty cycles* for different internal circuits. Some SoCs even have a *small internal ring oscillator* (like a tiny backup generator) that can produce a clock when no external source is connected — used for boot-up and configuration.

### 1.3 Classification of Internally Generated Clocks

Internally generated clocks are broadly classified into the following categories used in the KTU 2024 SoC Design curriculum:

| Classification | Primary Hardware Source | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Divided Clocks** | Counter flip-flops, BUFR, BUFH | Low-speed peripherals, UART, I²C |
| **Multiplied Clocks** | PLL, MMCM | High-speed fabric, DDR memory |
| **Synthesized Clocks** | PLL + fractional divider | Non-integer frequency synthesis |
| **Phase-Shifted Clocks** | MMCM (P, M, D taps) | Source-synchronous interfaces |
| **Recovery Clocks** | MMCM feedback path | Clock-tree de-skew |
| **On-Chip Oscillator Clocks** | Ring/RC oscillator (e.g., Xilinx `STARTUPE2`) | Boot, configuration, low-power modes |

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are universally used by KTU examiners when evaluating IGC problems:
> - **Input Frequency (f_in)** — measured in **MHz** or **GHz**.
> - **Output Frequency (f_out)** — measured in **MHz** or **GHz**.
> - **VCO Frequency (f_VCO)** — internal voltage-controlled oscillator frequency; bounded between **400 MHz and 1600 MHz** for most Xilinx 7-series MMCMs.
> - **Duty Cycle** — usually targeted at **50%**; tolerance typically **±5%**.
> - **Phase Shift (φ)** — expressed in **degrees** or **picoseconds**.
> - **Jitter (peak-to-peak / RMS)** — measured in **picoseconds (ps)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Block-by-Block Logic

Internally generated clocks in modern SoCs are produced by a **Clock Management Tile (CMT)** which is the integration of a **PLL** and an **MMCM**. The flow of operations is as follows:

1. **Reference Input Reception**: An incoming clock (from an external pad or an internal oscillator) enters the PLL/MMCM through the `CLK_IN` port.
2. **Input Divider (D)**: The reference frequency is divided by a programmable integer `D` to bring it into the operating range of the **Phase Frequency Detector (PFD)**.
3. **Phase Frequency Detection**: The PFD compares the divided reference signal with a feedback signal and generates an *error voltage* proportional to the phase/frequency difference.
4. **Charge Pump + Loop Filter**: Converts the error signal into a smooth analog control voltage for the **VCO**.
5. **Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO)**: Generates a high-frequency oscillation governed by $f_{VCO} = f_{in} \cdot M / D$.
6. **Multiplication Feedback (M)**: The VCO output is divided by `M` and routed back to the PFD — this is what makes the loop "lock" at a multiplied output.
7. **Output Dividers (O0…O6)**: Each output port has an independent post-divider, allowing **multiple distinct frequencies** to be derived from one VCO.
8. **Phase-Shift Tap Selection**: MMCMs additionally provide *fine-grained phase taps* (e.g., 1/56 of a VCO period in Xilinx 7-series).
9. **Global Clock Buffer Routing (BUFG/BUFR/BUFH)**: The synthesized clock is distributed across the device fabric.

### 2.2 Why and How — Conceptual Justification

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use internally generated clocks?**
> - Off-chip oscillators are *expensive*, *noisy*, and *consume board area*.
> - Different internal blocks (CPU, DDR controller, PCIe, ADC) require **different frequencies** from one reference.
> - Phase alignment between transmit/receive paths of a high-speed serial link requires **sub-nanosecond precision**, which is impossible from an external pin.

> [!NOTE]
> **How does the PLL "lock"?**
> The PLL operates as a **negative-feedback control system**. The PFD continuously computes the phase error $\Delta \phi = \phi_{ref} - \phi_{fb}$. The loop filter integrates this error and drives the VCO until $\Delta \phi \to 0$, meaning the divided VCO output is in *phase and frequency* match with the divided reference — this stable condition is called the **"locked" state**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the *highest-yield* equation set for any KTU question on Internally Generated Clocks. Master these before attempting any numerical problem.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | VCO Frequency | $f_{VCO} = f_{in} \cdot \dfrac{M}{D}$ | $f_{VCO}$ in **MHz**, $M, D$ are **integers** | $400 \le f_{VCO} \le 1600$ MHz (7-series) |
| 2 | Output Frequency (per port) | $f_{out,i} = \dfrac{f_{VCO}}{O_i}$ | $O_i$ is the *i-th* output divider, integer | $O_i \ge 1$ |
| 3 | Combined Output | $f_{out,i} = \dfrac{f_{in} \cdot M}{D \cdot O_i}$ | All integers | — |
| 4 | Lock Range | $f_{lock,min} < f_{in} < f_{lock,max}$ | — | 10 MHz < f_in < 800 MHz typical |
| 5 | Period | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | T in **ns** if f in **GHz** | — |
| 6 | Phase Shift (deg) | $\phi = \dfrac{360^\circ \cdot \Delta t}{T_{VCO}}$ | $\Delta t$ in **ps** | $-360^\circ < \phi < 360^\circ$ |
| 7 | Phase Shift (taps) | $\phi_{tap} = \dfrac{360^\circ}{56}$ (fixed) | Static for 7-series MMCM | 56 fine taps |
| 8 | Duty Cycle Correction | $DC = \dfrac{T_{high}}{T} \times 100\%$ | $T_{high}$ = high time | Target 50% |
| 9 | Feedback Divider Constraint | $M = \dfrac{f_{VCO} \cdot D}{f_{in}}$ | Integer constraint | $M \in [1, 64]$ (typical) |
| 10 | VCO Period | $T_{VCO} = \dfrac{1}{f_{VCO}}$ | T_VCO in **ps** if f in **MHz** | $T_{VCO,min} \approx 625$ ps |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students frequently write $f_{out} = f_{in} \cdot M / D$ — this is **WRONG**. The correct expression is $f_{out} = f_{VCO}/O = f_{in} \cdot M/(D \cdot O)$. The `M` and `D` together produce $f_{VCO}$, which is *then* post-divided.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DDR Memory Interfaces**: DDR3/DDR4 use 90°/180°/270° phase-shifted clocks from an MMCM to center-align DQS with DQ.
- **PCIe / SerDes**: Multi-gigabit transceivers use PLL-based frequency synthesis at 100 MHz reference to derive 2.5/5/8 GHz.
- **Microprocessors in SoC**: ARM Cortex-A9 in Zynq-7000 uses internally synthesized clocks for CPU, DDR, and peripheral domains.
- **MIPI/CSI-2 Cameras**: Lane clocks are derived from a high-speed PLL output divided by the per-lane count.
- **SoC Boot-Up**: The on-chip ring oscillator (e.g., Xilinx `STARTUPE2` `CFGMCLK`) provides an *unguaranteed* clock during bitstream configuration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output Frequency from Reference Frequency

**Problem Statement:** An MMCM receives a reference clock of 100 MHz. The user programs $M = 10$, $D = 1$, and $O_0 = 2$. Determine the output frequency on port 0.

**Step 1 — Identify given values**

We are given the following quantities from the problem:

$$f_{in} = 100 \text{ MHz}, \quad M = 10, \quad D = 1, \quad O_0 = 2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the VCO Frequency**

Apply the VCO frequency equation $f_{VCO} = f_{in} \cdot M / D$ directly:

$$f_{VCO} = 100 \text{ MHz} \times \dfrac{10}{1} = 1000 \text{ MHz} = 1 \text{ GHz}$$

**Step 3 — Validate VCO bounds**

Check whether the computed $f_{VCO}$ lies within the legal operating range of the MMCM. For a typical 7-series Xilinx MMCM, the VCO must operate between **600 MHz and 1440 MHz**.

$$f_{VCO} = 1000 \text{ MHz} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 600 \le 1000 \le 1440 \; \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Compute Output Frequency on Port 0**

Apply the output divider equation $f_{out,0} = f_{VCO} / O_0$:

$$f_{out,0} = \dfrac{1000 \text{ MHz}}{2} = 500 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer**

The synthesized clock at port 0 is **500 MHz**, derived internally from a 100 MHz external reference.

$$\boxed{f_{out,0} = 500 \text{ MHz}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Phase Shift in Time Domain

**Problem Statement:** An MMCM has $f_{VCO} = 1200$ MHz. The user requires a phase shift of $\phi = 45^\circ$ on output port 1. Compute the time delay $\Delta t$ in picoseconds.

**Step 1 — Recall the VCO Period**

The VCO period is the inverse of the VCO frequency:

$$T_{VCO} = \dfrac{1}{f_{VCO}} = \dfrac{1}{1200 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Picoseconds**

Since $1 \text{ ns} = 1000 \text{ ps}$, we have $1/1200 \times 10^{-6} = 0.8333 \text{ ns}$, which equals 833.33 ps.

$$T_{VCO} = 833.33 \text{ ps}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Phase-to-Time Conversion**

The relation between phase and time delay is $\phi = 360^\circ \cdot \Delta t / T_{VCO}$. Rearranging for $\Delta t$:

$$\Delta t = \dfrac{\phi \cdot T_{VCO}}{360^\circ}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Values**

Insert $\phi = 45^\circ$ and $T_{VCO} = 833.33$ ps into the equation:

$$\Delta t = \dfrac{45^\circ \times 833.33 \text{ ps}}{360^\circ}$$

**Step 5 — Final Numerical Result**

Compute the division:

$$\Delta t = 0.125 \times 833.33 \text{ ps} = 104.17 \text{ ps}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta t \approx 104.17 \text{ ps}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reverse-Engineering M, D, O from a Target Frequency

**Problem Statement:** A reference clock of 200 MHz must produce 150 MHz on output port 2. Propose valid integer values of $M$, $D$, $O_2$ such that $f_{VCO}$ remains within 600–1440 MHz.

**Step 1 — Write the Governing Equation**

The combined output equation is $f_{out} = f_{in} \cdot M / (D \cdot O)$. Substituting known values:

$$150 = 200 \cdot \dfrac{M}{D \cdot O_2}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the Ratio**
