Cancel the common factor of 50 from both sides of the equation:

$$\dfrac{150}{200} = \dfrac{3}{4} = \dfrac{M}{D \cdot O_2}$$

**Step 3 — Choose Integers**

The simplest integer solution is to set the numerator to 3 and the denominator to 4, which gives $M = 3$ and $D \cdot O_2 = 4$.

**Step 4 — Distribute the Denominator**

Select $D = 1$ and $O_2 = 4$, since both are valid positive integers and $D \cdot O_2 = 1 \times 4 = 4$.

**Step 5 — Compute and Verify VCO**

Apply the VCO frequency formula to validate the operating range:

$$f_{VCO} = 200 \text{ MHz} \times \dfrac{3}{1} = 600 \text{ MHz}$$

Check the lower VCO bound: $600 \le 600 \le 1440 \; \checkmark$ — at the lower edge, which is acceptable.

**Step 6 — Final Answer**

$$\boxed{M = 3, \quad D = 1, \quad O_2 = 4, \quad f_{VCO} = 600 \text{ MHz}, \quad f_{out} = 150 \text{ MHz}}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — PLL vs. MMCM

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a high-frequency KTU exam topic. The examiner often frames a 7-mark question as *"Compare PLL and MMCM"* or *"Justify why MMCM is preferred over PLL in DDR interfaces."*

| Parameter | PLL (Phase-Locked Loop) | MMCM (Mixed-Mode Clock Manager) |
|---|---|---|
| Core Block | Pure analog feedback loop | PLL + additional digital duty-cycle corrector (DCC) |
| Frequency Synthesis | Yes (M, D, O dividers) | Yes (M, D, O dividers) |
| Fine Phase Shifting | Coarse (45° increments) | **Fine (1/56 of VCO period ≈ 7.14°)** |
| Duty Cycle Correction | Limited (approximate 50%) | **Precise 50/50 output** via fractional DCC |
| Output Ports | 6 (typical) | 7 (O0–O6) |
| Use Case | General fabric clocks | **DDR, source-synchronous, video timing** |
| Feedback Paths | 5 (CLKFB) | 5 (CLKFBIN/CLKFBOUT) |
| Power Consumption | Lower | Slightly higher due to DCC |

### 3.5 Edge-Case Numerical Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners sometimes test whether you can detect an **invalid** MMCM configuration. Below is a sanity-check example.

**Example:** $f_{in} = 50 \text{ MHz}, M = 50, D = 1, O_0 = 1$.

Apply the VCO formula to find the VCO operating frequency:

$$f_{VCO} = 50 \times 50 / 1 = 2500 \text{ MHz} = 2.5 \text{ GHz}$$

Check against the valid VCO operating range:

$$2500 \text{ MHz} > 1440 \text{ MHz} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{INVALID configuration}$$

**Resolution:** Either increase $D$ to 2 (giving $f_{VCO} = 1250$ MHz) or reduce $M$ to 28 (giving $f_{VCO} = 1400$ MHz).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of an MMCM (Internal Clock Generator)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    extClk[A] -->|f_in| pfdIn[B]
    intOsc[C] -->|f_int| pfdIn[B]
    pfdIn[B] -->|error signal| chPump[D]
    chPump[D] -->|control voltage| lpf[E]
    lpf[E] -->|analog Vctrl| vcoBlock[F]
    vcoBlock[F] -->|f_VCO| fbMux[G]
    fbMux[G] -->|divide by M| pfdIn[B]
    vcoBlock[F] --> divO0[H]
    vcoBlock[F] --> divO1[I]
    vcoBlock[F] --> divO2[J]
    divO0[H] --> outA0[K]
    divO1[I] --> outA1[L]
    divO2[J] --> outA2[M]
    outA0[K] --> bufgN[N]
    outA1[L] --> bufrP[O]
    outA2[M] --> bufhQ[P]
    bufgN[N] --> fabricR[Q]
    bufrP[O] --> regRegion[R]
    bufhQ[P] --> hRowBlock[S]
```

**Figure 4.1** — Functional architecture of an internally generated clock path inside an MMCM/PLL tile. The two input sources (`extClk` and `intOsc`) feed the PFD, which closes the feedback loop via divider `M`. The VCO drives three independent output divider paths (`divO0`, `divO1`, `divO2`), each routed through a different clock-buffer primitive (`BUFG`, `BUFR`, `BUFH`) into three distinct clock regions of the device fabric.

### 4.2 State Diagram of PLL Lock Acquisition

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Reset
    Reset --> WaitRef: Power-up complete
    WaitRef --> CoarseLock: Reference detected, f_in valid
    CoarseLock --> FineLock: Frequency within 1% tolerance
    FineLock --> Locked: Phase error below threshold
    Locked --> Locked: Steady-state operation
    Locked --> CoarseLock: Loss of reference / glitch
    CoarseLock --> WaitRef: Reference dropout
    Locked --> [*]: Power-down
```

**Figure 4.2** — State transition diagram of a PLL/MMCM lock-acquisition sequence. The locked state is the *steady-state*; any disturbance (jitter, reference dropout, reconfiguration) can cause a *fallback* to coarse-lock or complete reset.

### 4.3 Clock Distribution Hierarchy (Global Buffer Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pllOut[A] --> muxes[B]
    muxes[B] --> bufgCtrl[C]
    bufgCtrl[C] --> bufgA[D]
    bufgCtrl[C] --> bufgB[E]
    bufgA[D] --> horizRows[F]
    bufgB[E] --> horizRows[G]
    horizRows[F] --> logicCells[H]
    horizRows[G] --> logicCells[I]
    horizRows[F] --> bufhX[J]
    bufhX[J] --> clockRegionK[K]
    clockRegionK[K] --> blockRamL[L]
    clockRegionK[K] --> dspSlicesM[M]
```

**Figure 4.3** — Hierarchical clock distribution from a single internal source (`pllOut`) through `BUFGCTRL` multiplexers, into global horizontal clock rows, and finally to logic cells, Block RAM, and DSP slices. Note that **horizontal clock rows drive both logic and routing**, while **BUFH** drives only a *single regional column*.

### 4.4 Worked Example Data-Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refClkA[Z] --> divDB[Y]
    divDB[Y] --> pfdFB[X]
    pfdFB[X] --> chpumpFC[W]
    chpumpFC[W] --> lpfVC[V]
    lpfVC[V] --> vcoCoreU[U]
    vcoCoreU[U] --> oDivT[T]
    oDivT[T] --> bufgGlobalS[S]
    vcoCoreU[U] --> phaseShiftTapR[R]
    phaseShiftTapR[R] --> ddrBlockQ[Q]
    bufgGlobalS[S] --> fabricLogicP[P]
```

**Figure 4.4** — Data-flow for a typical SoC use case: a 200 MHz reference is internally divided, multiplied by the PLL, and then split into two output paths — one feeding a phase-shifted DDR block, the other feeding generic fabric logic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define an Internally Generated Clock. List three hardware primitives used to produce one inside an FPGA/SoC.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An *Internally Generated Clock* is a periodic timing signal synthesized entirely from on-chip resources (PLL, MMCM, ring oscillator, or logic-counter divider) without depending on an external pin.

Three hardware primitives used to produce internally generated clocks are:

1. **PLL (Phase-Locked Loop)** — multiplies a reference frequency and locks phase.
2. **MMCM (Mixed-Mode Clock Manager)** — PLL + duty cycle correction + fine phase shifting.
3. **On-chip ring/RC oscillator** — provides a low-accuracy clock during configuration and boot.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the role of the Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO) in an MMCM. What is the typical VCO frequency range for Xilinx 7-series devices?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **VCO** generates a high-frequency oscillation that is locked to the divided reference clock through the PLL feedback loop. The output frequency is given by $f_{VCO} = f_{in} \cdot M / D$.

The typical VCO frequency range for Xilinx 7-series MMCMs is **600 MHz to 1440 MHz**. The output divider `O` is then applied to produce the final user-visible clock.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Module 3 questions carry a 14-mark weight with internal choice. Both choices below are *fully independent* and exam-ready.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Frequency Synthesis using MMCM*

**(a)** *Explain the architecture of an MMCM with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each of the following: input divider D, multiplier M, output divider O, VCO, PFD, and charge pump.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *An MMCM receives a 200 MHz reference clock. It is configured with M = 12, D = 2, O₀ = 4, and O₁ = 6. Compute (i) the VCO frequency, (ii) the output frequencies at ports 0 and 1, and (iii) verify whether the VCO is operating within valid limits for a Xilinx 7-series device.* **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Architecture Explanation (7 marks):**

1. **Input Divider (D)** (1 mark): The reference clock is divided by an integer `D` before reaching the PFD. This allows the MMCM to accept a wide range of input frequencies and bring the PFD comparison rate down to a reasonable value.
2. **Multiplier (M) — Feedback Divider** (1 mark): The VCO output is divided by `M` and fed back to the PFD. This forces the VCO to oscillate at `M/D` times the input frequency.
3. **Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO)** (1 mark): The analog oscillator whose frequency is controlled by the loop-filter voltage. It produces $f_{VCO} = f_{in} \cdot M / D$.
4. **Phase-Frequency Detector (PFD)** (1 mark): Compares phase and frequency of the divided reference and feedback signals; outputs an error signal.
5. **Charge Pump + Loop Filter** (1 mark): Converts the PFD error pulses into a smooth analog control voltage for the VCO.
6. **Output Divider (O)** (1 mark): Post-divides the VCO output independently for each of the seven output ports.
7. **Block diagram** (1 mark): A labeled diagram showing all of the above connected in a closed feedback loop with VCO feeding the output dividers.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $f_{in} = 200$ MHz, $M = 12$, $D = 2$, $O_0 = 4$, $O_1 = 6$.

**Step 1 — Compute VCO Frequency (2 marks):**

Apply the VCO frequency equation $f_{VCO} = f_{in} \cdot M / D$ directly:

$$f_{VCO} = 200 \times \dfrac{12}{2} = 200 \times 6 = 1200 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 2 — Output Frequency at Port 0 (2 marks):**

Apply the output divider equation $f_{out,0} = f_{VCO} / O_0$:

$$f_{out,0} = \dfrac{1200}{4} = 300 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 3 — Output Frequency at Port 1 (2 marks):**

Apply the output divider equation $f_{out,1} = f_{VCO} / O_1$:

$$f_{out,1} = \dfrac{1200}{6} = 200 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 4 — Validity Check (1 mark):**

For Xilinx 7-series MMCM, the VCO must operate between 600 MHz and 1440 MHz. The computed $f_{VCO} = 1200$ MHz lies comfortably within this range.

$$\boxed{f_{VCO} = 1200 \text{ MHz}, \quad f_{out,0} = 300 \text{ MHz}, \quad f_{out,1} = 200 \text{ MHz}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *PLL vs. MMCM, Phase Shift, and On-Chip Oscillators*

**(a)** *Compare the architecture of a PLL and an MMCM. Why is MMCM preferred for DDR memory interfaces? Compute the time delay corresponding to a 30° phase shift if $f_{VCO} = 1000$ MHz.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Explain the role of on-chip ring oscillators in SoC boot-up. Describe the STARTUPE2 primitive in Xilinx FPGAs and its CFGMCLK output. List two advantages and two limitations of using ring-oscillator clocks.* **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — PLL vs. MMCM + Phase Shift Calculation (7 marks):**

**Architecture Comparison (4 marks):**

| Feature | PLL | MMCM |
|---|---|---|
| Internal VCO | Yes | Yes |
| Duty-cycle corrector (DCC) | No | **Yes** |
| Fine phase-shift taps | Limited (≈45°) | **Fine (1/56 of VCO period)** |
| Output ports | 6 | 7 (O0–O6) |
| Feedback paths | 5 | 5 |

**Why MMCM is preferred for DDR (2 marks):**
- DDR memory interfaces require **90°/180°/270° phase-shifted clocks** to center-align DQS with DQ strobes.
- The MMCM provides **fine-grained phase shifting** (≈ 7.14° per tap), enabling sub-nanosecond alignment.
- The integrated DCC provides a **precise 50% duty cycle** required by DDR sampling.

**Phase Shift Calculation (1 mark):**

**Step 1:** Compute the VCO period from $T_{VCO} = 1 / f_{VCO}$:

$$T_{VCO} = 1 / 1000 \text{ MHz} = 1 \text{ ns} = 1000 \text{ ps}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the phase-to-time equation $\Delta t = \phi \cdot T_{VCO} / 360°$:

$$\Delta t = 30^\circ \times 1000 \text{ ps} / 360^\circ = 83.33 \text{ ps}$$

**Part (b) — On-Chip Ring Oscillators + STARTUPE2 (7 marks):**

**Role in Boot-Up (2 marks):**
- During bitstream configuration, *no external clock is guaranteed*.
- An on-chip **ring oscillator** (typically 50–200 MHz) provides a free-running clock for the configuration logic, JTAG, and the `STARTUPE2` interface.

**STARTUPE2 Primitive (2 marks):**
- `STARTUPE2` is a Xilinx primitive that connects the FPGA fabric to the **configuration-mode pins**.
- Its `CFGMCLK` output provides access to the internal ring-oscillator clock to the user logic during and after configuration.
- It is commonly used in low-power designs to *avoid* an external oscillator when only a rough clock is acceptable (e.g., blinking an LED, polling a sensor).

**Two Advantages (1.5 marks):**
1. Eliminates the need for an external oscillator → reduces BOM cost and PCB area.
2. Always available; can be used as a *fallback* when the primary clock fails.

**Two Limitations (1.5 marks):**
1. **High jitter and process variation** — frequency is *unguaranteed* and may vary by ±20% across PVT corners.
2. **Not suitable for high-speed interfaces** (DDR, PCIe, SerDes) — these require precise, low-jitter clocks.

---

### 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Internally Generated Clocks questions:**
> 1. **Confusing $f_{VCO}$ with $f_{out}$** — KTU examiners specifically deduct 1 mark if you write $f_{out} = f_{in} \cdot M/D$ instead of the correct $f_{out} = f_{in} \cdot M / (D \cdot O)$.
> 2. **Forgetting to validate the VCO range** — when computing $f_{VCO}$, you *must* check 600 ≤ f_VCO ≤ 1440 MHz (Xilinx 7-series). Skipping this loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Phase-shift unit mismatch** — converting between degrees and picoseconds incorrectly. Always use $T = 1/f$ first, then $\Delta t = (\phi / 360) \cdot T$.
> 4. **Mixing up BUFG / BUFR / BUFH** — BUFG is global (whole chip), BUFR is regional (clock region), BUFH is half-row (one column of clock regions). A wrong answer here is an instant 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Not drawing a closed-loop feedback path** — a PLL block diagram without the *feedback divider M going back to the PFD* is incomplete and loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Internally Generated Clocks**
> - **Definition:** Clock synthesized *on-chip* from PLL, MMCM, ring oscillator, or counter logic.
> - **MMCM = PLL + DCC + fine phase shifting** (1/56 of VCO period ≈ 7.14° per tap).
> - **Core equation:** $f_{VCO} = f_{in} \cdot M / D$; **Output:** $f_{out} = f_{VCO} / O$.
> - **Valid VCO range (Xilinx 7-series):** **600 MHz to 1440 MHz**.
> - **Valid input range:** 10 MHz to 800 MHz (typical).
> - **Seven output ports** in MMCM (O0–O6), each independently divisible.
> - **Five feedback paths** allow internal/external feedback, divided feedback, etc.
> - **Phase shift time:** $\Delta t = (\phi / 360^\circ) \cdot T_{VCO}$, with $T_{VCO} = 1 / f_{VCO}$.
> - **BUFG** = global clock buffer; **BUFR** = regional; **BUFH** = half-row.
> - **PLL feedback structure:** Input → Divider D → PFD → Charge Pump → Loop Filter → VCO → Output Divider O → Buffer → Fabric; VCO output is divided by M and fed back to PFD.
> - **STARTUPE2.CFGMCLK** = on-chip ring-oscillator clock available during/after configuration.
> - **PLL is preferred** for general fabric clocks; **MMCM is preferred** for DDR, video, and source-synchronous interfaces.
> - **Jitter** = timing deviation of clock edges; ring-oscillator clocks have the *worst* jitter; PLL/MMCM have the *best*.
> - **Duty cycle correction (DCC)** is a *defining* feature of MMCM (not present in basic PLL).
> - **Lock time** = duration from power-up to "locked" state; typically 100 µs – 1 ms.
> - **Loss of lock** occurs on reference dropout, frequency out of range, or excessive jitter.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internally Generated Resets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT602 - SYSTEM ON CHIP DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Internally Generated Resets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internally Generated Resets** are reset signals that originate from within the System-on-Chip (SoC) boundary itself, triggered by on-chip monitoring circuits, embedded processors, watchdog timers, or fault-detection logic. Unlike external resets (which are asserted by off-chip sources like a push-button, JTAG controller, or power management IC), internally generated resets are produced by IP blocks that reside physically inside the die.

These resets are typically classified into **hardware-generated** resets (caused by physical events such as voltage droops or clock failures) and **software-generated** resets (initiated by firmware executing on an embedded CPU). In modern SoCs, internally generated resets are orchestrated by a centralized **Reset Management Unit (RMU)** or **Reset Controller** that aggregates, synchronizes, and distributes them to the appropriate reset domains.

### 1.2 Key Categories of Internally Generated Resets

| Category | Trigger Source | Typical Use Case |
|----------|---------------|-----------------|
| Power-on Reset (POR) | Voltage detector on VDD ramp | Initial system bring-up |
| Brown-out Reset (BOR) | Voltage dips below threshold | Protection during under-voltage |
| Watchdog Timer Reset | Counter overflow | Recovery from code hang |
| Software Reset | Firmware write to reset register | Controlled subsystem restart |
| Clock Loss Reset | Clock monitor circuit | PLL unlock / oscillator failure |
| Thermal Reset | Thermal sensor trip | Over-temperature protection |
| JTAG / Debug Reset | Debug controller command | Development and testing |
| Lockup Reset | CPU fault detection | HardFault / Lockup state recovery |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition - The Building's Self-Correcting Nervous System:**
> Imagine a large office building. The **external reset** is like the master power switch on the building's exterior, controlled by the city power company. The **internal resets** are like the building's *own* safety systems: smoke detectors (thermal reset), circuit breakers (brown-out reset), sprinkler systems (over-temperature), and a supervisor who notices if the security guard falls asleep at his post (watchdog reset). The building is intelligent enough to *reset* specific sections of itself without waiting for the city to cut power. SoC internally generated resets operate in exactly the same way — the chip can "self-heal" sub-sections without requiring the external world to intervene.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Logic Threshold (CMOS):** $V_{IL} \le 0.3 \times V_{DD}$ and $V_{IH} \ge 0.7 \times V_{DD}$
- **Power-on Reset Trip Voltage (typical):** $V_{POR} \approx 0.7 \times V_{DD}$ (range **1.0 V to 1.8 V**)
- **Brown-out Reset Trip Voltage:** $V_{BOR} \approx 0.85 \times V_{DD_{nominal}}$
- **Watchdog Timeout Range:** **1 ms to 60 s** (configurable)
- **Reset Pulse Width (minimum):** $t_{RST_{min}} \ge 1$ clock cycle of the slowest target domain

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Taxonomy of Internal Resets

Internally generated resets are best understood through a hierarchical taxonomy based on **trigger source**, **scope of effect**, and **synchronization behavior**.

#### 2.1.1 By Trigger Source

1. **Voltage-Domain Resets (Analog)**
   - Power-on Reset (POR)
   - Brown-out Reset (BOR)
2. **Time-Domain Resets (Digital)**
   - Watchdog Timer (WDT) reset
   - Software-requested reset
3. **Fault-Domain Resets (Protective)**
   - Clock loss / PLL unlock reset
   - Thermal shutdown reset
   - ECC (Error-Correcting Code) unrecoverable error reset
4. **Debug-Domain Resets**
   - JTAG reset (TAP-driven)
   - CPU halt / lockup reset

#### 2.1.2 By Scope of Effect

- **Cold Reset (Full Chip):** Clears all flip-flops, re-initializes RAMs, restarts PLLs. Equivalent to a power-cycle.
- **Warm Reset (Subsystem):** Clears logic state but retains certain configuration registers, PLL lock, and debug state.
- **Local Reset (Peripheral):** Resets only a specific IP block without disturbing the rest of the SoC.

### 2.2 The Reset Generation and Distribution Pipeline

The journey of an internal reset traverses four canonical stages:

1. **Detection Stage:** A sensor or monitor circuit identifies the triggering event (e.g., voltage falling below $V_{BOR}$).
2. **Generation Stage:** The detector asserts a raw, often asynchronous reset signal.
3. **Synchronization Stage:** A two-flip-flop synchronizer removes metastability risks when crossing clock domains.
4. **Distribution Stage:** The synchronized reset is fanned out to the relevant reset domain, optionally through a reset de-assertion sequencer to control the order of release.

### 2.3 Reset Assertion vs De-assertion Semantics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Asymmetric Behavior:** The *assertion* of an internal reset is typically **asynchronous** (must catch faults at any time, even between clock edges), but the *de-assertion* (release) is **synchronous** to a target clock domain to prevent metastability on flip-flops coming out of reset.

This dual-nature is captured by the acronym **"AARD"** — **A**synchronous **A**ssertion, **R**eset, **S**ynchronous **D**e-assertion — a design pattern taught in advanced digital design courses.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description |
|--------------------|------------|-------------|
| POR Trip Voltage | $V_{POR} = k_{POR} \cdot V_{DD_{nom}}$ | Typical $k_{POR} \in [0.6, 0.8]$ |
| Brown-out Trip Voltage | $V_{BOR} = k_{BOR} \cdot V_{DD_{nom}}$ | Typical $k_{BOR} \approx 0.85$ |
| Hysteresis (Voltage Reset) | $V_{hys} = V_{release} - V_{trip}$ | Prevents chattering |
| Watchdog Timeout | $T_{WDT} = N_{counts} \cdot T_{clk}$ | Where $N_{counts}$ is the prescaler count |
| Reset Synchronizer MTBF | $MTBF = \frac{e^{t_{r}/\tau}}{f_{clk} \cdot f_{async}}$ | $\tau$ = metastability resolution time |
| De-assertion Skew | $t_{skew} = t_{last} - t_{first}$ | Difference across reset fan-out |
| Reset Recovery Time | $t_{rr} \ge t_{cq} + t_{setup}$ | Time before clock can be applied after reset release |
| Removal Time | $t_{rem} \ge t_{cq} + t_{su}$ | Same as recovery in modern terminology |
| Reset Distribution Delay | $t_{dist} = t_{wire} + t_{buffer} \cdot N_{levels}$ | Wire + buffer tree delay |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-pipe Note:** All absolute values in the table above use $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation to avoid breaking the markdown table structure.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Safety-Critical SoCs (ISO 26262, ASIL-D):** Internally generated resets are mandated by functional safety standards. A watchdog timeout, for example, is a **detection mechanism** for software faults.
- **Low-Power IoT SoCs:** Brown-out reset ensures the system is never operational in an indeterminate voltage state, which could corrupt non-volatile memory.
- **High-Performance Compute SoCs:** Per-subsystem local resets allow partial re-initialization without bringing down the entire chip, improving availability and fault isolation.
- **Automotive Microcontrollers:** Thermal and voltage resets form the backbone of the safety chain, often orchestrated by a dedicated **Fault Collection and Control Unit (FCCU)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Watchdog Timer Reset Generation

The watchdog timer is one of the most frequently examined internal resets. Below is the complete derivation of its timing behavior.

**Given:**
- System clock frequency: $f_{clk} = 50$ MHz
- Watchdog prescaler: $N_{pre} = 4096$
- Watchdog counter width: $n = 16$ bits
- The counter increments every $N_{pre}$ clock cycles.

**Step 1: Compute the effective clock period at the counter input.**

The counter sees a tick once every $N_{pre}$ system clock cycles. Therefore, the effective tick period is:

$$
T_{tick} = N_{pre} \cdot T_{clk} = N_{pre} \cdot \frac{1}{f_{clk}}
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
T_{tick} = 4096 \cdot \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{6}} = 4096 \cdot 20 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} = 81.92 \text{ \mu s}
$$

**Step 2: Determine the maximum count value before overflow.**

A 16-bit counter wraps from $2^{16} - 1$ to $0$. The maximum count is:

$$
N_{max} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536
$$

**Step 3: Derive the watchdog timeout.**

The total time before the counter overflows and triggers a reset is:

$$
T_{WDT} = N_{max} \cdot T_{tick}
$$

$$
T_{WDT} = 65{,}536 \cdot 81.92 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{WDT} = 5.3687 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4: Express the general formula.**

$$
\boxed{T_{WDT} = 2^{n} \cdot N_{pre} \cdot \frac{1}{f_{clk}}}
$$

**Step 5: Realistic engineering example.**

For an automotive ECU running at $f_{clk} = 200$ MHz with $N_{pre} = 1024$ and $n = 32$ bits:

$$
T_{WDT} = 2^{32} \cdot 1024 \cdot \frac{1}{200 \times 10^{6}} = 4.398 \times 10^{6} \cdot 5.12 \times 10^{-6} \approx 22{,}517 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{WDT} \approx 6.25 \text{ hours}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Reset Synchronizer Metastability MTBF

A reset synchronizer uses two flip-flops in series. The probability of metastability propagation failure is governed by the Mean Time Between Failures formula.

**Step 1: Identify the parameters.**

Let:
- $f_{clk}$ = sampling clock frequency
- $f_{async}$ = frequency of asynchronous reset assertion
- $t_{r}$ = available resolution time (typically one clock period)
- $\tau$ = metastability time constant of the flip-flop technology (typically 10–50 ps)

**Step 2: Write the MTBF expression.**

The standard metastability MTBF for a single-flop synchronizer is:

$$
MTBF = \frac{e^{t_{r}/\tau}}{f_{clk} \cdot f_{async}}
$$

**Step 3: Apply a two-flop synchronizer.**

For a two-flop synchronizer, the failure probability is squared (in a simplified first-order analysis), giving:

$$
MTBF_{2FF} = \frac{e^{2 \cdot t_{r}/\tau}}{f_{clk} \cdot f_{async}}
$$

**Step 4: Plug in typical values.**

Suppose $f_{clk} = 1$ GHz, $f_{async} = 10$ kHz, $t_{r} = 1$ ns, $\tau = 20$ ps:

$$
MTBF_{2FF} = \frac{e^{2 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-9} / 20 \times 10^{-12}}}{10^{9} \cdot 10^{4}}
$$

$$
MTBF_{2FF} = \frac{e^{100}}{10^{13}} = \frac{2.688 \times 10^{43}}{10^{13}} = 2.688 \times 10^{30} \text{ seconds}
$$

Converting to years:

$$
MTBF_{2FF} = \frac{2.688 \times 10^{30}}{3.154 \times 10^{7}} \approx 8.52 \times 10^{22} \text{ years}
$$

This astronomical MTBF justifies the ubiquitous use of the two-flop reset synchronizer in production silicon.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Internal Reset Types

| Reset Type | Latency | Software Awareness | Power Impact | Typical Use |
|------------|---------|--------------------|--------------|-------------|
| Power-on Reset | **High** (ms) | None (cold start) | Full chip | Initial boot |
| Brown-out Reset | **Low** (µs) | Often masked by hardware | Full chip | Voltage sag protection |
| Watchdog Reset | **Programmable** (ms–s) | Yes (firmware must service WDT) | Full or partial | Hang recovery |
| Software Reset | **Low** (few cycles) | Yes (intentional) | Subsystem only | Graceful restart |
| Clock Loss Reset | **Medium** (µs) | Optional interrupt | Full chip | PLL unlock recovery |
| Thermal Reset | **Medium** (ms) | Often only interrupt | Full chip | Over-temp shutdown |
| Lockup Reset | **Low** (cycles) | None (CPU is hung) | CPU subsystem | HardFault recovery |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    POR[PON Detector VDD Ramp]
    BOR[Brownout Comparator VDD Sense]
    WDT[Watchdog Timer Overflow]
    SWRST[Software Reset Register]
    CLKLO[Clock Loss Monitor PLL]
    THERM[Thermal Sensor Trip]
    DBG[JTAG Debug TAP Reset]

    RMU[Reset Management Unit RMU]

    SYNC1[Sync Stage Domain A]
    SYNC2[Sync Stage Domain B]
    SYNC3[Sync Stage Domain C]

    CPU[CPU Subsystem Reset]
    MEM[Memory Controller Reset]
    PER[Peripheral Bus Reset]
    DBGSYS[Debug Logic Reset]

    POR --> RMU
    BOR --> RMU
    WDT --> RMU
    SWRST --> RMU
    CLKLO --> RMU
    THERM --> RMU
    DBG --> RMU

    RMU --> SYNC1
    RMU --> SYNC2
    RMU --> SYNC3

    SYNC1 --> CPU
    SYNC2 --> MEM
    SYNC3 --> PER
    SYNC1 --> DBGSYS
```

### 4.2 Reset Synchronizer (Two-Flop) Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    ASYNC[Async Reset Input] --> FF1[Sync FF1]
    FF1 --> FF2[Sync FF2]
    CLK_TARGET[Target Domain Clock] --> FF1
    CLK_TARGET --> FF2
    FF2 --> RST_DIST[Distributed Reset Output]
```

**Explanation:** The asynchronous reset is sampled by the first flip-flop on the rising edge of the target clock. The output of FF1 is then sampled by FF2, which produces a clean, metastability-free reset output synchronized to the target domain.

### 4.3 Reset De-assertion Sequencer Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Internal Reset Asserted] --> CLKOK[Wait for Clock Stable]
    CLKOK --> PLL[Wait for PLL Lock]
    PLL --> CPUREL[Release CPU Reset]
    CPUREL --> MEMREL[Release Memory Controller Reset]
    MEMREL --> PERREL[Release Peripheral Bus Reset]
    PERREL --> DBGREL[Release Debug Reset]
    DBGREL --> DONE[System Ready]
```

**Explanation:** This staged de-assertion prevents race conditions where, for example, the CPU might fetch instructions from a memory controller that is still in reset.

### 4.4 Watchdog Timer Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    CLK[System Clock] --> PRE[Prescaler N pre]
    PRE --> CNT[16 bit Up Counter]
    CNT --> OVF[Overflow Detector]
    OVF --> RSGEN[Reset Generator Pulse Shaper]
    SVC[Service Routine from Firmware] --> CLR[Counter Clear]
    CLR --> CNT
    RSGEN --> OR[OR with Other Resets]
```

**Explanation:** The counter increments based on a prescaled clock. If firmware fails to clear the counter before it overflows, a reset pulse is generated and ORed with the global reset tree.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**
>
> **Q1.** Differentiate between externally and internally generated resets in an SoC. Give two examples of each.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - *External resets* are asserted by off-chip sources such as a manual push-button, power management IC, or JTAG probe.
> - *Internal resets* are generated within the chip boundary by on-die circuits.
> - Examples of internal resets: **Power-on Reset (POR)** and **Watchdog Timer Reset**.
> - Examples of external resets: **External reset pin (nRST)** and **JTAG nTRST**.

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
>
> **Q2.** Why is the de-assertion of an internal reset performed synchronously even though its assertion is asynchronous?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. *Assertion must be asynchronous* so that the reset captures the system immediately, even if the clock is unstable or absent due to the fault condition.
> 2. *De-assertion must be synchronous* to the target domain clock to prevent metastability in flip-flops exiting the reset state.
> 3. Asynchronous release could violate setup/hold times of flip-flops, causing unpredictable initial states. The synchronous release, typically through a two-flop synchronizer, guarantees a clean exit.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper, Module 3]**

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an internally generated reset distribution network in a modern SoC. Highlight the role of the Reset Management Unit (RMU). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a watchdog timer for a Cortex-M4 based SoC clocked at 72 MHz, with a prescaler of 256 and a 16-bit counter. Compute the maximum timeout. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) RMU Architecture (7 marks):**

The Reset Management Unit sits at the heart of the SoC reset tree. Its primary functions are:

1. **Aggregation:** Collects reset requests from POR, BOR, WDT, software, clock monitor, and thermal sensor.
2. **Prioritization:** A fixed or programmable priority encoder decides which reset dominates when multiple sources assert simultaneously (e.g., a watchdog reset during a brown-out).
3. **Synchronization:** Each reset source is passed through a two-flop synchronizer before fan-out to its target domain.
4. **Sequencing:** The de-assertion follows a strict order — clocks first, then PLLs, then CPU, then memory, then peripherals, then debug.
5. **Masking and Status:** Some resets can be software-masked (e.g., during ROM bootloader execution). A status register records the last reset cause for post-mortem diagnostics.

A typical RMU block diagram is shown in Section 4.1 above, and the key roles are as enumerated. **(Full 7 marks for naming all 5 roles with brief description.)**

**(b) Watchdog Design (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $f_{clk} = 72$ MHz
- Prescaler $N_{pre} = 256$
- Counter width $n = 16$ bits

**Step 1:** Compute the system clock period.

$$
T_{clk} = \frac{1}{72 \times 10^{6}} = 13.89 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the prescaled tick period.

$$
T_{tick} = 256 \cdot 13.89 \text{ ns} = 3.556 \text{ \mu s}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the maximum count value.

$$
N_{max} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the maximum timeout.

$$
T_{WDT} = 65{,}536 \cdot 3.556 \text{ \mu s} = 0.2330 \text{ s} \approx 233 \text{ ms}
$$

**Answer:** The maximum watchdog timeout is approximately **233 ms**, suitable for a fast-recovery automotive safety loop. **(7 marks complete.)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the Power-on Reset (POR) and Brown-out Reset (BOR) generation mechanisms. Why is hysteresis critical in these circuits? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a waveform diagram (textual representation), describe the *asynchronous assertion — synchronous de-assertion* (AARD) pattern. Show how metastability is avoided using a two-flop synchronizer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) POR and BOR (7 marks):**

- **POR Generation:** A bandgap reference and a resistor divider compare $V_{DD}$ against a fixed internal reference. When $V_{DD}$ rises above $V_{POR}$ (typically $0.7 \times V_{DD_{nom}}$), the comparator output toggles and releases the reset. POR is used only at cold start.
- **BOR Generation:** BOR is similar to POR but has a *lower* trip voltage and remains active during normal operation. When $V_{DD}$ dips below $V_{BOR} \approx 0.85 \times V_{DD_{nom}}$ during operation, BOR asserts to prevent logic malfunction.
- **Hysteresis Need:** Without hysteresis, the BOR comparator would chatter at the threshold boundary, generating spurious resets. The hysteresis band is implemented using a Schmitt trigger:

$$
V_{hys} = V_{release} - V_{trip}
$$

A typical value is **50 mV to 200 mV**, ensuring clean transitions.

**(b) AARD Pattern (7 marks):**

```
   Async Reset   ___________________
   (asserted)   |                   |
                |                   |
                |    ASYNCHRONOUS   |   SYNCHRONOUS
                |    ASSERTION      |   DE-ASSERTION
                |___________________| 
                                   |
   Target Clock __|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__
                                  ↑
                              De-asserted
                              on clock edge
```

- The reset is asserted *immediately* on detection of the fault, without waiting for any clock edge.
- The reset is released *synchronously* with the rising edge of the target domain clock, after being filtered through a two-flop synchronizer.
- The two-flop synchronizer chain (FF1 → FF2) ensures that any metastable state in FF1 resolves to a stable value by the time it reaches the output of FF2.
- This pattern guarantees that no flip-flop in the SoC experiences a setup/hold violation on reset release.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing "reset" with "clock" synchronizers.** Reset synchronizers must tolerate an *asynchronous input* that can stay asserted for thousands of clock cycles. Using a single-flop synchronizer for reset is incorrect and will fail in silicon.
> 2. **Forgetting de-assertion sequencing.** A common student mistake is to assume all resets release simultaneously. In reality, releasing the CPU before the memory controller causes the CPU to fetch garbage instructions — a fatal error. Always mention *sequencing* in your answer.
> 3. **Ignoring metastability MTBF.** Examiners love the numerical MTBF calculation. If a question mentions a 1 GHz clock and 10 kHz reset assertion rate, expect a follow-up asking for MTBF.
> 4. **Not stating the trip voltages.** Even in descriptive answers, mention the typical $V_{POR}$ and $V_{BOR}$ percentages — this shows depth and earns extra marks.
> 5. **Treating software reset and watchdog reset as identical.** Software reset is *intentional* and *predictable*; watchdog reset is *involuntary* and indicates a fault. Examiners will deduct marks for conflating the two.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Internally Generated Resets**

- **Definition:** Resets generated *inside* the SoC die by on-chip monitoring circuits, processors, or IP blocks.
- **POR (Power-on Reset):** Cold-start reset triggered by $V_{DD}$ ramp. Trip voltage $\approx 0.7 \cdot V_{DD_{nom}}$.
- **BOR (Brown-out Reset):** Active during operation. Triggers when $V_{DD}$ droops below $\approx 0.85 \cdot V_{DD_{nom}}$.
- **Watchdog Timer (WDT):** Firmware must periodically service the counter; overflow triggers reset. Formula:

$$
T_{WDT} = 2^{n} \cdot N_{pre} \cdot \frac{1}{f_{clk}}
$$

- **Software Reset:** Firmware writes to a reset control register; typically warm, scoped, and intentional.
- **Clock Loss Reset:** Issued when the PLL loses lock or the input clock disappears.
- **Thermal Reset:** Triggered by the on-die thermal sensor crossing the shutdown threshold (typically $125°C$ to $150°C$ for automotive).
- **Lockup Reset:** Triggered by the CPU itself upon entering an unrecoverable fault (e.g., ARM Cortex-M Lockup state).
- **AARD Pattern:** Asynchronous Assertion, Reset, Synchronous De-assertion. Mandatory for all internal resets.
- **Two-Flop Synchronizer:** The standard structure to safely bring an asynchronous reset into a clock domain.
- **MTBF Equation:**

$$
MTBF_{2FF} = \frac{e^{2 t_{r}/\tau}}{f_{clk} \cdot f_{async}}
$$

- **De-assertion Order:** Clocks → PLLs → CPU → Memory → Peripherals → Debug. Never release the CPU before its memory subsystem.
- **Hysteresis:** Critical in POR/BOR comparators; typically 50–200 mV; prevents chattering at the threshold boundary.
- **Cold vs Warm vs Local Reset:** Cold = full chip; Warm = subsystem with retention; Local = single IP block.
- **Standards:** In automotive SoCs (ISO 26262), internal resets are part of the safety mechanism. In medical (IEC 62304), they are considered fault-recovery primitives.
- **Examiner Triggers:** Always mention metastability MTBF, de-assertion sequencing, and the difference between assertion and de-assertion semantics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Issues for Hard Macros - Design for Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT602 - SYSTEM ON CHIP DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design Issues for Hard Macros - Design for Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hard Macros in SoC Design — Formal Definition

A **Hard Macro** is a pre-designed, pre-verified, and fully placed-and-routed Intellectual Property (IP) block that is delivered to the System-on-Chip (SoC) integrator as a **black-box with fixed geometry, fixed layout, and fixed timing closure**. Unlike soft macros (RTL/behavioral code) or firm macros (gate-level netlists), hard macros are physically opaque, meaning the internal logic cannot be modified, re-synthesized, or optimized by the SoC integrator.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, hard macros are typically delivered with:
- A **GDS-II layout view** (fully physical)
- A **timing library** (.lib with SDF back-annotation)
- A **power library** (switching activity based)
- A **test interface** (often pre-defined and limited)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PCEVT602 Module 4):**
> Hard macros in modern SoCs include SRAM compilers, PLL/DLL blocks, ADCs/DACs, SerDes, PHY IPs (USB, PCIe, DDR), ROM, and standard cell macros. Their hard nature introduces **unique Design-for-Test (DFT) challenges** because internal scan chains, test points, and observability/controllability of internal nodes are restricted.

## 1.2 Design for Test (DFT) — Formal Definition

**Design for Test (DFT)** refers to the set of Hardware Description Language (HDL) and physical design techniques used during chip design to **enhance the testability of a digital circuit**, allowing manufacturing defects to be detected economically through structured Automatic Test Pattern Generation (ATPG) and Built-in Self-Test (BIST) mechanisms.

The KTU 2024 Scheme defines DFT as a **non-functional design requirement** that must be addressed concurrently with functional, timing, and power design — not as an afterthought.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Real-World Intuition

**Analogy 1 — The Pre-Built Apartment Building:**
Imagine you are an architect constructing a large residential complex. Instead of designing each apartment from scratch, you order **pre-built apartment modules** (hard macros) from a vendor. You cannot open these modules to rewire the internal plumbing. To test whether the bathroom pipes work, the vendor must give you **external test ports** (valves) and a **standard test procedure** (test protocol). DFT is essentially this "external test protocol" — it ensures that the internals of a pre-built block can be tested from the outside without opening the box.

**Analogy 2 — The Car Diagnostic System (OBD-II):**
Modern cars have an On-Board Diagnostics (OBD-II) port. The engine (a hard macro from the supplier's perspective) cannot be opened by the mechanic, but a standard test interface (OBD-II) allows the mechanic to:
- Inject test signals (controllability)
- Observe sensor outputs (observability)
- Diagnose faults systematically (structured test)

This is exactly the role of **JTAG/IEEE 1149.1** and **IEEE 1500** wrapper structures for hard macros.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Terminology Lock-In (Mandatory for KTU Exams):**
> - **Controllability:** Ability to drive any internal node to a 0 or 1 from primary inputs.
> - **Observability:** Ability to propagate the state of any internal node to a primary output.
> - **Fault Coverage:** Ratio of detected faults to total possible faults.
> - **ATPG:** Automatic Test Pattern Generation — algorithmic generation of input vectors.
> - **DUT:** Device Under Test.
> - **TAP:** Test Access Port (the JTAG controller interface).

## 1.4 Why Hard Macros Pose a DFT Challenge

| Aspect | Soft Macro | Hard Macro | DFT Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Internal Visibility | Full (RTL available) | None (GDS-II only) | Cannot insert scan internally |
| Modification Freedom | Complete | None | Must use external wrappers |
| Test Pattern Source | ATPG on full design | Vendor-supplied patterns | Pattern compatibility issues |
| Test Interface | Customizable | Fixed by vendor | Standardization (IEEE 1500) needed |
| Timing Closure | Adjustable | Fixed | Test-mode timing must respect macro timing |

The fundamental DFT problem with hard macros is: **"How do you test something you cannot see inside?"** The solution is to wrap the macro in a structured test architecture and rely on **self-test (BIST)** for the internals.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fault Model Hierarchy

To test a hard macro, the SoC integrator must first understand the **fault model** assumed by the vendor. The most common models, in increasing complexity, are:

1. **Stuck-at Fault Model (SAF):** Assumes any signal line is permanently stuck at logic 0 (s-a-0) or logic 1 (s-a-1). This is the **most widely used model** for digital hard macros.
2. **Bridging Fault Model:** Models unintended shorts between two signal lines.
3. **Transition Delay Fault Model (TDF):** Models slow-to-rise or slow-to-fall defects.
4. **Path Delay Fault Model:** Models cumulative delay along a specific combinational path.
5. **IDDQ Fault Model:** Detects defects by measuring quiescent supply current.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For hard macros (especially mixed-signal and memory), the **functional fault model** is often more practical than the structural SAF model. Memories use **March tests**, analog blocks use **specification-based tests**, and RF blocks use **loopback tests**.

## 2.2 The DFT Architecture for Hard Macros

A modern SoC uses a **three-tier DFT architecture** to manage hard macros:

### Tier 1 — Chip-Level Access
The **IEEE 1149.1 (JTAG)** TAP controller provides a serial test access port (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO, optional TRST*) that allows the tester to communicate with the entire chip. This is the **on-ramp** for test data.

### Tier 2 — Core-Level Wrapper
The **IEEE 1500** standard defines a scalable, core-based test architecture. Each hard macro is wrapped with:
- A **Wrapper Boundary Register (WBR)** — a chain of wrapper cells around the macro I/Os.
- A **Wrapper Instruction Register (WIR)** — defines the test mode.
- A **Wrapper Bypass Register (WBY)** — provides a short path through inactive macros.

### Tier 3 — Internal Self-Test
**Built-in Self-Test (BIST)** structures embedded inside the hard macro:
- **Memory BIST (MBIST)** for SRAM/DRAM macros.
- **Logic BIST (LBIST)** for digital logic macros.
- **Analog BIST (ABIST)** for PLLs, ADCs, DACs.

## 2.3 Built-in Self-Test (BIST) — Deep Dive

BIST is the **dominant DFT technique for hard macros** because it eliminates the need for external test pattern storage and expensive Automatic Test Equipment (ATE).

### 2.3.1 BIST Architecture Components

1. **Test Pattern Generator (TPG):** Generates patterns on-chip. Common types:
   - **Linear Feedback Shift Register (LFSR)** — generates pseudo-random patterns.
   - **Counter** — generates exhaustive patterns for small macros.
   - **ROM-stored patterns** — for deterministic ATPG patterns.
2. **Response Analyzer (RA):** Compresses the macro's output responses. Common types:
   - **Multiple Input Signature Register (MISR)** — produces a final "signature" that is compared to the golden signature.
   - **Counters** — count transitions (transition fault testing).
3. **BIST Controller:** Coordinates the TPG, RA, and timing.
4. **Comparator:** Compares the final MISR signature with the stored golden value to declare **Pass/Fail**.

### 2.3.2 LFSR — The Heart of Pseudo-Random BIST

An **n-bit LFSR** produces a maximum-length pseudo-random sequence of length $2^n - 1$ (when using a primitive polynomial). The sequence is generated by XOR-ing specific bits (taps) and feeding back to the input.

For an **n-bit external-XOR LFSR**, the recurrence relation is:

$$d_i = s_i \oplus s_{i-t}$$

where $s_i$ is the i-th bit of the current state, and $t$ is the tap position defined by the primitive polynomial.

### 2.3.3 MISR Signature Compression

The MISR compresses the response sequence into a single **n-bit signature** using the recurrence:

$$S_{k+1} = (M \cdot S_k + R_k) \mod 2$$

where:
- $S_k$ is the k-th state of the MISR (an n-bit vector).
- $M$ is the companion matrix of the chosen primitive polynomial.
- $R_k$ is the k-th response bit (or word) from the macro output.

A mismatch between the final $S_N$ and the golden signature $S_{gold}$ indicates a fault. The probability of **aliasing** (faulty circuit producing the correct signature by chance) is:

$$P_{alias} = 2^{-n}$$

This is why MISRs are chosen with $n \geq 16$ for production-quality BIST.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All absolute values and conditionals below use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the vertical pipe `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | KTU Exam Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fault Coverage (FC) | $FC = \dfrac{F_d}{F_t} \times 100\%$ | $F_d$ = detected faults, $F_t$ = total faults | Direct 3-mark definition question |
| 2 | Defect Level (DL) | $DL = 1 - Y^{(1-FC)}$ | $Y$ = process yield, $FC$ = fault coverage | Williams-Brown formula; numerical problem |
| 3 | Test Length (TL) | $TL = L \times N$ | $L$ = pattern length per chain, $N$ = number of patterns | Scan chain design calculation |
| 4 | LFSR Max Sequence Length | $L_{max} = 2^n - 1$ | $n$ = number of LFSR flip-flops | BIST architecture question |
| 5 | MISR Aliasing Probability | $P_{alias} = 2^{-n}$ | $n$ = MISR length in bits | Justify MISR bit-width |
| 6 | Scan Chain Test Time | $T_{scan} = (L_{chain} + N_{pat}) \times T_{clock}$ | $L_{chain}$ = chain length, $N_{pat}$ = # patterns, $T_{clock}$ = period | Test cost optimization |
| 7 | BIST Area Overhead | $OH_{BIST} = \dfrac{A_{BIST}}{A_{core}} \times 100\%$ | $A_{BIST}$ = BIST logic area, $A_{core}$ = core area | Design trade-off question |
| 8 | March Test Complexity | $C_{march} = \sum_{i} O_i$ | $O_i$ = number of operations in i-th march element | Memory BIST design |
| 9 | Toggle Coverage | $TC = \dfrac{N_{toggled}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $N_{toggled}$ = toggled nodes, $N_{total}$ = total nodes | Stuck-at coverage surrogate |
| 10 | Stuck-at Fault Count | $F_{total} = 2 \times N_{lines}$ | $N_{lines}$ = total signal lines in netlist | Initial fault count |
| 11 | IDDQ Test Limit | $I_{DDQ,max} = I_{leak,max} \times N_{gates}$ | $I_{leak,max}$ = max leakage per gate | Quiescent current test design |
| 12 | Compressed Test Data | $CR = \dfrac{T_{orig}}{T_{comp}}$ | $T_{orig}$ = uncompressed test data, $T_{comp}$ = compressed | Test compression ratio |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in Industry

In the semiconductor industry, **DFT is mandatory** for any SoC targeting high-volume production (qualcomm, intel, nvidia, etc.). The reasons are:

1. **Cost:** ATE time costs \$0.05–\$0.50 per second. BIST reduces test time by **10x to 100x**.
2. **Defect Coverage:** Modern nanometer processes (7nm, 5nm, 3nm) have defect rates that mandate **>99% fault coverage**.
3. **In-Field Test:** JTAG enables in-system test after deployment (e.g., automotive, aerospace).
4. **Yield Learning:** IDDQ and BIST data feed back to process engineers for yield improvement.
5. **Hard Macro Integration:** Without standardized DFT (IEEE 1500, IEEE 1149.1), integrating third-party IP would be impossible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Learning Outcome Mapping:**
> This topic directly addresses:
> - **CO4:** Understand design challenges in deep submicron SoC integration.
> - **CO5:** Apply DFT techniques for manufacturability of complex SoCs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fault Coverage and Defect Level Relationship

The **Williams-Brown equation** relates fault coverage, yield, and defect level. This is a classic KTU numerical problem.

### Given:
- Process Yield $Y$ (fraction of chips that are defect-free).
- Fault Coverage $FC$ (fraction of faults detected by test).
- Defect Level $DL$ (fraction of shipped chips that are defective).

### Derivation (Step-by-Step):

**Step 1:** Define the probability that a good chip passes the test.
A good chip has no defects, so it always passes. Probability = $Y$.

**Step 2:** Define the probability that a defective chip passes the test (escapes).
A defective chip escapes test if the test does not detect its fault. The probability of escape for a defective chip is $(1 - FC)$.

**Step 3:** The probability that a shipped chip is defective AND escaped test is:
$$P_{bad\_and\_escaped} = (1 - Y) \times (1 - FC)$$

**Step 4:** The total probability of a chip being shipped is:
$$P_{shipped} = P_{good\_shipped} + P_{bad\_and\_escaped}$$
$$P_{shipped} = Y + (1 - Y)(1 - FC)$$

**Step 5:** The Defect Level (DL) is the conditional probability that a shipped chip is defective:
$$DL = \dfrac{P_{bad\_and\_escaped}}{P_{shipped}}$$
$$DL = \dfrac{(1 - Y)(1 - FC)}{Y + (1 - Y)(1 - FC)}$$

**Step 6:** Simplify by dividing numerator and denominator by $(1 - Y)$:
$$DL = \dfrac{1 - FC}{\dfrac{Y}{1-Y} + 1 - FC}$$

**Step 7:** Let $z = \dfrac{Y}{1 - Y}$ (the yield odds). Then:
$$DL = \dfrac{1 - FC}{z + 1 - FC}$$

**Step 8:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $z^{-1}$:
$$DL = \dfrac{(1 - FC) / z}{1 + (1 - FC) / z}$$

**Step 9:** Substitute back $z^{-1} = \dfrac{1 - Y}{Y}$:
$$DL = \dfrac{(1 - FC) \cdot (1 - Y) / Y}{1 + (1 - FC)(1 - Y) / Y}$$

**Step 10:** The standard KTU form (after algebraic simplification, multiplying both num and den by $Y$):
$$DL = \dfrac{(1 - Y)(1 - FC)}{Y + (1 - Y)(1 - FC)}$$

**Step 11:** An alternative simplified form often used in textbooks assumes $(1 - Y)(1 - FC) \ll Y$, giving the approximation:
$$DL \approx (1 - Y)(1 - FC) / Y$$

Or, equivalently:
$$DL \approx 1 - Y^{(1 - FC)}$$

This is the **Williams-Brown approximation**, used when FC is high (>95%) and Y is moderate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU style):**
> A 28nm SoC has a process yield $Y = 0.85$ and a fault coverage $FC = 0.98$. Calculate the defect level.
>
> **Solution:**
> $$DL = (1 - Y)(1 - FC) / Y = (0.15)(0.02) / 0.85 = 0.00353$$
> $$DL = 353 \text{ DPPM (Defective Parts Per Million)}$$
>
> This is within typical industry targets (< 1000 DPPM).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — LFSR Sequence Length and Period

### Given:
An n-bit LFSR with a primitive feedback polynomial $p(x) = x^n + c_{n-1}x^{n-1} + \ldots + c_1 x + c_0$.

### Derivation:

**Step 1:** The state of the LFSR is an n-bit vector $S_k = [s_{k,0}, s_{k,1}, \ldots, s_{k,n-1}]^T$.

**Step 2:** For an **external-XOR (Galois) LFSR**, the next state is computed as:
$$S_{k+1} = M \cdot S_k \pmod{2}$$

where $M$ is the **companion matrix** of the polynomial.

**Step 3:** The state evolves as:
$$S_k = M^k \cdot S_0 \pmod{2}$$

**Step 4:** Since $M$ is a matrix over GF(2), and the state space has $2^n$ possible values (excluding the all-zero state for a non-trivial cycle), the maximum period is:
$$L_{max} = 2^n - 1$$

**Step 5:** This maximum is achieved if and only if the feedback polynomial is **primitive** over GF(2). A primitive polynomial of degree $n$ exists for every $n$ except for a few small values.

**Step 6:** Example — for $n = 4$, a primitive polynomial is $p(x) = x^4 + x + 1$. The LFSR has period $2^4 - 1 = 15$.

**Step 7:** The sequence of 15 unique non-zero 4-bit states is generated before the cycle repeats.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Test Pattern Count for Stuck-at Faults

### Given:
A combinational circuit with $N$ primary inputs (PI) and $M$ primary outputs (PO).

### Derivation:

**Step 1:** Each signal line in the circuit can be either stuck-at-0 (s-a-0) or stuck-at-1 (s-a-1). For a netlist with $L$ signal lines (including PIs, POs, and internal lines), the total number of possible single stuck-at faults is:
$$F_{total} = 2L$$

**Step 2:** Fault collapsing (equivalence and dominance) reduces this number. A typical collapse ratio is **40–60%**, giving:
$$F_{collapsed} \approx F_{total} \times 0.5$$

**Step 3:** The number of test patterns required for 100% fault coverage of a combinational circuit is bounded by:
$$N_{patterns} \leq 2^{N_{PI}}$$

where $N_{PI}$ is the number of primary inputs. This is the **exhaustive testing upper bound**.

**Step 4:** In practice, ATPG algorithms (D-algorithm, PODEM, FAN) achieve 100% coverage of collapsed faults with $N_{patterns} \ll 2^{N_{PI}}$.

**Step 5:** For a sequential circuit, the bound becomes:
$$N_{patterns,seq} \leq 2^{N_{PI} + N_{FF}}$$

where $N_{FF}$ is the number of flip-flops. This is why **scan design** (converting flip-flops to controllable/observable elements) is essential.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — BIST Area Overhead vs. Test Time Trade-off

### Given:
- Core area $A_{core}$ = 1,000,000 $\mu m^2$ (1 mm²).
- LFSR area = 200 gates.
- MISR area = 200 gates.
- BIST controller area = 400 gates.
- Gate area = 5 $\mu m^2$ each (in 28nm).

### Derivation:

**Step 1:** Total BIST logic gates:
$$G_{BIST} = 200 + 200 + 400 = 800 \text{ gates}$$

**Step 2:** Total BIST area:
$$A_{BIST} = 800 \times 5 = 4000 \ \mu m^2 = 0.004 \ mm^2$$

**Step 3:** Area overhead:
$$OH_{BIST} = \dfrac{4000}{1,000,000} \times 100\% = 0.4\%$$

**Step 4:** Test time for $N_{patterns} = 10,000$ patterns at $T_{clock} = 10$ ns:
$$T_{test} = 10,000 \times 10 \text{ ns} = 100 \ \mu s$$

**Step 5:** Without BIST (using external ATE), the test data transfer time for 10,000 patterns × 64 bits per pattern at 100 MHz ATE clock:
$$T_{ATE} = 10,000 \times 64 / (100 \times 10^6) = 6.4 \ ms$$

**Step 6:** Speedup factor:
$$Speedup = T_{ATE} / T_{BIST} = 6.4 \ ms / 100 \ \mu s = 64 \times$$

This demonstrates why BIST is preferred for hard macros: **negligible area overhead, massive test time reduction**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — DFT Technique Selection for Hard Macros

| Hard Macro Type | Primary Fault Model | Recommended DFT | Standard Protocol | Typical Coverage Target |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SRAM Compiler | Functional (memory) | Memory BIST (MBIST) | IEEE 1500 + March SS | 100% of memory faults |
| DRAM Controller | Functional | MBIST + Parity/ECC | Custom wrapper | 100% |
| PLL / DLL | Functional (frequency, jitter) | Analog BIST (ABIST) | Loopback test | Spec-based |
| ADC / DAC | Functional (SNR, INL, DNL) | ABIST | Histogram test | 16-bit linearity |
| USB / PCIe PHY | Functional + protocol | Loopback + BIST | Compliance test | Protocol-aware |
| Standard Cell Macro | Stuck-at + transition | Wrapper + Scan | IEEE 1500 | > 99% |
| ROM | Stuck-at | Signature analysis | Checksum | 100% |
| SerDes | Functional (BER, eye) | Eye monitor + PRBS | Loopback + PRBS | BER < 1e-12 |
| RF Transceiver | Functional (gain, NF) | Loopback + ABIST | Spec-based | Spec-based |

## 3.6 Step-by-Step — Memory March Test Design

The **March SS** test detects all unlinked static faults (stuck-at, transition, coupling, address decoder faults) in a memory.

**March SS Notation:**
- $\Uparrow$ : address goes from 0 to N-1 (ascending).
- $\Downarrow$ : address goes from N-1 to 0 (descending).
- $r0$ : read operation expecting 0.
- $r1$ : read operation expecting 1.
- $w0$ : write 0.
- $w1$ : write 1.

**March SS Sequence (length = 22N):**

$$\{\Uparrow w0; \Uparrow r0, w0, r0, r0, w1, r1; \Uparrow r1, w1, r1, w1, w0, w0; \Downarrow r0, w0, w0, w1, r1; \Downarrow r1, w1, w1, w0, r0\}$$

**Complexity Analysis:**
- Total operations = 22N (where N is word count).
- Test time = $22N \times T_{cycle}$.
- For a 1Mb memory with $T_{cycle} = 5$ ns:
  $$T_{test} = 22 \times 1,048,576 \times 5 \text{ ns} = 115.3 \text{ ms}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — SoC DFT Architecture with Hard Macros

flowchart TB
    soc["SoC Top Level"]

    wir["Wrapper Instruction Register<br/>WIR"]

    soc --> tap

```

flowchart TD

    req --> dft_strategy
    dft_strategy --> wrapper_design

```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Scan Chain Architecture for Digital Hard Macros

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    si["Scan In<br/>Primary Input"]
    ff1["FF 1<br/>Scan Flip Flop"]
    ff2["FF 2<br/>Scan Flip Flop"]
    ff3["FF 3<br/>Scan Flip Flop"]
    ff4["FF N<br/>Scan Flip Flop"]
    combo["Combinational Logic<br/>Cloud"]
    so["Scan Out<br/>Primary Output"]
    mux_a["MUX A<br/>Function or Scan"]
    mux_b["MUX B<br/>Function or Scan"]

    si --> ff1
    ff1 --> ff2
    ff2 --> ff3
    ff3 --> ff4
    ff4 --> so
    combo --> ff1
    combo --> ff2
    combo --> ff3
    combo --> ff4
    mux_a --> ff1
    mux_b --> ff4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for KTU Exam:**
> In the scan chain, each flip-flop has a 2:1 MUX at its input. In **functional mode**, the MUX selects the normal combinational logic output. In **scan mode**, the MUX selects the previous flip-flop's output, creating a long shift register. This makes all flip-flops **controllable and observable** through scan-in (SI) and scan-out (SO) pins.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Design for Test (DFT) and explain why it is essential for hard macro integration in SoCs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Design for Test (DFT) is the discipline of incorporating extra hardware structures (such as scan chains, BIST engines, boundary scan wrappers, and test access ports) into an integrated circuit during the design phase to enhance the **controllability** and **observability** of internal nodes, thereby enabling efficient detection of manufacturing defects using Automatic Test Equipment (ATE) or self-test mechanisms.

**Why essential for hard macros (1.5 marks):**
Hard macros are delivered as pre-designed, fully placed-and-routed GDS-II blocks with **no internal visibility** to the SoC integrator. Without DFT, it is impossible to:
- Apply test patterns to internal nodes (controllability failure).
- Observe internal responses (observability failure).
- Achieve the >99% fault coverage required by industry standards.

DFT solves this by providing **standardized test access** (IEEE 1149.1 JTAG + IEEE 1500 wrapper) and **self-test capability (BIST)** embedded within the macro, allowing comprehensive testing without modifying the macro internals.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between Memory BIST (MBIST) and Logic BIST (LBIST). Mention one application each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Memory BIST (MBIST) | Logic BIST (LBIST) |
|---|---|---|
| Target | Memory arrays (SRAM, DRAM, ROM) | Random logic blocks |
| Pattern Generation | Algorithmic (March tests: MATS+, March SS) | Pseudo-random (LFSR-based) |
| Response Analysis | MISR or comparator | MISR |
| Test Length | Short (22N to 14N operations) | Long (10⁴ to 10⁶ patterns) |
| Coverage | 100% of memory faults | ~95–99% of logic faults |
| Application (1 mark) | SRAM compiler in an SoC | Standard cell-based digital hard macro |

**Application of MBIST (0.5 mark):** Testing the 4MB L2 cache SRAM in a processor SoC.
**Application of LBIST (0.5 mark):** Testing the USB 3.0 controller logic block.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, Choice A]**

**a)** Explain the architecture of Built-in Self-Test (BIST) for hard macros with a neat block diagram. List the main components and describe the role of LFSR and MISR. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A 32nm SoC integrates a hard macro with 500,000 signal lines. The process yield is 90% and the desired defect level is 1000 DPPM. Calculate the minimum fault coverage required. Use the Williams-Brown approximation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to Mermaid Diagram 2 in Section 4.2 of these notes. The BIST architecture consists of:

1. **BIST Controller (FSM):** Coordinates all BIST activities — initialization, pattern application, response capture, signature comparison, and result reporting.

2. **Test Pattern Generator (TPG) — LFSR (2 marks):**
   - A Linear Feedback Shift Register is an n-bit shift register with XOR feedback taps.
   - When configured with a primitive polynomial, it generates a maximum-length pseudo-random sequence of $2^n - 1$ states.
   - For example, a 16-bit LFSR with primitive polynomial $p(x) = x^{16} + x^{15} + x^{13} + x^4 + 1$ generates 65,535 unique 16-bit patterns.
   - Each pattern is applied to the hard macro's primary inputs.

3. **Device Under Test (DUT):** The hard macro itself.

4. **Response Analyzer (RA) — MISR (2 marks):**
   - A Multiple Input Signature Register is an n-bit LFSR-like structure that compresses the hard macro's output responses into a single n-bit "signature" after all patterns are applied.
   - The recurrence is $S_{k+1} = (M \cdot S_k + R_k) \pmod{2}$.
   - The final signature is compared with the pre-computed "golden" signature (the signature obtained from a fault-free simulation).

5. **Comparator:** XORs the final MISR signature with the golden signature. If all bits match → PASS; otherwise → FAIL.

**Advantages (extra credit):** Self-contained testing, low ATE cost, at-speed testing capability, and in-field test support.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Identify the given data (1 mark).**
- Yield $Y = 0.90$
- Defect Level $DL = 1000 \text{ DPPM} = 1000 \times 10^{-6} = 0.001$
- Number of signal lines = 500,000 (extra information, not needed for this formula).

**Step 2: Recall the Williams-Brown approximation formula (1 mark):**
$$DL \approx 1 - Y^{(1 - FC)}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the known values (2 marks):**
$$0.001 = 1 - (0.90)^{(1 - FC)}$$

**Step 4: Rearrange to solve for $(1 - FC)$ (1 mark):**
$$(0.90)^{(1 - FC)} = 1 - 0.001 = 0.999$$

**Step 5: Take the natural logarithm of both sides (1 mark):**
$$(1 - FC) \cdot \ln(0.90) = \ln(0.999)$$

**Step 6: Substitute the numerical values of the logarithms (0.5 mark):**
- $\ln(0.90) = -0.10536$
- $\ln(0.999) = -0.0010005$

**Step 7: Solve for $(1 - FC)$ (0.5 mark):**
$$1 - FC = \dfrac{-0.0010005}{-0.10536} = 0.009497$$

**Step 8: Solve for $FC$ (0.5 mark):**
$$FC = 1 - 0.009497 = 0.9905$$

**Final Answer (0.5 mark):**
$$\boxed{FC_{min} \approx 99.05\%}$$

This is the **minimum fault coverage** required to keep the defect level at or below 1000 DPPM for a process with 90% yield. The 500,000 signal lines information could be used to compute the total stuck-at fault count: $F_{total} = 2 \times 500,000 = 1,000,000$ faults, of which 990,500 must be detected.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4, Choice B]**

**a)** What is an LFSR? With a neat diagram, explain the working of a 4-bit LFSR using the primitive polynomial $p(x) = x^4 + x + 1$. List all the states it cycles through. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Explain the IEEE 1500 standard for testing embedded cores (hard macros). List its main components and explain the function of the Wrapper Boundary Register (WBR). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (1 mark):** A Linear Feedback Shift Register (LFSR) is a shift register whose input bit is a linear function (typically XOR) of its previous state. When the feedback polynomial is primitive over GF(2), the LFSR cycles through all $2^n - 1$ non-zero states before repeating.

**Diagram (2 marks):**

```
       +----+    +----+    +----+    +----+
   --> | Q3 | --> | Q2 | --> | Q1 | --> | Q0 | -->
       +----+    +----+    +----+    +----+
         |                     |
         |                     |
         +------- XOR <--------+
                  ^
                  |
        (also XOR with Q3 going to Q2, etc.)
```

For the **Galois (external-XOR) LFSR** with $p(x) = x^4 + x + 1$, the taps are at positions 4 and 1 (i.e., the outputs of Q3 and Q0 are XORed and fed back to the input of Q3).

**State Sequence (4 marks):**

Starting from the seed state `[1, 0, 0, 0]` (Q3 Q2 Q1 Q0):

| Step | Q3 | Q2 | Q1 | Q0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 3 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| 5 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 6 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| 7 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| 8 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| 9 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 10 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| 11 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| 12 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 13 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 14 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| 15 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| 16 (=1) | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

The cycle length is $2^4 - 1 = 15$, confirming that $p(x) = x^4 + x + 1$ is indeed a primitive polynomial.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**IEEE 1500 Standard Overview (2 marks):**
IEEE 1500 is a standard for **scalable, core-based test architecture** for embedded cores in System-on-Chip designs. It defines a standardized test wrapper that allows:
- Isolation of each core for individual test.
- Parallel test of multiple cores.
- Reuse of test patterns across different SoC integrations.

**Main Components (3 marks):**

1. **Wrapper Boundary Register (WBR):** A series of wrapper cells (one per core I/O) that can capture functional data, inject test data, or observe test responses. The WBR is the key interface between the core and the SoC.

2. **Wrapper Instruction Register (WIR):** Decodes the current test mode (normal, Intest, Extest, Bypass) and configures the WBR accordingly.

3. **Wrapper Bypass Register (WBY):** A single-bit register that provides a short path through the wrapper when the core is not being tested, allowing the test data to flow to other cores.

4. **Wrapper Interface Port (WIP):** The serial and parallel test access ports (WSI, WSO, WPI, WPO).

5. **Wrapper Parallel Control (WPC) and Wrapper Serial Control (WSC):** Control signals for parallel and serial test access.

**Function of WBR (2 marks):**
The Wrapper Boundary Register:
- Surrounds the hard macro with a chain of shift-register-like cells.
- In **Extest mode** (testing interconnect between cores): drives known values from the wrapper outputs and captures responses at the wrapper inputs of the next core.
- In **Intest mode** (testing the core's internals): applies test patterns to the core's inputs and captures responses at the core's outputs.
- In **Normal mode**: is transparent, passing functional data through unchanged.
- In **Bypass mode**: routes the serial test data directly from WSI to WSO via the WBY.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Hard Macro DFT Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing LFSR with MISR (–1 mark):** Students often interchange the roles. Remember: **LFSR generates patterns** (output is the test stimulus); **MISR compresses responses** (output is the final signature).
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "$-1$" in LFSR length (–1 mark):** The maximum sequence length is $2^n - 1$, NOT $2^n$, because the all-zero state is a fixed point and is excluded in a primitive LFSR.
>
> 3. **Wrong base for logarithm in Williams-Brown (–2 marks):** The formula uses the **natural logarithm (ln)**, not $\log_{10}$. Students using $\log_{10}$ get incorrect answers.
>
> 4. **Not specifying "single stuck-at fault" assumption (–0.5 mark):** When asked to count faults, always state the assumption. The default in KTU exams is the **single stuck-at fault model**.
>
> 5. **Missing the role of IEEE 1500 vs. IEEE 1149.1 (–1 mark):** IEEE 1149.1 (JTAG) is the **chip-level** serial test access; IEEE 1500 is the **core-level** wrapper standard. They are **complementary, not alternatives**.
>
> 6. **Skipping the units in numerical answers (–0.5 mark):** Always specify units (e.g., DPPM, mm², ns, %, etc.). A numerical answer without units is considered incomplete.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the seed state in LFSR diagrams (–0.5 mark):** When drawing an LFSR, always specify the **initial state (seed)**. A 4-bit LFSR has 15 possible non-zero seed states, all of which generate the same cycle but in different starting positions.
>
> 8. **Aliasing in MISR (–1 mark):** When a MISR is used, there is a non-zero probability of **aliasing** ($P = 2^{-n}$), where a faulty circuit produces the correct signature. Always mention this and justify the MISR bit-width (typically $n \geq 16$).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- [ ] **Hard Macro:** Pre-designed, GDS-II-level IP block with fixed layout, timing, and no internal visibility.
- [ ] **Soft Macro:** RTL-level deliverable, fully modifiable by the SoC integrator.
- [ ] **Firm Macro:** Gate-level netlist with place-and-route information, partially modifiable.
- [ ] **DFT Goal:** Maximize **controllability** and **observability** of internal nodes.
- [ ] **Stuck-at Fault Model:** Each line is assumed to be permanently s-a-0 or s-a-1.
- [ ] **Fault Coverage Formula:** $FC = F_d / F_t \times 100\%$.
- [ ] **Williams-Brown Defect Level:** $DL \approx 1 - Y^{(1-FC)}$ or $DL = (1-Y)(1-FC)/Y$ for high-coverage cases.
- [ ] **LFSR:** Linear Feedback Shift Register with primitive polynomial, generates $2^n - 1$ pseudo-random patterns.
- [ ] **MISR:** Multiple Input Signature Register, compresses responses into an n-bit signature with $2^{-n}$ aliasing probability.
- [ ] **March SS Test:** 22N complexity, detects all unlinked static faults in memories.
- [ ] **BIST Components:** TPG (LFSR), Response Analyzer (MISR), BIST Controller (FSM), Comparator.
- [ ] **IEEE 1149.1 (JTAG):** Chip-level serial test access via TAP controller (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO, TRST*).
- [ ] **IEEE 1500:** Core-level wrapper standard with WBR, WIR, WBY for testing embedded hard macros.
- [ ] **Wrapper Modes:** Normal, Intest (test core), Extest (test interconnect), Bypass.
- [ ] **Scan Chain:** Series connection of flip-flops via 2:1 MUX, enables serial shift-in/out of test data.
- [ ] **At-Speed Testing:** Testing at the functional clock frequency to catch delay defects.
- [ ] **DFT Area Overhead:** Typically 5–15% for scan, 1–3% for BIST.
- [ ] **Hard Macro Test Strategies:** MBIST for memory, ABIST for analog, LBIST for logic, wrapper for isolation.
- [ ] **Pattern Compatibility:** Hard macro test patterns from vendor must be integrated with SoC-level ATPG.
- [ ] **Industry Standard Coverage Target:** > 99% for high-volume SoCs; > 95% for low-cost mixed-signal ICs.
- [ ] **Test Compression:** Reduces ATE data volume by 10x–100x using techniques like Golomb coding, LFSR reseeding.
- [ ] **BIST vs. External ATE:** BIST saves ATE cost, enables in-field test, at-speed operation; external ATE provides higher coverage and easier debug.
- [ ] **Key Trade-off:** DFT area overhead vs. test time vs. fault coverage — these three parameters must be balanced for optimal SoC design.
- [ ] **Memory Test Operations:** w0, w1, r0, r1 with $\Uparrow$ (ascending) and $\Downarrow$ (descending) address sequences.
- [ ] **Aliasing:** Probability that a faulty circuit produces the same MISR signature as a fault-free circuit.
- [ ] **Primitive Polynomial:** A polynomial that generates the maximum-length LFSR sequence of $2^n - 1$.
- [ ] **Test Access Mechanism (TAM):** The transport mechanism (wires, buses) for delivering test data to/from the hard macro.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
