# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Operating Frequency for a Hard Macro Pipeline Stage

**Problem Setup (KTU 2024 Style):**
A hard macro contains a 4-stage synchronous pipeline clocked at frequency $f_{clk}$. Each stage has the following worst-case parameters (at the SS, 0.81V, 125°C corner):

- $T_{cq} = 70$ ps
- $T_{logic,max} = 320$ ps
- $T_{su} = 45$ ps
- $T_{skew} = 25$ ps
- $T_{margin,setup} = 30$ ps (for OCV, jitter, derate)

**Derive the maximum safe clock frequency $f_{max}$.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Write the **setup-time timing equation** for a single pipeline register.

The clock period $T_{clk}$ must be long enough to accommodate the data path delay plus all the timing overheads.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{clk} &\geq T_{cq} + T_{logic,max} + T_{su} + T_{skew} + T_{margin,setup} \\
T_{clk} &\geq 70 + 320 + 45 + 25 + 30 \\
T_{clk} &\geq 490 \text{ ps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert the minimum clock period to frequency.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{max} &= \frac{1}{T_{clk,min}} \\
f_{max} &= \frac{1}{490 \times 10^{-12}} \\
f_{max} &= \frac{1}{4.9 \times 10^{-10}} \\
f_{max} &= 2.0408 \times 10^{9} \text{ Hz} \\
f_{max} &\approx 2.04 \text{ GHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply a **guard-band multiplier** of 5% (industry-standard for PVT derate) for sign-off.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{sign-off} &= f_{max} \times (1 - 0.05) \\
f_{sign-off} &= 2.04 \times 0.95 \\
f_{sign-off} &= 1.938 \text{ GHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Sanity check with the hold-time equation (must always be satisfied — it's a *race-through* check, independent of $T_{clk}$).

Given $T_{logic,min} = 180$ ps (best-case data path):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{hold,slack} &= T_{cq} + T_{logic,min} - T_{skew} - T_{hold,flop} \\
T_{hold,slack} &= 70 + 180 - 25 - 35 \\
T_{hold,slack} &= 190 \text{ ps} \quad (\text{positive } \Rightarrow \text{ no hold violation})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Always check **both** setup (frequency-bound) **and** hold (race-bound) at sign-off. A common KTU trap is to compute only setup, missing the hold violation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Power Budget for a Hard Macro

**Problem Setup:**
A hard macro contains $N = 1{,}200{,}000$ gates. Each gate has:
- Average load capacitance $C = 1.5$ fF (including wire cap)
- Activity factor $\alpha = 0.12$ (typical for a control-dominated block)
- $V_{dd} = 0.75$ V
- Leakage current per gate $I_{leak} = 8$ nA
- Clock frequency $f = 1.5$ GHz

**Compute the total power.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the **dynamic power**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{dynamic} &= \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f \cdot N \\
P_{dynamic} &= 0.12 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-15} \times (0.75)^{2} \times 1.5 \times 10^{9} \times 1.2 \times 10^{6} \\
P_{dynamic} &= 0.12 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-15} \times 0.5625 \times 1.5 \times 10^{9} \times 1.2 \times 10^{6} \\
P_{dynamic} &= (0.12 \times 0.5625 \times 1.5 \times 1.2) \times (1.5 \times 10^{-15} \times 10^{9} \times 10^{6}) \\
P_{dynamic} &= 0.1215 \times 1.5 \times 10^{0} \\
P_{dynamic} &= 0.18225 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — re-checking the order-of-magnitude:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{dynamic} &= 0.12 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-15} \times 0.5625 \times 1.5 \times 10^{9} \times 1.2 \times 10^{6} \\
&= 0.12 \times 1.5 \times 0.5625 \times 1.5 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-15+9+6} \\
&= 0.18225 \times 10^{0} \\
P_{dynamic} &\approx 0.182 \text{ W} = 182 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the **leakage power**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{leakage} &= V_{dd} \cdot I_{leak} \cdot N \\
P_{leakage} &= 0.75 \times 8 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.2 \times 10^{6} \\
P_{leakage} &= 7.2 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the **total power**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{total} &= P_{dynamic} + P_{leakage} \\
P_{total} &= 182 \text{ mW} + 7.2 \text{ mW} \\
P_{total} &= 189.2 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** At 1.5 GHz, dynamic power dominates by **~25×** over leakage. If we were to lower the frequency to 100 MHz (a low-power mode), leakage would become comparable, and **Power Gating (MTCMOS)** techniques would be required.

## 3.3 Derivation: IR Drop on a Power Mesh

**Problem Setup:**
A hard macro has a power mesh made of M1/M2 stripes. The grid resistance from the pad to the center of the macro is $R_{grid} = 0.45 \,\Omega$. The average current drawn by the core is $I_{avg} = 250$ mA. The nominal supply is $V_{dd,nom} = 0.75$ V.

**Compute the worst-case IR drop and the effective voltage at the macro center.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Apply Ohm's law.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{drop} &= I_{avg} \cdot R_{grid} \\
V_{drop} &= 0.25 \times 0.45 \\
V_{drop} &= 0.1125 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the effective voltage.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{eff} &= V_{dd,nom} - V_{drop} \\
V_{eff} &= 0.75 - 0.1125 \\
V_{eff} &= 0.6375 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the **percentage drop** and check against the foundry limit (typically 2–5%).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%V_{drop} &= \frac{V_{drop}}{V_{dd,nom}} \times 100\% \\
\%V_{drop} &= \frac{0.1125}{0.75} \times 100\% \\
\%V_{drop} &= 15\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A **15% drop is unacceptable** — the macro will fail timing and may not function. The mitigation is to **thicken the power mesh** (use M3/M4 stripes), **add more power pads**, or **reduce $R_{grid}$** by switching from a 2-layer to a 3-layer mesh.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Students often forget that IR drop is **dynamic** as well. Peak current (e.g., during a clock edge) can be **2–3×** the average, leading to even larger transient drops.

## 3.4 KTU Analytical Matrix: Hard Macro vs. Soft Macro Trade-off

| **Design Attribute** | **Soft Macro (RTL)** | **Hard Macro (GDSII)** | **Verdict** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Time-to-Market | Fast (RTL) | Slow (full phys) | Soft wins for prototyping |
| Performance | Variable | Best (PPA-tuned) | Hard wins for production |
| Power | Variable | Lowest (Vt opt, clock gating) | Hard wins |
| Area | Variable (synthesis-dependent) | Smallest (cell-level opt) | Hard wins |
| Flexibility | High | Low (frozen layout) | Soft wins |
| Testability | DFT inserted by integrator | DFT pre-inserted | Tie |
| Portability | High (process-independent) | Low (process-bound) | Soft wins |
| IP Cost | Low (RTL licensing) | High (GDSII licensing) | Soft wins on cost |
| Risk of Re-spin | Low | Higher (any PVT bug = mask re-spin) | Soft wins |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Takeaway:** Hard macros are the **right choice for high-volume, performance-critical blocks** (CPUs, GPUs, PHYs). Soft macros are the **right choice for low-volume, design-flexibility-driven blocks** (control logic, glue logic).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between a hard macro and a soft macro in the context of SoC design. List two examples of blocks typically delivered as hard macros.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Soft Macro** | **Hard Macro** |
|---|---|---|
| Form of Delivery | RTL (synthesizable HDL) | GDSII (physical layout) |
| Flexibility | Fully flexible | Frozen / opaque |
| Performance | Synthesis-dependent | Pre-optimized (best PPA) |
| Portability | Process-independent | Process / node-locked |
| Design Effort | Low (RTL only) | High (full phys + sign-off) |

**Two examples of hard macros:**
1. **CPU core** (e.g., Arm Cortex-A series).
2. **Memory block** (e.g., compiled SRAM, ROM).

**Other valid examples:** GPU, PHY, PLL, ADC/DAC, SerDes, standard cell library.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is macro characterization? Why is it necessary before integrating a hard macro into an SoC?**

**Model Answer:**

**Macro characterization** is the process of generating a Liberty (`.lib`) timing/power library and a LEF abstract view from a layout-frozen hard macro, by sweeping input slew and output load combinations across all PVT corners using SPICE simulation.

**Why it is necessary:**

1. The SoC integrator needs **timing, power, and noise models** to perform top-level synthesis, placement, and STA.
2. The integrator cannot see *inside* the macro (it's opaque), so the `.lib` is the only way to communicate electrical behavior.
3. Without characterization, **timing sign-off at the SoC level is impossible**.
4. The `.lib` enables **synthesis tools** (DC, Genus) to optimize the logic *around* the macro correctly.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse **macro characterization** with **macro verification**. Characterization generates the *abstract behavioral model*; verification proves the macro itself is *functionally and physically correct*. They are distinct steps.

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**
**Explain the seven phases of the hard macro design process with a neat block diagram. Highlight the deliverables at each stage.**

**Model Answer:**

The hard macro design process comprises seven sequential, iterative phases:

**Phase 1 — Micro-Architectural Specification:**
- Define PPA (Power-Performance-Area) budget.
- Choose process node, Vt-mix, and clocking strategy.
- **Deliverable:** Architectural spec document, PPA budget sheet.

**Phase 2 — RTL Design & Functional Verification:**
- Write synthesizable Verilog/SystemVerilog.
- Build UVM testbench, run constrained-random + directed tests.
- **Deliverable:** Sign-off-clean RTL + UVM testbench + coverage report.

**Phase 3 — Logic Synthesis & DFT Insertion:**
- Synthesize to target standard cell library.
- Insert scan chains, BIST, boundary scan (JTAG).
- **Deliverable:** Gate-level netlist, SDC, scan DEF.

**Phase 4 — Floorplanning & Power Planning:**
- Define macro shape, pin positions, power mesh, tap/end-cap cells.
- **Deliverable:** Floorplan view, power planning report.

**Phase 5 — Placement, CTS, and Routing:**
- Place cells, synthesize clock tree, route signals.
- **Deliverable:** Placed & routed DEF, post-CTS timing report.

**Phase 6 — Timing Closure & Sign-off:**
- MCMM STA, IR drop analysis, EM, physical verification (DRC/LVS/Antenna).
- **Deliverable:** Sign-off-clean database, STA reports across all corners.

**Phase 7 — Macro Characterization:**
- SPICE-based extraction of timing/power tables.
- **Deliverable:** `.lib`, `.lef`, behavior model, macro datasheet.

(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in SECTION 4.1 for the visual flow.)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**
**A hard macro operates at 1.2 GHz at the SS, 0.78 V, 125 °C corner. The following worst-case values are reported: $T_{cq}$ = 60 ps, $T_{logic,max}$ = 380 ps, $T_{su}$ = 40 ps, $T_{skew}$ = 30 ps, $T_{margin}$ = 25 ps. Determine the worst-case setup slack. Comment on the result.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Compute the minimum required clock period.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{clk,min} &= T_{cq} + T_{logic,max} + T_{su} + T_{skew} + T_{margin} \\
&= 60 + 380 + 40 + 30 + 25 \\
&= 535 \text{ ps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the actual clock period at 1.2 GHz.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{clk,actual} &= \frac{1}{f_{clk}} \\
&= \frac{1}{1.2 \times 10^{9}} \\
&= 833.33 \text{ ps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the setup slack.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Setup Slack} &= T_{clk,actual} - T_{clk,min} \\
&= 833.33 - 535 \\
&= 298.33 \text{ ps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation.

The setup slack is **+298.33 ps (positive)**, which means the design has a healthy timing margin at the SS corner. The macro can potentially be operated at a higher frequency or at a lower Vdd (for power savings) without violating setup timing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not forget to check the HOLD slack separately** — a positive setup slack does NOT guarantee hold closure. Hold is a race condition independent of the clock period.
> 2. **Do not use the FF (fast) corner for setup** — setup is checked at the SS (slow) corner. Using FF will give a falsely optimistic slack.
> 3. **Do not omit $T_{skew}$ and $T_{margin}$** — students often drop these, losing 1–2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]**
**With the help of a block diagram, explain the macro characterization flow. List the key outputs and their formats.**

**Model Answer:**

**Macro Characterization Flow** (Refer to the Mermaid diagram in SECTION 4.3):

1. **GDSII Input:** Start with the **frozen, sign-off-clean GDSII** of the hard macro.
2. **SPICE Netlist Extraction:** Use a **layout-vs-schematic (LVS)-clean** extraction to get a transistor-level SPICE netlist with parasitic RC.
3. **Input Slew / Output Load Grid Definition:** Define a 7×7 (or finer) grid of input transition times and output load capacitances based on the SoC's expected operating environment.
4. **SPICE Simulation Sweep:** For *every* cell, *every* timing arc, *every* PVT corner, *every* (slew, load) point, run a SPICE transient simulation.
5. **Table Generation:** From the SPICE waveforms, compute **delay, output slew, and energy per transition** at each grid point.
6. **Liberty Compilation:** Format the data into a **Liberty (.lib)** file containing lookup tables for delay, output slew, internal power, switching power, and leakage.
7. **LEF Abstract Generation:** Extract pin positions, blockages, and antenna info into a **LEF** file.
8. **Behavior Model:** Write or generate a **Verilog/VHDL** functional model.
9. **QA & Sign-off:** Verify the `.lib` is **golden** by re-running STA on the macro using the generated `.lib` and comparing to the original SPICE results.

**Key Outputs:**

| **Output** | **Format** | **Used For** |
|---|---|---|
| Liberty timing/power library | `.lib` | Top-level synthesis, STA, power analysis |
| LEF abstract view | `.lef` | Top-level floorplanning, placement |
| Verilog behavior model | `.v` | Functional simulation at SoC level |
| Datasheet | PDF / HTML | Marketing, IP licensing |
| CDL netlist | `.cdl` | ECO, re-simulation |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]**
**A hard macro's power mesh has an effective resistance of 0.6 Ω from the pad to the geometric center. The peak current drawn by the core is 320 mA during a clock edge. The nominal Vdd is 0.8 V. Compute the peak IR drop and the effective voltage at the macro center. If the foundry allows only a 5% drop, is the design acceptable? Suggest one mitigation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Compute the peak IR drop.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{drop,peak} &= I_{peak} \cdot R_{grid} \\
&= 0.32 \times 0.6 \\
&= 0.192 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the effective voltage at the center.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{eff} &= V_{dd} - V_{drop,peak} \\
&= 0.8 - 0.192 \\
&= 0.608 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the percentage drop.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%V_{drop} &= \frac{V_{drop,peak}}{V_{dd}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{0.192}{0.8} \times 100\% \\
&= 24\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Check against the foundry limit.

The drop is **24%**, far exceeding the **5%** limit. **The design is NOT acceptable.**

**Step 5:** Suggested mitigations (any one of the following is valid):

1. **Thicken the power mesh** — upgrade from a 2-layer M1/M2 mesh to a 3-layer M1/M2/M3 mesh to reduce $R_{grid}$.
2. **Add more power pads** — distribute the current over multiple entry points.
3. **Switch to higher metal layers** (e.g., M4/M5) for the global mesh, which have lower sheet resistance.
4. **Decap insertion** — add decoupling capacitance to supply instantaneous current.
5. **Reduce current density** — break the macro into power domains with separate supplies.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often use the **average** current instead of the **peak** current. IR drop must be analyzed at **peak** (dynamic) — the average is for power estimation, not for voltage integrity. A 1-mark penalty is typical for this confusion.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — The Hard Macro Design Process**

- **Definition:** Hard macro = layout-frozen, GDSII-delivered IP block with fixed geometry, pins, and routing.
- **Distinguish:** Soft (RTL, flexible) vs. Firm (netlist, medium) vs. Hard (GDSII, frozen) — *know the trade-offs in the table.*
- **7 Phases:** Spec → RTL/Verify → Synthesis/DFT → Floorplan/Power → Place/CTS/Route → Sign-off (MCMM STA + DRC/LVS + IR/EM) → Characterization.
- **Timing Sign-off Equation:**
  $$T_{clk} \geq T_{cq} + T_{logic,max} + T_{su} + T_{skew} + T_{margin}$$
- **Hold Check (race):**
  $$T_{hold} \leq T_{cq} + T_{logic,min} - T_{skew}$$
- **Power Equation:**
  $$P_{total} = \alpha C V_{dd}^{2} f + V_{dd} I_{leak}$$
- **IR Drop:**
  $$V_{drop} = I \cdot R_{grid}$$ — use **peak** current, not average.
- **Clock Skew:**
  $$T_{skew} = T_{max,leaf} - T_{min,leaf}$$
- **Macro Characterization:** Generates `.lib` (timing/power), `.lef` (abstract), and `.v` (behavior).
- **Standard Examples of Hard Macros:** CPU cores, GPUs, SRAM, ROM, PHYs, PLLs, ADCs/DACs, SerDes.
- **Foundry Limits to Remember:** 2–5% IR drop, EM ratio ≤ 1, fault coverage ≥ 95%.
- **Key Verification Tools (Be aware, not memorized):** Synopsys ICC2/PT, Cadence Innovus/Tempus, Siemens Calibre, Synopsys VCS.
- **Why Hard Macros Win:** Best PPA, fully sign-off-clean, no risk of integrator-induced bugs.
- **Why Hard Macros Lose:** Inflexible, process-locked, expensive to develop, hard to re-spin.

> **Closing Note for Students:** Hard macro design is the **most rigorous and most employable** skill in VLSI/SoC engineering. KTU questions on this topic will **always** test the *flow* and the *sign-off equations*. Memorize the 7 phases and the 4 key equations (timing, hold, power, IR drop) — together they cover 80% of the marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Integration for Hard Macros
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT602 - SYSTEM ON CHIP DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Block Integration for Hard Macros

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hard Macro** in the context of System-on-Chip (SoC) physical design is a **fully routed, timing-closed, and physically verified Intellectual Property (IP) block** that is delivered to the integrator as a **fixed, opaque, geometric black-box** with a precisely known GDSII layout, timing model (Liberty `.lib`), power model (CPF/UPF), and physical abstraction (LEF/DEF). The integrator is **NOT permitted to modify, resize, or re-route** the internal geometry of a hard macro; only the surrounding logic (the *chip top* or *top-level netlist*) can be synthesized and physically implemented around it.

**Block Integration for Hard Macros** is the structured top-down physical design methodology that **places, orients, connects (routes), powers, and verifies** one or more hard macro blocks inside the SoC die area, while satisfying timing closure, signal integrity, manufacturability (DFM), and reliability constraints. It is the bridge between *block-level design* and *chip-level assembly*, and is governed by the **KTU 2024 Scheme** VLSI/SoC design flow under the topic of *Chip Integration and Top-Level Physical Design*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Syllabus Anchor):**
> *Block integration is the process of assembling pre-designed, pre-verified, fixed-geometry IP blocks (hard macros) such as SRAMs, PLLs, ADCs, standard cell libraries, and analog/RF blocks into a single System-on-Chip, ensuring that the top-level timing, power, signal-integrity, and Design Rule Check (DRC) constraints are globally satisfied.*

## 1.2 Classification of Macros — The Three Flavors of IP

Before diving into integration, it is mandatory to classify the macro:

| Macro Type | Physical Form | Modifiable? | Typical Use |
|------------|---------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Hard Macro** | Fully routed GDSII (fixed layout) | **No** — black box | SRAM, ROM, PLL, ADC, I/O pads |
| **Firm Macro** | Placed-but-not-routed (partial layout) | Partially (router only) | Datapath blocks, custom logic |
| **Soft Macro** | RTL (synthesizable code) | **Yes** (synthesize, place, route) | Processors, controllers, glue logic |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hard macros are the MOST RIGID** to integrate because their internal layout is frozen. All flexibility must be handled *outside* the macro boundary.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Building-Block City

Imagine you are an **urban city planner** tasked with constructing a new metropolis:

- **Hard Macro = Pre-Built Skyscraper** 🏢: It arrives fully constructed with fixed floors, staircases, plumbing, and electrical conduits. You CANNOT move the internal rooms, but you MUST decide:
  - *Where to place it in the city* (floorplanning)
  - *How to connect roads to its entrances* (pin access + routing)
  - *How to supply power and water* (power planning)
  - *How to handle traffic around it* (signal integrity / congestion)
- **Soft Macro = Plot of Land with a Blueprint** 🏗️: You can re-design it as needed.
- **Standard Cell = LEGO Brick** 🧱: Tiny, fully flexible building units.

The job of **block integration for hard macros** is exactly the city planner's job: orchestrate pre-built structures into a working, connected, powered, and verified whole.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Cue:** In the 2024 scheme, examiners LOVE asking the difference between *hard*, *firm*, and *soft* macros. Always mention: *flexibility, delivered form, and verification level*.

## 1.4 Typical Hard Macros Encountered in KTU 2024 SoC Design

1. **SRAM / ROM / Register-File compilers** (memory arrays with bit-cells, sense amplifiers, decoders)
2. **PLL / DLL** (clock-generation blocks with sensitive analog circuits)
3. **ADC / DAC** (mixed-signal blocks)
4. **SerDes / PHY blocks** (PCIe, USB, Ethernet, MIPI)
5. **Standard cell library "macros"** (e.g., large multiplexers, clock-tree buffers)
6. **Custom I/O pads** with ESD protection

## 1.5 Physical Metrics in Hard-Macro Integration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key metrics** (memorize for Part A 3-mark questions):
> - **Utilization** = (Standard-cell area) ÷ (Core area − Hard-macro area) × **100%**
> - **Pin density** (per micron of macro edge) — affects routability
> - **Routing blockage** (in metal layers) — specified in LEF
> - **Halo / Keep-out margin** (typically 2 µm – 10 µm around the macro)
> - **Aspect ratio** of the macro boundary (impacts floorplan)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hard-Macro Integration Flow (KTU Standard)

The integration process follows a strict 7-step pipeline. Memorize the order:

1. **Macro Selection & Library Preparation**
   - Receive hard macro in GDSII + LEF (abstract) + Liberty (`.lib`) + UPF/CPF (power intent).
   - Validate that the LEF *obstruction* layers match the GDSII physical layers.

2. **Floorplanning (Macro Placement)**
   - Decide (x, y) coordinates, orientation (N, S, E, W, FN, FS), and any channel width between macros.

3. **Pin Assignment & Access Pattern Analysis**
   - Verify each macro pin has an *accessible* routing track on the preferred metal layer.

4. **Standard-Cell Placement**
   - Place standard cells in the *channels* (gaps) between macros and in the *core area* not occupied by macros.

5. **Power Planning (Power Network Synthesis — PNS)**
   - Create top-level **power rings** around each macro and a **global power mesh** (straps) across the die.

6. **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)**
   - Tap clock from buffers placed *outside* the macro. Hard macros typically have *built-in* clock spines — the integrator only routes the top-level clock to the macro's clock pin.

7. **Global & Detailed Routing + Signoff**
   - Route signals *around* macros respecting the obstruction layers.
   - Sign-off checks: **DRC, LVS, Antenna, ERC, IR-drop, Static Timing Analysis (STA), Signal Integrity (SI)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Integration is an ITERATIVE process.** Convergence is reached when timing, power, congestion, and DRC are all simultaneously clean.

## 2.2 Why Hard-Macro Integration is HARD — The Five Core Challenges

| # | Challenge | Root Cause | Mitigation |
|---|-----------|------------|------------|
| 1 | **Routing Congestion** around macro edges | Pin-dense edges create "traffic jams" | Spread pins, add **soft blockage** (channel) |
| 2 | **Timing Closure** between macros | Long global wires + macro internal delay | Buffer insertion, useful-skew CTS, gate sizing |
| 3 | **IR-Drop & Power Integrity** | High current draw through narrow straps | Widen straps, add **power rings** around macro |
| 4 | **Signal Integrity (Crosstalk, Noise)** | Long parallel routes near macro edges | Shielding, spacing, layer assignment |
| 5 | **DRC & Antenna Violations** | Macro obstruction mismatch | Verify LEF/GDSII; add diode-fix cells |

## 2.3 Floorplanning Math — The KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THESE FORMULAS — they appear in almost every 14-mark question.**

| Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---------|---------|-------|
| $$U \;=\; \dfrac{A_{\text{stdcell}}}{A_{\text{core}} - A_{\text{macro}}} \times 100\%$$ | Effective utilization of free area | dimensionless (%) |
| $$A_{\text{core}} \;=\; W_{\text{die}} \times H_{\text{die}} - 2(W_{\text{pad}})H_{\text{pad}}$$ | Core area after pad ring | $\mu m^2$ |
| $$\text{Channel Width} \;=\; \dfrac{N_{\text{routes}} \times w_{\text{track}} + 2 \times S_{\text{spacing}}}{\text{Track pitch}}$$ | Minimum free space between macros for routing | $\mu m$ |
| $$R_{\text{strap}} \;=\; \rho \cdot \dfrac{L}{W \cdot T}$$ | Resistance of a power strap | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| $$V_{\text{drop}} \;=\; I_{\text{peak}} \times R_{\text{strap}}$$ | IR drop across the strap | Volts (V) |
| $$\tau_{\text{wire}} \;=\; 0.4 \cdot r_{\text{int}} \cdot c_{\text{wire}} \cdot L^2$$ | Elmore wire delay (RC) | seconds (s) |
| $$\text{Pin Density} \;=\; \dfrac{N_{\text{pins}}}{P_{\text{perimeter}}}$$ | Routability indicator | pins/µm |
| $$\text{Halo} \;=\; 2 \cdot \lambda_{\text{min}} \cdot k$$ | Keep-out distance from macro edge (k = 3–10) | $\mu m$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX NOTE**: In the markdown tables above, the `\vert` and `\mid` symbols are used to AVOID the vertical pipe `|` which would break table syntax. Do not confuse `\vert x \vert` (absolute value) with table delimiter.

## 2.4 Pin Access — The "Hidden" Hard Problem

A macro pin is accessible only if a **routing track** can *physically reach it* without crossing the macro boundary or violating DRC. Two failure modes:

1. **Blocked pin**: Pin lies on a track that is occupied by the macro's internal metal — *must use upper-layer jump*.
2. **Unreachable pin**: Pin is sandwiched between two obstruction layers — *needs dedicated access cell (cut layer)*.

**Pin access optimization techniques:**
- **Pin spreading**: Re-distribute equivalent pins along the macro edge (only allowed for soft/firm).
- **Layer promotion**: Route *over* the macro on higher metal layers (M3, M4) using the macro's *partial blockage* (PB) zones.
- **Channel insertion**: Add a physical channel (e.g., 5–20 µm) between macro and standard-cell area.

## 2.5 Power Planning Around Hard Macros

A hard macro is a **power island** in SoC parlance. The integrator must:

1. Place a **power ring** (vertical + horizontal straps in upper metals) hugging the macro boundary.
2. Connect the macro's **power pins** to the ring using **follow-pin** routes.
3. Drop **power straps** (M6/M7) across the entire die forming a *power mesh*.
4. Add **decap cells** near macro outputs to reduce dynamic IR-drop.
5. Run **static IR-drop analysis** to verify $V_{\text{drop}} < 5\%$ of $V_{DD}$ (typical KTU threshold).

## 2.6 Signal Integrity Around Hard Macros

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 emphasizes this in the Module 4 syllabus.**

Crosstalk between adjacent routes increases near macro edges because of **long parallel segments**. Key SI checks:
- **Glitch analysis** (capacitive coupling): $\Delta V = C_m \cdot \frac{dV}{dt} / C_{\text{load}}$
- **Delay degradation**: $\Delta \tau = k \cdot \frac{C_m}{C_{\text{load}}}$
- **Shielding**: Insert GND/VSS routes between victim and aggressor signals.

## 2.7 Engineering Real-World Utility

In industry, block integration is the **#1 source of SoC tape-out delays**. Tools used:
- **Cadence Innovus** (place & route)
- **Synopsys IC Compiler II (ICC2)**
- **Mentor Olympus / Calibre nmDRC**

A mis-integrated hard macro can cause **silicon re-spin** costing **$1M–$10M**, hence the rigorous KTU emphasis on this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Core Utilization in a Macro-Dominated SoC

We will derive, in exhaustive steps, the formula for the **effective utilization** when a SoC contains **N hard macros**.

**Given:**
- Total die area: $A_{\text{die}}$
- Number of hard macros: $N$
- Area of the $i^{\text{th}}$ macro: $A_{m,i}$
- Area occupied by standard cells: $A_{\text{stdcell}}$
- Area occupied by power/clock routing: $A_{\text{utility}}$

**Step 1: Compute the macro-occupied area.**

$$A_{\text{macro}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} A_{m,i}$$

**Step 2: Compute the area unavailable to standard cells.**

The "free" area is the die area minus the macros and the utility infrastructure:

$$A_{\text{free}} \;=\; A_{\text{die}} - A_{\text{macro}} - A_{\text{utility}}$$

**Step 3: Express the effective utilization as a ratio.**

By KTU definition, utilization is the fraction of *free* area actually used by standard cells:

$$U_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \frac{A_{\text{stdcell}}}{A_{\text{free}}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4: Substitute the free-area expression.**

$$U_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \frac{A_{\text{stdcell}}}{A_{\text{die}} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} A_{m,i} - A_{\text{utility}}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 5: Insert a numerical example for clarity.**

Suppose:
- $A_{\text{die}} = 2{,}500{,}000 \;\mu m^2$ (a $1{,}500 \times 1{,}667\;\mu m$ die)
- 2 SRAM macros, each $A_m = 400{,}000 \;\mu m^2$
- 1 PLL macro, $A_m = 50{,}000 \;\mu m^2$
- $A_{\text{utility}} = 100{,}000 \;\mu m^2$ (power mesh + clock)
- $A_{\text{stdcell}} = 700{,}000 \;\mu m^2$

**Step 5a:** Sum the macro areas.

$$A_{\text{macro}} = 400{,}000 + 400{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 850{,}000 \;\mu m^2$$

**Step 5b:** Compute the free area.

$$A_{\text{free}} = 2{,}500{,}000 - 850{,}000 - 100{,}000 = 1{,}550{,}000 \;\mu m^2$$

**Step 5c:** Compute utilization.

$$U_{\text{eff}} = \frac{700{,}000}{1{,}550{,}000} \times 100\% = 45.16\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Even though the *die* utilization appears to be $\frac{700{,}000 + 850{,}000}{2{,}500{,}000} = 62\%$, the *effective* utilization (excluding macros) is only **45.16%**. This gap is what forces engineers to over-provision the die area.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Channel Width for N Routes Between Two Macros

**Given:**
- Two parallel macros with a routing channel of width $W_{\text{ch}}$ between them.
- $N$ signal nets must pass through the channel.
- Track pitch = $P_{\text{track}}$ (e.g., 0.1 µm at 7 nm).
- Min spacing = $S_{\min}$ (e.g., 0.05 µm).
- Wire width = $w$ (e.g., 0.05 µm).

**Step 1: Number of tracks required per net.**

Each net needs **1 track** of width $w$ with spacing $S_{\min}$ on either side:

$$W_{\text{per net}} = w + 2 \cdot S_{\min}$$

**Step 2: Total width consumed by N nets.**

$$W_{\text{consumed}} = N \cdot (w + 2 \cdot S_{\min})$$

**Step 3: Convert to number of tracks.**

$$\text{Number of tracks} = \frac{W_{\text{consumed}}}{P_{\text{track}}}$$

**Step 4: Solve for the minimum channel width.**

$$W_{\text{ch,min}} = N \cdot (w + 2 \cdot S_{\min}) + 2 \cdot S_{\text{macro-to-first-track}}$$

where $S_{\text{macro-to-first-track}}$ is the spacing from the macro obstruction edge to the first legal routing track.

**Step 5: Numerical example.**

Let $N = 50$, $w = 0.05\;\mu m$, $S_{\min} = 0.05\;\mu m$, $P_{\text{track}} = 0.1\;\mu m$, $S_{\text{macro}} = 0.05\;\mu m$.

**Step 5a:**

$$W_{\text{per net}} = 0.05 + 2(0.05) = 0.15 \;\mu m$$

**Step 5b:**

$$W_{\text{consumed}} = 50 \times 0.15 = 7.5 \;\mu m$$

**Step 5c:**

$$W_{\text{ch,min}} = 7.5 + 2(0.05) = 7.6 \;\mu m$$

> [!TIP]
> This is why **floorplanning engines add a halo** of 5–10 µm around hard macros — to guarantee that the channel-width formula is satisfiable.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: IR-Drop Across a Power Strap Feeding a Macro

**Given:**
- Strap length $L = 500\;\mu m$ (between two vias along the macro edge)
- Strap width $W = 5\;\mu m$, thickness $T = 0.3\;\mu m$ (M6 metal)
- Copper resistivity $\rho = 1.72 \times 10^{-8}\;\Omega \cdot m$
- Macro peak current $I_{\text{peak}} = 50\;mA$

**Step 1: Strap resistance.**

$$R_{\text{strap}} = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{W \cdot T}$$

**Step 2: Insert numbers (with unit conversion).**

$$R_{\text{strap}} = 1.72 \times 10^{-8} \cdot \frac{500 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.3 \times 10^{-6}}$$

**Step 3: Compute the denominator.**

$$W \cdot T = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.3 \times 10^{-6} = 1.5 \times 10^{-12}\;m^2$$

**Step 4: Compute the ratio.**

$$\frac{L}{W \cdot T} = \frac{500 \times 10^{-6}}{1.5 \times 10^{-12}} = 3.33 \times 10^{8}\;m^{-1}$$

**Step 5: Compute resistance.**

$$R_{\text{strap}} = 1.72 \times 10^{-8} \times 3.33 \times 10^{8} = 5.73\;\Omega$$

**Step 6: Compute IR drop.**

$$V_{\text{drop}} = I_{\text{peak}} \times R_{\text{strap}} = 50 \times 10^{-3} \times 5.73 = 0.286\;V$$

**Step 7: Compare to KTU threshold.**

If $V_{DD} = 0.8\;V$, the drop is $\frac{0.286}{0.8} \times 100\% = 35.8\%$, which is **WAY ABOVE** the 5% KTU limit.

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion:** This strap is grossly under-sized. Either widen it to $W = 35\;\mu m$ or shorten the strap spacing to $L = 70\;\mu m$. This derivation is a CLASSIC 14-mark question.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Macro Integration Strategy

| SoC Characteristic | Recommended Strategy | Justification |
|--------------------|----------------------|---------------|
| **Pin-dense analog macro (PLL, ADC)** | Place near edge, add **wide halo (10 µm)**, shield routes | Minimize digital switching noise coupling |
| **High-current macro (CPU core)** | Multi-strap power ring (M6+M7), **distributed vias** | Reduce IR drop, electromigration |
| **Memory macro (SRAM)** | **Tiled array** in core, single power ring, BIST pins exposed | Maximize density, simplify BIST routing |
| **Mixed-signal SoC** | **Dual power ring** (digital VDD + analog AVDD), **deep-N-well isolation** | Isolate noisy digital return paths |
| **Multi-voltage domain** | UPF/CPF-specified islands, **level-shifter insertion** at boundary | Prevent cross-domain short-circuit current |
| **High-frequency SerDes macro** | **C4 bumps directly under macro**, no power ring | Minimize supply inductance |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The Hard-Macro Integration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: SoC Top Netlist] --> B[Macro Library Preparation]
    B --> B1[Verify GDSII / LEF / LIB / UPF]
    B1 --> C[Floorplanning: Macro Placement]
    C --> C1[Choose position, orientation, channel width]
    C1 --> D[Pin Access Analysis]
    D --> D1{Reachable?}
    D1 -- No --> D2[Layer promotion / Cut cells]
    D2 --> D
    D1 -- Yes --> E[Standard Cell Placement]
    E --> F[Power Planning: PNS]
    F --> F1[Power rings + global straps + decaps]
    F1 --> G[Clock Tree Synthesis CTS]
    G --> H[Global and Detailed Routing]
    H --> I[Signoff Checks]
    I --> I1[DRC]
    I --> I2[LVS]
    I --> I3[STA]
    I --> I4[IR-Drop]
    I --> I5[Signal Integrity SI]
    I1 --> J{All Clean?}
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    I4 --> J
    I5 --> J
    J -- No --> K[ECO: Engineering Change Order]
    K --> E
    J -- Yes --> L[Tape-Out Ready GDSII]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Macro Floorplan Architecture (Top-Down View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DIE["SoC DIE: 1500um x 1667um"]
        CORE["Core Area: 1200 x 1400 um"]
        IOBLK["IO Pad Ring: 4 sides"]
        CORE --> SRAM1["SRAM 1: 400 x 400 um"]
        CORE --> SRAM2["SRAM 2: 400 x 400 um"]
        CORE --> CPU["CPU Cluster: 600 x 600 um"]
        CORE --> PLL["PLL Macro: 200 x 250 um"]
        CORE --> ADC["ADC Macro: 300 x 200 um"]
        SRAM1 --- CH1["Channel 10um"]
        SRAM2 --- CH1
        CH1 --- CPU
        CPU --- CH2["Channel 8um"]
        CH2 --- PLL
        CPU --- CH3["Channel 12um"]
        CH3 --- ADC
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Power Network Around a Single Hard Macro

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MACRO["Hard Macro Boundary"]
        P1["VDD Pin 1"]
        P2["VDD Pin 2"]
        P3["VSS Pin 1"]
        P4["VSS Pin 2"]
        INT["Internal Logic"]
    end
    subgraph RING["Power Ring: M6 + M7"]
        VDDR["VDD Ring on Top and Right"]
        VSSR["VSS Ring on Bottom and Left"]
    end
    subgraph MESH["Global Power Mesh"]
        VDDM["VDD Strap M6 horizontal"]
        VSSM["VSS Strap M6 horizontal"]
    end
    P1 --> VDDR
    P2 --> VDDR
    P3 --> VSSR
    P4 --> VSSR
    VDDR --> VDDM
    VSSR --> VSSM
    VDDM --> DECAP["Decap cells distributed along edge"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Pin-Access Failure and Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PIN["Macro Pin at M2 layer"]
    OBS["M3 obstruction above pin"]
    PIN --> Q1{Is M2 track clear?}
    Q1 -- No --> FIX1["Solution: Use cut cell to jump to M3"]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is M3 reachable?}
    Q2 -- No --> FIX2["Solution: Promote to M4 with via stack"]
    Q2 -- Yes --> SUCCESS["Route reaches the pin"]
    FIX1 --> SUCCESS
    FIX2 --> SUCCESS
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GDS["GDSII Full Layout"] -->|Abstracted by| LEF["LEF: Pins, Obstructions, Blockage"]
    LEF --> LEF1[OBS: M1 to M7 layers]
    LEF --> LEF2[PIN: Access points]
    LEF --> LEF3[BLOCKAGE: Soft and Hard zones]
    LEF --> LEF4[Site: Placement class]

    LEF2 --> PNR
    LEF3 --> PNR
    LEF4 --> PNR
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Hard, Soft, and Firm macros in SoC design. List TWO examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Hard macro** is delivered as a fully placed-and-routed GDSII layout with fixed geometry, characterized timing, and power models. It is a *black box* that cannot be modified internally. *Examples:* SRAM compiler output, PLL, ADC.

A **Soft macro** is delivered as synthesizable RTL code (e.g., Verilog) along with synthesis scripts. It is fully flexible and can be re-targeted to any process or size. *Examples:* CPU core (e.g., RISC-V), peripheral controller, DMA engine.

A **Firm macro** is a compromise — delivered as a placed-but-not-routed netlist (or gate-level netlist with a partial floorplan). The integrator may route the internals but cannot move cells. *Examples:* Custom datapath blocks, mixed-signal front-ends.

> [!TIP]
> Use this exact three-line structure in the exam — it is a 3-mark question and examiners reward clean tabular answers.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is a "halo" in hard-macro integration? Why is it necessary, and what is its typical width?**

**Model Answer:**

A **halo** is a *keep-out region* of width **2 µm – 10 µm** (typically 5 µm) drawn around the boundary of a hard macro, in which the placement engine is forbidden to drop any standard cell.

It is necessary for **three reasons**:
1. To prevent standard-cell placement *under* the macro's power rings or pin access paths.
2. To give the global router a clean **channel** for signals entering/exiting the macro pins.
3. To allow **buffer insertion** for timing closure near the macro.

> [!NOTE]
> Width is process-dependent: at 28 nm, halos are ~5 µm; at 7 nm, they shrink to ~2 µm but are augmented by **coloring** and **grid-based placement** restrictions.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **seven-step flow** for integrating a hard macro into a System-on-Chip. For each step, list the key tool inputs and the principal outputs.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A SoC die measures $1{,}600 \;\mu m \times 1{,}800 \;\mu m$ with an IO pad ring of width $80 \;\mu m$ on each side. It contains:
- Three SRAM macros, each $500 \;\mu m \times 500 \;\mu m$
- One PLL macro, $200 \;\mu m \times 300 \;\mu m$
- One ADC macro, $300 \;\mu m \times 250 \;\mu m$
- Power-mesh + clock-tree area: $A_{\text{util}} = 250{,}000 \;\mu m^2$
- Standard-cell area: $A_{\text{std}} = 1{,}100{,}000 \;\mu m^2$

Compute the **effective utilization** and comment on whether this design is **placement-feasible** if the target utilization is **55%**.

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

The **seven-step hard-macro integration flow** is:

**Step 1: Macro Library Preparation**
- *Inputs:* GDSII, LEF (abstract), Liberty (`.lib`), UPF/CPF, IBIS.
- *Outputs:* Validated LEF merged into the PnR library; timing/power libraries read in.

**Step 2: Floorplanning (Macro Placement)**
- *Inputs:* Netlist, LEF, floorplan constraints (aspect ratio, utilization target, blockage maps).
- *Outputs:* Macro (x, y, orientation) coordinates; channel widths; pin coordinates.

**Step 3: Pin Access Optimization**
- *Inputs:* Macro pin coordinates, blockage maps, routing grid.
- *Outputs:* Adjusted pin access patterns; cut-cell insertion where needed.

**Step 4: Standard-Cell Placement**
- *Inputs:* Synthesized netlist, macro locations, timing constraints (SDC), congestion map.
- *Outputs:* Placed DEF with legalization, optimization for timing and density.

**Step 5: Power Network Synthesis (PNS)**
- *Inputs:* Power intent (UPF/CPF), macro power-pin locations, IR-drop target.
- *Outputs:* Power rings, global power mesh, follow-pin connections, decap cells.

**Step 6: Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)**
- *Inputs:* Clock spec, macro clock-pin locations, clock-buffer library.
- *Outputs:* Balanced clock tree, clock-net SPEF, skew report.

**Step 7: Global + Detailed Routing + Signoff**
- *Inputs:* Placed DEF, LEF, SDC, blockage, SI settings.
- *Outputs:* Routed DEF, GDSII, sign-off reports (DRC, LVS, STA, IR-drop, SI, antenna).

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1: Die area.**

$$A_{\text{die}} = 1{,}600 \times 1{,}800 = 2{,}880{,}000 \;\mu m^2$$

**Step 2: Core area (subtract pad ring).**

$$W_{\text{core}} = 1{,}600 - 2(80) = 1{,}440 \;\mu m$$

$$H_{\text{core}} = 1{,}800 - 2(80) = 1{,}640 \;\mu m$$

$$A_{\text{core}} = 1{,}440 \times 1{,}640 = 2{,}361{,}600 \;\mu m^2$$

**Step 3: Sum the macro areas.**

$$A_{\text{SRAM,total}} = 3 \times (500 \times 500) = 3 \times 250{,}000 = 750{,}000 \;\mu m^2$$

$$A_{\text{PLL}} = 200 \times 300 = 60{,}000 \;\mu m^2$$

$$A_{\text{ADC}} = 300 \times 250 = 75{,}000 \;\mu m^2$$

$$A_{\text{macro}} = 750{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 75{,}000 = 885{,}000 \;\mu m^2$$

**Step 4: Free area for standard cells.**

$$A_{\text{free}} = A_{\text{core}} - A_{\text{macro}} - A_{\text{util}} = 2{,}361{,}600 - 885{,}000 - 250{,}000 = 1{,}226{,}600 \;\mu m^2$$

**Step 5: Effective utilization.**

$$U_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1{,}100{,}000}{1{,}226{,}600} \times 100\% = 89.68\%$$

**Step 6: Comparison with target.**

The target is **55%** but the effective utilization is **89.68%** — i.e., the design is **grossly over-packed** and **NOT placement-feasible**.

**Step 7: Remediation suggestions.**
- Increase the die size to $1{,}800 \times 2{,}000 \;\mu m$.
- Reduce standard-cell area by re-synthesizing with area optimization.
- Remove one SRAM macro.
- Move some logic to a **chiplet** in a 2.5D/3D package.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Many students compute the utilization against $A_{\text{die}}$ instead of $A_{\text{core}}$. That gives $\frac{1{,}100{,}000}{2{,}880{,}000} = 38.2\%$, which is INCORRECT because macros are placed in the *core*, not in the pad ring. Always use the *core area* formula.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **Pin Access Analysis** in detail. With neat sketches (describe the layout), discuss the **three failure modes** and **two solutions** for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A power strap on metal layer M7 has the following parameters:
- Length $L = 200\;\mu m$, Width $W = 2\;\mu m$, Thickness $T = 0.5\;\mu m$
- Resistivity of copper $\rho = 1.72 \times 10^{-8}\;\Omega \cdot m$
- Peak current drawn by the hard macro $I_{\text{peak}} = 30\;mA$
- Supply voltage $V_{DD} = 0.85\;V$

Compute the **resistance of the strap** and the **IR-drop**, and decide whether the design meets the KTU 5% IR-drop rule. If not, propose a fix.

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Pin Access Analysis** is the process of verifying that every pin of a hard macro can be reached by a routing track on the *preferred metal layer* without crossing the macro's obstruction zones or violating DRC.

**Three Failure Modes:**

1. **Pin-on-obstruction:** The pin lies on a metal layer that is obstructed by the macro's internal routing. A track cannot land on the pin.
2. **Pin sandwiched between two blocked layers:** The pin is on M2, but M1 (below) and M3 (above) are both obstructed, leaving no via space.
3. **Pin off-grid:** The pin's coordinates do not align with the routing grid pitch, so a track cannot snap to the pin.

**Two Solutions for each:**

| Failure Mode | Solution 1 | Solution 2 |
|--------------|------------|------------|
| Pin-on-obstruction | **Layer promotion** — route on a higher metal (M3, M4) using a via stack. | **Cut cell insertion** — place a *cut cell* adjacent to the pin to expose a via. |
| Pin sandwiched | **Use a different layer** for entry (e.g., M4 instead of M2). | **Halo widening** to allow via-stacking area. |
| Pin off-grid | **Grid snapping** with sub-grid resolution. | **Re-orient the macro** by 90° or 180° to align pins with the grid. |

> [!TIP]
> In a 14-mark answer, draw the pin layout (top view) and label the layers. KTU examiners reward **sketch quality**.

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1: Strap resistance formula.**

$$R_{\text{strap}} = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{W \cdot T}$$

**Step 2: Unit conversion.**

$$L = 200\;\mu m = 2 \times 10^{-4}\;m$$

$$W = 2\;\mu m = 2 \times 10^{-6}\;m$$

$$T = 0.5\;\mu m = 5 \times 10^{-7}\;m$$

**Step 3: Compute the cross-sectional area.**

$$W \cdot T = 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 \times 10^{-7} = 1 \times 10^{-12}\;m^2$$

**Step 4: Compute resistance.**

$$R_{\text{strap}} = 1.72 \times 10^{-8} \times \frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{1 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.72 \times 10^{-8} \times 2 \times 10^{8} = 3.44\;\Omega$$

**Step 5: Compute IR drop.**

$$V_{\text{drop}} = I_{\text{peak}} \times R_{\text{strap}} = 30 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.44 = 0.1032\;V$$

**Step 6: Compare to the 5% KTU rule.**

$$V_{\text{drop, max}} = 0.05 \times V_{DD} = 0.05 \times 0.85 = 0.0425\;V$$

$$V_{\text{drop}} = 0.1032\;V > V_{\text{drop, max}} = 0.0425\;V$$

> **Conclusion:** The strap **FAILS** the IR-drop rule. The drop is **12.1%** of $V_{DD}$ (more than 2× the allowed limit).

**Step 7: Propose a fix.**

We need $V_{\text{drop}} \le 0.0425\;V$, so:

$$R_{\text{strap, max}} = \frac{V_{\text{drop, max}}}{I_{\text{peak}}} = \frac{0.0425}{0.03} = 1.4167\;\Omega$$

From $R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{W \cdot T}$, we need:

$$W \cdot T \ge \rho \cdot \frac{L}{R_{\text{max}}} = 1.72 \times 10^{-8} \times \frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{1.4167} = 2.428 \times 10^{-12}\;m^2$$

If $T = 0.5\;\mu m = 5 \times 10^{-7}\;m$ is fixed, then:

$$W \ge \frac{2.428 \times 10^{-12}}{5 \times 10^{-7}} = 4.86 \times 10^{-6}\;m = 4.86\;\mu m$$

**Fix:** **Widen the strap to 5 µm (from 2 µm)** OR add a **second parallel strap** of width 2 µm to halve the effective resistance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** Students often forget to **convert µm to m** when substituting into the resistivity formula. The error factor is $10^{12}$ — a single missed conversion makes the answer 12 orders of magnitude wrong, costing full marks. Always write the unit conversion as an explicit step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Block Integration for Hard Macros**

- [ ] **Definition:** Hard macro = fixed-geometry, pre-verified, opaque IP block (e.g., SRAM, PLL, ADC).
- [ ] **Three macro types:** Hard (GDSII, no edit), Firm (partial layout, route only), Soft (RTL, fully flexible).
- [ ] **Seven-step integration flow:** Lib prep → Floorplan → Pin access → Std-cell placement → Power planning → CTS → Routing + Signoff.
- [ ] **Five key challenges:** Congestion, Timing, IR-drop, Signal Integrity, DRC/Antenna.
- [ ] **Effective Utilization Formula:** $U_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{A_{\text{stdcell}}}{A_{\text{core}} - A_{\text{macro}} - A_{\text{utility}}} \times 100\%$
- [ ] **Pin density** is the primary routability indicator (pins per µm of edge).
- [ ] **Halo** = 2 µm – 10 µm keep-out zone around the macro.
- [ ] **Channel width** for N routes: $W_{\text{ch,min}} = N \cdot (w + 2 S_{\min}) + 2 S_{\text{macro-edge}}$.
- [ ] **IR-drop rule (KTU):** $V_{\text{drop}} \le 5\%$ of $V_{DD}$.
- [ ] **Strap resistance:** $R = \rho L / (W T)$ — always convert µm → m.
- [ ] **Power ring** = M6/M7 vertical+horizontal straps hugging the macro.
- [ ] **Crosstalk** is worst at macro edges due to long parallel routes — use shielding.
- [ ] **Tools:** Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC2, Mentor Olympus, Calibre.
- [ ] **Sign-off checks:** DRC, LVS, STA, IR-drop, SI, Antenna, ERC.
- [ ] **Iteration:** Integration is NEVER a one-shot flow — ECO loops are standard.
- [ ] **Industry cost of failure:** A bad integration can cause a $1M–$10M silicon re-spin.
- [ ] **Use LEF abstractions**, not GDSII, during integration to keep the PnR tool fast.
- [ ] **Soft blockage vs Hard blockage:** Soft = router may use, Hard = router must avoid.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Productization for Hard Macros
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT602 - SYSTEM ON CHIP DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Productization for Hard Macros

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Productization for Hard Macros** is the systematic, multi-stage engineering process of transforming a functionally verified Register Transfer Level (RTL) Intellectual Property (IP) block into a fully placed, routed, timing-closed, and silicon-proven **Hard Macro** that can be delivered, licensed, and dropped into downstream SoC designs as a black-box, layout-fixed, pre-characterized block.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard Macro (KTU Definition):** A fully placed-and-routed (APR) physical IP block with frozen layout geometry, characterized timing (.lib), power (.lib), and physical views (LEF/DEF/GDS), supplied to integrators as a non-modifiable, pre-qualified building block.

A **Hard Macro** is the final, hardened, geometrically frozen representation of an IP. Productization is the industrial workflow that *guarantees* this hard macro meets a published **datasheet** across Process, Voltage, and Temperature (**PVT**) corners and across multiple **Multi-Project Wafer (MPW)** runs.

The three IEEE/IP classification categories of macro hardness are:

| Macro Type | Delivered Form | Modifiable? | Characterization |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Soft Macro** | RTL (synthesizable HDL) | Yes (full flexibility) | Vendor-estimated, not silicon-proven |
| **Firm Macro** | Gate-level netlist + partial floorplan | Partially | Vendor-estimated + post-layout partial |
| **Hard Macro** | GDSII/LEF/DEF + .lib | **No** (frozen) | **Silicon-proven, fully characterized** |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes that a Hard Macro is the **"highest IP-readiness level"** and productization is the engineering pipeline that elevates an IP from Level 1 (soft) to Level 3 (hard).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine you are buying a **prefab kitchen cabinet** for your house:

- A **Soft Macro** is like an architect's **blueprint** of the kitchen — you must still buy the wood, cut it, assemble it, and install it. The blueprint can be reshaped to your kitchen's exact shape.
- A **Firm Macro** is like a **flat-pack cabinet** — most parts are cut, but you must still bolt them together and make minor adjustments to fit your kitchen.
- A **Hard Macro** is like a **finished, bolted-down, granite-topped kitchen island** delivered to your door. The plumbing, gas, and electrical fittings are pre-tested. You cannot resize it, but you can confidently drop it into your kitchen knowing **it just works**.

**Productization** is the **factory process** that converts the blueprint (RTL) into the finished granite island (hard macro). This includes:
- Sourcing the materials (synthesis)
- Manufacturing every panel with exact dimensions (P&R)
- Stress-testing the gas connection under hot/cold conditions (PVT corner characterization)
- Writing an instruction manual (datasheet)
- Certifying it as safe for consumer use (silicon qualification)

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Test:** If the block ships with a **`.lib` file, a `LEF` file, a `GDS` file, and a published datasheet** — it is a hard macro. If it ships with only **Verilog/VHDL code** — it is a soft macro.

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **TTV (Time-to-Volume)** — Time from RTL handoff to first qualified hard macro: typically **9–18 months** in industry.
- **PVT Corners** — **Process (FF/SS/TT) × Voltage (1.0V/0.9V/0.8V) × Temperature (-40°C / 25°C / 125°C)** = up to **27 corners**.
- **Silicon-Area Efficiency (A_eff)**: A_eff = (A_macro / A_pitch) where A_pitch is the standardized row pitch.
- **Yield (Y)** — Typical mature-node hard macro yield target: **> 95%** at **D0 = 0.05 µm²**.
- **D0 (Critical Defect Density)** — Defects per cm².
- **FOM (Figure of Merit)**: FOM = (PDP)⁻¹ = Power-Delay-Product inverse, used to compare hard macros.

> [!WARNING]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, students must explicitly distinguish **three productization maturity tiers**: *Beta Hard Macro*, *Production Hard Macro*, and *Silicon-Proven Hard Macro*. Mixing them in viva costs marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Productization Flow — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The transformation of an RTL into a hard macro is an **8-stage** industrial pipeline. Each stage has explicit **gates** (i.e., exit criteria) that must be passed before progressing.

### Stage 1: RTL Sign-Off (Input Gate)
- RTL must pass **LINT checks** (e.g., SpyGlass, Verilator), **CDC (Clock Domain Crossing) checks**, and **lint cleanliness** of **0 errors / 0 warnings**.
- Functional verification must achieve **100% code coverage** and **95% functional coverage** at block level.

### Stage 2: Synthesis
- Technology mapping to a target standard-cell library at the **worst-case corner (SS / 0.81V / 125°C)**.
- **Design Compiler / Genus** generates the gate-level netlist and a preliminary SDC.
- Output: `.v` netlist + `.sdf` (estimated) + `.sdc`.

### Stage 3: Floorplanning
- Macro dimensions (Width × Height), pin placement, and **blockages** are frozen.
- Utilization target: **70%–80%**.
- Power planning: **power ring** + **power straps** in higher metal layers.
- Boundary NWell / DP (Double Patterning)-aware pin access.

### Stage 4: Placement
- Standard cells placed within rows; congestion-driven placement.
- Tap cell insertion (every **< 60 µm** spacing) to mitigate **latch-up**.
- Well-bias / DPO (Design-Point Optimization) cells placed.

### Stage 5: Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)
- Skew target: typically **< 50 ps** for high-performance blocks.
- H-tree, fishbone, or mesh topology depending on clock-domain count.

### Stage 6: Routing
- Global routing → Track assignment → Detailed routing.
- **DRC (Design Rule Check)** clean (zero DRC violations in production).
- **Signal integrity**: cross-talk, IR-drop, EM (Electromigration) checks.

### Stage 7: Optimization & Engineering Change Orders (ECOs)
- Hold-time fixing, setup-time slack improvement, area/leakage reduction.
- Size-up / size-down of cells, buffer insertion, Vt-swapping (LVT → SVT → HVT).

### Stage 8: Verification & Sign-Off
- **STA (Static Timing Analysis)** across **all 27 PVT corners**.
- **IR-drop analysis**, **EM analysis**, **SI analysis**.
- **DRC, LVS, ERC** clean on the GDS.
- **Formal equivalence** vs. original RTL.

> [!NOTE]
> The above 8 stages are precisely the **flow that KTU expects students to enumerate** in the 14-mark questions. Memorize them in this exact order.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are exam-critical. The pipe symbol is rendered as `\vert` to preserve table integrity.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{pd} = \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{dd}}{I_{drv}}$ | Propagation delay of a CMOS gate (alpha-power model simplified) | **seconds (s)** |
| 2 | $PDP = P_{avg} \cdot T_{pd}$ | Power-Delay Product (energy per switching event) | **Joules (J)** |
| 3 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f$ | Dynamic power (switching) | **Watts (W)** |
| 4 | $P_{leak} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{dd}$ | Static / leakage power | **Watts (W)** |
| 5 | $A_{macro} = W_{core} \cdot H_{core}$ | Hard macro area (core region) | **µm²** |
| 6 | $U = \dfrac{A_{stdcell}}{A_{core}}$ | Standard-cell utilization (typical 0.65–0.80) | **dimensionless** |
| 7 | $D_{0} = \dfrac{-\ln(Y)}{A}$ | Defect density given yield Y and die area A | **defects/cm²** |
| 8 | $Y = e^{-D_{0} \cdot A}$ | Poisson yield model (for redundancy-less macros) | **dimensionless** |
| 9 | $FOM = \dfrac{1}{PDP}$ | Figure of Merit (higher = better macro) | **J⁻¹** |
| 10 | $T_{skew} = \vert t_{clk,arr} - t_{clk,req} \vert$ | Clock skew between launch and capture flip-flops | **seconds (s)** |
| 11 | $T_{setup} = T_{clk\to Q} + T_{comb} + T_{setup,FF} \le T_{clk}$ | Setup-time inequality | **seconds (s)** |
| 12 | $T_{hold} = T_{clk\to Q} + T_{comb} \ge T_{hold,FF}$ | Hold-time inequality | **seconds (s)** |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hard macros are the **backbone of every modern SoC**. Real-world applications:

1. **Smartphone SoCs (Qualcomm Snapdragon, Apple M-series, MediaTek Dimensity)** — CPU cores, GPU cores, ISP, Modem are all **hardened** for power, performance, and area (PPA) gains.
2. **Memory Compilers → SRAM Hard Macros** — Generated as hard macros with **bit-cell arrays, sense-amps, write-drivers**, and characterized across PVT.
3. **SerDes / PLLs** — Analog mixed-signal hard macros that cannot be re-synthesized downstream.
4. **Standard I/O Pads** — Hardened to meet **ESD, latch-up, and drive-strength** specifications.
5. **AI Accelerators (NPU / TPU blocks)** — Hardened once and reused across multiple product SKUs.

> [!TIP]
> KTU board examiners frequently ask: *"Why cannot a hard macro be re-synthesized?"* — The correct answer is that **layout parasitics (RC) and physical geometry are frozen**, so re-synthesis would invalidate the published `.lib` timing models.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hard Macro Figure of Merit (FOM) from First Principles

The **Figure of Merit (FOM)** is the *single scalar metric* that allows two hard macros to be compared. We will derive it from the underlying power and delay formulas.

### Step 1 — Start with the alpha-power law delay model
For a CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance $C_L$ at supply $V_{dd}$:

$$
T_{pd} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{dd}}{\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{dd} - V_t)^{\alpha}}
$$

where:
- $C_L$ is the load capacitance in **Farads**.
- $V_{dd}$ is the supply voltage in **Volts**.
- $\mu_n C_{ox}$ is the process transconductance.
- $W/L$ is the transistor aspect ratio.
- $\alpha$ is the velocity-saturation index ($\approx 1.3$ for short-channel devices).

### Step 2 — Simplify for hard-macro benchmarking
In productization, we do not need the full physics. We collapse all process constants into a single technology-dependent constant $K_p$:

$$
T_{pd} = \frac{K_p \cdot C_L \cdot V_{dd}}{(V_{dd} - V_t)^{\alpha}}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the dynamic switching power
The dynamic power consumed by the macro when switching at frequency $f$ with switching activity factor $\alpha_a$ (note: $\alpha$ reused; in this derivation we use $\beta$ for activity to avoid clash):

$$
P_{dyn} = \beta \cdot C_{total} \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f
$$

where $C_{total}$ is the total switched capacitance of the macro.

### Step 4 — Form the Power-Delay Product (PDP)
PDP represents **energy per switching event** — a fundamental property of a logic gate family:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PDP &= P_{dyn} \cdot T_{pd} \\
&= \left[\beta \cdot C_{total} \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f\right] \cdot \left[\frac{K_p \cdot C_L \cdot V_{dd}}{(V_{dd} - V_t)^{\alpha}}\right] \\
&= \frac{\beta \cdot K_p \cdot C_{total} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{dd}^{3} \cdot f}{(V_{dd} - V_t)^{\alpha}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic of the multiplication:** Energy = Power × Time. We multiplied the power equation (energy per unit time) by the delay equation (time per switching event) to obtain energy per event. The $V_{dd}^2$ and $V_{dd}$ combine to $V_{dd}^3$. Capacitances multiply.

### Step 5 — Invert PDP to form FOM
By KTU definition:

$$
FOM = \frac{1}{PDP} = \frac{(V_{dd} - V_t)^{\alpha}}{\beta \cdot K_p \cdot C_{total} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{dd}^{3} \cdot f}
$$

**Higher FOM = better macro** (lower energy per operation).

### Step 6 — Engineering interpretation
A good hard macro is one that **minimizes** $PDP$, equivalently **maximizes** FOM. Practical levers in the productization engineer’s hand:
- **Reduce $V_{dd}$** → cubes the energy saving (3rd power).
- **Increase $V_t$** (use HVT cells) → increases $(V_{dd} - V_t)^{\alpha}$ in denominator, wait — no, $(V_{dd} - V_t)$ shrinks, so $FOM$ drops. Use selectively.
- **Reduce $C_L$** → better buffer-tree sizing.
- **Reduce $\beta$ (clock-gating)** → reduces dynamic power.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Power Breakdown of a Production Hard Macro

The table below is the **canonical productization breakdown** that KTU expects in 7-mark sub-questions.

| Power Component | Symbol | Origin | Mitigation Strategy in Productization |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Switching Power** | $P_{sw}$ | $C_{total} V_{dd}^{2} f \beta$ | Clock-gating, multi-Vt, multi- $V_{dd}$ |
| **Internal Short-Circuit Power** | $P_{sc}$ | Crowbar current during transition | Reduce rise/fall times, taper buffers |
| **Gate Leakage Power** | $P_{gate}$ | Tunneling through thin gate oxide | High-K dielectric, longer-channel devices |
| **Subthreshold Leakage** | $P_{sub}$ | $I_{off}$ when $V_{gs} = 0$ | Power gating, body-bias, HVT cells |
| **Total Power** | $P_{total} = P_{sw} + P_{sc} + P_{gate} + P_{sub}$ | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU trap: students write $P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{leak}$ which is correct **only if** $P_{sc}$ is included in $P_{dyn}$. Board examiners want the 4-component explicit form.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Yield Calculation for a Hard Macro

**Given:**
- Hard macro die area: $A = 4 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.04 \text{ cm}^2$
- Defect density: $D_0 = 0.5 \text{ defects/cm}^2$

**Find:** Yield $Y$ using the Poisson model.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= e^{-D_0 \cdot A} \\
  &= e^{-(0.5)(0.04)} \\
  &= e^{-0.02} \\
  &= 0.9802 \\
  &\approx 98.02\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic of each step:**
- Convert 4 mm² to 0.04 cm² (1 cm² = 100 mm²).
- Multiply $D_0 \cdot A = 0.5 \times 0.04 = 0.02$.
- Apply exponential.
- Result: 98.02% yield — exceeds the **> 95% production threshold**.

This is a **typical KTU 14-mark sub-question pattern**: numerical values provided, formula invoked, units checked.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: The Productization Flow (Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This diagram maps the **8-stage pipeline** and the **3 deliverables** that exit the productization flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rtlA[Stage 1: RTL Signoff] --> synA[Stage 2: Logic Synthesis]
    synA --> fpA[Stage 3: Floorplanning]
    fpA --> plcA[Stage 4: Standard Cell Placement]
    plcA --> ctsA[Stage 5: Clock Tree Synthesis]
    ctsA --> rteA[Stage 6: Global and Detailed Routing]
    rteA --> optA[Stage 7: Optimization and ECO]
    optA --> vfyA[Stage 8: STA and Signoff Verification]
    vfyA --> gate1{Maturity Gate}

    gate1 -- Beta Macro --> outB[Beta Hard Macro GDSII LEF DEF lib preliminary]
    gate1 -- Production Macro --> outP[Production Hard Macro with PVT corners .lib 27]
    gate1 -- Silicon Proven --> outS[Silicon Proven Hard Macro with measured silicon data]

    style rtlA fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style synA fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style fpA fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style plcA fill:#8c564b,color:#fff
    style ctsA fill:#e377c2,color:#fff
    style rteA fill:#7f7f7f,color:#fff
    style optA fill:#bcbd22,color:#000
    style vfyA fill:#17becf,color:#fff
    style gate1 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
```

**Block-by-block functional mapping:**

| Node | Function |
|---|---|
| rtlA | LINT, CDC, code-coverage gate |
| synA | Technology mapping → `.v` + preliminary `.sdf` |
| fpA | Macro dimensions, pin placement, power-grid |
| plcA | Cell placement, tap-cell insertion, congestion |
| ctsA | Skew minimization, clock-driver sizing |
| rteA | DRC-clean routing, SI/EM check |
| optA | Setup/hold fixing, Vt-swapping, area recovery |
| vfyA | Cross-corner STA, LEC, IR-drop, EM |
| gate1 | Maturity classifier |
| outB / outP / outS | Three shipping tiers of the hard macro |

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Hard Macro Deliverable Package (Mermaid Block View)

A **productized hard macro** is delivered as a *set of files*. The downstream SoC integrator consumes the package through **three views**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    hmCore[Hard Macro Core GDSII] --> ship[Shipping Package]
    lefA[LEF Abstract View] --> ship
    defA[DEF Detailed View] --> ship
    libA[lib Timing Power View] --> ship
    sdfA[sdf Delay Annotation] --> ship
    docA[datasheet Documentation] --> ship
    spiceA[spice Netlist for Analog] --> ship
    verilogA[verilog Functional Model] --> ship

    ship --> view1[Physical View: GDSII LEF DEF]
    ship --> view2[Timing View: lib sdf]
    ship --> view3[Behavioral View: verilog VHDL]
    ship --> view4[Documentation: datasheet App Note]

    style hmCore fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style ship fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff

    style view2 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style view3 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style view4 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Decision Tree — Soft vs. Firm vs. Hard Macro

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ipQ{What is delivered?}
    ipQ -- Synthesizable HDL --> softM[Soft Macro RTL Modifiable]
    ipQ -- Netlist plus Floorplan --> firmM[Firm Macro Partial Modifiable]
    ipQ -- GDSII plus LEF plus lib --> hardM[Hard Macro Not Modifiable]

    softM -- Hardening --> firmM
    firmM -- Hardening --> hardM
    hardM -- Qualification --> spM[Silicon Proven Hard Macro]

    style ipQ fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
```
