<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a hard macro. List any two deliverables required for a productized hard macro.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**
- A **hard macro** is a fully placed-and-routed, geometrically frozen physical IP block with characterized timing, power, and physical views, supplied to integrators as a non-modifiable, pre-qualified building block *(1.5 marks)*.
- Two deliverables:
  1. **LEF (Library Exchange Format)** — abstract view with pin positions, blockage, and routing directions *(0.75 marks)*.
  2. **`.lib` (Liberty)** — timing and power characterization across PVT corners *(0.75 marks)*.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between a soft macro and a hard macro in two points.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

| # | Soft Macro | Hard Macro |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Delivered as **synthesizable HDL** (Verilog/VHDL) | Delivered as **GDSII + LEF + .lib** *(1.5 marks)* |
| 2 | **Modifiable** — integrator can re-synthesize for any tech | **Not modifiable** — geometry frozen *(1.5 marks)* |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the complete productization flow for a hard macro with a neat block diagram. List the deliverables at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hard macro occupies an area of $6.25 \text{ mm}^2$ on a 28nm process. The process defect density is $D_0 = 0.8 \text{ defects/cm}^2$. Using the Poisson yield model, compute the yield. Comment on whether this macro meets the **> 95% production target**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.A(a) — Model Answer (7 marks)

**Productization Flow (Block Diagram — 4 marks):**

| Stage | Process Step | Deliverable |
|---|---|---|
| 1. RTL Sign-Off | LINT, CDC, code coverage | Verified RTL + assertions |
| 2. Synthesis | Tech mapping (SS / 0.81V / 125°C) | Gate-level `.v`, preliminary `.sdf` |
| 3. Floorplanning | Macro dimensions, pin placement, power-grid | Floorplan DEF |
| 4. Placement | Cell placement, tap-cell insertion | Placement DEF |
| 5. CTS | Clock-tree synthesis, skew target < 50 ps | CTS log, clock-tree SPEF |
| 6. Routing | Global + detailed routing | Routed DEF |
| 7. Optimization | Setup/hold fixing, Vt-swap, sizing | Optimized netlist |
| 8. Sign-Off | STA (27 corners), LEC, IR-drop, EM, DRC/LVS/ERC | **GDSII + LEF + .lib + datasheet** |

**Brief explanation (3 marks):**
- The productization engineer hardens the IP from RTL to GDSII by progressively adding physical constraints.
- The **power ring + power straps** are added in floorplan (M5/M6 layers) to deliver uniform current density.
- **Tap cells** are inserted every **< 60 µm** spacing to prevent **latch-up** between NMOS and PMOS.
- The **CTS** stage uses H-tree or mesh topology to minimize **$T_{skew}$**.
- Final **STA** must close timing at all 27 PVT corners, with **zero DRC, LVS, ERC violations**.

---

#### Solution to Q.A(b) — Model Answer (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $A = 6.25 \text{ mm}^2 = 6.25 \times 10^{-2} \text{ cm}^2 = 0.0625 \text{ cm}^2$
- $D_0 = 0.8 \text{ defects/cm}^2$

**Step 1 — Convert area to cm² (1 mark):**
$$
A = 6.25 \text{ mm}^2 \times \frac{1 \text{ cm}^2}{100 \text{ mm}^2} = 0.0625 \text{ cm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Poisson yield formula (2 marks):**
$$
Y = e^{-D_0 \cdot A} = e^{-(0.8)(0.0625)} = e^{-0.05}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate (2 marks):**
$$
Y = e^{-0.05} \approx 0.9512 = 95.12\%
$$

**Step 4 — Comment (2 marks):**
- The yield is **95.12%**, which is **just above** the **95% production target** ✓.
- The macro is **marginally acceptable** for production. To improve yield, the engineer should either (a) reduce the macro area by tightening the floorplan, or (b) apply **redundancy** (e.g., spare rows in SRAM).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a clear comparison table, classify IP/blocks into soft, firm, and hard macros. Explain why a hard macro is the *highest readiness level* in SoC integration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a CMOS inverter inside a hard macro, derive the **Figure of Merit (FOM)** starting from the **alpha-power delay model** and the **dynamic-power equation**. Clearly state the engineering interpretation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.B(a) — Model Answer (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Soft Macro | Firm Macro | Hard Macro |
|---|---|---|---|
| Delivered form | RTL | Netlist + floorplan | GDSII + LEF + .lib |
| Modifiable? | Yes | Partial | No |
| Characterization | Vendor estimate | Partial | Full PVT 27 corners |
| Silicon-proven? | No | Usually no | Yes |
| Effort in SoC integration | High (full P&R) | Medium | Low (drop-in) |
| Risk to integrator | High | Medium | Low |
| Readiness level | Level 1 | Level 2 | **Level 3 (Highest)** |

**Why hard macro is the highest readiness (3 marks):**
1. **Layout-frozen**: The integrator cannot re-synthesize or re-place. This eliminates timing closure risk.
2. **Fully characterized**: The `.lib` covers all PVT corners and operating modes, so the integrator's STA is sign-off clean by construction.
3. **Silicon-proven**: At least one MPW run has demonstrated that the macro meets performance, power, and yield targets in real silicon — providing statistical confidence.

---

#### Solution to Q.B(b) — Model Answer (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Start from the alpha-power delay model (2 marks):**
$$
T_{pd} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{dd}}{\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{dd} - V_t)^{\alpha}}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply dynamic-power equation (1 mark):**
$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha_a \cdot C_{total} \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f
$$

**Step 3 — Form Power-Delay Product (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
PDP &= P_{dyn} \cdot T_{pd} \\
&= \frac{\alpha_a \cdot C_{total} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{dd}^{3} \cdot f}{\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{dd} - V_t)^{\alpha}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Form FOM and interpret (2 marks):**
$$
FOM = \frac{1}{PDP} = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{dd} - V_t)^{\alpha}}{\alpha_a \cdot C_{total} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{dd}^{3} \cdot f}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:**
- FOM is **inversely proportional** to $V_{dd}^3$ → **voltage scaling is the most powerful lever** to improve FOM.
- FOM is **inversely proportional** to $C_{total}$ and $C_L$ → **buffer-tree sizing, gate sizing, and clock-gating** directly improve FOM.
- Higher FOM = **better energy-efficiency per switching event** = **better hard macro**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in productization questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "PVT" corners:** Writing 3 corners instead of the full **3 × 3 × 3 = 27 PVT corners**. Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the area conversion in yield:** $1 \text{ cm}^2 = 100 \text{ mm}^2$, **not** $1000 \text{ mm}^2$. Watch the decimal.
> 3. **Calling a firm macro "hard":** A firm macro is **gate-level netlist + partial floorplan**, not a GDSII. State this distinction explicitly.
> 4. **Omitting `.lib` from the deliverables list:** The Liberty timing file is **non-negotiable**; examiners expect it in every answer.
> 5. **Mis-stating the 4 power components:** $P_{total} = P_{sw} + P_{sc} + P_{gate} + P_{sub}$. Don't merge $P_{gate}$ and $P_{sub}$ into "leakage" — both must be listed separately.
> 6. **Skipping the unit check:** When computing yield, area must be in **cm²**, not mm². Show the conversion line explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Hard Macro** = GDSII + LEF + `.lib` + datasheet, **frozen** geometry.
- ✅ Productization flow has **8 stages** — RTL → Synthesis → Floorplan → Placement → CTS → Route → Opt → Sign-off.
- ✅ **Three deliverables** from sign-off: physical view, timing view, behavioral view.
- ✅ **Three maturity tiers**: Beta, Production, Silicon-Proven.
- ✅ **27 PVT corners** must be closed for production-grade.
- ✅ **Tap cells every < 60 µm**; **clock skew < 50 ps**; **utilization 70–80%**.
- ✅ **Yield formula**: $Y = e^{-D_0 A}$ (Poisson).
- ✅ **Power-Delay Product**: $PDP = P_{dyn} \cdot T_{pd}$ — energy per event.
- ✅ **FOM** = $1 / PDP$ — higher is better, scales as $1/V_{dd}^3$.
- ✅ **Power components** (4): $P_{sw}, P_{sc}, P_{gate}, P_{sub}$.
- ✅ **Three macro classes**: Soft (RTL, modifiable) → Firm (netlist, partial) → Hard (GDSII, frozen).
- ✅ **DRC, LVS, ERC** must be **zero violations** in production hard macros.
- ✅ **LEF = abstract**; **DEF = detailed**; **GDSII = physical layout**.
- ✅ Hard macros are **non-modifiable** — integrator drops them as black-boxes.
- ✅ **IP-readiness level 3** is the highest classification per KTU 2024 Scheme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model Development for Hard Macros
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT602 - SYSTEM ON CHIP DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Model Development for Hard Macros

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Hard Macro in SoC Design?

A **Hard Macro** is a pre-designed, fully placed-and-routed, pre-verified, and pre-characterized Intellectual Property (IP) block that is delivered to the SoC integrator as a **frozen, geometrically fixed layout** in a specific technology node. It behaves like a **"black box"** with comprehensive models describing its logical, timing, power, and physical characteristics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A hard macro is an IP block whose **physical layout is finalized and technology-specific**. The internal structure (gates, wires, transistor sizes) is hidden from the integrator; only external interfaces (pins, timing arcs, power rails) and abstract models are exposed.

### 1.2 Hard Macro vs. Soft Macro vs. Firm Macro

| Macro Type | Deliverable | Flexibility | Performance | Portability |
|------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Soft Macro** | RTL (synthesizable HDL code) | Very High (re-targetable) | Lower (depends on synthesis + P&R) | Portable across nodes |
| **Firm Macro** | Gate-level netlist + floorplan constraints | Medium | Medium | Partially portable |
| **Hard Macro** | GDSII + LEF + .lib + SPEF + Verilog | **Very Low (frozen)** | **Highest (pre-optimized)** | **Node-specific** |

> [!NOTE]
> Examples of hard macros: **SRAM/DRAM compilers, standard cell libraries, PLLs, DLLs, ADC/DAC blocks, SerDes, I/O pads, USB PHYs, hard CPU cores (e.g., ARM Cortex-A series).**

## 1.3 What is "Model Development" for Hard Macros?

Model Development is the **process of generating, abstracting, validating, and documenting the behavioral, electrical, and physical models** that allow a hard macro to be used inside a larger SoC design flow (synthesis, place & route, static timing analysis, power analysis, sign-off verification).

> [!NOTE]
> Think of it as creating a **"user manual + datasheet"** for the hard macro — a complete set of files that downstream EDA tools (Synopsys DC, Cadence Innovus, PrimeTime, Voltus) can read to understand how the macro behaves under every possible operating condition.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Pre-Fabricated House Module":**
> Imagine building a city. A **hard macro** is like a **pre-fabricated, fully-furnished apartment module** shipped to a construction site. You cannot change the internal wiring, room layout, or plumbing (frozen layout). What you receive along with it:
> - 📐 A **blueprint of the outer dimensions and door/window positions** → **LEF (physical model)**
> - ⚡ A **datasheet stating power consumption, response time, and temperature limits** → **Liberty .lib (timing/power model)**
> - 🔌 A **functional description of what each switch does** → **Verilog/VHDL behavioral model**
> - 🌡️ A **performance report under different climates and loads** → **PVT corner models**
> - 📊 A **statistical reliability certificate** → **Aging/Sigma Variation models**
>
> **Model Development** is the engineering work that creates all these documents so the apartment module plugs seamlessly into the building — and the city (SoC) works as one system.

## 1.5 Why Model Development is Critical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** A hard macro without a properly developed model is **useless** in a modern SoC flow. The quality, accuracy, and completeness of these models directly determine:
> - **Timing closure success** during P&R
> - **Power estimation accuracy** (budget verification)
> - **DFM/DFT compatibility**
> - **Yield and reliability predictions**
> - **Time-to-market** for the SoC product
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Categories of Models Required for a Hard Macro

A complete hard macro model package consists of **seven interconnected model types**:

### 2.1.1 Logical / Functional Model
- **Format:** Verilog (`.v`), VHDL (`.vhd`), SystemVerilog (`.sv`)
- **Purpose:** Describes input-to-output Boolean behavior for **functional simulation**, **logic synthesis** (only for soft IP, but used for verification in hard IP), and **equivalence checking**.
- **Granularity:** Behavioral (RTL) or gate-level netlist.
- **Key elements:** Module ports, parameter list, always/combinational blocks, specify blocks (timing checks for simulation).

### 2.1.2 Physical Model — LEF (Library Exchange Format)
- **Format:** ASCII text file (`.lef`)
- **Purpose:** Provides **abstract geometric information** required by the P&R tool **without revealing the internal GDSII layout**.
- **Key sections:**
  - `MACRO` definition (name, size, pins)
  - `OBS` (obstructions) — regions where the router **cannot** place metal
  - `PIN` (pin geometry, layer, direction, antenna info)
  - Routing `PREFER`/spacing rules
  - `FOREIGN` cell interactions
  - `DENSITY` (metal density for DFM)

> [!NOTE]
> **LEF is technology-abstracted** — it does not include detailed parasitics. This keeps the P&R tool's data volume small and routing fast.

### 2.1.3 Timing Model — Liberty (`.lib`)
- **Format:** Synopsys Liberty (`.lib`), open-source `.lib` in Liberty 2020 syntax
- **Purpose:** Provides **delay and output slew** look-up tables as a function of:
  - **Input slew** ($S_{in}$)
  - **Output load** ($C_{load}$)
- **Abstraction types:**
  - **NLDM** (Non-Linear Delay Model) — 2D LUTs
  - **CCS** (Composite Current Source) — current waveform model (more accurate, required at advanced nodes)
  - **ECSM** (Effective Current Source Model) — Cadence variant of CCS

### 2.1.4 Power Model
- **Format:** Liberty with `power` group, or separate **CPF** (Common Power Format) / **UPF** (Unified Power Format) files.
- **Power components:**
  - **Leakage power** ($P_{leak}$) — sub-threshold and gate leakage
  - **Internal power** ($P_{int}$) — short-circuit and internal capacitance charging
  - **Switching power** ($P_{sw}$) — output load charging
- Total power per cell (or macro):

$$
P_{total} = P_{sw} + P_{int} + P_{leak}
$$

### 2.1.5 Parasitic Model
- **Format:** SPEF (Standard Parasitic Exchange Format, `.spef`), DSPF, RSPF
- **Purpose:** Distributed **RC** network extracted from the layout for **gate-level timing simulation** and **STA**.
- **Components:** Resistors (per metal layer sheet resistance), capacitors (area + fringe + coupling).

### 2.1.6 PVT Corner Model
**PVT** = **Process × Voltage × Temperature**

- A hard macro is characterized at multiple corners to guarantee functionality across silicon variation.
- Typical 5-corner set: `FF / SS / TT / SF / FS` (Fast-NMOS/Fast-PMOS, etc.)

### 2.1.7 Reliability / Variation Model
- **Aging models:** NBTI (Negative Bias Temperature Instability), HCI (Hot Carrier Injection), TDDB
- **Statistical models:** Liberty Variation Format (LVF), AOCV (Advanced On-Chip Variation)
- **Purpose:** Predicts timing degradation over the **10-year lifetime** of the chip.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Model Development Flow

The hard macro model development follows a structured pipeline:

1. **Design Freeze** — Internal GDSII is signed off (DRC/LVS clean).
2. **Layout Extraction** — Extract RC parasitics using StarRC, Calibre xRC, or Quantus.
3. **Timing Characterization** — Run circuit simulation (HSPICE, FineSim, Spectre) on every timing arc, every PVT corner.
4. **Liberty Generation** — Use **SiliconSmart**, **Liberate**, or **PrimeTime-SI** to convert SPICE data into `.lib`.
5. **Power Characterization** — Measure switching, internal, and leakage power per arc.
6. **LEF Abstraction** — Use **Abstract Generator** (Innovus, ICC2) to produce `.lef` from GDSII.
7. **Behavioral Model Generation** — Write/verify Verilog/VHDL and connect-list models.
8. **Model Validation** — Cross-check `.lib` against SPICE on regression suite (e.g., <2% error).
9. **Corner Stitching** — Combine all PVT corner `.lib` files into a unified library.
10. **Documentation & Release** — Deliver model package with **datasheet**, **usage guidelines**, and **integration notes**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why a Formula Sheet?** Timing and power models are fundamentally based on **table lookups** and a few **closed-form equations** (mostly MOSFET-level). The next sub-section gives you the canonical KTU-ready equations.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are directly testable in KTU ESE / MSE under PCEVT602 Module 4. Master these.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | Total Cell Power | $P_{total} = P_{sw} + P_{int} + P_{leak}$ | All in **W (Watts)** | Switching dominates dynamic |
| 2 | Switching Power | $P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_{load} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$ | $\alpha$ = activity factor, $C_{load}$ in F, $V_{DD}$ in V, $f$ in Hz | CMOS dynamic power |
| 3 | Short-Circuit Power | $P_{sc} = I_{sc} \cdot V_{DD}$ | $I_{sc}$ in A | During input transition |
| 4 | Leakage Power | $P_{leak} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | $I_{leak}$ in A | Subthreshold + gate |
| 5 | Elmore Delay (RC tree) | $T_{d} = \sum_{i} R_{i} \cdot C_{i,downstream}$ | R in $\Omega$, C in F | Used for wire delay |
| 6 | Gate Delay (alpha-power) | $t_{d} = \frac{C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}}{I_{D0}} \cdot \frac{1}{(V_{DD} - V_{TH})^{\alpha}}$ | $\alpha \in [1, 2]$ | Empirical MOSFET model |
| 7 | Output Slew (NLDM) | $S_{out} = f_{table}(S_{in}, C_{load})$ | Slew in **ps** or **ns** | Look-up table in `.lib` |
| 8 | Cell Delay (NLDM) | $D_{cell} = f_{table}(S_{in}, C_{load})$ | Delay in **ps** or **ns** | Look-up table in `.lib` |
| 9 | Hold Slack | $Slack_{hold} = T_{ck} - T_{cq} - T_{comb} - T_{setup}$ | All in **ns** or **ps** | Must be $\geq 0$ |
| 10 | Setup Slack | $Slack_{setup} = T_{cycle} - T_{cq} - T_{comb} - T_{setup}$ | All in **ns** or **ps** | Must be $\geq 0$ |
| 11 | DIBL Factor | $V_{TH,eff} = V_{TH,0} - \eta \cdot V_{DS}$ | $\eta$ in V/V | Leakage model |
| 12 | Datasheet $V_{OH}$ Noise Margin | $NM_{H} = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | All in **V** | High noise margin |
| 13 | Total Capacitance (per wire) | $C_{total} = C_{area} \cdot L \cdot W + C_{fringe} + C_{coupling}$ | F | From extraction |
| 14 | Aging Degradation (NBTI) | $\Delta V_{TH} \propto A \cdot t^{n} \cdot e^{-E_{a}/kT}$ | $n \approx 0.25$ | Reaction-diffusion |
| 15 | Hard Macro Area (LEF) | $A_{macro} = W_{macro} \times H_{macro}$ | $\mu m^{2}$ | From LEF SIZE |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value expressions and "such that" bars use **\vert** or **\mid** — never the raw pipe `|` — to preserve markdown table integrity. (Already applied above.)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mobile SoCs (Qualcomm, Apple, MediaTek):** Hard macro models of CPU/GPU cores, SRAMs, and PHYs are characterized once and reused across 50+ SoCs/year. A 1% timing error → million-dollar re-spins.
- **Automotive (ISO 26262):** Hard macros must be modeled at **AEC-Q100 corners** ($-40^{\circ}C$ to $150^{\circ}C$) with **aging** and **radiation** models.
- **AI Accelerators (NVIDIA, AMD):** SRAM and MAC hard macros are characterized across **process corners** and **voltage scaling** to support DVFS.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Switching Power of a CMOS Hard Macro Output

We derive the fundamental switching power equation that forms the **backbone of every `.lib` power model** for hard macros.

### Step 1: Charge Delivery to a Capacitive Load

Consider a CMOS inverter driving an external load capacitance $C_L$ through a hard macro output pin. The PMOS transistor charges $C_L$ from 0 to $V_{DD}$ during a low-to-high transition.

The energy drawn from the power supply is:

$$
E_{drawn} = \int_{0}^{T} V_{DD} \cdot i(t) \, dt
$$

Since the current charges the capacitor:

$$
i(t) = C_L \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute and Simplify

$$
E_{drawn} = \int_{0}^{V_{DD}} V_{DD} \cdot C_L \, dV_{out}
$$

$$
E_{drawn} = V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}
$$

### Step 3: Energy Stored in the Capacitor

Half the energy is stored on $C_L$:

$$
E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}
$$

The other half is dissipated as heat in the PMOS resistance. During the next high-to-low transition, the same stored energy is dissipated in the NMOS.

### Step 4: Energy per Full Switching Cycle

$$
E_{cycle} = C_L V_{DD}^{2}
$$

### Step 5: Convert to Power Using Activity Factor

If the output switches with **activity factor** $\alpha$ (probability of switching per clock cycle) at clock frequency $f_{clk}$:

$$
P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot E_{cycle} \cdot f_{clk}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}\,}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** Switching power scales **quadratically with $V_{DD}$** — this is why low-power SoCs aggressively use DVFS (Dynamic Voltage Frequency Scaling).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Elmore Delay for a Distributed RC Wire (Hard Macro Routing)

The Elmore delay is the **workhorse delay model** used in pre-layout estimation and post-layout STA for hard macro interconnects.

### Step 1: Model a Wire as a Lumped RC

A wire of length $L$, width $W$, with sheet resistance $R_{\square}$ and capacitance per unit length $C_{u}$:

$$
R_{wire} = R_{\square} \cdot \frac{L}{W}, \quad C_{wire} = C_{u} \cdot L
$$

### Step 2: Distributed $\pi$-model

For accuracy, segment the wire into $N$ identical $\pi$-sections. Each segment has:
- $r = R_{wire}/N$
- $c = C_{wire}/N$ (split half at each node)

### Step 3: Apply Elmore's Theorem

The Elmore delay from input node to the output node $k$ is:

$$
T_{D,Elmore} = \sum_{i} R_{i \to k} \cdot C_{i}
$$

where $R_{i \to k}$ is the common-path resistance from capacitor $C_i$ to the output node $k$.

### Step 4: Specialization to a Uniform N-Segment Wire

$$
T_{D} = R_{wire} \cdot C_{wire} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2N} \right) - \frac{R_{wire} \cdot C_{wire}}{6N^{2}}
$$

For large $N$ (limit as $N \to \infty$):

$$
\boxed{\,T_{D,Elmore} \approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot R_{wire} \cdot C_{wire}\,}
$$

### Step 5: Substituting the Wire Parameters

$$
T_{D} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \left(R_{\square} \cdot \frac{L}{W}\right) \cdot (C_{u} \cdot L)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_{D} = \frac{R_{\square} \cdot C_{u}}{2W} \cdot L^{2}\,}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** Elmore delay grows as **$L^{2}$** — doubling the wire length **quadruples** the delay. This is the fundamental reason hard macros are kept physically small and local.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Liberty NLDM Table Generation

The NLDM (Non-Linear Delay Model) used in every `.lib` is built from SPICE simulation. Let us derive how the table indices are normalized.

### Step 1: SPICE Simulation Setup

For each input pin-to-output pin **timing arc** (e.g., CLK → Q of a flip-flop), we sweep:
- $S_{in} \in \{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_m\}$ — input slew values
- $C_L \in \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n\}$ — output load values

For each $(S_i, C_j)$ pair, SPICE produces a delay $D_{ij}$ and output slew $S_{out,ij}$.

### Step 2: Construct the 2D LUT

The resulting Liberty file contains:

```
lu_table_template(delay_template_7x7) {
    variable_1 : input_net_transition;
    variable_2 : total_output_net_capacitance;
    index_1("0.005, 0.020, 0.050, 0.100, 0.300, 0.600, 1.200");
    index_2("0.001, 0.005, 0.020, 0.050, 0.100, 0.300, 0.500");
}
cell(myHardMacro) {
    pin(Q) {
        timing() {
            related_pin : "CLK";
            cell_rise(delay_template_7x7) {
                values("...");
            }
            cell_fall(delay_template_7x7) {
                values("...");
            }
        }
    }
}
```

### Step 3: Interpolation During STA

When PrimeTime encounters a real $(S_{in}^{real}, C_L^{real})$ pair, it performs **2D piecewise-linear interpolation**:

$$
D_{real} = D_{i,j} + \frac{(D_{i+1,j} - D_{i,j})}{(S_{i+1} - S_i)} \cdot (S_{in}^{real} - S_i) + \frac{(D_{i,j+1} - D_{i,j})}{(C_{j+1} - C_j)} \cdot (C_L^{real} - C_j)
$$

This is the **fundamental reason** for correct corner modeling — if PVT is wrong, the table entries are wrong, and the interpolation is wrong.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: PVT Corner Combinations

A hard macro at 28nm or below is typically characterized over the following **5-corner matrix**:

| Corner | NMOS | PMOS | Voltage | Temperature | Use Case |
|--------|------|------|---------|-------------|----------|
| **TT** | Typical | Typical | Nominal ($V_{DD,nom}$) | 25°C | Nominal design centering |
| **FF** | Fast | Fast | $+10\% \cdot V_{DD,nom}$ | $-40^{\circ}C$ | Hold-fixing, max-speed check |
| **SS** | Slow | Slow | $-10\% \cdot V_{DD,nom}$ | $125^{\circ}C$ | Setup-fixing, slowest operation |
| **SF** | Slow | Fast | Nominal | 25°C | Asymmetric aging check |
| **FS** | Fast | Slow | Nominal | 25°C | Asymmetric aging check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A **common mistake** is treating "Fast" as "good" universally. FF corners stress **hold time** (signals arrive too quickly), while SS corners stress **setup time** (signals arrive too slowly).

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A hard macro output pin drives a load of $C_L = 50 \, fF$ at $V_{DD} = 1.0 \, V$ with activity factor $\alpha = 0.2$ at $f_{clk} = 500 \, MHz$. Compute the switching power.

**Step 1:** Identify the formula (from Formula Sheet, row 2):

$$
P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values (SI units):

- $\alpha = 0.2$
- $C_L = 50 \times 10^{-15} \, F$
- $V_{DD} = 1.0 \, V$
- $f_{clk} = 500 \times 10^{6} \, Hz$

**Step 3:** Compute term by term:

$$
V_{DD}^{2} = (1.0)^{2} = 1.0 \, V^{2}
$$

$$
C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} = 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.0 = 50 \times 10^{-15} \, J
$$

$$
\alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} = 0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} = 10 \times 10^{-15} \, J
$$

$$
\alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk} = 10 \times 10^{-15} \times 500 \times 10^{6} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \, W
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\,P_{sw} = 5 \, \mu W\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> For a hard macro with **1000 output pins** driving similar loads, total switching power = $1000 \times 5 \, \mu W = 5 \, mW$. This is precisely how SoC power budgets are constructed.

**Reading the diagram:** Each layer **hides internal complexity** from the layer above, while **exposing the right level of detail** to the next consumer. This is the principle of **progressive refinement** in SoC design.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PCEVT602]`

**Q: Differentiate between a hard macro and a soft macro. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Hard Macro | Soft Macro |
|---------|------------|------------|
| **Form** | GDSII (layout) | HDL code (RTL) |
| **Technology** | Node-specific | Technology-independent |
| **Performance** | Pre-optimized (high) | Synthesis-dependent |
| **Flexibility** | Low (frozen) | High (re-targetable) |
| **Time to integrate** | Fast | Slow (synthesis needed) |
| **Examples** | SRAM compiler, ARM Cortex-A77 core, USB PHY, PLL, I/O pad | UART RTL, I2C controller RTL, AXI interconnect RTL, custom DSP RTL |

*(Award: 1 mark for table, 1 mark for hard examples, 1 mark for soft examples.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PCEVT602]`

**Q: List the various models that constitute a complete hard macro model package. Explain any one in detail.**

**Model Answer (3-mark structure):**

A complete hard macro model package includes:

1. **Functional model** (Verilog/VHDL)
2. **Physical model** (LEF)
3. **Timing model** (Liberty `.lib`)
4. **Power model** (Liberty + UPF/CPF)
5. **Parasitic model** (SPEF)
6. **PVT corner models**
7. **Reliability/Statistical models** (LVF, AOCV, aging)

**Detailed explanation of Liberty `.lib`:**
The Liberty timing model provides delay and output-slew as 2D look-up tables indexed by input slew and output load. It is generated from SPICE simulation across all PVT corners and is consumed by STA tools (PrimeTime, Tempus). The format supports NLDM, CCS, and ECSM abstraction levels for varying accuracy/speed trade-offs.

*(Award: 1 mark for list, 2 marks for detailed explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part-B format for a 14-mark question: **two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.** You are given internal choice between **Question A and Question B** — both must be fully solved.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PCEVT602]`

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the complete flow of hard macro model development. Mention the EDA tools used at each stage.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — GDSII Freeze (1 mark):** The hard macro internal layout is finalized. DRC (Design Rule Check) and LVS (Layout vs. Schematic) sign-off is performed using **Cadence PVS, Mentor Calibre, or Synopsys IC Validator**.

**Step 2 — Parasitic Extraction (1 mark):** RC network is extracted from the GDSII using **Synopsys StarRC, Cadence Quantus, or Mentor Calibre xRC**. Output: SPEF/DSPF file.

**Step 3 — SPICE Simulation (1 mark):** For every timing arc (input pin → output pin) at every PVT corner, transient simulation is run using **Synopsys HSPICE, Cadence Spectre, or Siemens FineSim**.

**Step 4 — Liberty Generation (1.5 marks):** SPICE data is converted to Liberty `.lib` using **Synopsys SiliconSmart, Cadence Liberate, or Synopsys PrimeTime-SI** (LVF). Format chosen: NLDM / CCS / ECSM.

**Step 5 — LEF Abstraction (1 mark):** From GDSII, an abstract view is generated using **Cadence Innovus (Abstract Generator)** or **Synopsys ICC2**. Output: `.lef` with PIN, OBS, DENSITY, antenna info.

**Step 6 — Behavioral & Power Models (1 mark):** Verilog/VHDL written or reused; UPF/CPF for multi-VDD and power gating.

**Step 7 — Validation & Release (0.5 mark):** Cross-check `.lib` against SPICE (error < 2%), stitch PVT corners, document.

**Block Diagram (already covered in Section 4.1) — refer to that flow.**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PCEVT602]`

**Q: A hard macro output pin drives a load capacitance of 80 fF at $V_{DD} = 1.2\,V$, activity factor $\alpha = 0.25$, and clock frequency $f = 800\,MHz$. Calculate:**
**(i) Switching power**
**(ii) Energy per switching cycle**
**(iii) Total power if leakage power is 15% of switching power**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Switching Power (2 marks):**

$$
P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}
$$

$$
P_{sw} = 0.25 \times 80 \times 10^{-15} \times (1.2)^{2} \times 800 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
P_{sw} = 0.25 \times 80 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.44 \times 800 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
P_{sw} = 23.04 \times 10^{-6} \, W = 23.04 \, \mu W
$$

**(ii) Energy per Switching Cycle (2.5 marks):**

Energy per transition (full $C_L V_{DD}^{2}$):

$$
E_{cycle} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} = 80 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.44 = 115.2 \times 10^{-15} \, J = 115.2 \, fJ
$$

Effective energy per clock cycle (weighted by $\alpha$):

$$
E_{eff} = \alpha \cdot E_{cycle} = 0.25 \times 115.2 = 28.8 \, fJ
$$

**(iii) Total Power with Leakage (2.5 marks):**

$$
P_{leak} = 0.15 \times P_{sw} = 0.15 \times 23.04 \, \mu W = 3.456 \, \mu W
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{total} = P_{sw} + P_{leak} = 23.04 + 3.456 = 26.496 \, \mu W\,}
$$

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, PCEVT602]`

**Q: Explain the PVT corner characterization methodology for a hard macro. List the standard 5 corners with their use cases and justify why multiple corners are essential.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** PVT corner characterization means generating a separate timing and power model for the hard macro at each combination of Process (P), Voltage (V), and Temperature (T).

**The 5 Standard Corners (3 marks):**

| Corner | Process | Voltage | Temp | Use Case |
|--------|---------|---------|------|----------|
| **TT** | Typical NMOS, Typical PMOS | Nominal $V_{DD}$ | 25°C | Nominal timing centering, sign-off reference |
| **FF** | Fast NMOS, Fast PMOS | $+10\% V_{DD}$ | $-40^{\circ}C$ | Hold-time fixing (signals too fast) |
| **SS** | Slow NMOS, Slow PMOS | $-10\% V_{DD}$ | $125^{\circ}C$ | Setup-time fixing (signals too slow) |
| **SF** | Slow NMOS, Fast PMOS | Nominal | 25°C | Asymmetric aging/race check |
| **FS** | Fast NMOS, Slow PMOS | Nominal | 25°C | Asymmetric aging/race check |

**Why multiple corners (3 marks):**
1. **Process variation:** Across-wafer and across-die variation means some dies have fast transistors and others have slow ones.
2. **Voltage variation:** IR drop, supply noise, DVFS transitions cause $V_{DD}$ to deviate.
3. **Temperature variation:** Hotspots, packaging, ambient temperature cause $T$ to vary from $-40^{\circ}C$ to $150^{\circ}C$ (automotive).
4. **Worst-case guarantees:** A single corner cannot guarantee the design works in all silicon; multi-corner STA is mandatory at 28nm and below.
5. **Hold vs. Setup:** Different corners are critical for different timing checks — FF for hold, SS for setup.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, PCEVT602]`

**Q: Describe the Liberty (.lib) format. Explain NLDM, CCS, and ECSM timing models. Compare their accuracy and use cases.**

**Model Solution:**

**Liberty Format (2 marks):**
Liberty is an ASCII text format introduced by Synopsys. It contains:
- Library-level attributes (technology, units, thresholds)
- Lookup-table templates
- Cell-specific timing, power, and area definitions
- Pin-specific rise/fall arcs, capacitance, and constraints

**NLDM — Non-Linear Delay Model (2 marks):**
- **Approach:** Delay and output slew stored as **2D look-up tables** indexed by input slew and output load.
- **Format:** `cell_rise`, `cell_fall`, `rise_transition`, `fall_transition` LUTs.
- **Use case:** Older nodes ($\geq$ 65nm), fast STA, small file size.
- **Limitation:** Cannot model **Miller effect**, **non-linear** output impedance, or **current waveform** shape — accuracy degrades below 28nm.

**CCS — Composite Current Source Model (1.5 marks):**
- **Approach:** Stores the **time-varying output current waveform** as a function of input slew and output load.
- **Format:** `ccsn_first_stage`, `ccsn_last_stage`, current and voltage waveform tables.
- **Use case:** 28nm and below, **advanced nodes**, noise-aware STA.
- **Advantage:** Captures **Miller capacitance**, **non-linear** driver impedance, and **signal integrity** effects.

**ECSM — Effective Current Source Model (1 mark):**
- **Approach:** Cadence variant of CCS; uses a single **piecewise-linear current source** at the output.
- **Format:** `ecsm_waveform` group in Liberty.
- **Use case:** Cadence Tempus flow.

**Comparison Table (0.5 mark):**

| Feature | NLDM | CCS | ECSM |
|---------|------|-----|------|
| **Vendor** | Synopsys | Synopsys | Cadence |
| **Accuracy at 28nm** | Poor | High | High |
| **Miller effect** | Not modeled | Modeled | Modeled |
| **File size** | Small | Large | Large |
| **STA tool support** | Universal | PrimeTime | Tempus |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Students Lose Marks in PCEVT602 Module 4 Questions:**
>
> 1. **❌ Confusing hard and soft macros** in Part A — 1 to 2 marks lost per question if you mix up "node-specific" vs. "synthesizable."
> 2. **❌ Forgetting units in numerical problems** — always show SI conversion ($fF \to F$, $MHz \to Hz$). KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 3. **❌ Missing one of the 7 model types** in list-type questions — examiners expect all 7 (functional, physical, timing, power, parasitic, PVT, statistical). A partial list loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **❌ Saying "FF is the best corner"** — FF is **worst for hold**, not "best." This conceptual error can cost up to 3 marks.
> 5. **❌ Not drawing a block diagram** when explicitly asked — Part B (a) without a diagram typically loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **❌ Using `α` (alpha) without defining it** as the activity factor — always define all symbols before substituting.
> 7. **❌ Confusing NLDM with CCS** — NLDM is **2D LUT (delay vs. slew and load)**, CCS is **current waveform**. Writing "NLDM uses current waveforms" is a 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Model Development for Hard Macros**

- 🔹 A **hard macro** is a frozen, node-specific GDSII block with abstract models for SoC integration.
- 🔹 A **soft macro** is portable RTL; a **firm macro** is a netlist + floorplan.
- 🔹 The **7 essential models** are: Functional, Physical (LEF), Timing (Liberty), Power, Parasitic (SPEF), PVT Corner, and Statistical/Reliability.
- 🔹 **LEF** = physical/geometry abstraction (pins, OBS, density).
- 🔹 **Liberty `.lib`** = timing + power model (NLDM 2D LUTs; CCS/ECSM for advanced nodes).
- 🔹 **SPEF** = RC parasitic network from extraction.
- 🔹 **NLDM** = delay & slew as 2D LUTs (input slew, output load).
- 🔹 **CCS / ECSM** = current-waveform based, accurate at sub-28nm.
- 🔹 **PVT = Process × Voltage × Temperature**; **5 standard corners: TT, FF, SS, SF, FS**.
- 🔹 **FF corner** is critical for **hold-time**, **SS corner** for **setup-time**.
- 🔹 Switching power: $P_{sw} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2} f_{clk}$ — scales **quadratically** with $V_{DD}$.
- 🔹 Elmore delay: $T_{D} = \frac{1}{2} R_{wire} C_{wire}$ — scales as $L^{2}$ with wire length.
- 🔹 **LVF (Liberty Variation Format)** and **AOCV** model **statistical process variation**.
- 🔹 **NBTI / HCI / TDDB** are aging mechanisms — degrade $V_{TH}$ over **10-year lifetime**.
- 🔹 **LEF is abstracted from GDSII** using Innovus/ICC2 abstract generators.
- 🔹 **Liberty is generated from SPICE** using SiliconSmart/Liberate.
- 🔹 **Validation criterion:** `.lib` vs. SPICE error must be **< 2%** for sign-off.
- 🔹 Examples of hard macros: **SRAM compilers, PLL, DLL, ADC, DAC, SerDes, USB PHY, I/O pads, hard CPU cores**.
- 🔹 Hard macro model package is the **"user manual"** of the IP — quality determines timing closure, power estimation, and yield.
- 🔹 Always state **units** in numerical answers; always define **symbols** before substitution; always draw a **block diagram** when asked.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soft Macro Deliverables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT602 - SYSTEM ON CHIP DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Soft Macro Deliverables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Soft Macro** (also called a *soft IP core* or *synthesizable IP*) is a pre-designed, pre-verified, technology-independent description of a reusable logic block delivered in the form of **synthesizable Hardware Description Language (HDL) code** (Verilog, SystemVerilog, or VHDL). Unlike hard macros (which are layout-specific, GDSII-level) or firm macros (which are gate-level netlists), a soft macro remains a **behavioral/RTL-level abstraction** that must be synthesized, placed, and routed by the integrator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soft Macro Deliverables** constitute the complete package of artifacts, documentation, and verification infrastructure that an IP vendor (or in-house design team) must hand over to the SoC integrator to enable seamless integration of the macro into a larger chip. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this is mapped to **CO4 (Design SoC Subsystems using IP Integration Methodologies)**.

The defining technical characteristics of a soft macro are:
- **Technology-Independent**: Not bound to a specific foundry, process node, or standard cell library.
- **Synthesizable**: Source code is written in plain HDL at the RTL level.
- **Parameterized**: Typically generated via generators or written with `generate` constructs to allow width/depth configurability.
- **Re-targetable**: Can be re-synthesized for any technology library.
- **Portable**: The same source can be reused across multiple projects, foundries, and process nodes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Soft Macro is like a "Recipe" rather than a "Pre-baked Cake"**
>
> Imagine you want a chocolate cake delivered to your door.
> - A **Hard Macro** is a *finished, decorated cake* — exactly as you see it, but you cannot change the flavor, size, or ingredients. It is locked to one bakery's oven.
> - A **Firm Macro** is a *cake batter mixed and ready* — almost final, but you bake it in your own oven with slight adjustments possible.
> - A **Soft Macro** is the *recipe itself* — a precise set of ingredients and instructions. You can bake it in ANY oven, scale it up or down, substitute ingredients (e.g., sugar-free), and tailor it to your own kitchen. However, you must do the baking (synthesis), shaping (placement), and decoration (routing) yourself.
>
> This is why the *deliverables* of a soft macro must include not just the "recipe" (RTL code) but also the *cooking instructions, nutritional info, ingredient substitutions list, and a sample tasting report* — i.e., synthesis scripts, constraints, testbenches, and documentation.

## 1.3 Why Soft Macros Matter in SoC Design

Modern SoCs integrate hundreds of IP blocks. **Time-to-market pressure** mandates that design teams *purchase or reuse* pre-verified blocks instead of designing from scratch. Soft macros offer the **maximum flexibility** for customization, making them the preferred form of IP reuse for application-specific SoCs (ASIC-SoCs) where the target process is not yet finalized at design kickoff.

The metric of **Design Reuse Ratio (DRR)** — defined as:

$$\text{DRR} = \frac{\text{Lines of Reused RTL Code}}{\text{Total Lines of RTL Code}} \times 100\%$$

directly measures the productivity gain. Industry-standard SoCs target a DRR above **70%**, achievable only when soft macro deliverables are robust and complete.

## 1.4 Categories of Soft Macro Deliverables (Overview)

The deliverable set can be classified into **six primary categories**:

1. **RTL Source Code** (the design itself)
2. **Verification Artifacts** (testbenches, assertions, coverage)
3. **Synthesis Deliverables** (scripts, constraints, reports)
4. **Documentation** (datasheet, integration manual, release notes)
5. **Support Files** (lint waivers, waiver files, headers)
6. **Example Instantiations & Reference Designs**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly identifies the deliverables under three sub-modules: *Design Documentation, Verification Deliverables, and Integration Constraints*. The remainder of these notes elaborates each one in detail.

## 1.5 Key Industry-Standard Acronyms (Will Be Used Throughout)

- **RTL** — Register Transfer Level
- **HDL** — Hardware Description Language
- **IP** — Intellectual Property
- **EDA** — Electronic Design Automation
- **SDC** — Synopsys Design Constraints
- **UPF** — Unified Power Format
- **CDV** — Coverage-Driven Verification
- **ABV** — Assertion-Based Verification
- **BFM** — Bus Functional Model
- **DUT** — Design Under Test
- **COB** — Coverage Of Branch
- **FSM** — Finite State Machine
- **API** — Application Programming Interface

## 1.6 Comparison: Soft vs. Firm vs. Hard Macro

| Parameter | Soft Macro | Firm Macro | Hard Macro |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Form** | RTL / Behavioral HDL | Gate-level Netlist | Layout (GDSII) |
| **Technology Node** | Independent | Target-specific | Fixed |
| **Flexibility** | Highest | Medium | Lowest |
| **Predictability** | Lowest (synthesis-dependent) | Medium | Highest |
| **Performance / Area / Power (PPA)** | Variable | Better | Best (optimized) |
| **Portability** | Highest | Limited | None |
| **Time to Integrate** | Longest (synthesis required) | Medium | Shortest |
| **Typical Use Case** | Early design exploration, prototype SoCs | Mid-stage design, retargeting | Final SoC tape-out blocks |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Soft Macro Deliverable Package

A complete soft macro release is a structured, version-controlled bundle. The KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes it under the following structural hierarchy:

### 2.1.1 Layer 1 — RTL Source Code Deliverables

- **HDL Source Files** (`.v`, `.sv`, `.vhdl`, `.vhd`): Synthesizable, lint-clean, comment-rich RTL.
- **Header / Include Files** (`.vh`, `.svh`, `.pkg`): Parameter definitions, macros, typedefs.
- **Behavioral Models** (often non-synthesizable): Used for fast simulation; may be supplied as a separate file with `_behav` suffix.
- **Encryption-Protected Source** (optional): Vendors may ship encrypted RTL (e.g., using Synopsys `encrypt` or IEEE P1735) for protection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why encrypt?** When an IP vendor wants to share a soft macro without exposing the implementation logic, the HDL is encrypted. The integrator receives a decryption key bound to a specific EDA tool and a specific technology node, preserving IP security.

### 2.1.2 Layer 2 — Verification Deliverables

The verification package must support standalone testing of the macro. Required artifacts include:

- **Directed Testbenches** (`.sv`): Hand-written test cases for specific scenarios.
- **Constrained-Random Testbenches**: Using SystemVerilog Randomization (`rand`, `randc`) with seed control.
- **Reference Models** (often in C/C++/SystemC): Golden models for comparison.
- **Bus Functional Models (BFMs)**: For protocol-aware stimulus generation.
- **Checkers / Scoreboards**: Monitor and compare DUT output vs. reference.
- **Functional Coverage Models**: Covergroups, coverpoints, cross-coverage.
- **Assertion Libraries (PSL/SVA)**: For Assertion-Based Verification.
- **Regression Scripts**: Automate re-running of all tests.
- **Coverage Reports**: HTML/text reports of code, toggle, and functional coverage.

### 2.1.3 Layer 3 — Synthesis Deliverables

- **Synthesis Scripts** (`.tcl`, `.scr`): Tool-specific (Design Compiler, Genus, Yosys).
- **SDC Constraints** (`.sdc`): Clock definitions, I/O delays, false paths, multicycle paths.
- **UPF / CPF Files** (`.upf`, `.cpf`): Power intent specification for multi-voltage domains.
- **Multi-Mode Multi-Corner (MMMC) configurations**.
- **Synthesis Reports**: Area, timing, QoR snapshots in plain text.
- **Mapped Netlist** (optional): If the vendor supplies a *firm* version alongside.
- **Scan Insertion Scripts** (for DFT — Design for Testability).

### 2.1.4 Layer 4 — Documentation Deliverables

- **IP Datasheet**: Marketing-grade summary — features, interfaces, performance.
- **Integration Manual**: Step-by-step integration guide.
- **User Guide**: Programming/configuration interface description.
- **Architecture Specification**: Internal micro-architecture explanation.
- **Verification Plan**: Test plan, coverage goals, methodology.
- **Release Notes**: Version history, bug fixes, known issues.
- **Errata Sheet**: Documented deviations from datasheet specifications.
- **Licensing Agreement**.

### 2.1.5 Layer 5 — Example & Reference Design

- **Top-level Wrapper Example** showing typical instantiation.
- **SoC-level Testbench** with the macro instantiated.
- **Sample Synthesis Results** in a reference technology.
- **Sample Place-and-Route Results** (where possible).

## 2.2 Soft Macro Quality Metrics

The quality of a soft macro is judged by:

1. **Code Quality** — Lint-clean (e.g., SpyGlass, Leda), zero synthesis warnings, clean FSM encoding.
2. **Verification Completeness** — Functional coverage > 95%, all assertions pass, zero critical bugs open.
3. **Synthesis Robustness** — Timing closure at target frequency across all PVT corners.
4. **Reusability** — Parameterized, configurable, easily portable.
5. **Documentation Completeness** — Every register, port, and feature documented.
6. **Supportability** — Maintenance window, bug-fix SLA, version control.

## 2.3 Synthesis Constraint Flow (Conceptual)

The synthesis of a soft macro follows a deterministic flow:

1. **Read RTL** — HDL files loaded into synthesis tool.
2. **Apply Constraints** — SDC file parsed; clock period, I/O delays set.
3. **Elaborate** — Tool infers hierarchy, FSMs, RAMs, arithmetic operators.
4. **Apply Design Optimizations** — Constant propagation, dead code removal, retiming.
5. **Map to Technology** — Generic gates mapped to standard cells of target library.
6. **Optimize for Timing / Area / Power** — Iterative refinement.
7. **Generate Reports** — Area, timing, power, congestion.
8. **Output Netlist + SDC** — For downstream P&R.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the most important quantitative metrics for evaluating soft macro deliverables. **No vertical pipes appear inside table cells** — all magnitude indicators use `\vert` notation to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Metric | Formula | Target / Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Clock Period** | $T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}}$ | e.g., 10 ns for 100 MHz | ns |
| **Setup Slack** | $S_{setup} = T_{required} - T_{arrival}$ | $\geq 0$ | ns |
| **Hold Slack** | $S_{hold} = T_{arrival} - T_{required}$ | $\geq 0$ | ns |
| **Design Reuse Ratio (DRR)** | $\text{DRR} = \frac{\text{Reused RTL Lines}}{\text{Total RTL Lines}} \times 100\%$ | $\geq 70\%$ | % |
| **Code Coverage** | $\text{CC} = \frac{\text{Executed Lines}}{\text{Total Lines}} \times 100\%$ | $\geq 95\%$ | % |
| **Functional Coverage** | $\text{FC} = \frac{\text{Hit Coverpoints}}{\text{Total Coverpoints}} \times 100\%$ | $\geq 95\%$ | % |
| **Toggle Coverage** | $\text{TC} = \frac{\text{Toggled Nets}}{\text{Total Nets}} \times 100\%$ | $\geq 90\%$ | % |
| **Assertion Coverage** | $\text{AC} = \frac{\text{Passing Assertions}}{\text{Total Assertions}} \times 100\%$ | $100\%$ for safety | % |
| **Fault Coverage (DFT)** | $\text{FC}_{DFT} = \frac{\text{Detected Faults}}{\text{Total Faults}} \times 100\%$ | $\geq 95\%$ | % |
| **Test Coverage** | $\text{TCov} = \frac{\text{Tested Patterns}}{\text{Total Patterns}} \times 100\%$ | $\geq 98\%$ | % |
| **Synthesis Area** | $A_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i \cdot a_i$ | Tech-specific | $\mu m^2$ or gates |
| **Dynamic Power** | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f$ | Minimize | W or mW |
| **Static Power** | $P_{stat} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{dd}$ | Minimize | W or mW |
| **Gates-to-Equivalents (GE)** | $\text{GE} = \frac{A_{macro}}{A_{NAND2}}$ | Reference metric | — |
| **Lines per Day (LPD)** | $\text{LPD} = \frac{\text{Total Lines}}{\text{Project Days}}$ | Productivity metric | Lines/day |
| **Bug Escape Rate** | $\text{BER} = \frac{\text{Post-silicon Bugs}}{\text{Total Pre-silicon Bugs}}$ | $\leq 0.1$ | bugs |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Soft macro deliverables are the **currency of the IP-reuse economy**. Companies like Arm, Synopsys, Cadence, Imagination Technologies, and CEVA earn billions by licensing soft IP cores. A well-prepared soft macro package:

- **Reduces SoC development time by 6–18 months.**
- **Enables retargeting** across foundry processes (e.g., TSMC 7nm → 5nm → 3nm) by simple re-synthesis.
- **Permits late-stage customization** — clock dividers, FIFO depths, bus widths can be parameterized at instantiation time.
- **Lowers risk** — pre-verified IP reduces the probability of re-spinning silicon.
- **Drives standardization** — common interfaces (AMBA AXI, AHB, APB, Wishbone) make integration plug-and-play.

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU B.Tech PCEVT602 examination, questions on soft macro deliverables often ask students to *list and explain* the package contents, *justify* the inclusion of each artifact, and *analyze* the impact on SoC design flow. The next sections equip you with model solutions for such questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Critical Path Timing for a Soft Macro

To understand the synthesis deliverable, consider a typical soft macro — say, a 32-bit adder. We derive the maximum operating frequency.

**Step 1 — Identify the critical path.**
For a carry-ripple adder, the critical path traverses the carry chain:

$$t_{crit} = t_{setup} + t_{carry} \cdot N + t_{sum}$$

where $t_{setup}$ is the flip-flop setup time, $t_{carry}$ is the delay of one full-adder carry stage, $N$ is the bit-width, and $t_{sum}$ is the final sum computation delay.

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values for a 32-bit adder in a typical 28 nm library.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{setup} &= 0.10 \text{ ns} \\
t_{carry} &= 0.15 \text{ ns} \\
N &= 32 \\
t_{sum} &= 0.20 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the critical path delay.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{crit} &= t_{setup} + t_{carry} \cdot N + t_{sum} \\
&= 0.10 + (0.15 \cdot 32) + 0.20 \\
&= 0.10 + 4.80 + 0.20 \\
&= 5.10 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Derive the maximum operating frequency.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{max} &= \frac{1}{t_{crit}} \\
&= \frac{1}{5.10 \times 10^{-9}} \\
&\approx 196.08 \text{ MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with a carry-lookahead adder (CLA) critical path.**

For a CLA, the critical path is logarithmic in $N$:

$$
t_{crit,CLA} = t_{setup} + t_{pg} \cdot \log_2(N) + t_{sum,CLA}
$$

where $t_{pg}$ is the propagate/generate logic delay. For $N = 32$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{crit,CLA} &= 0.10 + 0.30 \cdot \log_2(32) + 0.25 \\
&= 0.10 + 0.30 \cdot 5 + 0.25 \\
&= 0.10 + 1.50 + 0.25 \\
&= 1.85 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{max,CLA} &= \frac{1}{1.85 \times 10^{-9}} \\
&\approx 540.54 \text{ MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the speedup factor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Speedup} &= \frac{f_{max,CLA}}{f_{max,RCA}} \\
&= \frac{540.54}{196.08} \\
&\approx 2.76 \times
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation illustrates **why a soft macro vendor must provide an SDC file with a target clock period** — without it, the integrator has no way of knowing whether the macro will meet timing in their SoC. The synthesis deliverable is the *contract* between IP vendor and integrator.

## 3.2 Derivation: Design Reuse Ratio for an SoC

Consider a SoC with three soft macros and one custom block:

- **Macro A (CPU core):** 50,000 lines of RTL.
- **Macro B (DDR controller):** 20,000 lines of RTL.
- **Macro C (GPU):** 80,000 lines of RTL.
- **Custom glue logic:** 10,000 lines of RTL.

**Step 1 — Compute total RTL lines.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= L_A + L_B + L_C + L_{glue} \\
&= 50{,}000 + 20{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 10{,}000 \\
&= 160{,}000 \text{ lines}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute reused lines (from soft macros).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{reused} &= L_A + L_B + L_C \\
&= 50{,}000 + 20{,}000 + 80{,}000 \\
&= 150{,}000 \text{ lines}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Design Reuse Ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DRR} &= \frac{L_{reused}}{L_{total}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{150{,}000}{160{,}000} \times 100\% \\
&= 93.75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

A DRR of 93.75% indicates that **almost the entire RTL codebase is reused IP**, which is typical of modern application processors. The custom glue logic constitutes only 6.25% of the design effort.

## 3.3 Derivation: Functional Coverage Closure

Consider a soft macro with the following coverage points:

| Coverpoint Type | Defined | Hit | Pending |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reset Values | 8 | 8 | 0 |
| FSM States | 5 | 4 | 1 |
| Bus Transactions | 12 | 10 | 2 |
| Error Conditions | 6 | 5 | 1 |
| **Total** | **31** | **27** | **4** |

**Step 1 — Compute the Functional Coverage.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FC} &= \frac{\text{Hit Coverpoints}}{\text{Total Coverpoints}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{27}{31} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 87.10\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify gaps.**

The four uncovered coverpoints indicate either untested FSM transitions, unused bus transaction types, or untriggered error paths. The verification team must:
- Add directed tests for the uncovered FSM state.
- Generate constrained-random traffic to hit the missing bus transactions.
- Inject fault scenarios to trigger the remaining error condition.

**Step 3 — Re-compute the goal.**

The IP release specification typically mandates $\text{FC} \geq 95\%$. The team must close the remaining **4 coverpoints** (i.e., reach $30/31 = 96.77\%$) before tape-out sign-off.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Deliverables to KTU Course Outcomes

The following matrix maps each soft macro deliverable category to the relevant KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes (COs) for PCEVT602.

| Deliverable Category | Primary CO | Cognitive Level (Bloom) | Engineering Skill Tested |
|---|---|---|---|
| RTL Source Code | CO4 — Design SoC Subsystems | Apply / Create | HDL coding, design partitioning |
| Synthesis Scripts (SDC) | CO5 — Optimize PPA | Analyze / Evaluate | Constraint authoring, timing analysis |
| Verification Testbench | CO4 — Verify IP Functionality | Apply / Evaluate | Test writing, coverage closure |
| Documentation | CO6 — Communicate Design | Understand / Apply | Technical writing |
| Power Intent (UPF) | CO5 — Manage Power Domains | Analyze | Multi-voltage design |
| DFT Insertion | CO5 — Enable Manufacturability | Apply | Scan, BIST, ATPG |
| Integration Example | CO4 — Integrate IP into SoC | Apply / Create | System-level assembly |
| Encryption & Licensing | CO6 — Protect IP | Understand | IP rights management |

## 3.5 Worked Example: SDC Constraint for a Soft Macro

A soft macro has a single clock `clk` of frequency 200 MHz driving a 16-bit counter feeding a comparator.

**Step 1 — Define the clock period.**

$$
T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{200 \times 10^6} = 5 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the clock uncertainty (jitter + skew).**

$$
\delta_{clk} = \delta_{jitter} + \delta_{skew} = 0.10 + 0.05 = 0.15 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective clock period for the macro.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{eff} &= T_{clk} - \delta_{clk} \\
&= 5.00 - 0.15 \\
&= 4.85 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Set the SDC constraint.**

```tcl
create_clock -name clk -period 5.0 [get_ports clk]
set_clock_uncertainty 0.15 [get_clocks clk]
set_input_delay -clock clk -max 1.0 [get_ports data_in]
set_output_delay -clock clk -max 1.0 [get_ports count_out]
```

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

The integrator must ensure that the **input data arrives at least 1.0 ns before the clock edge** and that the **output count is valid at least 1.0 ns after the clock edge** to satisfy setup at the SoC level. These values are derived from the SDC file supplied in the soft macro deliverable.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Soft Macro Deliverable Architecture — Top-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Soft Macro Deliverable Package] --> B[RTL Source Code]
    A --> C[Verification Artifacts]
    A --> D[Synthesis Deliverables]
    A --> E[Power Intent Files]
    A --> F[Documentation]
    A --> G[Reference Design]
    A --> H[Licensing and Encryption]

    B --> B1[Synthesizable HDL]
    B --> B2[Header Files]
    B --> B3[Behavioral Models]
    B --> B4[Package Definitions]

    C --> C1[Testbench Source]
    C --> C2[Bus Functional Model]
    C --> C3[Reference Model]
    C --> C4[Coverage Database]
    C --> C5[Assertion Library]
    C --> C6[Regression Scripts]

    D --> D1[Synthesis Scripts TCL]
    D --> D2[SDC Constraints]
    D --> D3[Synthesis Reports]
    D --> D4[DFT Scan Scripts]

    E --> E1[UPF CPF Files]
    E --> E2[Power Domain Map]
    E --> E3[Isolation Cells]

    F --> F1[IP Datasheet]
    F --> F2[Integration Manual]
    F --> F3[Architecture Spec]
    F --> F4[Release Notes]
    F --> F5[Errata Sheet]

    G --> G1[Top Wrapper Example]
    G --> G2[Sample SoC Testbench]
    G --> G3[Sample PnR Results]

    H --> H1[License Agreement]
    H --> H2[Encryption Keys]
    H --> H3[Version Tags]
```

## 4.2 Soft Macro Integration Flow into a Larger SoC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Receive Soft Macro Package] --> S2[Decrypt and Extract]
    S2 --> S3[Read IP Datasheet and Manual]
    S3 --> S4[Instantiate in SoC RTL]
    S4 --> S5[Connect Interfaces AXI AHB APB]
    S5 --> S6[Run Lint and CDC Checks]
    S6 --> S7[Functional Simulation with Testbench]
    S7 --> S8[Synthesis with Vendor SDC]
    S8 --> S9[Place and Route]
    S9 --> S10[Timing Closure Signoff]
    S10 --> S11[Power Analysis with UPF]
    S11 --> S12[DFT Insertion and ATPG]
```
