# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Soft Macro Deliverables

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PCEVT602, Module 4]**

**Model Answer:**

Soft Macro Deliverables refer to the complete set of artifacts, documentation, and verification infrastructure provided by an IP vendor (or design team) along with a synthesizable HDL description of a reusable logic block. These deliverables include RTL source code, synthesis scripts and SDC constraints, verification testbenches, reference models, coverage reports, power intent files (UPF), documentation (datasheet, integration manual, architecture specification), and licensing/encryption materials. The objective is to enable the SoC integrator to seamlessly synthesize, verify, integrate, and deploy the macro into a larger chip.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip (3-mark question):** Students must mention at least *three categories* (RTL, Verification, Documentation) and the *purpose* of each. A one-line definition alone will fetch only 1 mark.

### Question 2: Differentiate Soft, Firm, and Hard Macros

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PCEVT602, Module 4]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Soft Macro | Firm Macro | Hard Macro |
|---|---|---|---|
| Abstraction | RTL / Behavioral HDL | Gate-level netlist | Physical layout (GDSII) |
| Technology Binding | Independent | Target-specific | Fully fixed |
| Flexibility | Highest | Medium | Lowest |
| Synthesis Required | Yes | No (already mapped) | No |
| Performance Predictability | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Typical Delivery | `.v`/`.sv` source + scripts | Netlist + SDC | GDSII + LEF/DEF |
| Portability Across Foundries | Yes | Limited | No |

A **soft macro** offers maximum flexibility and is technology-independent, but the integrator bears the burden of synthesis, placement, and routing. A **firm macro** is a gate-level netlist optimized for a specific technology, while a **hard macro** is a fully placed-and-routed layout that is ready for drop-in use.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PCEVT602, Module 4, Dec 2024 pattern]**

**(a)** Enumerate and explain in detail the various deliverables that constitute a complete soft macro package for an SoC design. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Discuss the role of synthesis deliverables (SDC constraints, synthesis scripts, and UPF files) in the soft macro integration flow. Illustrate with a sample SDC snippet for a clock-driven soft macro operating at 250 MHz. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

A complete soft macro package is organized into the following six deliverable categories:

**1. RTL Source Code (1.5 marks):**
- Synthesizable HDL files (Verilog/SystemVerilog/VHDL) describing the macro functionality.
- Header/include files defining parameters, macros, and typedefs.
- Behavioral (non-synthesizable) models for fast simulation.
- Optionally, encrypted RTL using IEEE P1735 to protect IP.

**2. Verification Artifacts (1.5 marks):**
- Testbenches (directed + constrained-random) using SystemVerilog.
- Bus Functional Models (BFMs) for protocol-aware stimulus.
- Reference models (often in C/C++/SystemC) for golden comparison.
- Functional coverage models and assertion libraries (SVA/PSL).
- Regression scripts and coverage databases.
- Verification plan outlining methodology, goals, and signoff criteria.

**3. Synthesis Deliverables (1.5 marks):**
- Synthesis scripts in TCL for tools like Design Compiler, Genus, or Yosys.
- SDC constraints defining clocks, I/O delays, false paths, multicycle paths, and clock groups.
- Multi-Mode Multi-Corner (MMMC) configuration files.
- UPF/CPF power intent files for multi-voltage domain designs.
- DFT scripts for scan insertion and ATPG preparation.
- Sample synthesis reports (area, timing, power) in reference technology.

**4. Power Intent (UPF) Files (0.5 mark):**
- Define power domains, supply ports, isolation cells, level shifters, and retention strategies.
- Enable multi-voltage, power-gating-aware synthesis.

**5. Documentation (1.5 marks):**
- IP Datasheet (features, performance, interfaces, electrical specs).
- Integration Manual (instantiation, port connections, clock/reset requirements).
- User Guide (configuration registers, programming model).
- Architecture Specification (internal micro-architecture).
- Verification Plan and Release Notes.
- Errata Sheet documenting deviations from datasheet.

**6. Example & Reference Design (0.5 mark):**
- Top-level wrapper showing typical instantiation.
- Sample SoC testbench with the macro integrated.
- Sample synthesis and place-and-route results in a reference technology node.
- License agreement and version-control tags.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Award 1 mark for naming all six categories correctly; 1 mark for explaining the role of RTL and Verification; 1 mark for Synthesis and Power; 1 mark for Documentation; 0.5 mark for reference design; remaining 2.5 marks for depth, examples, and logical flow.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Role of Synthesis Deliverables in Soft Macro Integration (3 marks):**

The synthesis deliverables are the *contractual interface* between the soft macro vendor and the SoC integrator. They specify *how* the macro must be synthesized, what timing constraints apply, and how power intent is managed. The key artifacts are:

- **SDC Constraints:** Define the clocking structure, I/O timing, false paths, and multicycle paths. The integrator uses the SDC to perform top-level synthesis and timing closure.
- **Synthesis Scripts (TCL):** Automate the synthesis flow, library setup, optimization goals, and report generation. They ensure consistent, reproducible synthesis results.
- **UPF/CPF Files:** Specify the power architecture — domains, isolation, level shifting, retention, and power states — enabling the integrator to perform power-aware synthesis.

Without these deliverables, the integrator faces *unknown timing behavior*, *unpredictable area/power*, and *integration risk*. The synthesis package ensures the macro is **predictable, repeatable, and portable** across technology nodes.

**Sample SDC for a 250 MHz Clock-Driven Soft Macro (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute the clock period.**

$$
T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{250 \times 10^6} = 4.0 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2 — Set clock uncertainty.**

$$
\delta_{clk} = 0.20 \text{ ns} \quad \text{(jitter + skew)}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective clock period.**

$$
T_{eff} = 4.0 - 0.20 = 3.80 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 4 — SDC Snippet.**

```tcl
# Define the primary clock
create_clock -name sys_clk -period 4.0 [get_ports clk]

# Define generated clocks if any
# create_generated_clock -name div_clk -source [get_ports clk] \
#                        -divide_by 2 [get_ports clk_div_out]

# Set clock uncertainty to model jitter and skew
set_clock_uncertainty 0.20 [get_clocks sys_clk]

# Set input delay for data inputs
set_input_delay -clock sys_clk -max 0.80 [get_ports data_in*]
set_input_delay -clock sys_clk -min 0.20 [get_ports data_in*]

# Set output delay for data outputs
set_output_delay -clock sys_clk -max 0.80 [get_ports data_out*]
set_output_delay -clock sys_clk -min 0.20 [get_ports data_out*]

# Set false paths for asynchronous reset
set_false_path -from [get_ports rst_n]

# Set multicycle path if applicable
# set_multicycle_path -setup 2 -from [get_pins reg_a/D] -to [get_pins reg_b/D]

# Set maximum transition and capacitance
set_max_transition 0.15 [current_design]
set_max_capacitance 0.20 [current_design]
```

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

- The clock `sys_clk` is defined with a period of **4.0 ns** (250 MHz).
- An uncertainty of **0.20 ns** models jitter and skew, leaving **3.80 ns** for the actual logic delay.
- Input data must arrive at least **0.80 ns before** the clock edge.
- Output data becomes valid at least **0.80 ns after** the clock edge.
- The asynchronous reset is declared a **false path** (no timing check required).
- The synthesis tool will optimize the design to meet all these constraints.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Award 2 marks for the role explanation, 1 mark for the SDC snippet (basic structure), 1 mark for the timing analysis ($\delta_{clk}$, $T_{eff}$), and 1 mark for the proper formatting and comments in the SDC.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PCEVT602, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the soft macro verification deliverables in detail. Describe the components of a typical testbench architecture and illustrate with a block diagram. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A soft macro vendor releases a 32-bit FIFO IP. The IP uses 4096 memory locations and operates at 200 MHz. Compute the maximum data throughput. If the FIFO is parameterized with a depth parameter, explain how the soft macro deliverable supports parameterization. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Verification Deliverables Overview (2 marks):**

Verification deliverables are the set of artifacts that enable the SoC integrator to verify the functional correctness of the soft macro before and after integration. They include:

- **Testbench source code** (SystemVerilog/Verilog/VHDL).
- **Bus Functional Models (BFMs)** — generate protocol-aware stimulus.
- **Reference models** — golden behavioral models for comparison.
- **Stimulus generators** — directed or constrained-random.
- **Monitors and Checkers** — observe DUT behavior.
- **Scoreboards** — compare DUT output with expected output.
- **Coverage models** — covergroups, coverpoints, cross-coverage.
- **Assertion libraries (SVA/PSL)** — for Assertion-Based Verification (ABV).
- **Regression scripts** — automate test execution.
- **Coverage reports** — text/HTML reports of code, toggle, and functional coverage.
- **Verification plan** — document outlining methodology and signoff criteria.

**Testbench Architecture (3 marks):**

A typical testbench for a soft macro consists of the following blocks:

1. **Stimulus Generator:** Produces input vectors — either directed (hand-written) or constrained-random (using `rand`/`randc` qualifiers).
2. **Bus Functional Model (BFM):** Encapsulates protocol logic (e.g., AXI, AHB) and translates high-level transactions into pin-level activity.
3. **Design Under Test (DUT):** The soft macro instance.
4. **Reference Model:** A golden model (often in C/C++/SystemC) that computes expected output.
5. **Monitor:** Observes DUT output signals and captures transactions.
6. **Checker / Scoreboard:** Compares DUT output with reference model output; reports mismatches.
7. **Coverage Collector:** Samples coverage points during simulation.
8. **Assertion Engine:** Evaluates SVA/PSL assertions at runtime.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    STIM[Stimulus Generator] --> BFM[BFM]
    BFM --> DUT[Soft Macro DUT]
    DUT --> MON[Monitor]
    REF[Reference Model] --> SB[Scoreboard]
    MON --> SB
    SB --> DEC{Pass Fail}
    DUT --> ASRT[Assertion Engine]
    REF --> ASRT
    STIM --> COV[Coverage Collector]
    MON --> COV
    BFM --> COV
    COV --> REP[Coverage Report]
    DEC --> REG[Regression Suite]
    REP --> REG
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** A block diagram earns 2 marks if all eight blocks are shown with correct dataflow. Mention of constrained-random, BFM, and coverage model is essential.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- FIFO width: 32 bits = 4 bytes.
- FIFO depth: 4096 locations.
- Operating frequency: 200 MHz.

**Step 1 — Compute the clock period.**

$$
T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{200 \times 10^6} = 5.0 \text{ ns}
```

**Step 2 — Compute the maximum data throughput (one transfer per clock cycle).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Throughput}_{bytes/sec} &= \text{width} \times f_{clk} \\
&= 4 \text{ bytes} \times 200 \times 10^6 \\
&= 800 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/sec} \\
&= 800 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Express in bits per second.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Throughput}_{bits/sec} &= 32 \text{ bits} \times 200 \times 10^6 \\
&= 6400 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/sec} \\
&= 6.4 \text{ Gbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the time to fill the FIFO completely.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{fill} &= \text{depth} \times T_{clk} \\
&= 4096 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-9} \\
&= 20.48 \text{ \mu s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Parameterization in the soft macro deliverable (3 marks):**

A soft macro supports parameterization using HDL constructs like `parameter` (Verilog) or `generic` (VHDL). The macro is delivered with default parameters, and the integrator can override them at instantiation time:

```verilog
module sync_fifo #(
    parameter WIDTH = 32,
    parameter DEPTH = 4096,
    parameter ADDR_WIDTH = $clog2(DEPTH)
) (
    input  wire              clk,
    input  wire              rst_n,
    input  wire              wr_en,
    input  wire [WIDTH-1:0]  wr_data,
    output wire              full,
    input  wire              rd_en,
    output wire [WIDTH-1:0]  rd_data,
    output wire              empty
);
    // ... RTL body ...
endmodule
```

**Integrator instantiation with custom parameters:**

```verilog
sync_fifo #(
    .WIDTH(64),
    .DEPTH(8192),
    .ADDR_WIDTH(13)
) u_custom_fifo (
    .clk(soc_clk),
    .rst_n(soc_rst_n),
    .wr_en(soc_wr_en),
    .wr_data(soc_wr_data),
    .full(soc_fifo_full),
    .rd_en(soc_rd_en),
    .rd_data(soc_rd_data),
    .empty(soc_fifo_empty)
);
```

**Key parameterization features of the soft macro deliverable:**
- The deliverable ships with **sensible defaults** (32-bit, 4096 deep) but is **fully overridable**.
- The synthesis scripts must include a **commented template** for non-default instantiations.
- The datasheet must list all parameters with their **legal ranges and impact on area/timing**.
- The verification testbench must include **parameterized tests** that sweep depth and width to catch corner-case bugs (e.g., depth = 1, depth = 2, very wide).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Award 1 mark each for throughput calculation (parts 1–3), 1 mark for fill-time, and 3 marks for parameterization explanation with code snippet.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Soft Macro Deliverable Questions:**
>
> 1. **One-line answers for "define" questions:** Examiners allocate 2 of 3 marks for *elaboration*. A bare definition like "soft macro is an RTL block" earns only 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing RTL deliverables with verification deliverables:** Students often merge them. Remember: RTL = the *design*; Verification = the *testbench, coverage, and assertions*.
> 3. **Ignoring UPF/CPF:** Power intent is a **mandatory** deliverable in modern SoC flows. Omitting it costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting documentation:** Datasheet, Integration Manual, and Errata Sheet are often overlooked. They account for ~1.5 marks in a 7-mark question.
> 5. **No numerical analysis in long-answer questions:** Always *derive* the clock period, slack, throughput, or DRR with formulas — a qualitative answer alone loses 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Skipping the encryption/licensing aspect:** In KTU 2024 Scheme, IP protection (IEEE P1735 encryption, licensing models) is in the syllabus — failing to mention it loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 7. **Mixing up soft/firm/hard macro definitions:** Examiners often test this distinction. Memorize the table in Section 1.6.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Soft Macro Deliverables (Module 4)**
>
> - **Definition:** Soft macro = technology-independent, synthesizable HDL IP; deliverables = complete package handed to integrator.
> - **Six Deliverable Categories:** RTL Source Code, Verification Artifacts, Synthesis Deliverables, Power Intent (UPF/CPF), Documentation, Example/Reference Design + Licensing.
> - **RTL includes:** `.v`/`.sv` files, headers, behavioral models, optional encryption (IEEE P1735).
> - **Verification includes:** Testbenches (directed + random), BFMs, reference models, coverage models, assertions (SVA/PSL), regression scripts, coverage reports.
> - **Synthesis includes:** TCL scripts, SDC constraints, MMMC configs, UPF/CPF, DFT scripts, sample reports.
> - **Documentation includes:** Datasheet, Integration Manual, User Guide, Architecture Spec, Verification Plan, Release Notes, Errata Sheet.
> - **SDC Key Constructs:** `create_clock`, `set_clock_uncertainty`, `set_input_delay`, `set_output_delay`, `set_false_path`, `set_multicycle_path`, `set_max_transition`, `set_max_capacitance`.
> - **Key Formulas to Memorize:** $T_{clk} = 1/f_{clk}$, $f_{max} = 1/t_{crit}$, $\text{DRR} = L_{reused}/L_{total} \times 100\%$, $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{dd}^2 f$, $\text{FC} = \text{Hit}/\text{Total} \times 100\%$.
> - **Quality Metrics:** Code Coverage $\geq 95\%$, Functional Coverage $\geq 95\%$, Toggle Coverage $\geq 90\%$, DRR $\geq 70\%$, Fault Coverage $\geq 95\%$.
> - **Soft vs Firm vs Hard:** Soft = RTL (most flexible), Firm = Netlist (medium), Hard = GDSII (most predictable).
> - **Parameterization:** Use `parameter` (Verilog) or `generic` (VHDL) to make the macro configurable; integrator overrides at instantiation.
> - **Power Intent:** UPF (Unified Power Format) is the IEEE 1801 standard for specifying power domains, isolation, and level shifting.
> - **Encryption Standard:** IEEE P1735 is the standard for HDL encryption to protect IP while allowing synthesis.
> - **Industry Interfaces to Know:** AMBA AXI, AHB, APB (Arm); Wishbone (OpenCores); TileLink (RISC-V).
> - **Verification Methodology:** Coverage-Driven Verification (CDV) + Assertion-Based Verification (ABV) is the industry standard.
> - **Real-World Examples:** Arm Cortex-M0 (soft core), Synopsys DesignWare, Cadence IP Portfolio, OpenCores (open-source soft cores).
> - **KTU 2024 CO Mapping:** Soft macro deliverables align with **CO4 (Design SoC Subsystems using IP Integration Methodologies)**, **CO5 (Optimize PPA)**, and **CO6 (Communicate Design)**.
> - **Examiner's Mantra:** Always list categories → explain role → give a numerical example → close with a real-world relevance statement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hard Macro Deliverables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEVT602 - SYSTEM ON CHIP DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Hard Macro Deliverables # Hard Macro Deliverables — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Macro in SoC Design? In the System-on-Chip (SoC) ecosystem, a **macro** (also called an **IP block** or **Intellectual Property core**) is a reusable, pre-verified functional block that is integrated into a larger chip design. KTU 2024 Scheme classifies macros into three major families based on their level of physical abstraction and the flexibility they offer to the integrator: > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > A **Hard Macro** is a fully placed, routed, and physically verified IP block delivered with a frozen layout (GDSII), complete with a fixed footprint, pin positions, timing, and power characteristics. It behaves as a **black-box physical object** to the integrator — the internal layout is opaque, but its interface to the rest of the SoC is fully characterized. | Macro Type | Form of Delivery | Flexibility | Risk to Integrator | |---|---|---|---| | **Soft Macro** | Synthesizable RTL (Verilog/VHDL) | Very High (process-portable) | High (timing/area unpredictable) | | **Firm Macro** | Gate-level netlist + floorplan constraints | Medium | Medium | | **Hard Macro** | GDSII + LEF + Liberty + CDL | Low (process-locked) | Low (fully characterized) | > [!NOTE] > The phrase **"Hard Macro Deliverables"** refers to the complete, signed-off set of files, models, views, reports, and documentation that the IP vendor hands over to the SoC integrator, enabling seamless plug-and-play integration of the macro into a chip-level implementation flow. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Think of designing a modern kitchen at home. You have three ways to source your **refrigerator**: - **Soft Macro:** You receive a *blueprint* (circuit diagram) of the refrigerator. You must build it yourself with available tools, materials, and labor. The final size, weight, and power draw are estimates until you actually build it. - **Firm Macro:** You receive a *pre-assembled frame* with the major compartments placed, but you must wire the electrical circuits and connect the plumbing yourself. - **Hard Macro:** You receive a **fully assembled, tested, certified refrigerator** — already painted, leveled, with labeled ports for water and power, and a specification sheet that tells you **exactly** how much space it occupies, how much heat it generates, and how much current it draws. You just slide it into the designated slot in your kitchen and connect the labeled plugs. A **Hard Macro Deliverable** is exactly that — the *fully assembled refrigerator* plus the **datasheet, user manual, installation guide, and warranty certificate** that the SoC integrator (the kitchen designer) needs to integrate it flawlessly. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Reality:** Once a hard macro is delivered, the IP vendor **does not change the internal layout**. Any post-silicon bug fix is impossible without a costly **metal/spin revision**. Hence, the deliverables must be **bulletproof** before sign-off. --- ## 1.3 Why Hard Macros are Used in Modern SoCs - **Time-to-Market Pressure:** Custom-designing every block (CPU core, DDR PHY, PLL, ADC, SerDes) from scratch is infeasible within 12–18 month tape-out windows. - **Yield Predictability:** Hard macros are pre-characterized for process corners, so the chip-level timing closure becomes a **block-level timing aggregation** problem. - **Heterogeneous Integration:** Modern SoCs mix logic CMOS, RF, High-Voltage I/O, MRAM, and FinFET — these **must** be delivered as hard macros because each requires a different process recipe. - **Licensing & Business Model:** Companies like Arm, Synopsys, Cadence, and TSMC monetize hard IP (e.g., Arm Artisan Physical IP, TSMC PDK hard macros). > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics in Bold** that govern hard macro acceptance: > - **Operating Process Node** (e.g., 28 nm, 16 nm, 7 nm, 5 nm, 3 nm) > - **Supply Voltage (V_DD):** typically **0.7 V – 1.2 V** for core, **1.8 V / 3.3 V** for I/O > - **Operating Frequency (f_clk):** typically **100 MHz – 5 GHz** depending on macro > - **Power Budget (P_total):** typically **µW to mW** range > - **Process Corners:** **SS / TT / FF** at **−40°C / 25°C / 125°C** # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Hard Macro Design Closure Flow A hard macro reaches "deliverable" status only after passing the following sequential closure steps. Each step produces a **specific deliverable artifact** (file or document). > [!NOTE] > Every step below is **mandatory** before the macro can be signed off as a hard deliverable under the KTU 2024 outcome-based assessment framework. | # | Closure Step | Purpose | Resulting Deliverable Artifact | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Logic Synthesis** | Convert RTL → Gate-level netlist | Netlist (CDL/Verilog gate-level) | | 2 | **Floorplanning** | Define macro dimensions, pin positions | Floorplan DEF / LEF MACRO | | 3 | **Placement** | Place standard cells inside the core | Placed DEF | | 4 | **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)** | Distribute clock with low skew | Routed clock structure (in DEF) | | 5 | **Routing** | Connect all signals with metal layers | Fully routed GDSII | | 6 | **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** | Verify setup/hold across PVT | Timing Reports (.lib, SDF) | | 7 | **Power Analysis** | Compute dynamic & leakage power | Power Reports (CPF/UPF, .lib) | | 8 | **Physical Verification (DRC/LVS/ERC)** | Verify foundry rules | Clean DRC/LVS/ERC reports | | 9 | **Antenna & EM/IR Check** | Check for manufacturing reliability | Antenna & EM/IR clean report | | 10 | **Sign-off Documentation** | Bundle all artifacts | Datasheet, Integration Guide | --- ## 2.2 The Complete List of Hard Macro Deliverables A **production-grade hard macro deliverable package** typically consists of the following items, organized into seven functional groups: ### Group A — Physical Design Files 1. **GDSII (`.gds` / `.gdsii`)** — The full mask layout database, the ultimate physical representation of the macro. All metal layers, vias, poly, diffusion, and pins are described. 2. **LEF (Library Exchange Format) — MACRO view** (`.lef`): Contains the abstract physical view used during chip-level placement and routing. It specifies: - Macro dimensions (width × height in microns) - Pin geometry, layer, and offset - Blockage and obstruction layers - Site symmetry 3. **DEF (Design Exchange Format)** (`.def`): Optionally provided for detailed routing reuse. 4. **FRAM View (`.fram`)** — Used by Cadence Innovus for floorplan-level integration. 5. **OA (OpenAccess) database** (`.oa`): Synopsys ICC2 / Cadence Genus compatible. ### Group B — Logical Netlist & Connectivity 6. **CDL (Circuit Description Language) netlist** (`.cdl`): Transistor-level SPICE netlist for LVS. 7. **Verilog gate-level netlist** (`.v`): Used for functional simulation, gate-level STA, and DFT. 8. **VHDL netlist** (`.vhd`): For VHDL-based design environments. 9. **VEC (Verilog Electrical Connectivity) file**: Contains power/ground connectivity. ### Group C — Timing Models 10. **Liberty (`.lib`)** — Synopsys standard timing/power library. Contains: - Cell (here, macro-level) delay, transition, and power lookup tables as a function of input slew and output load. - PVT derating factors. 11. **SDF (Standard Delay Format)** (`.sdf`): Back-annotated timing for gate-level simulation. 12. **ECSMD / ECT (Effective Current Source Model)** — Advanced current-source based timing model for sub-28 nm nodes. ### Group D — Power Models 13. **CPF (Common Power Format)** (`.cpf`): Power intent specification. 14. **UPF (Unified Power Format)** (`.upf`): IEEE 1801 standard power intent. 15. **Power Report**: Static & dynamic power breakdown across corners. ### Group E — Signal Integrity & Parasitics 16. **SPEF (Standard Parasitic Exchange Format)** (`.spef`): RC parasitics for back-annotated STA. 17. **DSPF (Detailed Standard Parasitic Format)** (`.dspf`): Detailed RC for critical paths. 18. **IBIS (I/O Buffer Information Specification)** (`.ibs`): I/O buffer behavioral model for signal integrity and board-level simulation. ### Group F — Verification & Test 19. **DRC (Design Rule Check) report** — Clean against foundry rule deck. 20. **LVS (Layout vs. Schematic) report** — Layout matches schematic netlist. 21. **ERC (Electrical Rule Check) report** — Power/ground shorts, floating gates. 22. **DFT (Design-for-Test) files** — Scan chains, BIST patterns, ATPG vectors. 23. **Test Pattern Files** (`.wgl`, `.stil`, `.atpg`): For ATE (Automatic Test Equipment). ### Group G — Documentation 24. **Datasheet**: Operating conditions, electrical specs, AC/DC characteristics. 25. **Integration Guide**: How to integrate the macro into a chip-level PnR flow. 26. **User Manual**: Functional description, configuration registers, programming model. 27. **Errata Sheet**: Known limitations and workarounds. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (with Units) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes **every formula** you are expected to know for examination purposes when dealing with hard macro power, timing, and area acceptance metrics. | # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Description / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Dynamic Power** | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$ | $\alpha$ = switching activity, $C_L$ = load capacitance (F), $V_{DD}$ in V, $f$ in Hz | | 2 | **Static (Leakage) Power** | $P_{stat} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$ | $I_{leak}$ in A, $V_{DD}$ in V → Power in W | | 3 | **Total Power** | $P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{stat}$ | Sum of dynamic and leakage | | 4 | **Setup Time Slack** | $T_{slack} = T_{cycle} - (T_{ck2q} + T_{logic} + T_{setup} + T_{skew})$ | Must be $\geq 0$ for timing closure | | 5 | **Hold Time Slack** | $T_{hold,slack} = (T_{ck2q} + T_{logic}) - (T_{hold} + T_{skew})$ | Must be $\geq 0$ | | 6 | **Maximum Clock Frequency** | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{cycle,min}}$ | Where $T_{cycle,min}$ is the smallest feasible clock period | | 7 | **Buffer Insertion Delay (Elmore)** | $T_{delay} \approx \sum_{i} R_{i} \cdot C_{i}$ | Sum of RC products along a path | | 8 | **Energy per Operation** | $E_{op} = P_{total} \cdot t_{op}$ | Useful for low-power SoC benchmarking (Joules) | | 9 | **Power-Delay Product (PDP)** | $PDP = P_{dyn} \cdot T_{delay}$ | Energy-quality metric of a logic family | | 10 | **Yield (Poisson model)** | $Y = e^{-D \cdot A}$ | $D$ = defect density (per cm²), $A$ = die area in cm² | | 11 | **Derating Factor** | $V_{derated} = V_{nom} \times (1 - k_{v})$ | $k_v$ = voltage derate fraction (e.g., 10%) | | 12 | **Temperature Derate** | $T_{j} = T_{a} + P_{total} \cdot \theta_{JA}$ | $T_j$ = junction, $T_a$ = ambient, $\theta_{JA}$ = thermal resistance (°C/W) | | 13 | **Effective Channel Length** | $L_{eff} = L_{drawn} - 2L_{diff}$ | For short-channel MOS modeling | | 14 | **Areal Capacitance** | $C_{area} = C_{ox}^{'} \cdot W \cdot L$ | $C_{ox}^{'}$ = oxide capacitance per unit area | | 15 | **Interconnect Delay** | $T_{interconnect} \approx 0.38 \cdot R_{wire} \cdot C_{wire}$ | Elmore approximation for a distributed RC line | --- ## 2.4 The Role of Each Deliverable in the SoC Flow A hard macro deliverable is **not just a GDSII file**. Each file has a specific consumer: > [!NOTE] > The **integrator** (chip-level PnR engineer) is the primary customer of a hard macro. They need to know: > - **Where** the macro goes → **LEF** > - **How it behaves** functionally → **Verilog/VHDL model** > - **When signals arrive** → **Liberty / SDF** > - **How much power it draws** → **Liberty / CPF / UPF** > - **How to verify it after placement** → **SPEF, GDSII** > - **How to manufacture it** → **Clean DRC/LVS/ERC reports** > - **How to test it in production** → **Test patterns, BIST documentation** ### Real-World Utility In industry, a hard macro is integrated using the **block-level to chip-level** methodology. Tools used include: - **Cadence Innovus / Genus, Synopsys ICC2 / Design Compiler, Siemens Calibre** for PnR and verification. - **PrimeTime (Synopsys) / Tempus (Cadence)** for STA. - **StarRC, Quantus** for parasitic extraction. - **Calibre / IC Validator / Pegasus** for physical verification. The acceptance gate for a hard macro delivery includes sign-off from: - **Foundry** (DRC/LVS on their rule deck) - **SoC Integration team** (PVT-clean STA at chip level) - **Package team** (IBIS model, ball-map compliance) - **Test team** (DFT coverage, ATE-ready patterns) - **Reliability team** (EM/IR, ESD, latch-up clean) # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Scenario: Power Budget Validation of a Hard Macro > **Problem Statement (KTU ESE-style):** > A hard SRAM macro is delivered with the following specifications: > - Operating voltage: $V_{DD} = 0.85$ V > - Operating frequency: $f_{clk} = 1.2$ GHz > - Switching activity: $\alpha = 0.15$ > - Total effective switched capacitance: $C_L = 250$ fF > - Leakage current: $I_{leak} = 320$ µA > - Ambient temperature: $T_a = 45$ °C > - Thermal resistance: $\theta_{JA} = 28$ °C/W > > **Required:** Compute (a) Dynamic Power, (b) Static Power, (c) Total Power, (d) Junction Temperature, (e) Energy per clock cycle, (f) Whether the macro is thermally safe if $T_{j,max} = 95$ °C. ### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Solution #### (a) Dynamic Power $P_{dyn}$ The KTU 2024-scheme energy equation for a CMOS digital macro is: $$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$$ Substituting the given values (note: $1 \text{ fF} = 10^{-15}$ F): $$P_{dyn} = 0.15 \times (250 \times 10^{-15}) \times (0.85)^{2} \times (1.2 \times 10^{9})$$ Step-by-step numerical evaluation: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{DD}^{2} &= (0.85)^{2} = 0.7225 \ \text{V}^{2} \\ \alpha \cdot C_{L} &= 0.15 \times 250 \times 10^{-15} = 37.5 \times 10^{-15} \ \text{F} \\ \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} &= 37.5 \times 10^{-15} \times 0.7225 = 27.09375 \times 10^{-15} \\ P_{dyn} &= 27.09375 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.2 \times 10^{9} \\ P_{dyn} &= 32.5125 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{W} \\ P_{dyn} &\approx 32.51 \ \mu\text{W} \end{aligned} $$ > **Conversion logic:** Multiplication of femto-Farads ($\times 10^{-15}$) with GHz ($\times 10^{9}$) yields micro-Watts ($\times 10^{-6}$) because $10^{-15} \times 10^{9} = 10^{-6}$. #### (b) Static Power $P_{stat}$ $$P_{stat} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$$ $$P_{stat} = (320 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.85 = 272 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{W} = 272 \ \mu\text{W}$$ #### (c) Total Power $P_{total}$ $$P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{stat} = 32.51 \ \mu\text{W} + 272 \ \mu\text{W} = 304.51 \ \mu\text{W}$$ #### (d) Junction Temperature $T_j$ $$T_{j} = T_{a} + P_{total} \cdot \theta_{JA}$$ $$T_{j} = 45 + (304.51 \times 10^{-6}) \times 28$$ $$T_{j} = 45 + 8.526 \times 10^{-3} = 45.0085 \ ^\circ\text{C}$$ #### (e) Energy per Clock Cycle $E_{cycle}$ The clock period is: $$T_{cycle} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{1.2 \times 10^{9}} = 0.8333 \ \text{ns}$$ Therefore the energy consumed per cycle is: $$E_{cycle} = P_{total} \cdot T_{cycle} = (304.51 \times 10^{-6}) \times (0.8333 \times 10^{-9})$$ $$ \begin{aligned} E_{cycle} &= 304.51 \times 0.8333 \times 10^{-15} \\ E_{cycle} &\approx 253.75 \times 10^{-15} \ \text{J} \\ E_{cycle} &\approx 253.75 \ \text{fJ/cycle} \end{aligned} $$ #### (f) Thermal Safety Check $$T_{j} = 45.0085 \ ^\circ\text{C} \quad \text{vs.} \quad T_{j,max} = 95 \ ^\circ\text{C}$$ Since $T_{j} \ll T_{j,max}$, the macro is **thermally safe** with a margin of approximately $49.99 \ ^\circ\text{C}$. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Scenario: Setup-Time Slack Validation > **Problem Statement:** > A hard macro is integrated into a chip with the following timing path parameters: > - Clock period: $T_{cycle} = 2.0$ ns > - Clock-to-Q delay of the launching flop: $T_{ck2q} = 120$ ps > - Combinational logic delay (including macro internal + inter-block routing): $T_{logic} = 1.25$ ns > - Setup time of the capturing flop: $T_{setup} = 90$ ps > - Clock skew (capture – launch): $T_{skew} = 60$ ps > > **Required:** Calculate the setup slack and determine if timing closure is achieved. ### Solution The setup time slack equation (from Section 2.3) is: $$T_{slack} = T_{cycle} - (T_{ck2q} + T_{logic} + T_{setup} + T_{skew})$$ Substituting the values (in picoseconds, $1$ ns $= 1000$ ps): $$T_{slack} = 2000 - (120 + 1250 + 90 + 60)$$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Delay sum} &= 120 + 1250 + 90 + 60 = 1520 \ \text{ps} \\ T_{slack} &= 2000 - 1520 = 480 \ \text{ps} \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** $T_{slack} = +480$ ps $\geq 0$, so the path **meets setup timing** with a positive margin. The path can absorb up to 480 ps of additional delay (e.g., due to voltage drop or aging) before failing. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: File → Tool → Purpose The following matrix is the **single most important reference** a KTU ESE student should memorize for the Hard Macro Deliverables topic: | # | Deliverable File | Extension | Primary Tool Consumer | Purpose / Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | GDSII | `.gds` | Foundry (Mask shop) | Mask data for fabrication | | 2 | LEF MACRO | `.lef` | Innovus, ICC2, OpenROAD | Abstract physical model for PnR | | 3 | Verilog netlist | `.v` | VCS, ModelSim, Genus | Functional simulation & DFT | | 4 | CDL netlist | `.cdl` | Calibre LVS, HSPICE | Transistor-level LVS & SPICE sim | | 5 | Liberty | `.lib` | PrimeTime, Tempus | STA, power, noise analysis | | 6 | SDF | `.sdf` | VCS, ModelSim | Back-annotated gate-level sim | | 7 | SPEF | `.spef` | PrimeTime, Tempus | RC parasitic-based STA | | 8 | IBIS | `.ibs` | HyperLynx, SI-Wave | Signal integrity at package/board | | 9 | UPF/CPF | `.upf`, `.cpf` | ICC2, Innovus, Genus | Power intent (multi-rail, power gating) | | 10 | DRC report | `.rpt` | Calibre, IC Validator | Manufacturing rule compliance | | 11 | LVS report | `.rpt` | Calibre | Layout-vs-Schematic match | | 12 | ERC report | `.rpt` | Calibre | Electrical rule compliance | | 13 | ATPG vectors | `.stil`, `.wgl` | Tessent, DFT Compiler, ATE | Production test | | 14 | BIST patterns | `.bin` | Tessent, MBIST | Built-in self-test | | 15 | Datasheet | `.pdf` | Integrator / Product team | Specification reference | | 16 | Integration Guide | `.pdf` | Integrator | Tool flow & constraint setup | | 17 | OA Database | `.oa` | Synopsys IC Compiler II | Native format for direct integration | --- ## 3.4 Worst-Case Derivation: Maximum Frequency of Operation Given the timing path in Section 3.2, compute the **maximum clock frequency** the macro can tolerate at the chip level. The minimum allowable clock period is: $$T_{cycle,min} = T_{ck2q} + T_{logic} + T_{setup} + T_{skew}$$ $$T_{cycle,min} = 120 + 1250 + 90 + 60 = 1520 \ \text{ps} = 1.52 \ \text{ns}$$ Therefore, the maximum operating frequency is: $$f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{cycle,min}} = \frac{1}{1.52 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$ \begin{aligned} f_{max} &= 0.6579 \times 10^{9} \ \text{Hz} \\ f_{max} &\approx 657.9 \ \text{MHz} \end{aligned} $$ > **Conclusion:** If the SoC intends to operate at 1.0 GHz clocking the macro, the timing budget is **violated** since $f_{clk} = 1.0$ GHz $> f_{max} = 657.9$ MHz. The integrator must either reduce $f_{clk}$ to below 657.9 MHz, perform **pipeline insertion** in the macro boundary, or request a **higher-speed hard macro variant** (called a "speed bin") from the IP vendor. # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hard Macro Design & Delivery Lifecycle (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[RTL Design

Why is a hard macro preferred for analog and mixed-signal IPs?** **Model Answer:** A **hard macro** is delivered as a fully placed, routed, and verified GDSII layout with a frozen physical footprint, characterized timing (Liberty), and power models. Its internal layout is opaque to the integrator. A **firm macro** is delivered as a gate-level netlist with a floorplan and partial layout; the integrator performs placement and routing, allowing some physical flexibility. A **soft macro** is delivered as synthesizable RTL (Verilog/VHDL). The integrator synthesizes, places, and routes the block, giving maximum flexibility but no timing or area guarantees until implementation is complete. **Why hard macros are preferred for analog/mixed-signal:** - Analog circuits (PLLs, ADCs, SerDes) have **strict device matching, symmetry, and parasitic dependencies** that are destroyed if placement changes. - The layout is hand-crafted for **noise isolation, shielding, and guard rings**. - Re-synthesizing analog cells is **impossible** because they are not cell-based. - Hard macros guarantee the **same silicon-proven performance** every time. > **Mark Distribution:** Definition of 3 types (2 marks), justification (1 mark). --- > **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > **List any six mandatory deliverables of a hard macro and state the role of the LEF file in the chip-level Place-and-Route flow.** **Model Answer:** **Six mandatory deliverables:** 1. **GDSII** – Mask layout database for fabrication. 2. **LEF** – Abstract physical view for PnR. 3. **Liberty (.lib)** – Timing and power model. 4. **Verilog/VHDL netlist** – Functional model. 5. **CDL netlist** – Transistor-level netlist for LVS. 6. **SPEF** – RC parasitic model for back-annotated STA. 7. *(Bonus)* UPF/CPF, IBIS, DRC/LVS reports, Datasheet. **Role of LEF in PnR:** The **LEF (Library Exchange Format)** provides an **abstracted** description of the hard macro that the chip-level PnR tool (Innovus/ICC2) uses **without revealing the internal layout**. It specifies: - The **bounding box** of the macro (width × height in microns). - The **pin geometry** (metal layer, offset, direction) for routing. - **Blockage and obstruction layers** that prevent the router from placing cells or wires inside the macro. - **Site definition** for row alignment. - **Symmetry information** to allow mirroring during floorplanning. > **Mark Distribution:** Listing deliverables (1.5 marks), LEF role explanation (1.5 marks). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) > **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** ### (a) Explain the complete list of hard macro deliverables with their file extensions and primary consumers. Classify them into physical, logical, timing, power, signal integrity, and documentation groups. (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** The hard macro deliverable package consists of **seven functional groups**: **Group 1 — Physical Design Files:** - **GDSII** (`.gds`): Full mask layout consumed by the foundry mask shop. - **LEF** (`.lef`): Abstract physical view consumed by PnR tools (Innovus, ICC2). - **FRAM** (`.fram`): Floorplan block view consumed by Cadence tools. - **OA Database** (`.oa`): Native Synopsys database. **Group 2 — Logical Netlists:** - **Verilog** (`.v`): Behavioral and gate-level netlist for simulation and DFT. - **VHDL** (`.vhd`): VHDL-based netlist. - **CDL** (`.cdl`): Transistor-level netlist for LVS and SPICE. **Group 3 — Timing Models:** - **Liberty** (`.lib`): PVT-aware timing and power model for STA. - **SDF** (`.sdf`): Back-annotated delay for gate-level simulation. **Group 4 — Power Models:** - **UPF** (`.upf`): IEEE 1801 power intent. - **CPF** (`.cpf`): Common Power Format. - **Power Report** (`.rpt`): Static and dynamic power breakdown. **Group 5 — Signal Integrity & Parasitics:** - **SPEF** (`.spef`): Reduced RC parasitics. - **DSPF** (`.dspf`): Detailed RC parasitics for critical nets. - **IBIS** (`.ibs`): I/O buffer model for board-level signal integrity. **Group 6 — Verification & Test:** - **DRC Report** (`.rpt`): Manufacturing rule compliance. - **LVS Report** (`.rpt`): Layout-vs-Schematic match. - **ERC Report** (`.rpt`): Electrical rule check. - **ATPG Vectors** (`.stil`, `.wgl`): Production test patterns. - **BIST Patterns** (`.bin`): Built-in self-test programs. **Group 7 — Documentation:** - **Datasheet** (`.pdf`): Electrical and operational specifications. - **Integration Guide** (`.pdf`): Tool flow and constraints. - **User Manual** (`.pdf`): Programming model and register descriptions. - **Errata Sheet** (`.pdf`): Known limitations. > **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark per group (approximately), with full marks for naming files and identifying consumers. --- ### (b) A hard macro operates at $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V, clock frequency $f_{clk} = 500$ MHz, switching activity $\alpha = 0.2$, total load capacitance $C_L = 1$ pF, and leakage current $I_{leak} = 1.5$ mA. The package thermal resistance is $\theta_{JA} = 40$ °C/W and ambient temperature is $T_a = 25$ °C. Calculate (i) dynamic power, (ii) static power, (iii) total power, (iv) junction temperature, and (v) energy per clock cycle. Is the macro thermally safe if $T_{j,max} = 85$ °C? (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **(i) Dynamic Power:** $$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$$ $$P_{dyn} = 0.2 \times (1 \times 10^{-12}) \times (1.0)^{2} \times (500 \times 10^{6})$$ $$ \begin{aligned} P_{dyn} &= 0.2 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 5 \times 10^{8} \\ &= 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{W} = 100 \ \mu\text{W} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Static Power:** $$P_{stat} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD} = (1.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 1.0 = 1.5 \ \text{mW}$$ **(iii) Total Power:** $$P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{stat} = 0.1 \ \text{mW} + 1.5 \ \text{mW} = 1.6 \ \text{mW}$$ **(iv) Junction Temperature:** $$T_{j} = T_{a} + P_{total} \cdot \theta_{JA} = 25 + (1.6 \times 10^{-3}) \times 40$$ $$T_{j} = 25 + 0.064 = 25.064 \ ^\circ\text{C}$$ **(v) Energy per Clock Cycle:** $$T_{cycle} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{500 \times 10^{6}} = 2 \ \text{ns}$$ $$E_{cycle} = P_{total} \cdot T_{cycle} = (1.6 \times 10^{-3}) \times (2 \times 10^{-9}) = 3.2 \ \text{pJ/cycle}$$ **Thermal Safety:** $T_{j} = 25.064 \ ^\circ\text{C} \ll T_{j,max} = 85 \ ^\circ\text{C}$. The macro is **thermally safe** with a large margin of approximately 59.94 °C. > **Mark Distribution:** (i) 1.5, (ii) 1, (iii) 1, (iv) 1.5, (v) 1, conclusion 1. --- > **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** ### (a) With a neat diagram, explain the hard macro design and delivery lifecycle from RTL to tape-out. Highlight the role of sign-off checks. (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** The hard macro design and delivery lifecycle proceeds in **six major phases**: **Phase 1 — RTL Design & Synthesis:** The macro's functional specification is captured in Verilog/VHDL, simulated for functional correctness, and then synthesized to a gate-level netlist using a standard cell library. **Phase 2 — Physical Implementation:** Floorplanning establishes the macro dimensions, pin positions, and power grid. Placement positions the standard cells, Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS) builds a low-skew clock distribution network, and routing completes the metal interconnect. **Phase 3 — Sign-off Verification:** - **STA** verifies setup/hold across SS, TT, FF corners and −40 °C, 25 °C, 125 °C. - **Power analysis** confirms dynamic and leakage power within budget. - **DRC/LVS/ERC** confirm manufacturing and electrical rule compliance using the foundry's rule deck. - **Antenna and EM/IR checks** ensure reliability. - **Parasitic extraction** generates SPEF for back-annotation. **Phase 4 — Model Generation:** Liberty (.lib) files are generated for each PVT corner. SPEF and SDF are back-annotated. UPF/CPF documents the power intent. **Phase 5 — Documentation & Packaging:** Datasheet, Integration Guide, User Manual, and Errata Sheet are authored. The complete deliverable bundle is archived. **Phase 6 — Delivery to Integrator:** The integrator imports LEF and LIB into the chip-level PnR tool, places the macro, and runs top-level STA, power, and physical verification. *(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes for a visual representation.)* > **Mark Distribution:** Phases (4 marks), sign-off checks (2 marks), diagram (1 mark). --- ### (b) A setup-timing path through a hard macro has the following parameters: $T_{ck2q} = 100$ ps, $T_{logic} = 900$ ps, $T_{setup} = 80$ ps, $T_{skew} = 50$ ps, and $T_{cycle} = 1.5$ ns. Calculate (i) setup slack, (ii) maximum operating frequency, and (iii) recommend a design change if the target frequency is 1.0 GHz. (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **(i) Setup Slack:** $$T_{slack} = T_{cycle} - (T_{ck2q} + T_{logic} + T_{setup} + T_{skew})$$ $$T_{slack} = 1500 - (100 + 900 + 80 + 50) = 1500 - 1130 = +370 \ \text{ps}$$ The path **meets timing** with a 370 ps positive margin. **(ii) Maximum Frequency:** $$T_{cycle,min} = T_{ck2q} + T_{logic} + T_{setup} + T_{skew} = 1130 \ \text{ps} = 1.13 \ \text{ns}$$ $$f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{cycle,min}} = \frac{1}{1.13 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 884.96 \ \text{MHz}$$ **(iii) Recommendation for 1.0 GHz Target:** Since $f_{target} = 1.0$ GHz $> f_{max} = 884.96$ MHz, the path will **fail** timing. The recommended design changes are: 1. **Pipeline Insertion:** Add a flip-flop at the macro boundary to break the long combinational path, reducing $T_{logic}$ from 900 ps to approximately 450 ps. 2. **Macro Speed Bin Upgrade:** Request a **higher-speed variant** of the hard macro from the IP vendor (e.g., a "fast" or "ultra-fast" bin). 3. **Operating Corner Adjustment:** Operate the chip at a **lower voltage** (with reduced power) to relax the delay; though this is generally discouraged for performance-critical paths. 4. **Logic Restructuring:** Reduce the combinational depth through the macro boundary using **logic retiming** in the soft logic surrounding the macro. 5. **VT Swapping:** Replace high-Vt cells with low-Vt (or SVT) cells along the critical path to gain delay, accepting higher leakage as a trade-off. > **Mark Distribution:** (i) 2, (ii) 2, (iii) 3 marks for justification and recommendation. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting File Extensions:** Students frequently write "Liberty file" without specifying `.lib`. Always mention extensions — KTU examiners allocate half a mark per file. > 2. **Confusing LEF and GDSII:** LEF is an *abstract* (rectangles only, no internal detail); GDSII is the *full* layout with all polygons. Confusing them costs 1–2 marks. > 3. **Unit Mismatch in Power:** Mixing µW and mW without conversion is a frequent mistake. Always state the final unit explicitly. > 4. **Skipping Sign-off Stages:** Any answer that omits DRC/LVS/ERC or STA is considered incomplete and is penalized 1–2 marks. > 5. **Mixing Soft, Firm, Hard:** If the question asks specifically for *hard* macro deliverables, do not list soft-macro items like "Verilog RTL" — list only the **post-synthesis** gate-level netlist. > 6. **Forgetting IBIS Model:** A surprising number of students omit IBIS; it is mandatory for analog/mixed-signal hard macros with package-level I/O. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Hard Macro Deliverables** - ✅ A **hard macro** is a **fully laid-out, process-locked, pre-verified** IP block delivered as GDSII. - ✅ Macros are categorized as **Soft (RTL) → Firm (Netlist + floorplan) → Hard (GDSII)** in increasing physical rigidity. - ✅ A complete hard macro deliverable package has **seven groups**: Physical, Logical, Timing, Power, Signal Integrity, Verification/Test, and Documentation. - ✅ The **GDSII** is consumed by the **foundry**; the **LEF** is consumed by the **PnR tool**; the **Liberty (.lib)** is consumed by the **STA tool**. - ✅ **UPF (IEEE 1801)** is the modern standard for power intent; **CPF** is its predecessor still used in some legacy flows. - ✅ **SPEF** carries **reduced RC parasitics** for STA; **DSPF** carries detailed RC for critical nets. - ✅ **IBIS** models are required for **I/O buffers** and are used in **board-level signal integrity** simulation. - ✅ Hard macros require **DRC, LVS, ERC, Antenna, EM, IR** sign-off at the macro level itself; the integrator does not re-run them. - ✅ **Setup slack must be ≥ 0**; **hold slack must be ≥ 0**; both are evaluated across **SS, TT, FF** corners and **−40°C, 25°C, 125°C**. - ✅ **Dynamic power** scales as $V_{DD}^{2}$, which is why low-power designs aggressively reduce voltage. - ✅ **Leakage power** dominates in advanced nodes (≤ 28 nm); it is **independent of clock frequency**. - ✅ **Energy per cycle** ($E = P \cdot T_{cycle}$) is the key metric for **energy-efficient** SoC benchmarking. - ✅ The **junction temperature** is governed by $T_{j} = T_{a} + P_{total} \cdot \theta_{JA}$ and must remain below $T_{j,max}$. - ✅ **Hold-time violations** cannot be fixed by slowing the clock; they require **buffer insertion** in the data path. - ✅ **Speed bins** (slow / typical / fast) of the same hard macro exist for trade-offs between power and performance. - ✅ The **maximum operating frequency** of a macro is limited by the slowest setup path through it. - ✅ **Multi-rail/multi-Vt hard macros** require **UPF** to specify the power intent, including level shifters, isolation cells, and retention registers. - ✅ The **integration guide** is a non-technical but **critical** deliverable that helps the chip-level PnR engineer correctly instantiate the macro.
